title: Improving Code-Switching ASR with Code-Mixing Guided Synthetic Speech
summary: Code-switch (CS) Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) remains challenging due to limited availability of high quality CS text-speech pairs for training. Although synthetic data augmentation via Text-to-speech (TTS) has been explored, existing CS TTS approaches primarily optimise reconstruction fidelity and do not explicitly enforce language-boundary consistency, thereby limiting their effectiveness for CS ASR augmentation. This paper proposes a code-mixing guided preference-learning framework that steers synthetic speech generation toward improved code-switching fidelity using the Code Mixing Index (CMI). Experiments on the SEAME Mandarin-English conversational corpus demonstrate that the proposed method enhances the utility of synthetic data for ASR fine-tuning. Specifically, when fine-tuning Whisper Large, the proposed approach reduces Mixed Error Rate (MER) from 12.1%/17.8% to 8.9%/14.2% on the DevMAN and DevSGE sets, respectively.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a code-mixing guided preference-learning framework that uses the Code Mixing Index (CMI) to improve synthetic speech generation for code-switch ASR augmentation, reducing MER on Whisper Large from 12.1%/17.8% to 8.9%/14.2% on SEAME.
AI.motivation: Existing CS TTS approaches optimize reconstruction fidelity but do not explicitly enforce language-boundary consistency, limiting their effectiveness for CS ASR augmentation.
AI.method: A code-mixing guided preference-learning framework that uses the Code Mixing Index (CMI) to steer synthetic speech generation toward improved code-switching fidelity.
AI.result: When fine-tuning Whisper Large, the proposed approach reduces MER from 12.1%/17.8% to 8.9%/14.2% on DevMAN and DevSGE sets respectively.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method enhances the utility of synthetic data for CS ASR fine-tuning by explicitly enforcing code-switching fidelity through preference learning guided by CMI.

title: S-JEPA : Soft Clustering Anchors for Self-Supervised Speech Representation Learning
summary: Self-supervised speech encoders are predominantly trained by predicting discrete hard cluster IDs at masked positions, a recipe that collapses acoustic ambiguity at category boundaries and requires interrupting training to re-cluster the entire corpus between iterations. We introduce S-JEPA, a JEPA-style encoder-predictor pair trained to match the soft posteriors of a Gaussian Mixture Model at masked positions via KL divergence. Training runs as one continuous optimization trajectory in two phases: a fixed GMM over MFCC features, then an online GMM over encoder features, with the input layer selected adaptively from a label-free signal, removing both the offline re-cluster step and the hand-tuned choice of which transformer layer to cluster on. Under the SUPERB protocol, S-JEPA achieves the lowest WER among evaluated SSL methods below 90M parameters and matches HuBERT-Base on emotion recognition at roughly half its parameter count, establishing a new Pareto frontier without offline re-clustering or teacher distillation. An analysis of the predictor's per-frame entropy on held-out speech reveals a bimodal distribution with a substantial minority of frames near the entropy of a perfect two-cluster tie, providing direct empirical evidence that the soft-target objective preserves the acoustic ambiguity that hard targets would collapse. Code is available at https://github.com/gioannides/s-jepa.

AI.tldr: S-JEPA introduces a self-supervised speech encoder trained with soft GMM posteriors via KL divergence instead of hard cluster targets, removing offline re-clustering and achieving competitive WER and emotion recognition with fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: Existing self-supervised speech encoders rely on predicting discrete hard cluster IDs, which collapses acoustic ambiguity at category boundaries and requires interrupting training to re-cluster the entire corpus between iterations.
AI.method: A JEPA-style encoder-predictor trained to match soft posteriors of a Gaussian Mixture Model at masked positions via KL divergence, with a two-phase training: fixed GMM over MFCC features, then online GMM over encoder features, with adaptive input layer selection.
AI.result: Achieves lowest WER among SSL methods below 90M parameters under SUPERB; matches HuBERT-Base on emotion recognition at roughly half its parameter count; predictor entropy analysis reveals bimodal distribution with a minority of frames near perfect two-cluster tie entropy, confirming preserved acoustic ambiguity.
AI.conclusion: S-JEPA establishes a new Pareto frontier without offline re-clustering or teacher distillation, and provides direct empirical evidence that soft-target objectives preserve acoustic ambiguity that hard targets collapse.

title: Exploring Feature Extraction Technique Parameters for Acoustic Gunshot Classification
summary: Acoustic gunshot detection is a problem with applications across civilian public safety, military operations, and wildlife conservation, yet the field lacks a rigorous exploration of feature extraction techniques with a focus on generalization to realistic data. The mixed effectiveness of commercial gunshot detection and classification systems indicates an open problem that is not adequately addressed by the current literature. In this paper, we present a systematic investigation of common feature extraction techniques using a dataset of 23,000 gunshot recordings across 85 firearms and 21 calibers. We benchmark three feature extraction techniques with 12 total unique parameter sets using ResNet-18. Our results demonstrate that using the correct feature extraction technique can improve top-1 accuracy by up to 20%, and utilizing the correct parameters for a given feature extraction technique can improve that value by up to 4.7%.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically investigates feature extraction techniques for acoustic gunshot detection using a large dataset of 23,000 recordings across 85 firearms and 21 calibers, benchmarking three feature extraction techniques with 12 parameter sets on ResNet-18, finding that correct technique selection improves top-1 accuracy by up to 20% and parameter tuning adds up to 4.7% improvement.
AI.motivation: The field lacks rigorous exploration of feature extraction techniques for acoustic gunshot detection with a focus on generalization to realistic data, and commercial systems show mixed effectiveness, indicating an unresolved open problem.
AI.method: A systematic investigation of three feature extraction techniques with 12 total unique parameter sets, benchmarked using ResNet-18 on a dataset of 23,000 gunshot recordings across 85 firearms and 21 calibers.
AI.result: Correct feature extraction technique improves top-1 accuracy by up to 20%, and optimal parameter tuning within a technique yields an additional improvement of up to 4.7%.
AI.conclusion: Feature extraction technique choice and parameter tuning significantly impact acoustic gunshot detection performance, highlighting the need for careful selection to address the open problem.

title: FlowFake: Liquid Networks for Audio Deepfake Detection
comment: Accepted at the Workshop on Learning to Listen: Machine Learning for Audio at ICML 2026
summary: Audio deepfakes generated by neural text-to-speech and voice-cloning systems threaten speaker verification and public discourse at scale. The core challenge is cross-dataset generalization: detectors trained on one synthesis pipeline collapse on unseen forgeries. We argue that this failure is primarily because of structural synthetic speech artifacts which are multi-timescale trajectory anomalies. Though every existing detector aggregates a fixed-window frame statistics, this misaligns the architecture with the signal. We propose FlowFake, a Liquid Time-Constant (LTC) architecture whose hidden state evolves via a learned ODE, with per-neuron adaptive time constants simultaneously resolving spectral (10ms) and prosodic (2s) cues. At only 34K parameters FlowFake achieves formal BIBO stability and O(dt^4) integration error. On a four-dataset cross domain benchmark (ASVspoof2019-LA, FakeOrReal, InTheWild, MLAAD), FlowFake reaches 75.29% on ASVspoof2019 trained only on FakeOrReal and 79.97% trained only on MLAAD. It outperforms RawGAT-ST and Whisper-DF on every evaluated pair and matching SSL Wav2vec2 (300x larger) at 0.01% of its parameter count. The source code is available on : https://github.com/GhostRider2023/FlowFake

AI.tldr: FlowFake is a 34K-parameter deepfake audio detector using Liquid Time-Constant (LTC) ODE-based architecture with adaptive per-neuron time constants to capture multi-timescale synthetic speech artifacts, achieving state-of-the-art cross-dataset generalization and matching 300x larger SSL models at 0.01% parameter count.
AI.motivation: Existing audio deepfake detectors fail at cross-dataset generalization because they use fixed-window frame statistics that misalign with the multi-timescale nature of synthetic speech artifacts (spectral cues at ~10ms, prosodic cues at ~2s).
AI.method: FlowFake employs a Liquid Time-Constant (LTC) architecture where hidden states evolve via a learned ODE with per-neuron adaptive time constants, enabling simultaneous resolution of both spectral (10ms) and prosodic (2s) trajectory anomalies, with formal BIBO stability and O(dt^4) integration error.
AI.result: On a four-dataset cross-domain benchmark, FlowFake achieves 75.29% on ASVspoof2019 trained only on FakeOrReal and 79.97% trained only on MLAAD, outperforming RawGAT-ST and Whisper-DF on every evaluated pair, and matching Wav2vec2 (300x larger) at 0.01% of its parameter count.
AI.conclusion: The adaptive multi-timescale modeling via LTC ODE dynamics is a highly parameter-efficient solution for cross-domain audio deepfake detection, achieving strong generalization with only 34K parameters.

title: Supporting Design Decisions in Rule-Based Model Transformations
summary: Model Driven Engineering relies on model transformations to automate the derivation of target models or source code from source models. However, the design decisions that govern how source elements are mapped to target artifacts typically remain embedded in the transformation source code, limiting flexibility, reusability, and traceability. This paper proposes an approach to explicitly model and manage design decisions in rule-based model transformations. Design decisions are separated from transformation implementation through three mechanisms. First, a decision model captures design decisions and their options independently of any source modeling language for a given transformation domain. Second, a binding model connects these decisions to specific metamodel concepts in a given source modeling language, enabling reuse of the same design knowledge across transformations from similar languages. Third, a configuration model records the specific option chosen for each applicable source model element, with defaults pre-selected automatically. During execution, variability points in transformation rules are resolved dynamically according to configured choices. A trace model records which rules and options were applied to produce each target element. We establish a formal mathematical framework that defines the core concepts of variability-based transformations and proves key properties of the resulting transformations. The formal concepts are realized in a practical architecture of four interconnected artifact models. We demonstrate practical feasibility by extending an existing embedded domain-specific language for model-driven engineering with variability support and illustrate the approach with a complete ER-to-Relational transformation example.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a formal approach to explicitly model design decisions in rule-based model transformations by separating them into decision, binding, configuration, and trace models, enabling dynamic variability resolution and improved traceability.
AI.motivation: Design decisions in model transformations are typically embedded in transformation source code, limiting flexibility, reusability, and traceability.
AI.method: Three mechanisms: (1) a decision model capturing design decisions and options independently of source languages; (2) a binding model connecting decisions to metamodel concepts; (3) a configuration model recording chosen options per source element. Variability points in transformation rules are resolved dynamically, and a trace model records rule/option application. Formal mathematical framework and a practical architecture of four artifact models are provided.
AI.result: An extended embedded domain-specific language for MDE with variability support; demonstrated via a complete ER-to-Relational transformation example; formal proofs of key properties of variability-based transformations.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling design decisions improves flexibility, reusability, and traceability in model transformations, with formal guarantees and practical feasibility confirmed through implementation and case study.

title: GB-LSR: A Fast Local Spectral Image Representation with a Single Global Bandwidth for Continuous Reconstruction and Super-Resolution
summary: We present GB-LSR (Global-Bandwidth Local Spectral Representation), a fixed-grid local spectral representation for continuous image reconstruction. The image domain is partitioned into non-overlapping square patches, each carrying coefficients for a truncated Fourier basis predicted from shared convolutional-encoder features. A single trainable scalar bandwidth is shared globally across all patches and images, and reconstruction at any continuous coordinate is a fixed-size basis contraction whose cost is independent of image size. We study three bandwidth-handling variants: a trainable global scalar (main), a fixed global scalar, and a per-patch bandwidth field. On a standardized native-reconstruction benchmark across Kodak, Set14, and Urban100, the main variant outperforms matched-budget amortized LIIF / LTE / WIRE re-implementations by 2.8-3.6 dB PSNR and 0.11-0.15 LPIPS, while running at roughly one-quarter of the slowest baseline's inference cost. The single global scalar suffices empirically: per-patch adaptive-bandwidth alternatives do not improve over it on either a closed-form locality diagnostic or an end-to-end ablation. In a separate arbitrary-scale super-resolution (ASR) extension, GB-LSR achieves competitive PSNR-Y under a canonical-style SR protocol and runs 1.44x faster than LIIF-RDN and 3.25x faster than LTE-SwinIR at x4; within the same extension, a variant trained and evaluated without 4-corner local-ensemble averaging gives a 1.77x speedup with 35% lower peak memory and negligible PSNR change, while additionally widening the RDN encoder from 64 to 96 channels gives a small positive PSNR shift with a 1.58x speedup and 31% lower peak memory. Native-reconstruction claims are scoped to the matched-budget amortized protocol, and ASR claims are scoped to a separate canonical-style SR protocol.

AI.tldr: GB-LSR is a fixed-grid local spectral representation for continuous image reconstruction using globally-shared bandwidth and truncated Fourier bases, outperforming LIIF/LTE/WIRE by 2.8-3.6 dB PSNR at ~1/4 inference cost, with competitive arbitrary-scale super-resolution performance at 1.44-3.25x speedups.
AI.motivation: To develop a continuous image reconstruction method that balances representation quality and computational efficiency, overcoming limitations of prior implicit neural representations (LIIF, LTE, WIRE) which have high inference costs.
AI.method: Partitions image domain into non-overlapping square patches, each with truncated Fourier basis coefficients predicted from shared convolutional-encoder features. A single trainable global scalar bandwidth is shared across all patches. Three bandwidth variants are studied: trainable global scalar, fixed global scalar, and per-patch adaptive bandwidth.
AI.result: Outperforms matched-budget LIIF/LTE/WIRE by 2.8-3.6 dB PSNR and 0.11-0.15 LPIPS at ~1/4 inference cost. Per-patch adaptive bandwidth does not improve over global scalar. In ASR: 1.44x faster than LIIF-RDN, 3.25x faster than LTE-SwinIR at x4. Without local-ensemble averaging: 1.77x speedup, 35% lower memory. Wider RDN encoder: 1.58x speedup, 31% lower memory.
AI.conclusion: Global scalar bandwidth suffices empirically; per-patch adaptive bandwidth offers no improvement. GB-LSR achieves superior efficiency-accuracy tradeoffs for both native-reconstruction and arbitrary-scale super-resolution tasks.

title: Denoising Implicit Feedback for Cold-start Recommendation
summary: Implicit feedback is widely used in recommender systems due to its accessibility and generality, yet it usually presents noisy samples (e.g., clickbait, position bias). Meanwhile, recommenders inevitably face the item cold-start problem due to the continuous influx of new items. We identify that cold items are more prone to noisy samples due to the aforementioned factors, and researchers often overlook the significance of denoising implicit feedback for cold items. Previous denoising studies usually identify noisy samples based on heuristic patterns, such as higher loss values, and mitigate noise through sample selection or re-weighting. However, these methods have limited adaptability and are ineffective in cold-start scenarios. To achieve denoising implicit feedback for cold-start recommendation, we propose a model-agnostic denoising method called DIF. First, user preferences for content remain stable, which allows us to infer pseudo-labels indicating whether a user is interested in a cold item through content-similar warm items. Furthermore, to improve pseudo-label accuracy, we model the confidence of pseudo-labels based on the content similarity between the cold item and warm items, and then aggregate multiple pseudo-labels for each sample. Finally, we explicitly estimate the uncertainty of the noisy sample label by considering its relative entropy and the cold-start status of the item, which adaptively guides the role of pseudo-labels to correct the noisy labels at the sample level. DIF's superiority is supported by both theoretical justification and extensive experiments on real-world datasets. The method has been deployed on a billion-user scale short video application Kuaishou and has significantly improved various commercial metrics within cold-start scenarios.

AI.tldr: DIF is a model-agnostic denoising method for implicit feedback in cold-start recommendation, leveraging content-similar warm items to generate pseudo-labels, modeling confidence via content similarity, and estimating label uncertainty using relative entropy and cold-start status to correct noisy labels. It achieves significant improvements on real-world datasets and the Kuaishou platform.
AI.motivation: Cold-start items suffer from noisy implicit feedback (e.g., clickbait, position bias), but existing denoising methods (e.g., sample selection, re-weighting based on loss values) have limited adaptability and are ineffective for cold-start scenarios.
AI.method: (1) Infer pseudo-labels from content-similar warm items based on stable user preferences; (2) Model pseudo-label confidence using content similarity between cold and warm items; (3) Estimate noisy sample label uncertainty via relative entropy and item cold-start status to adaptively guide pseudo-label correction at the sample level.
AI.result: Theoretical justification and extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate DIF's superiority. Deployed on Kuaishou (billion-user scale), it significantly improved commercial metrics in cold-start scenarios.
AI.conclusion: DIF effectively denoises implicit feedback for cold-start recommendation by leveraging content-based pseudo-labels and uncertainty estimation, outperforming prior heuristic denoising approaches both theoretically and practically.

title: Scaling Generative Foundation Models for Chest Radiography with Rectified Flow Transformers
summary: We introduce the first generative foundation model for chest radiograph synthesis trained from scratch at the billion-parameter scale. Existing radiographic AI models often suffer from poor generalisation across patient subpopulations, institutions, and acquisition settings, resulting in limited real-world clinical utility. Controlled, high-fidelity synthesis of chest radiographs is a promising path toward diversifying clinical datasets and evaluating the robustness of diagnostic models. Therefore, we present the largest specialist generative foundation model for chest radiographs to date, with over 1.3B parameters, trained for 1.6T tokens on a curated, heterogeneous dataset comprising 1.2M radiographs and clinical expert-guided metadata. Our model supports controllable radiograph generation and editing across multiple demographic subgroups, acquisition views, and a dozen pathologies. Moreover, we significantly advance the state of the art in radiograph synthesis fidelity, producing images that are indistinguishable from real radiographs to clinical experts.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a 1.3B-parameter generative foundation model for chest radiograph synthesis, trained on 1.2M radiographs, supporting controllable generation across demographics, views, and pathologies, achieving state-of-the-art fidelity indistinguishable from real images to clinical experts.
AI.motivation: Existing radiographic AI models suffer from poor generalisation across patient subpopulations, institutions, and acquisition settings, limiting real-world clinical utility. Controlled, high-fidelity synthesis of chest radiographs can help diversify clinical datasets and evaluate diagnostic model robustness.
AI.method: A billion-parameter generative foundation model (1.3B parameters) trained from scratch on a curated, heterogeneous dataset of 1.2M radiographs and clinical expert-guided metadata, consuming 1.6T tokens during training. Supports controllable radiograph generation and editing across demographic subgroups, acquisition views, and multiple pathologies.
AI.result: The model produces chest radiographs that are indistinguishable from real radiographs to clinical experts, significantly advancing the state of the art in radiograph synthesis fidelity.
AI.conclusion: This is the largest specialist generative foundation model for chest radiographs, demonstrating that billion-parameter-scale generative models can achieve clinically indistinguishable synthesis quality, with controllability across key clinical dimensions.

title: Deontic Policies for Runtime Governance of Agentic AI Systems
comment: 10 pages, 1 figure. To be published in the 2026 IEEE Symposium on Agentic Services which is part of the IEEE Conference on Web Services
summary: Autonomous agentic AI systems driven by Large Language Models (LLMs) introduce a new class of security, privacy, and compliance challenges: an agent that can invoke tools, manipulate data, install software, and coordinate with peer agents across organizational boundaries must be constrained not just by authentication and access control, but by the full structure of enterprise governance. This includes specifying what agents are permitted and prohibited from doing, what they areobliged to do after certain actions (e.g., notify the CISO), under what conditions a standing obligation may be waived, and which rules take precedence when policies conflict. This governance problem exceeds what current policy engines provide. Systems such as XACML, Rego, and Cedar address only the permit/prohibit subset of this governance structure. They do not provide obligation lifecycle management, meta-policy conflict resolution, dispensations that waive obligations in specific circumstances, and ontological reasoning over domain class hierarchies commonly found in applications such as healthcare, cybersecurity, or data privacy. We propose AgenticRei, which realizes key governance requirements such as obligations, dispensations, policy conflict resolutions, and reasoning over policies, as well as the basic permit/prohibit constraints. We use a deontic policy language built on the Rei framework, expressed as OWL (Web Ontology Language) and evaluated at runtime by a high-performance logic engine entirely outside the LLM. The same pipeline governs both tool invocations by the agent and agent-to-agent messages. We show through examples that deontic policies capture governance constraints around security and privacy that mostly cannot be expressed in current production engines. Our approach composes naturally with industry-standard frameworks like A2AS.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AgenticRei, a deontic policy framework for governing autonomous LLM-driven AI agents, extending beyond traditional permit/prohibit access control to include obligations, dispensations, conflict resolution, and ontological reasoning, implemented via OWL and a high-performance external logic engine.
AI.motivation: Autonomous LLM-driven agents capable of invoking tools, manipulating data, and coordinating across organizations introduce governance challenges (obligations, waivers, conflict resolution) that exceed what current policy engines like XACML, Rego, and Cedar can handle, as they only cover permit/prohibit constraints.
AI.method: AgenticRei uses a deontic policy language built on the Rei framework, expressed in OWL (Web Ontology Language), and evaluated at runtime by a high-performance logic engine entirely outside the LLM. The same pipeline governs both tool invocations and agent-to-agent messages, composing with A2AS.
AI.result: Through examples, the authors demonstrate that deontic policies capture governance constraints around security and privacy that mostly cannot be expressed in current production engines (XACML, Rego, Cedar).
AI.conclusion: AgenticRei realizes key governance requirements—obligations, dispensations, conflict resolution, ontological reasoning, and permit/prohibit constraints—for autonomous AI agents, and composes naturally with industry-standard frameworks like A2AS.

title: Computational Identifiability
summary: Identification conditions describe the computability of a target query or parameter of interest as a function of the type and amount of information available. In causal identification, this information is often expressed in the form of a causal graph, and data are observed or collected for some subset of variables in the graph. Target queries may be for a single effect alone or for a class of effects in a given model. The derivation of an identification algorithm then defines mathematically the process by which the desired causal effect(s) can be uniquely determined, theoretically, in expectation. Identifiability in expectation, or 'theoretical identifiability,' generally assumes asymptotic properties, infinite data, or other mathematically idealized conditions. In this paper, we explore a fundamental distinction between this theoretical, idealized notion of identifiability and a proposed alternative that is computation-bound. The framework we propose - 'computational identifiability' - is to instead define a finite computational search procedure for an empirical estimator. If this process finds an estimator empirically, within a desired error tolerance, then identifiability is satisfied, conditional on the specified assumptions of the search (i.e., a prior distribution over the parameters) and conditional on the search procedure itself. Through several experiments, we demonstrate how this framework allows us to answer fine-grained, practical identification questions, such as identification with small finite samples, with ambiguous graphical criteria, with mixed observational-interventional data, and across counterfactual data and estimands. Code is available at https://github.com/lbynum/metadentify.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes "computational identifiability," a finite-search-based framework that relaxes traditional asymptotic identification conditions to enable practical causal identification with finite samples, ambiguous graphs, and mixed data types.
AI.motivation: Traditional causal identification relies on asymptotic, mathematically idealized conditions (infinite data, perfect graph knowledge), which are often unrealistic in practice. The authors aim to bridge the gap between theoretical identifiability and empirical feasibility by introducing a computation-bound alternative.
AI.method: The authors define a finite computational search procedure that seeks an empirical estimator within a desired error tolerance, conditional on a prior distribution over parameters and the search procedure itself. This shifts from theoretical uniqueness to empirical discovery via computation.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that the framework handles identification with small finite samples, ambiguous graphical criteria, mixed observational-interventional data, and counterfactual data and estimands—scenarios where classical identification may fail.
AI.conclusion: Computational identifiability offers a practical, fine-grained approach to causal identification that relaxes asymptotic assumptions, enabling identification in settings previously considered unidentifiable under classical theory.

title: PrefSQA: Pairwise Preference Prediction for Speech Quality Assessment and the Critical Role of High Quality Datasets
summary: Mean opinion scores (MOS) are widely used for speech quality assessment, yet scalar labels are sensitive to rater variability and listening test differences. This introduces labeling noise, which limits the reliability of MOS prediction. Preference prediction reduces this variability as listeners compare signals directly, producing cleaner labels. We study MOS-free preference prediction and propose PrefSQA, which incorporates uncertainty-aware logits, an impairment attention head, and a module based on non-matching-reference comparisons. We use and refine five datasets, including MOS-derived and low-noise simulated sets with matching and non-matching content, experiment with human preference sets, and test on unseen data. Experiments show small improvements on MOS-derived data, while other sets reveal clear improvement over the baselines, highlighting the value of high-quality preference data and demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: PrefSQA improves speech quality assessment by using preference prediction instead of MOS, incorporating uncertainty-aware logits, impairment attention, and non-matching-reference comparisons, showing clear improvements over baselines on high-quality preference datasets.
AI.motivation: MOS labels are sensitive to rater variability and listening test differences, introducing labeling noise that limits prediction reliability. Preference prediction reduces this variability by having listeners compare signals directly, producing cleaner labels.
AI.method: PrefSQA uses MOS-free preference prediction with uncertainty-aware logits, an impairment attention head, and a non-matching-reference comparison module. Five datasets (MOS-derived and low-noise simulated) with matching/non-matching content are used.
AI.result: Small improvements on MOS-derived data; clear improvements over baselines on other sets, highlighting the value of high-quality preference data.
AI.conclusion: Preference-based approaches with high-quality data yield more reliable speech quality assessment than traditional MOS-based methods.

title: AgentArmor: A Framework, Evaluation, \& Mitigation of Coding Agent Failures
summary: Software engineering and deployment are increasingly being delegated to AI coding agents. The scale of their adoption is surfacing rare, but highly destructive, failure modes. In this paper, we study these failure modes as stemming from three distinct mechanisms: underspecification, where default model behavior is unsafe; capability errors, where the safe action is available but the model does not adhere to it due to bias or capability limitations; and agent harness errors, where the model fails to execute the safe action through the harness. We evaluate these across 8 different evaluations, each inspired by real-life deployment failures, totaling 20 coding environments and 59 synthetic transcript templates. Based on this evaluation, we propose AgentArmor, an agent harness modification, to mitigate these errors. By adding an extended system prompt, a separate command classifier, a ``3 strikes'' policy, deterministic guardrails, and tools for the agent to edit its own context, we show that AgentArmor is safer across a statistically significant number of samples. Thus, we suggest concrete mitigations for current coding agents and a design philosophy for future agent harness features.

AI.tldr: This paper studies failure modes in AI coding agents (underspecification, capability errors, agent harness errors) across 8 evaluations, and proposes AgentArmor, an agent harness modification that improves safety via extended prompts, command classifier, "3 strikes" policy, guardrails, and self-context editing tools.
AI.motivation: The increasing delegation of software engineering and deployment to AI coding agents surfaces rare but highly destructive failure modes, which need systematic study and mitigation.
AI.method: The authors evaluate three failure mechanisms (underspecification, capability errors, agent harness errors) across 8 evaluations inspired by real-life deployment failures, using 20 coding environments and 59 synthetic transcript templates. They propose AgentArmor, a modification to agent harness that adds an extended system prompt, a separate command classifier, a "3 strikes" policy, deterministic guardrails, and tools for the agent to edit its own context.
AI.result: AgentArmor demonstrates statistically significant improvements in safety across samples compared to baseline agents.
AI.conclusion: The paper suggests concrete mitigations for current coding agents and outlines a design philosophy for future agent harness features.

title: MakeupMirror: Improving Facial Attribute Preservation in Diffusion Models for Makeup Transfer
summary: Makeup transfer models enable fun augmented reality (AR) experiences as well as virtual try-on (VTO) for online makeup shopping. While recent state-of-the-art diffusion based solutions such as Stable-Makeup dramatically improve the accuracy and realism of makeup transfer, they still face limitations in identity and skin color preservation, making production-level VTO for makeup shopping unrealistic. In this work, we propose MakeupMirror, a diffusion-based approach to makeup transfer that makes significant progress towards preserving facial features and skin tone. We introduce several technical innovations over Stable-Makeup: (1) integration of facial geometry conditioning with ControlNets to maintain facial fidelity; (2) region-specific makeup transfer control to enable precise makeup application across facial regions such as skin, eyes and lips; (3) skin tone-based makeup transfer modulation that prevent skin tone alteration in cross-subject transfer scenarios; and (4) integration of a Levenberg-Marquardt Langevin sampler to speed up inference while maintaining generation quality. Our experiments on CPM-Real, Makeup Wild, and (herein newly collected, more diverse) MakeupSelfies datasets show that MakeupMirror improves relative facial recognition similarity by +60%, reduces relative skin tone difference by -50% over Stable-Makeup, with a latency of 0.7s, while achieving expert acceptance rate of 94% across core facial identity preservation criteria.

AI.tldr: MakeupMirror is a diffusion-based makeup transfer model that improves identity preservation and skin tone accuracy over Stable-Makeup, achieving 60% better facial recognition similarity, 50% less skin tone difference, and 0.7s latency with 94% expert acceptance rate.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion-based makeup transfer methods like Stable-Makeup suffer from limitations in identity and skin color preservation, making production-level virtual try-on for online makeup shopping unrealistic.
AI.method: (1) Facial geometry conditioning via ControlNets for facial fidelity; (2) region-specific makeup transfer control across skin, eyes, and lips; (3) skin tone-based modulation to prevent alteration in cross-subject transfer; (4) Levenberg-Marquardt Langevin sampler for faster inference.
AI.result: Improves facial recognition similarity by +60%, reduces skin tone difference by -50% over Stable-Makeup, achieves 0.7s latency and 94% expert acceptance rate on CPM-Real, Makeup Wild, and MakeupSelfies datasets.
AI.conclusion: MakeupMirror significantly advances production-level virtual try-on for makeup shopping by preserving facial features and skin tone better than prior state-of-the-art diffusion-based methods.

title: LEAP: Layer-skipping Efficiency via Adaptive Progression for Vision Transformer Distillation
summary: Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) with Vision Transformer (ViT) backbones, such as DINOv2, have become essential for downstream tasks like object recognition and semantic segmentation. The immense computational requirements of backbones often necessitate distillation into smaller architectures for edge deployment. Feature-based knowledge distillation (KD) often suffers from the teacher-student gap; the student struggles to imitate teacher's complex feature map due to its limited capacity. To mitigate this bottleneck, we propose LEAP: Layer-skipping Efficiency via Adaptive Progression, a training curriculum for ViT feature-based knowledge distillation. By utilizing the teacher's intermediate feature maps as a sequence of progressively more difficult targets, our curriculum allows the student to build a foundational representation before tackling higher-level abstractions. Our results demonstrate that this paradigm significantly accelerates convergence through adaptive difficulty selection across various student model sizes and dataset scales. With our curriculum, the LEAP-distilled ViT-S achieves 90.1% accuracy on ImageNet-100, a +12.24% improvement compared with baseline. On ImageNet-1K, LEAP achieves +3.84% and +7.75% improvement for the instance retrieval task on the Oxford and Paris datasets, respectively. Furthermore, the curriculum enables 25.1% savings in training FLOPs and 21% savings in training time on ImageNet-100 by implementing early-stopping for teacher inference during the initial stages of training. Code is available at https://github.com/KevinZ0217/LEAP

AI.tldr: LEAP proposes a layer-skipping training curriculum for ViT feature-based knowledge distillation that progressively increases target difficulty, enabling faster convergence, better accuracy, and reduced training costs for edge-deployed student models.
AI.motivation: Feature-based knowledge distillation for Vision Transformers suffers from a teacher-student gap: students with limited capacity struggle to imitate the teacher's complex feature maps. Existing methods lack a structured way to bridge this gap efficiently.
AI.method: LEAP introduces a training curriculum that treats the teacher's intermediate feature maps as a sequence of progressively more difficult targets, starting with shallower layers and advancing to deeper ones, with adaptive difficulty selection based on student capacity.
AI.result: LEAP-distilled ViT-S achieves 90.1% accuracy on ImageNet-100 (+12.24% over baseline); on ImageNet-1K, +3.84% and +7.75% improvement on Oxford and Paris retrieval tasks; 25.1% training FLOPs savings and 21% training time savings via early-stopping of teacher inference.
AI.conclusion: The layer-skipping curriculum significantly accelerates convergence and improves performance across model sizes and dataset scales, effectively mitigating the teacher-student gap in ViT feature distillation.

title: Measuring Curriculum Alignment across Topical Coverage, Competency, and Cognitive Depth: A Longitudinal Framework Applied to CS2013 and CS2023
summary: Undergraduate computer science is governed by international curricular guidelines revised about once a decade, yet programs lack a reliable, reproducible way to measure how completely they cover the current guidelines and how that coverage shifts when the guidelines are restructured. We address this with a human-in-the-loop pipeline that measures a program's coverage of an external body of knowledge, applied longitudinally to one accredited BSc in Computer Science against Computer Science Curricula 2013 (CS2013) and 2023 (CS2023). The pipeline represents the program and each guideline as structured corpora, generates candidate course-to-knowledge-unit matches by semantic retrieval, and confirms them through human judgment under an explicit coverage definition. Of seven benchmarked retrievers, a reciprocal-rank-fusion ensemble was strongest, and a reputed long-context model underperformed a small sentence model, so retriever choice must be measured. Both maps were validated by an independent second rater (Cohen's kappa 0.64 for CS2023, 0.69 for CS2013). The program covers 49.7% of CS2023 and 50.9% of CS2013 knowledge units, near-constant across a decade. Extending the same retrieve-then-confirm design to competency articulation and cognitive depth shows that the program articulates the competency for ~88% of covered units under each guideline, yet delivers it at the recommended depth for 76% of present units under CS2023 against 95% under CS2013, a gap reflecting the newer guideline's raised expectations, not the program. The longitudinal comparison separates persistent structural gaps (parallel and distributed computing, foundations of programming languages, systems fundamentals), uncovered against both guidelines and ABET, from differences that reflect the standard's evolution. The instrument is reusable and available from the authors on request.

AI.tldr: A reusable human-in-the-loop pipeline measures a CS program's coverage of curricular guidelines (CS2013 vs CS2023), finding near-constant knowledge-unit coverage (~50%) but a drop in cognitive depth delivery (95%→76%) reflecting CS2023's raised expectations, with specific persistent gaps identified.
AI.motivation: Computer science programs lack a reliable, reproducible way to measure how completely they cover international curricular guidelines (revised ~decennially) and how coverage shifts when guidelines are restructured.
AI.method: A human-in-the-loop pipeline: (1) represents program and guidelines as structured corpora, (2) generates candidate course-to-knowledge-unit matches via semantic retrieval (benchmarking 7 retrievers; best was reciprocal-rank-fusion ensemble), (3) confirms matches via human judgment under an explicit coverage definition. Validated by independent second rater (Cohen's κ=0.64-0.69). Extended to competency articulation and cognitive depth analysis.
AI.result: Program covers 49.7% (CS2023) and 50.9% (CS2013) of knowledge units—near-constant over a decade. Competency articulated for ~88% of covered units under both guidelines. Cognitive depth delivered at recommended level for 76% (CS2023) vs 95% (CS2013). Persistent gaps in parallel/distributed computing, foundations of programming languages, systems fundamentals. Long-context model underperformed small sentence model.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline provides a reusable, validated instrument for measuring curriculum coverage longitudinally. The apparent drop in depth delivery is due to CS2023's raised expectations, not program degradation. The approach separates persistent structural gaps from differences caused by standard evolution.

title: When to Trust, How to Distill: Multi-Foundation Model Guidance for Lightweight, Robust Scientific Time Series Forecasting
comment: KDD 2026, paper decision: Accepted, track: AI for Science. total 12 pages including references and appendix
summary: The deployment of Time-Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) in physical sciences is hindered by a critical trade-off: while these models encode rich, universal temporal dynamics, they suffer from severe distributional misalignment when applied zero-shot to specific scientific domains, and their computational cost prohibits deployment in edge-computing sensor networks. We address a fundamental challenge: How can we extract latent structural knowledge from misaligned foundation models (FM) to train lightweight, specialized forecasters? We propose Gated Uncertainty-Aware Routing for Distillation (Guard), a novel framework that reframes multiteacher distillation as an instance-wise decision process with two adaptive mechanisms: (1) a Contextual Router that dynamically selects the most relevant teacher based on local input statistics, exploiting complementarity across diverse foundation models; and (2) an Uncertainty-Gated Temperature mechanism that acts as a "circuit-breaker," automatically attenuating distillation strength when teacher confidence diverges from domain reality. We evaluate our proposed lightweight framework on four climate-critical domains: meteorology, ecosystem carbon flux, soil moisture, and energy grids. Our method significantly reduces RMSE relative to a fixed-weight multi-teacher distillation baseline, successfully distilling knowledge from pretrained FMs (teachers) even when they exhibit suboptimal zero-shot accuracy due to distribution shift between the original and target data domains. We demonstrate that these domain-misaligned teachers can still serve as critical correctives, outperforming the globally superior FMs on 28.5% of the hardest instances. Ultimately, this enables high-precision scientific forecasting suitable for resource-constrained edge deployment. Code is available at https://github.com/RupasreeDey/GUARD-KDD2026.

AI.tldr: Guard (Gated Uncertainty-Aware Routing for Distillation) is a framework that distills knowledge from multiple misaligned Time-Series Foundation Models into a lightweight specialized forecaster via instance-wise routing and uncertainty-gated temperature, achieving high-precision scientific forecasting for edge deployment.
AI.motivation: TSFMs suffer from distributional misalignment when applied zero-shot to specific scientific domains, and their computational cost prohibits edge deployment. The key challenge is extracting latent structural knowledge from misaligned FMs to train lightweight, specialized forecasters.
AI.method: Guard reframes multi-teacher distillation as an instance-wise decision process with two mechanisms: (1) a Contextual Router that dynamically selects the most relevant teacher based on local input statistics; and (2) an Uncertainty-Gated Temperature mechanism that attenuates distillation strength when teacher confidence diverges from domain reality.
AI.result: Significantly reduces RMSE relative to fixed-weight multi-teacher distillation baseline on four climate-critical domains (meteorology, ecosystem carbon flux, soil moisture, energy grids). Domain-misaligned teachers outperform globally superior FMs on 28.5% of the hardest instances.
AI.conclusion: Domain-misaligned teachers can serve as critical correctives for specialized forecasting, enabling high-precision scientific forecasting suitable for resource-constrained edge deployment.

title: RIVET: Robust Idempotent Voice Attribute Editing
summary: Voice attribute editing models modify characteristics such as age and gender while preserving speaker identity. In large-scale speech datasets, however, attribute annotations are often noisy or inconsistent, which can cause conditional generative models to produce unstable edits. In this work, we show that idempotency provides an effective mechanism for improving robustness to noisy labels. An idempotent operator is one for which repeated application does not change the result, i.e., f(f(x)) = f(x). Enforcing this property acts as an implicit regularizer that reduces sensitivity to mislabeled examples. We introduce RIVET, a training framework that incorporates an idempotency objective to improve robustness to label noise. We evaluate RIVET under controlled label noise and on the GLOBE dataset with naturally noisy annotations. RIVET improves editing success and better preserves speaker identity than standard training, showing that idempotency improves robustness in voice editing models.

AI.tldr: RIVET uses idempotency as a regularization mechanism to improve robustness of voice attribute editing models against noisy labels, enhancing editing success and speaker identity preservation.
AI.motivation: Attribute annotations in large-scale speech datasets are often noisy or inconsistent, causing conditional generative models to produce unstable edits. The paper aims to improve robustness to label noise in voice attribute editing.
AI.method: The authors propose RIVET, a training framework that enforces an idempotency objective (f(f(x)) = f(x)), which acts as an implicit regularizer to reduce sensitivity to mislabeled examples during training.
AI.result: RIVET improves editing success and better preserves speaker identity compared to standard training, both under controlled label noise conditions and on the GLOBE dataset with naturally noisy annotations.
AI.conclusion: Idempotency is an effective mechanism for improving robustness to noisy labels in voice editing models.

title: DynAMO:Dynamic Asset Management Orchestration via Topological Multi-Agent Scheduling
comment: 11 pages, 2 figures, 7 tables, 4 algorithms. Evaluated on the AssetOpsBench industrial benchmark. Code: https://github.com/kushwaha001/DynAMO
summary:
While LLM-powered agents offer end-to-end automation for industrial asset lifecycles, real-world Industry 4.0 deployment is hindered by latency, concurrency instability, and safety risks. We present DynAMO (Dynamic Asset Management Orchestration), a deployment-ready engine using a Plan-then-Execute architecture to generate verifiable workflow graphs. DynAMO supports both SequentialWorkflow (topological execution) and ParallelWorkflow (dependency-aware concurrency). By dynamically identifying independent tasks, DynAMO preserves structural correctness and safety while significantly improving efficiency through controlled reasoning overlap.
  Across six controlled experiments on the AssetOpsBench industrial benchmark, DynAMO demonstrates substantial performance and robustness gains. Parallel execution reduces end-to-end latency by a median of 1.6x over sequential orchestration, rising to 1.8x on highly parallelizable workflows. After instrumenting external tool calls with realistic latencies, a latency decomposition shows that LLM reasoning and orchestration still account for more than 90% of execution time, identifying model inference as the primary system bottleneck. Structured context pruning reduces inference latency by approximately 30%, and DynAMO maintains correct functional behaviour (task completion, agent sequencing, and output quality) while exhibiting graceful degradation under controlled fault injection. Reproducibility analysis further confirms stable execution under repeated runs, with parallel scheduling reducing latency variance. These findings establish DynAMO as a practical blueprint for scalable, safe, and latency-aware agent deployment in Industry 4.0 automation pipelines. Code is available at: https://github.com/kushwaha001/DynAMO

AI.tldr: DynAMO is a deployment-ready engine for LLM-powered industrial agents that uses a Plan-then-Execute architecture with dynamic parallel task scheduling, achieving up to 1.8x latency reduction while preserving safety and correctness in Industry 4.0 automation.
AI.motivation: LLM-powered agents face latency, concurrency instability, and safety risks when deployed in real-world Industry 4.0 environments, hindering practical adoption despite their potential for end-to-end automation.
AI.method: DynAMO employs a Plan-then-Execute architecture generating verifiable workflow graphs with SequentialWorkflow (topological) and ParallelWorkflow (dependency-aware concurrency) modes, dynamically identifying independent tasks and using structured context pruning to reduce inference latency.
AI.result: Parallel execution achieves 1.6x median and 1.8x peak latency reduction; LLM reasoning accounts for >90% execution time; structured context pruning reduces inference latency ~30%; system maintains correctness under fault injection with reduced latency variance.
AI.conclusion: DynAMO provides a practical blueprint for scalable, safe, and latency-aware agent deployment in Industry 4.0 automation pipelines, with model inference identified as the primary bottleneck.

title: TriFlow: Generating Artist-Like 3D Mesh Topology via Nearest-Vertex Vector Fields
summary: We present TriFlow, a new generative approach for producing compact 3D meshes with artist-like triangle topology directly from input geometry conditions such as signed distance fields. Our key insight is to represent mesh topology as a nearest-vertex vector field (NVF) defined over the surface, where each point encodes its association to the nearest triangle vertex in the local barycentric frame. We train a latent flow-matching model to synthesize this field, enabling topology generation conditioned on the input geometry. To extract a coherent mesh, we cluster surface regions using the generated NVF and guide a constrained quadric error metric (QEM) mesh simplification with topology-aware optimization. This yields output meshes that closely match the input geometry while exhibiting structured, artist-like connectivity. Experiments demonstrate that TriFlow achieves stronger generalization and significantly improved topology quality compared to state-of-the-art learning-based approaches, alongside 90% lower Chamfer Distance and an 8x speedup.

AI.tldr: TriFlow is a generative method that produces compact 3D meshes with artist-like topology from input geometry (e.g., SDFs) by representing mesh topology as a nearest-vertex vector field (NVF), training a latent flow-matching model to synthesize it, and extracting meshes via constrained QEM simplification. It achieves stronger generalization, better topology quality, 90% lower Chamfer Distance, and 8x speedup over SOTA.
AI.motivation: To generate compact 3D meshes with structured, artist-like triangle topology directly from input geometry conditions, overcoming limitations in topology quality and generalization of existing learning-based approaches.
AI.method: (1) Represent mesh topology as a nearest-vertex vector field (NVF) over the surface, encoding each point's association to the nearest triangle vertex in the local barycentric frame. (2) Train a latent flow-matching model to synthesize the NVF conditioned on input geometry. (3) Extract a coherent mesh by clustering surface regions using the generated NVF and applying constrained quadric error metric (QEM) mesh simplification with topology-aware optimization.
AI.result: TriFlow achieves stronger generalization and significantly improved topology quality compared to SOTA learning-based approaches, with 90% lower Chamfer Distance and an 8x speedup.
AI.conclusion: The proposed NVF representation combined with latent flow-matching enables high-quality, efficient topology generation that closely matches input geometry while exhibiting structured, artist-like connectivity.

title: Hybrid Diffusion Transformer for Instruction-Guided Audio Editing via Rectified Flow
summary: Audio editing aims to modify specific content in an existing audio clip according to a natural language instruction while preserving the remaining acoustic content. Despite the remarkable progress of diffusion models, existing training-based editing methods mainly rely on the local inductive biases and cross-attention interaction in convolutional U-Net backbones, which often hinder long-range semantic alignment and precise understanding and localization of instructions. In contrast, diffusion transformers provide stronger global modeling and multimodal fusion, but existing editing architectures usually adopt a simple stack of MMDiT and DiT blocks. Applying joint attention over concatenated audio and text tokens in all blocks results in quadratic complexity with respect to token length. To balance editing performance and efficiency, we propose a hybrid two-stage diffusion transformer architecture for instruction-guided audio editing based on rectified flow matching. It performs joint attention over audio and text tokens to establish coarse semantic alignment at low-resolution stage, then switches to alternating joint-attention and cross-attention blocks to refine editing details at high-resolution stage. This coarse-to-fine strategy enables efficient and accurate instruction-guided audio editing. Experiments show that the proposed framework achieves notable performance gains on challenging editing tasks involving overlapping audio events and complex instructions, while substantially improving editing efficiency with a compact model.

AI.tldr: Proposes a hybrid two-stage diffusion transformer architecture with rectified flow matching for instruction-guided audio editing, using coarse-to-fine strategy: joint attention at low-resolution stage and alternating joint/cross-attention at high-resolution stage, achieving better efficiency and performance than existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing audio editing methods rely on convolutional U-Net backbones with limited long-range semantic alignment and instruction understanding. Diffusion transformers offer better global modeling but simple stacking of attention blocks leads to quadratic complexity. A balance between editing performance and efficiency is needed.
AI.method: A hybrid two-stage diffusion transformer architecture using rectified flow matching: (1) low-resolution stage with joint attention over audio and text tokens for coarse semantic alignment; (2) high-resolution stage with alternating joint-attention and cross-attention blocks for fine-grained editing refinement.
AI.result: Achieves notable performance gains on challenging editing tasks involving overlapping audio events and complex instructions, while substantially improving editing efficiency with a compact model.
AI.conclusion: The coarse-to-fine hybrid transformer strategy enables efficient and accurate instruction-guided audio editing, outperforming existing approaches.

title: LooseControlVideo: Directorial Video Control using Spatial Blocking
summary: Precise 3D spatial orchestration in text-to-video generation remains a significant challenge, particularly for multi-object scenes where semantic layout and temporal dynamics are often entangled. While existing depth-conditioned models achieve good structural fidelity, they necessitate dense, frame-accurate guidance that is labor-intensive to author for dynamic events involving deformable objects. We present LooseControlVideo, a framework that enables intuitive and expressive control by using sparse, oriented 3D boxes as a "blocking" proxy. This allows users to author high-level layout and trajectory while leveraging a video generative model to generate realistic occlusions, dynamics and interactions. We achieve this by fine-tuning a Wan 2.2 backbone on a video dataset annotated with DNOCS, a novel encoding for 3D size, orientation and depth-ordered occlusions. Furthermore, our method allows for localized refinement, such as adjusting a jump trajectory or adding an interaction, with minimal disruption to the global scene context. Extensive evaluations on the nuScenes, HO-3D, and BEHAVE benchmarks demonstrate that LooseControlVideo significantly outperforms existing 2D-box and flow-based baselines. Our findings indicate a 1.2x to 3x improvement in Trajectory Error; 2x improvement in Rigid Motion Consistency; and a 1.5x to 2x increase in Occlusion Accuracy over current state-of-the-art layout-conditioned models, demonstrating that oriented 3D primitives provide good geometric prior for complex, multi-agent video authoring.

AI.tldr: LooseControlVideo uses sparse, oriented 3D boxes as a "blocking" proxy to control multi-object spatial layout and trajectory in text-to-video generation, fine-tuned on Wan 2.2 with DNOCS encoding, achieving 1.2x–3x better trajectory error, 2x rigid motion consistency, and 1.5x–2x occlusion accuracy over baselines.
AI.motivation: Precise 3D spatial orchestration in text-to-video generation is challenging for multi-object scenes; existing depth-conditioned models require dense, frame-accurate guidance that is labor-intensive for dynamic events with deformable objects.
AI.method: Fine-tuning a Wan 2.2 backbone on video data annotated with DNOCS (a novel encoding for 3D size, orientation, and depth-ordered occlusions), using sparse oriented 3D boxes as a blocking proxy for high-level layout and trajectory control.
AI.result: 1.2x–3x improvement in Trajectory Error, 2x improvement in Rigid Motion Consistency, and 1.5x–2x increase in Occlusion Accuracy over state-of-the-art layout-conditioned models on nuScenes, HO-3D, and BEHAVE benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Oriented 3D primitives provide a strong geometric prior for complex, multi-agent video authoring, enabling intuitive control with minimal disruption to global scene context during localized refinement.

title: Diffusion Language Models: An Experimental Analysis
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized language modeling through autoregressive generation, enabling strong performance across a wide range of tasks. Recently, Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) have emerged as an alternative paradigm that generates text through iterative denoising rather than next-token prediction, allowing parallel refinement of entire sequences. While numerous diffusion-based architectures have been proposed, differences in evaluation protocols, datasets, inference budgets, and generation hyperparameters make it difficult to compare their capabilities and understand the trade-offs they offer. In this work, we present a systematic experimental analysis of modern DLMs. Specifically, we evaluate eight state-of-the-art DLMs across eight benchmarks spanning reasoning, coding, translation, knowledge, and structured problem solving, while explicitly considering both generation quality and computational efficiency. Beyond downstream evaluation, we analyze the impact of key inference-time factors, including denoising steps, context length, block size, and parallel unmasking strategies, and complement large-scale experiments with controlled comparisons of smaller models trained under identical conditions. Our analysis highlights the strengths and limitations of diffusion-based language modeling across different tasks, architectures, and inference budgets. We show that the behavior of DLMs is strongly influenced by generation-time design choices, leading to distinct trade-offs between performance and computational efficiency. Overall, our study provides practical insights into the capabilities and deployment characteristics of contemporary DLMs.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a systematic experimental analysis of eight state-of-the-art Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) across eight benchmarks covering reasoning, coding, translation, knowledge, and structured problem solving. The study evaluates both generation quality and computational efficiency, examining the impact of inference-time factors such as denoising steps, context length, block size, and parallel unmasking strategies. The results show that DLM behavior is strongly influenced by generation-time design choices, leading to distinct performance-efficiency trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Despite the growing number of diffusion-based language models, differences in evaluation protocols, datasets, inference budgets, and hyperparameters make it difficult to compare their capabilities and understand the trade-offs they offer. A systematic analysis is needed to fairly compare these models.
AI.method: The authors evaluate eight state-of-the-art DLMs across eight benchmarks covering diverse tasks. They control for inference-time factors (denoising steps, context length, block size, parallel unmasking) and complement large-scale experiments with controlled comparisons of smaller models trained under identical conditions.
AI.result: DLM behavior is strongly influenced by generation-time design choices, leading to distinct trade-offs between performance and computational efficiency. The analysis highlights strengths and limitations of diffusion-based language modeling across different tasks, architectures, and inference budgets.
AI.conclusion: The study provides practical insights into the capabilities and deployment characteristics of contemporary DLMs, emphasizing that generation-time design choices critically shape model behavior and efficiency.

title: Closing the Social-Semantic Gap: SPSD for Edge-Based Prompt Compression in Cloud LLM Inference
summary: The prefill stage of Large Language Model (LLM) inference is a growing contributor to cloud-scale energy cost. Many consumer-support and conversational prompts contain social scaffolding: politeness markers, apologetic preamble, repetition, and rapport-building language that is important for human communication but carries low marginal information for machine reasoning. We call this discrepancy the Social-Semantic Gap. We present SPSD (Sentiment Preserving Semantic Distillation), an edge-based pipeline that compresses user prompts using a 4-bit quantised Small Language Model before transmission to a cloud-deployed LLM. Evaluation on a 248-prompt corpus using Gemma-2-2B-Instruct (Q4_K_M) as the SLM and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct as the cloud evaluation model yields a mean input token saving of 99.9 tokens per distilled call, with all 146 distilled calls yielding positive savings. Response quality, assessed by blind LLM-as-judge scoring across 121 pairs, is non-inferior to the raw path within a pre-specified 1-point margin on a 15-point rubric; the judge awarded 43 percent ties, 28 percent distilled wins, and 29 percent raw wins. Cosine similarity is mixed: mean 0.682, median 0.712, with 54.1 percent of pairs above the 0.70 reference threshold. Safety-critical domains are conservatively routed to passthrough via rule-based gates. Per-call net energy saving is estimated at 70-270 uWh under stated assumptions. SPSD shows that on-device prompt distillation can reduce cloud LLM input-token cost while preserving response quality within a practical non-inferiority margin.

AI.tldr: SPSD is an edge-based pipeline that compresses user prompts (removing social scaffolding like politeness markers) using a small 4-bit quantized language model before sending to a cloud LLM, achieving ~100 tokens savings per call while preserving response quality within a 1-point non-inferiority margin on a 15-point scale.
AI.motivation: The prefill stage of LLM inference contributes significantly to cloud energy costs, and many user prompts contain social scaffolding (politeness markers, apologies, repetition) that carries low marginal information for machine reasoning but is important for human communication — a discrepancy termed the Social-Semantic Gap. The authors aim to reduce energy consumption by removing this redundant content.
AI.method: SPSD is an edge-based pipeline that: (1) uses a 4-bit quantized Small Language Model (Gemma-2-2B-Instruct Q4_K_M) on-device to distill/compress user prompts, (2) transmits the compressed prompt to a cloud-deployed LLM (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct), (3) applies rule-based safety gates to route safety-critical domains to passthrough. Evaluation on a 248-prompt corpus with blind LLM-as-judge scoring on 121 pairs using a 15-point rubric, plus cosine similarity analysis.
AI.result: Mean input token saving of 99.9 tokens per distilled call; all 146 distilled calls positive. Blind LLM judge: 43% ties, 28% distilled wins, 29% raw wins (non-inferior within 1-point margin). Cosine similarity: mean 0.682, median 0.712, 54.1% above 0.70 threshold. Estimated net energy savings of 70-270 uWh per call.
AI.conclusion: On-device prompt distillation can reduce cloud LLM input-token cost while preserving response quality within a practical non-inferiority margin, demonstrating a feasible approach to reducing energy costs in cloud-scale LLM inference.

title: Latency-Configurable Streaming Speech Enhancement via Asymmetric Temporal Padding
comment: 5 pages, 3 figures. Accepted for presentation at Interspeech 2026
summary: Streaming speech enhancement requires balancing algorithmic latency against quality, yet existing approaches largely treat this as a binary causal versus non-causal choice. LaCo-SENet addresses this issue with two mechanisms parameterized by a single training-time hyperparameter. First, asymmetric temporal padding redistributes past and future context in convolutions, enabling systematic latency configuration. Second, dual-buffer streaming combines state buffers for past context with lookahead buffers that supply future context at both the input and feature levels. Selective state updates also prevent future-frame leakage into the streaming state, ensuring training-inference consistency. On VoiceBank+DEMAND, a fixed-budget (1.37M parameters) backbone yields a family of models spanning 12.5-75.0 ms, with PESQ rising from 3.35 to 3.43. At just 12.5 ms (fully causal), a PESQ of 3.35 matches or exceeds the prior causal state-of-the-art (3.27 at 46.5 ms).

AI.tldr: LaCo-SENet introduces asymmetric temporal padding and dual-buffer streaming to flexibly trade off latency and quality in streaming speech enhancement, achieving PESQ of 3.35 at 12.5 ms latency with a compact 1.37M parameter model.
AI.motivation: Existing streaming speech enhancement approaches treat latency as a binary causal vs. non-causal choice, lacking systematic mechanisms to flexibly balance algorithmic latency and quality.
AI.method: Two mechanisms: (1) asymmetric temporal padding that redistributes past/future context in convolutions for latency control, and (2) dual-buffer streaming combining state buffers (past) with lookahead buffers (future) at input and feature levels, plus selective state updates to prevent future-frame leakage.
AI.result: On VoiceBank+DEMAND with 1.37M parameter backbone, models span 12.5-75.0 ms latency with PESQ rising from 3.35 to 3.43. At 12.5 ms (fully causal), PESQ of 3.35 exceeds prior causal SOTA (3.27 at 46.5 ms).
AI.conclusion: LaCo-SENet enables systematic latency-quality trade-offs in streaming speech enhancement via a single training-time hyperparameter, achieving superior causal performance with substantially lower latency.

title: Bistable by Construction: Wall-Clock-Calibrated State Monitors Have No Moment-Detection Regime at Agent Cadence
comment: 10 pages, 5 figures. Sequel to arXiv:2606.04296. Pre-registered; falsification clauses honored (H5 unsupported; H7 strict band 16/20) repo:https://github.com/2025eb1100268-tech/intervention-timing-saturation-trap
summary: Runtime monitors for autonomous agents commonly threshold an accumulated internal state - a behavioural baseline, a drift statistic, or, in our prior work, a modelled affective state. We previously reported a State Saturation Trap: threshold-on-state triggers over a continuous affect engine become near-constant alarms on SWE-bench debugging agents (Modgil 2026). A post-release audit found the engine received dt=0 between actions, so its exponential decay never operated: the published trap is a pure-accumulator result. We correct the record (erratum, v2) and treat the flaw as an experiment. The key variable it exposes is whether a monitor's dynamics are calibrated in sample time (per observation, as in CUSUM) or wall-clock time (half-lives in seconds, as in affect models and EMA baselines). On fixed-rate streams these coincide; on agent streams, where inter-action time varies by orders of magnitude, they do not. A pre-registered sweep over uniform intervals (dt in {0..600}s) on 20 trajectories shows the wall-clock level trigger has two regimes: at dt<=1s a constant alarm (20/20; median 18 firings); at dt>=60s silent. Every critical dt lies in (1,30]s. Real agent runs measure latency at median 1.53s (p90 2.33s); real coding cadence sits inside the trap regime, vindicating the empirical finding under a corrected mechanism. The structure is a property of the calibration class, not the engine: a minimal wall-clock accumulator over the raw error stream reproduces the same cliff, while a sample-time CUSUM over the identical stream is exactly dt-invariant (20/20). A rising-edge trigger with hysteresis fires 0-3 times per trajectory in every condition. We conclude that wall-clock-calibrated leaky-integrator monitors admit no regime in which they act as moment detectors on agent streams; transition detection escapes the trap at every cadence, but does not recover human intervention timing.

AI.tldr: This paper corrects a prior finding about a "State Saturation Trap" in runtime monitors for autonomous agents. The trap was originally attributed to a continuous affect engine but actually resulted from a pure accumulator (dt=0 between actions). The key variable is whether monitors are calibrated in sample time (per observation) or wall-clock time (real seconds). On agent streams with variable inter-action times, wall-clock-calibrated leaky integrators either trigger constant alarms (dt≤1s) or go silent (dt≥60s), with no useful intermediate regime. Sample-time CUSUM is dt-invariant, and rising-edge triggers avoid the trap but don't replicate human timing.
AI.motivation: To correct a previously published result about a State Saturation Trap in autonomous agent monitors, treat the discovered calibration flaw as an experiment, and expose the fundamental difference between sample-time vs. wall-clock-calibrated monitor dynamics on agent streams with variable inter-action times.
AI.method: Post-release audit identified dt=0 bug; pre-registered sweep over uniform intervals (dt in {0..600}s) on 20 trajectories; real agent latency measurements; comparison of wall-clock accumulator, sample-time CUSUM, and rising-edge trigger with hysteresis.
AI.result: Wall-clock level trigger has two regimes: constant alarm at dt≤1s (20/20 trajectories, median 18 firings) and silent at dt≥60s. Critical dt lies in (1,30]s. Real agent latency (median 1.53s) falls inside the trap regime. Sample-time CUSUM is exactly dt-invariant. Rising-edge trigger fires 0-3 times per trajectory in every condition.
AI.conclusion: Wall-clock-calibrated leaky-integrator monitors cannot serve as moment detectors on agent streams; transition detection escapes the trap but does not recover human intervention timing.

title: The Token Is a Group Element: On Lie-Algebra Attention over Matrix Lie Groups
summary: We place the attention token on the group: a token is an element $g_i$ of a matrix Lie group $G$ -- a bare transformation, with no feature payload and no external action $ρ(g)$ carrying it. To our knowledge this is the first attention construction whose tokens are bare matrix Lie group elements: their score is the closed-form algebra norm of the relative pose rather than a learned kernel, and it reaches the affine full-frame groups that every irrep- or surjective-exp-based method must exclude. We call it Lie-Algebra Attention. Once tokens are group elements, the rest follows with none of the usual representation-theoretic machinery. The relative geometry of a pair is canonical, $g_i^{-1} g_j$, so the pairwise invariant $w_{ij} = \log(g_i^{-1} g_j)$ is intrinsic rather than designed; equivariance under the diagonal $G$-action is tautological, and the cocycle condition holds automatically. The attention score is the negative squared algebra norm, $s_{ij} = -\|\log(g_i^{-1} g_j)\|_λ^2/τ$: the canonical proximity kernel under a block-weighted Frobenius inner product, with no irreducible representations, spherical harmonics, Clebsch-Gordan products, or learned kernel. The construction applies to any matrix Lie group on a chosen logarithm chart containing the relative poses, including the non-compact non-abelian affine groups with scale and shear that no vector-token attention method reaches: neither the irrep tradition nor surjective-exp methods. Three sequence-completion experiments, on SE(2), SO(3), and Aff(2), bear this out: the closed-form score matches a learned MLP kernel on the same invariant and outperforms it on SE(2), using 50 to 80x fewer score parameters, while a vector-token baseline breaks invariance by five to twelve orders of magnitude.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Lie-Algebra Attention, a novel attention mechanism where tokens are bare matrix Lie group elements (transformations with no feature payload or representation). The attention score is computed as the closed-form negative squared algebra norm of the relative pose $-\|\log(g_i^{-1} g_j)\|_\lambda^2/\tau$, eliminating the need for irreducible representations, spherical harmonics, Clebsch-Gordan products, or learned kernels. The method naturally handles non-compact non-abelian affine groups (including scale and shear) that all irrep- or surjective-exp-based methods cannot reach. Experiments on SE(2), SO(3), and Aff(2) show the closed-form score matches or outperforms learned MLP kernels with 50-80x fewer parameters, while vector-token baselines break invariance by 5-12 orders of magnitude.
AI.motivation: Existing attention mechanisms that aim for geometric invariance/equivariance rely on representation-theoretic machinery (irreps, spherical harmonics, Clebsch-Gordan products) or surjective exponential maps, which fundamentally cannot handle non-compact non-abelian affine groups with scale and shear. The paper seeks a simpler, more general construction where tokens are themselves group elements, making relative geometry canonical and equivariance tautological.
AI.method: Tokens are bare matrix Lie group elements $g_i \in G$ (transformations without feature vectors). The relative pose between tokens is $g_i^{-1}g_j$, whose Lie algebra logarithm $\log(g_i^{-1}g_j)$ provides an intrinsic pairwise invariant. The attention score is $s_{ij} = -\|\log(g_i^{-1}g_j)\|_\lambda^2/\tau$, a closed-form proximity kernel under a block-weighted Frobenius inner product. No representations, harmonics, or learned kernels are needed. The construction applies to any matrix Lie group on a chosen logarithm chart containing the relative poses.
AI.result: Experiments on SE(2), SO(3), and Aff(2) sequence completion show: (1) the closed-form score matches or outperforms learned MLP kernel on the same invariant; (2) achieves 50-80x fewer score parameters compared to learned kernels; (3) vector-token baselines break invariance by 5-12 orders of magnitude; (4) works on Aff(2) (affine group with scale and shear) which no vector-token attention method can reach.
AI.conclusion: Lie-Algebra Attention provides a fundamentally simpler and more general framework for geometric attention by treating tokens as group elements. It eliminates complex representation-theoretic machinery while extending applicability to non-compact affine groups previously inaccessible, achieving better parameter efficiency and exact invariance.

title: ImageWAM: Do World Action Models Really Need Video Generation, or Just Image Editing?
summary: World Action Models (WAMs) commonly rely on video generation to bridge visual world modeling and robot control.
