However, video-based WAMs face three coupled limitations: dense multi-frame future tokens make inference costly, full video prediction spends capacity on action-irrelevant temporal and appearance details, and long-horizon future imagination may introduce errors that mislead action prediction. These issues raise a simple question: Does world action model really need video generation? We propose ImageWAM, a simple WAM framework that repurposes pretrained image editing models for robot action prediction. In contrast to video generation, image editing provides a better-matched prior: it only needs to model a target-frame transformation, focuses on action-relevant current-to-target visual differences, and grounds task instructions to localized visual changes through edit pretraining. In practice, ImageWAM does not decode the target frame at inference time; instead, it conditions a flow-matching action expert on the KV caches produced by image-editing denoising, using them as a compact world-action context. ImageWAM outperforms standard VLA baselines and matching competitive WAMs without additional policy pretraining across different simulator and real-world experiments. It also reduces FLOPs to 1/6 and latency to 1/4 of video-based WAMs. Attention analysis further shows that editing caches focus on task-relevant change regions, supporting image editing as an effective alternative to video-based world-action modeling.

AI.tldr: ImageWAM replaces video generation in World Action Models with image editing, achieving superior robot control performance while reducing FLOPs to 1/6 and latency to 1/4 of video-based WAMs.
AI.motivation: Video-based WAMs suffer from three coupled limitations: costly dense multi-frame tokens, wasted capacity on action-irrelevant details, and error accumulation in long-horizon prediction, raising the question of whether video generation is necessary for world action modeling.
AI.method: ImageWAM repurposes pretrained image editing models for robot action prediction. It conditions a flow-matching action expert on KV caches from image-editing denoising (without decoding the target frame), using them as compact world-action context.
AI.result: Outperforms standard VLA baselines and competitive WAMs without additional policy pretraining across simulator and real-world experiments. Reduces FLOPs to 1/6 and latency to 1/4 of video-based WAMs. Attention analysis shows editing caches focus on task-relevant change regions.
AI.conclusion: Image editing is an effective and efficient alternative to video generation for world-action modeling, providing better-matched priors and superior computational efficiency.

title: Hidden Anchors in Multi-Agent LLM Deliberation
summary: Multi-agent LLM deliberation, where agents exchange and revise answers over several rounds, is increasingly used to improve reasoning and accuracy, yet how and why it works is rarely modelled. Such deliberation mirrors how humans reach decisions. As social animals we are pulled both by the group, the herd effect that classical opinion-dynamics models such as DeGroot and Friedkin--Johnsen capture, and by our own internal belief, which they do not. We model multi-agent deliberation as a closed-loop dynamical system in which each agent carries a hidden internal belief, its anchor, that continually pulls its opinion regardless of its neighbours. We show this anchor can be recovered from the deliberation alone, and that it explains a behaviour classical consensus rules forbid: an agent's confidence in the correct answer can climb past where any agent started, escaping the space (convexhull) formed by the initial beliefs. Checking whether the recovered anchor also predicts held-out runs (generalizes) gives a simple test for when a model is truly driven bysuch an anchor. Across three open-weight model families this is a spectrum, not all-or-nothing. All anchors' influence are about equally strongly, but they differ in where the anchor sits, and only when it sits far from the initial opinions does deliberation escape the hull and need the full closed-loop model.

AI.tldr: The paper models multi-agent LLM deliberation as a closed-loop dynamical system where each agent has a hidden internal belief (anchor) that pulls its opinion regardless of neighbors. This anchor can be recovered from deliberation alone and explains why confidence can exceed initial beliefs (escaping the convex hull). Testing anchor generalization across model families reveals a spectrum of behavior.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent LLM deliberation is increasingly used to improve reasoning, but the underlying mechanisms of how and why it works are rarely modeled. The authors aim to provide a formal model analogous to human opinion dynamics.
AI.method: The authors model multi-agent deliberation as a closed-loop dynamical system where each agent has a hidden internal belief (anchor) that continually pulls its opinion, extending classical models (DeGroot, Friedkin-Johnsen). They show anchors can be recovered from deliberation data and use generalization to held-out runs as a test for anchor-driven behavior.
AI.result: Anchors can be recovered from deliberation alone and explain why confidence can surpass initial beliefs (escaping the convex hull). Across three open-weight model families, anchor influence is equally strong but anchor positions differ; only when anchors sit far from initial opinions does deliberation escape the hull.
AI.conclusion: Multi-agent deliberation is best understood as a spectrum of anchor-driven behavior, and the closed-loop model is necessary specifically when anchors deviate significantly from initial opinions. The proposed test (generalization to held-out runs) can determine when such modeling is needed.

title: Performance Analysis and Optimization of 3D Generative Diffusion Models across GPU Architectures
summary: Diffusion models have become essential for high-fidelity 3D MRI synthesis, yet their deployment remains constrained by substantial GPU resource demands arising from hundreds of U-Net evaluations per sample and a highly heterogeneous kernel behavior. This paper performs a comprehensive performance analysis of the state-of-the-art medical diffusion model, Med-DDPM, across three generations of NVIDIA architectures to study kernel-level runtime breakdowns, instruction-mix characteristics, memory system utilization, warp-level activities, and profiler priority-score estimates. We show that training is overwhelmingly dominated by cuDNN convolution and implicit-GEMM kernels, with inefficiencies arising from memory-access patterns, tensor-layout conversions, and limited Tensor Core utilization. Guided by these insights, we evaluate two architecture-aware optimizations TF32 Tensor Core activation and a 3D channels-last layout and demonstrate that they reduce SM cycles by up to 100x, cut dynamic instructions by 100x, raise Tensor Core utilization from 1.45 to 9.98x, and increase IPC by 7% on A100, all without degrading synthesis quality.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the performance of Med-DDPM (a 3D MRI diffusion model) across NVIDIA GPU architectures, identifying bottlenecks like cuDNN convolutions and memory-access inefficiencies. It proposes TF32 Tensor Core activation and 3D channels-last layout optimizations, achieving up to 100x reduction in SM cycles and instructions, higher Tensor Core utilization, and improved IPC without quality loss.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models like Med-DDPM enable high-fidelity 3D MRI synthesis but are computationally prohibitive due to hundreds of U-Net evaluations per sample and heterogeneous GPU kernel behavior, limiting deployment.
AI.method: Comprehensive kernel-level performance analysis (runtime breakdowns, instruction-mix, memory system, warp-level activities, profiler priority scores) across three NVIDIA architectures (exact generations implied), followed by evaluating TF32 Tensor Core activation and 3D channels-last layout optimizations.
AI.result: Optimizations reduce SM cycles by up to 100x, cut dynamic instructions by 100x, raise Tensor Core utilization from 1.45 to 9.98x, and increase IPC by 7% on A100, with no degradation in synthesis quality.
AI.conclusion: Architecture-aware optimizations can dramatically improve computational efficiency of medical diffusion models, enabling more feasible deployment on current GPU hardware.

title: Exploring Pre-training Benefits on Phoneme Addition through Fine-tuning in Speech Synthesis
summary: Transfer learning is widely used for low-resource text-to-speech. When the target corpus contains phonemes unseen in pre-training, the model must expand its phoneme inventory during fine-tuning; we call the process "phoneme addition." However, it remains unclear whether the pre-trained ability to generate seen phonemes contributes to this process. This study investigates phoneme addition in two settings: (1) a simulation setup using LLM-generated phoneme-controlled corpora that enables investigation without considering confounding factors, and (2) a real-speech cross-lingual transfer setup (English to Japanese) to validate whether the findings hold in practice. Experiments in both settings showed that while fine-tuning achieved higher naturalness than training from scratch, it required as much or more data to achieve comparable PER for new phonemes. These results indicate that pre-training mainly contributes to naturalness improvement, but offers limited benefit for phoneme addition.

AI.tldr: Pre-training helps naturalness but not phoneme learning in low-resource TTS; fine-tuning needs as much data as scratch for new phoneme accuracy.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether pre-trained ability to generate seen phonemes contributes to learning unseen phonemes ("phoneme addition") during fine-tuning in low-resource text-to-speech transfer learning.
AI.method: Two experimental settings: (1) simulation with LLM-generated phoneme-controlled corpora to isolate confounding factors, and (2) real-speech cross-lingual transfer from English to Japanese.
AI.result: Fine-tuning achieved higher naturalness than training from scratch, but required as much or more data to achieve comparable PER for new phonemes.
AI.conclusion: Pre-training mainly contributes to naturalness improvement but offers limited benefit for phoneme addition (learning new phonemes).

title: Interpretable and Verifiable Hardware Generation with LLM-Driven Stepwise Refinement
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable success in software development. However, they are susceptible to hallucinations, meaning that they can introduce subtle semantic and logical errors. Due to the high stakes in chip design and manufacturing, hardware engineers are still reluctant to rely on LLMs for register-transfer level (RTL) generation. In this paper, we propose a hardware generation framework that combines the creativity and broad knowledge of LLMs with the explainability and mathematical rigor of formal methods. Specifically, we devise a set of transformation rules that cover various design decisions and hardware features. By iteratively applying these rules, an LLM agent can convert a design specification into an RTL program with guaranteed correctness. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of the framework.

AI.tldr: A hardware generation framework combining LLMs with formal methods to produce correct-by-construction RTL designs through iterative transformation rules.
AI.motivation: LLMs hallucinate subtle semantic/logical errors, making hardware engineers reluctant to use them for RTL generation due to high stakes in chip design and manufacturing.
AI.method: Proposes a framework with a set of transformation rules covering design decisions and hardware features; an LLM agent iteratively applies these rules to convert a design specification into a correct RTL program.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of the framework.
AI.conclusion: Combining LLMs with formal methods can yield correct-by-construction RTL generation, addressing LLM hallucination issues in hardware design.

title: PerceptionDLM: Parallel Region Perception with Multimodal Diffusion Language Models
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable progress in visual understanding tasks. However, most existing MLLMs rely on autoregressive generation, which limits their efficiency for perception tasks that require captioning multiple regions. In this work, we propose PerceptionDLM, a multimodal diffusion language model optimized for efficient parallel region perception. Built upon PerceptionDLM-Base, a strong foundational baseline that achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source diffusion MLLMs, our architecture fully leverages the parallel decoding nature of DLMs. Specifically, we introduce efficient prompting and structured attention masking to enable simultaneous perception of multiple masked regions, allowing the model to generate region descriptions in parallel at both the sequence and token levels. This design significantly improves inference efficiency compared with existing approaches that process regions sequentially. To systematically evaluate the parallelism property of visual perception capability for DLMs, we construct a new Parallel Detailed Localized Captioning Benchmark (ParaDLC-Bench) by scaling the DLC-Bench to include multiple region masks per image, enabling joint evaluation of both caption quality and inference efficiency. Experiments demonstrate that PerceptionDLM maintains competitive performance in region captioning while achieving substantial speed improvements for multi-region perception tasks. Our results highlight the potential of multimodal diffusion language models for efficient, parallel visual perception. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to achieve parallel region caption and perception by leveraging the advantages of diffusion language models. Code, models, and datasets are released.

AI.tldr: PerceptionDLM is a multimodal diffusion language model that achieves efficient parallel region perception by leveraging parallel decoding, structured attention masking, and efficient prompting, outperforming autoregressive MLLMs in speed while maintaining competitive caption quality, validated on the new ParaDLC-Bench dataset.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLMs rely on autoregressive generation, which limits efficiency for perception tasks requiring captioning of multiple regions sequentially. The goal is to enable parallel region perception for improved inference speed.
AI.method: Built on PerceptionDLM-Base, the model uses efficient prompting and structured attention masking to enable simultaneous perception of multiple masked regions, achieving parallel decoding at both sequence and token levels. A new benchmark (ParaDLC-Bench) is constructed for evaluation.
AI.result: PerceptionDLM maintains competitive region captioning performance while achieving substantial speed improvements for multi-region perception tasks compared to autoregressive counterparts.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal diffusion language models demonstrate strong potential for efficient, parallel visual perception. This is the first work to achieve parallel region caption and perception using diffusion language models.

title: DeXposure-Claw: An Agentic System for DeFi Risk Supervision
summary: Decentralized finance exposes supervisors to fast-moving, networked credit risks. General-purpose LLM agents fit this setting poorly: they over-read weak evidence and recommend high-stakes interventions, while existing evaluations offer no regulator-aligned way to measure the resulting false alarms. We introduce DeXposure-Claw, a forecast-grounded agentic supervision system that routes LLM decisions through structured evidence: (1) DeXposure-FM, a graph time-series foundation model, forecasts future exposure networks; (2) deterministic monitors and stress scenarios then turn those forecasts into typed alerts, attribution signals, and scenario evidence; and (3) data-health and confidence gates constrain escalation before DeXposure-Claw emits auditable supervisory tickets with rationales. We further develop DeXposure-Bench, a six-axis evaluation harness, whose decision axis scores tickets against a regulator-aligned absolute-loss ground truth and an explicit false-intervention rate. Experiments on five years of weekly real data fully support our system. Code is at https://github.com/EVIEHub/DeXposure-Claw.

AI.tldr: DeXposure-Claw is a forecast-grounded agentic supervision system for decentralized finance that routes LLM decisions through structured evidence (graph foundation model, monitors, confidence gates) to reduce false alarms, paired with DeXposure-Bench, a six-axis evaluation harness for regulator-aligned assessment.
AI.motivation: Decentralized finance exposes supervisors to fast-moving, networked credit risks. General-purpose LLM agents over-read weak evidence and recommend high-stakes interventions, while existing evaluations lack regulator-aligned metrics for false alarms.
AI.method: (1) DeXposure-FM: graph time-series foundation model forecasting future exposure networks; (2) deterministic monitors and stress scenarios generating typed alerts, attribution signals, and scenario evidence; (3) data-health and confidence gates constraining escalation; (4) DeXposure-Bench six-axis evaluation harness with regulator-aligned absolute-loss ground truth and false-intervention rate.
AI.result: Experiments on five years of weekly real data fully support the system.
AI.conclusion: The proposed structured evidence routing and evaluation approach effectively addresses LLM over-intervention issues in decentralized finance supervision, validated on real-world data.

title: Information Lattice Learning as Probabilistic Graphical Model Structure Learning
summary: Information lattice learning (ILL) learns interpretable rules of a signal by alternately projecting the signal onto a partition lattice that encodes a hierarchy of abstractions and lifting selected rules back to the signal domain. When the signal is a probability mass function, we show the probabilistic rules learned by ILL admit a natural probabilistic graphical model (PGM) interpretation and develop this interpretation in detail. A partition in ILL induces a deterministic quotient variable, and a rule is the marginal law of that quotient variable. A rule set is therefore a collection of marginal constraints over interpretable abstractions. General lifting is the feasible family of all joint distributions satisfying those constraints, while special lifting chooses a maximum-ignorance reconstruction, implemented in ILL by an L2 uniformity principle closely related to maximum entropy. Under a Shannon-entropy lifting, the same constraints yield a log-linear factor graph whose factors are indexed by learned abstractions. The information lattice itself, however, is not a Bayesian network: its edges encode refinement and coarsening of abstractions, not conditional dependence. Thus ILL is best viewed as structure learning for interpretable constraint-based factor graphs over quotient variables. This view clarifies how ILL relates to graphical models and maximum entropy models, while suggesting new directions for inference, identifiability, and hybrid symbolic-probabilistic learning.

AI.tldr: Information lattice learning (ILL) learns interpretable probabilistic rules by projecting signals onto partition lattices; the learned rules correspond to marginal constraints that define a family of joint distributions. Under Shannon-entropy lifting, these constraints yield a log-linear factor graph, positioning ILL as structure learning for interpretable constraint-based factor graphs over quotient variables.
AI.motivation: To provide a rigorous probabilistic graphical model interpretation of information lattice learning (ILL), clarifying how its learned rules, partition-induced quotient variables, and lifting operations connect to graphical models, maximum entropy models, and factor graphs.
AI.method: Formal analysis showing that partitions in ILL induce deterministic quotient variables with marginal laws as rules; general lifting yields the feasible set of joint distributions satisfying those marginal constraints; special lifting uses an L2 uniformity principle (closely related to maximum entropy); under Shannon-entropy lifting, constraints produce a log-linear factor graph indexed by learned abstractions.
AI.result: ILL rules are marginal constraints over interpretable abstractions; the information lattice encodes refinement/coarsening (not conditional dependence), so it is not a Bayesian network but rather a structure learning framework for constraint-based factor graphs over quotient variables.
AI.conclusion: ILL is best understood as structure learning for interpretable constraint-based factor graphs, clarifying its relationship to PGMs and maximum entropy models, and suggesting new directions for inference, identifiability, and hybrid symbolic-probabilistic learning.

title: PolSeT: Polish Semantics of Timbre Dataset
comment: 8 pages, 7 figures. Data descriptor for the PolSeT dataset (Polish Semantics of Timbre), available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.17830609 under CC BY 4.0
summary: This data report introduces PolSeT (Polish Semantic Timbre), a dataset designed to facilitate research in psychoacoustics and Music Information Retrieval (MIR) in Polish and cross-cultural contexts. The dataset contains data from two sequential experiments. Experiment 1 (N=60) was a free-verbalization task aimed at creating a lexicon of Polish semantic descriptors. Using 11 stimuli, a total of 1901 descriptors (701 unique) were gathered. Experiment 2 (N=105) utilized this lexicon to conduct a semantic differential study, where participants rated 18 instrument sounds on 8 bipolar scales, with repeated trials for reliability analysis. The released dataset includes raw listener responses, comprehensive demographics (experience, gender, age), audio stimuli, and extracted acoustic features with Python extraction code. This dataset addresses a gap in open timbre research data, providing both the qualitative linguistic groundwork and the quantitative ratings necessary for psychoacoustic research and the training of multilingual semantic embedding models.

AI.tldr: PolSeT is a dataset for psychoacoustics and MIR research in Polish, containing semantic descriptors from free-verbalization (N=60, 1901 descriptors) and semantic differential ratings (N=105, 18 sounds × 8 scales) with demographics, audio, and acoustic features.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in open timbre research data for the Polish language and cross-cultural contexts, providing both qualitative linguistic foundations and quantitative ratings for psychoacoustic research and multilingual semantic embedding model training.
AI.method: Two sequential experiments: (1) free-verbalization task (N=60) using 11 stimuli to gather 1901 Polish semantic descriptors (701 unique); (2) semantic differential study (N=105) where participants rated 18 instrument sounds on 8 bipolar scales with repeated trials for reliability. Dataset includes raw responses, demographics, audio stimuli, acoustic features, and extraction code.
AI.result: 1901 descriptors (701 unique) collected in Experiment 1; quantitative ratings from 105 participants across 18 sounds and 8 bipolar scales in Experiment 2; released raw responses, demographics, audio, and acoustic features with Python extraction code.
AI.conclusion: PolSeT fills a gap in open timbre research for Polish, enabling both psychoacoustic studies and multilingual semantic embedding model development through combined qualitative and quantitative data.

title: Beyond the GUI Paradigm: Do Mobile Agents Need the Phone Screen?
summary: Recent advances in mobile agents are dominated by the GUI paradigm, in which agents perceive UI information and emit screen interactions. However, mobile platforms also expose a command-line interface (CLI) that provides direct access to device services and data. We argue CLI deserves first-class consideration alongside GUI. We evaluate three coding agents (Claude Code, Terminus-2, mini-swe-agent) across four model APIs on AndroidWorld and MobileWorld without any mobile-specific post-training, comparing against three reproducible GUI baselines (GUI-Owl-1.5-32B, MAI-UI, Qwen3-VL-32B). Claude Code (Opus 4.7) reaches 71.8\% and 51.9\%, outperforming every reproducible GUI baseline (69.3/68.1/57.8\% on AndroidWorld; 43.2/26.3/13.3\% on MobileWorld), while every other CLI configuration remains competitive. To establish the paradigm's ceiling, we provide oracle CLI solutions that reach 88.8\% on AndroidWorld (103/116 tasks CLI-solvable) and 86.3\% on MobileWorld (101/117 tasks CLI-solvable), indicating substantial room for future improvement. To cover everyday user intents beyond the GUI scope, we introduce the \textbf{CLI-Advantage Task Suite}, comprising 45 templates across five categories: bulk operations, multi-condition filtering, aggregation, cross-app workflows, and hidden device state. Every CLI agent outperforms every GUI baseline in all five categories, with substantially fewer steps per task (10.7 vs.\ 18.6). To support future research on mobile CLI agents, we will open-source agent implementations, oracle solutions, the CLI-Advantage suite, and evaluation infrastructure.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that command-line interfaces (CLI) on mobile platforms deserve first-class consideration alongside GUI for mobile agents. Evaluating coding agents like Claude Code, Terminus-2, and mini-swe-agent on AndroidWorld and MobileWorld, they find CLI agents outperform all reproducible GUI baselines without any mobile-specific post-training. They introduce a CLI-Advantage Task Suite covering tasks beyond GUI scope, where CLI agents excel with fewer steps. Oracle solutions show substantial room for improvement (88.8% on AndroidWorld, 86.3% on MobileWorld).
AI.motivation: Current mobile agent research is dominated by the GUI paradigm. The authors argue that CLI (command-line interface) on mobile platforms, which provides direct access to device services and data, has been overlooked and deserves first-class consideration alongside GUI.
AI.method: The authors evaluate three coding agents (Claude Code, Terminus-2, mini-swe-agent) across four model APIs on AndroidWorld and MobileWorld without any mobile-specific post-training, comparing against three reproducible GUI baselines (GUI-Owl-1.5-32B, MAI-UI, Qwen3-VL-32B). They also provide oracle CLI solutions to establish the paradigm's ceiling and introduce the CLI-Advantage Task Suite covering five categories: bulk operations, multi-condition filtering, aggregation, cross-app workflows, and hidden device state.
AI.result: Claude Code (Opus 4.7) achieves 71.8% and 51.9%, outperforming all reproducible GUI baselines (69.3/68.1/57.8% on AndroidWorld; 43.2/26.3/13.3% on MobileWorld). Oracle CLI solutions reach 88.8% on AndroidWorld and 86.3% on MobileWorld. CLI agents outperform every GUI baseline in all five CLI-Advantage categories with fewer steps (10.7 vs. 18.6 per task).
AI.conclusion: CLI is a promising paradigm for mobile agents that deserves first-class consideration. CLI agents can outperform GUI baselines without mobile-specific training and handle tasks GUI cannot easily address. The authors will open-source their implementations to support future research.

title: Mix-QVLA: Task-Evidence-Aware Mixed-Precision Quantization of Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: We propose Mix-QVLA, a task-evidence-aware mixed-precision PTQ framework for VLA models. Mix-QVLA anchors each quantized variant to the full-precision action-token reference decision and evaluates whether quantization preserves task-relevant evidence across key VLA functional boundaries. It computes normalized gradient-weighted task-evidence maps from boundary activations and compares full-precision and quantized maps using evidence-mass and attribution-distribution distortion, capturing changes in both the strength and allocation of decision-supporting evidence. A soft-bottleneck objective aggregates boundary-level degradation into layer-wise sensitivity scores. Mix-QVLA further models sensitivity throughout task execution, capturing phase-dependent shifts in layer importance rather than assuming a fixed sensitivity profile. The resulting evidence- and time-aware scores guide mixed-precision bit allocation under model-size and BitOps budgets. Extensive evaluations on OpenVLA-style policies show that Mix-QVLA improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off of low-bit VLA deployment. On LIBERO, Mix-QVLA reduces OpenVLA-OFT memory from 15.4 GB to 4.1 GB, retains 96.3 average success compared with 97.1 for the BF16 model, and achieves a 1.52x inference speedup.

AI.tldr: Mix-QVLA is a task-evidence-aware mixed-precision post-training quantization framework for Vision-Language-Action models that uses gradient-weighted task-evidence maps and temporal sensitivity modeling to allocate bit-widths, reducing memory from 15.4GB to 4.1GB while retaining 96.3% success rate (vs 97.1% BF16) with 1.52x speedup on LIBERO.
AI.motivation: Deploying large Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models at low bit-widths is challenging because uniform quantization degrades task-relevant decision evidence. Existing PTQ methods fail to account for which activations carry discriminative signal for action prediction, leading to accuracy loss.
AI.method: Mix-QVLA (1) computes normalized gradient-weighted task-evidence maps from boundary activations; (2) compares full-precision vs quantized maps via evidence-mass and attribution-distribution distortion; (3) aggregates degradation via a soft-bottleneck objective into layer-wise sensitivity scores; (4) models temporal sensitivity shifts throughout task execution; (5) uses evidence- and time-aware scores for mixed-precision bit allocation under size and BitOps budgets.
AI.result: On LIBERO with OpenVLA-OFT, Mix-QVLA reduces memory from 15.4 GB to 4.1 GB, retains 96.3% average success rate (vs 97.1% for BF16), and achieves 1.52x inference speedup. Extensive evaluations on OpenVLA-style policies confirm improved accuracy-efficiency trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: Mix-QVLA demonstrates that evidence-aware, temporally-adaptive mixed-precision quantization significantly improves low-bit VLA deployment, offering strong accuracy retention, substantial memory savings, and practical inference speedups.

title: LLM Doesn't Know What It Doesn't Know: Detecting Epistemic Blind Spots via Cross-Model Attribution Divergence on Clinical Tabular Data
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to structured clinical data, yet whether they can recognize the limits of their own knowledge on such tasks remains unexplored. We study this question through the lens of cross-model attribution divergence with the goal of reducing epistemic uncertainty for structured tasks, comparing Qwen 2.5 7B and XGBoost on a prediction task via attribution divergence analysis. We report four findings. First, LLM verbalized confidence is epistemically vacuous, it outputs a near-constant (0.856-0.937) regardless of whether accuracy is 49% or 75.3%, tracking prompt format rather than prediction quality. Second, the LLM exhibits an inverse difficulty effect: accuracy drops to 64.8% when XGBoost is 99% correct, but matches XGBoost (73.8% vs. 73.1%) when it is moderately uncertain. Third, few-shot examples and SHAP-derived feature evidence are orthogonal, super-additive interventions: they reduce the Attribution Disagreement Score (ADS) from 1.54 to 0.38 and improve accuracy from 49% to 75.3% without training. Fourth, a cross-model calibrator that determined LLM reliability using attribution divergence signals reduces expected calibration error from 0.254 to 0.080, replacing uninformative verbalized confidence with patient-specific reliability estimates, without accessing model internals or requiring repeated inference. We frame these findings as a cold start problem for LLMs on structured data and outline a path toward genuine epistemic self-awareness.

AI.tldr: LLMs on structured clinical data show epistemically vacuous confidence and an inverse difficulty effect, but combining few-shot examples with SHAP-derived feature evidence reduces attribution disagreement and improves accuracy. Cross-model calibration using attribution divergence signals replaces uninformative verbalized confidence with patient-specific reliability estimates, reducing calibration error from 0.254 to 0.080.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether LLMs can recognize the limits of their own knowledge when applied to structured clinical data, specifically through cross-model attribution divergence analysis to reduce epistemic uncertainty.
AI.method: Compared Qwen 2.5 7B and XGBoost on a prediction task via attribution divergence analysis; used few-shot examples and SHAP-derived feature evidence as super-additive interventions; developed a cross-model calibrator using attribution divergence signals.
AI.result: (1) LLM verbalized confidence is epistemically vacuous (constant 0.856-0.937 regardless of accuracy 49%-75.3%). (2) Inverse difficulty effect: accuracy drops to 64.8% when XGBoost is 99% correct. (3) Few-shot + SHAP evidence reduces ADS from 1.54 to 0.38, accuracy from 49% to 75.3%. (4) Cross-model calibrator reduces ECE from 0.254 to 0.080.
AI.conclusion: Frames LLM limitations on structured data as a cold start problem and outlines a path toward genuine epistemic self-awareness via cross-model attribution divergence.

title: Weibull Weight-Scale Parameter Evolution under AdamW Training Dynamics
summary: Building on a two-parameter Weibull framework for diagnosing transformer weight distributions, we study why the Weibull weight-scale parameter $λ$ grows, overshoots, and then relaxes during AdamW training. We derive a leading-order three-force decomposition of the squared weight norm from the AdamW update: an alignment force measuring the correlation between weights and the adaptive update direction, an injection force from adaptive step magnitude, and a decay force from decoupled weight decay. On self-trained Pythia-70M models with ground-truth optimizer moments, alignment dominates the rise phase, contributing 88-94% of the absolute force budget across four random seeds and remaining robust to super-weight removal. Near saturation, alignment and decay approach balance, explaining the transition from weight-scale growth to relaxation. These force dynamics directly govern the squared-norm component underlying $λ(t)$; the remaining RMS-to-Weibull reconstruction offset is measurable and decomposes into bridge and integration components, totaling approximately 5-6% in densely sampled regions. To extend the analysis to real models where optimizer moments are unavailable, we introduce a spline displacement method that recovers the alignment force from sparse checkpoints with approximately 92-94% accuracy, about twice the naive two-point baseline. We further observe that the peak value of $λ(t)$ varies with training-data coherence in our experiments, suggesting a data-dependent component of weight-scale growth that we leave to a controlled follow-up study. Code and data are available at https://github.com/tiexinding/NPM-Weibull-public.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how the Weibull weight-scale parameter λ evolves during AdamW training, identifying a three-force decomposition (alignment, injection, decay) that governs its growth, overshoot, and relaxation. Alignment dominates the rise phase, decay balances it near saturation, and a spline displacement method recovers alignment forces from sparse checkpoints with ~92-94% accuracy.
AI.motivation: To understand why the Weibull weight-scale parameter λ grows, overshoots, and relaxes during AdamW training, and to develop methods for diagnosing these dynamics even when optimizer moments are unavailable.
AI.method: Derives a leading-order three-force decomposition of squared weight norm from the AdamW update: alignment force (weight-update correlation), injection force (adaptive step magnitude), and decay force (weight decay). Uses self-trained Pythia-70M models with ground-truth optimizer moments, and introduces a spline displacement method to recover alignment forces from sparse checkpoints.
AI.result: Alignment dominates the rise phase (88-94% of absolute force budget). Near saturation, alignment and decay approach balance, explaining the transition. The RMS-to-Weibull reconstruction offset is ~5-6%. The spline displacement method recovers alignment force with ~92-94% accuracy (2× better than two-point baseline). Peak λ varies with training-data coherence.
AI.conclusion: The three-force decomposition explains λ dynamics during AdamW training. The spline displacement method enables analysis on real models. Data-dependent components of weight-scale growth warrant future study.

title: Segment-Level Mandarin Chinese Speech-Based Cognitive Impairment Detection via an Autoencoder with Contrastive Learning
summary:
oindent	extbf{Background and Objective:} Speech has emerged as a low-cost and non-invasive digital biomarker with considerable potential for cognitive impairment detection. However, limited labeled data and cross-dataset variability remain major challenges for robust speech-based screening systems.
  \par
oindent	extbf{Methods:} We developed a segment-level representation learning framework for speech-based cognitive impairment detection. Speech recordings were divided into short segments and converted into spectrogram representations. To improve robustness under limited-data conditions, offline and online augmentation strategies were combined with autoencoder-based representation learning and contrastive objectives to enhance discriminative latent representations.
  \par
oindent	extbf{Results:} Experiments conducted on four independent Mandarin Chinese speech datasets demonstrated stable and competitive performance in both binary and three-class classification tasks, with particularly notable improvements in the clinically challenging three-class setting. Ablation studies further supported the effectiveness of the proposed framework.
  \par
oindent	extbf{Conclusions:} The findings suggest that segment-level speech representation learning may provide a scalable and practical approach for cognitive impairment screening in resource-constrained clinical settings.

AI.tldr: A segment-level representation learning framework using spectrograms, autoencoders, and contrastive learning with data augmentation improves speech-based cognitive impairment detection across multiple Mandarin datasets, especially for three-class classification.
AI.motivation: Speech is a low-cost digital biomarker for cognitive impairment, but limited labeled data and cross-dataset variability hinder robust screening systems.
AI.method: Speech recordings are divided into short segments, converted to spectrograms, and processed via autoencoder-based representation learning with contrastive objectives; offline and online data augmentation strategies are combined to improve robustness under limited data.
AI.result: Stable competitive performance on four independent Mandarin Chinese datasets in binary and three-class classification tasks, with notable improvements in the challenging three-class setting; ablation studies confirm framework effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Segment-level speech representation learning offers a scalable, practical approach for cognitive impairment screening in resource-constrained clinical settings.

title: Execution-bound advisory automation for agentic AI: a reproducible AIBOM-driven CSAF-VEX framework
summary: A protocol driven framework is presented that binds SBOM and AIBOM artefacts to deterministic environment capture and structured runtime telemetry. Exploitability is computed from declared artefacts, observed activation conditions, and enforced execution policies. CSAF VEX advisories are generated from combined static and runtime evidence, cryptographically signed, and validated through deterministic replay. Evaluation uses approximately 10000 component entries across synthetic Agentic AI workloads 50 to 5000 components, incorporating OSV, GitHub Advisory, KEV, and EPSS datasets.

AI.tldr: A protocol-driven framework uses SBOM/AIBOM, deterministic environment capture, and runtime telemetry to compute exploitability, generate signed CSAF VEX advisories, and validate via deterministic replay. Evaluated on ~10,000 components across synthetic AI workloads (50–5000 components) using OSV, GitHub Advisory, KEV, and EPSS datasets.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of binding software and AI bill of materials (SBOM/AIBOM) to runtime evidence for accurate exploitability assessment and actionable security advisory generation in complex AI-driven workflows.
AI.method: 1) Binds SBOM/AIBOM artefacts to deterministic environment capture and structured runtime telemetry. 2) Computes exploitability from declared artefacts, observed activation conditions, and enforced execution policies. 3) Generates CSAF VEX advisories from combined static and runtime evidence. 4) Cryptographically signs advisories and validates through deterministic replay.
AI.result: Evaluated with ~10,000 component entries across synthetic Agentic AI workloads ranging from 50 to 5,000 components, incorporating OSV, GitHub Advisory, KEV, and EPSS datasets. The framework successfully bridges static declarations with runtime observability to produce verifiable, signed exploitability assessments.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates a viable approach for continuous, evidence-driven vulnerability management in AI systems, enabling cryptographically verifiable VEX advisories grounded in both static (SBOM/AIBOM) and dynamic (runtime telemetry) evidence, validated through deterministic replay.

title: Language-Instructed Vision Embeddings for Controllable and Generalizable Perception
summary: Vision foundation models are typically trained as static feature extractors, placing the burden of task adaptation onto large downstream models. We propose an alternative paradigm: instead of solely feeding visual features into language models, we use language itself to dynamically guide the vision encoder. Our method, Language-Instructed Vision Embeddings (LIVE), leverages language as high-level guidance to produce task-centric embeddings at inference time, removing the need for task-specific retraining. This enables the encoder to focus on contextually relevant aspects of the input, yielding more controllable and generalizable representations. Empirically, LIVE reduces visual hallucinations (+34 points on MMVP), surpasses vision-language models with orders of magnitude more parameters on visual question answering, and generalizes to unseen instructions and tasks -- offering a direct path toward adaptive, instruction-driven visual intelligence.

AI.tldr: LIVE uses language to dynamically guide vision encoders at inference time, producing task-centric embeddings that reduce hallucinations, outperform larger models on VQA, and generalize to unseen tasks without retraining.
AI.motivation: Vision foundation models are typically static feature extractors, requiring large downstream models for task adaptation. The goal is to reduce this burden by using language to dynamically guide the vision encoder at inference time.
AI.method: LIVE leverages language as high-level guidance to dynamically steer the vision encoder, producing task-centric embeddings at inference time without task-specific retraining.
AI.result: Reduces visual hallucinations by +34 points on MMVP, surpasses vision-language models with orders of magnitude more parameters on VQA, and generalizes to unseen instructions and tasks.
AI.conclusion: LIVE offers a direct path toward adaptive, instruction-driven visual intelligence by using language to dynamically guide vision encoders, yielding more controllable and generalizable representations.

title: REVEAL++: Differentiable Phenotypic Grouping for Vision-Language Retinal Modeling of Alzheimer's Disease Risk
summary: The retina offers a noninvasive window into neurodegenerative disease, capturing subtle structural patterns associated with a risk of future cognitive decline. Vision-language alignment frameworks such as REVEAL have shown that pairing retinal fundus images with structured clinical risk narratives improves early prediction of Alzheimer's disease (AD). A key design choice in these approaches is the use of phenotypic grouping, where individuals with similar risk profiles are treated as multi-positive pairs during contrastive learning. However, existing methods operationalize phenotypic similarity as a discrete construct, relying on hard group assignments that impose rigid supervision and decouple group formation from representation learning. We propose a continuous formulation of phenotypic structure within contrastive learning. Rather than assigning samples to fixed clusters, we model inter-subject similarity as a differentiable weighting function derived from intra-modality embedding similarities in both retinal images and risk profiles. These weights define soft multi-positive relationships through a continuous aggregation operator, enabling graded supervision that reflects the spectrum nature of disease risk. We further introduce a soft-target contrastive objective that jointly learns cross-modal alignment and phenotypic structure in an end-to-end manner. Evaluated on UK Biobank retinal imaging data for incident AD prediction, the proposed framework consistently outperforms discrete group-based contrastive learning and standard vision-language baselines. By treating phenotypic similarity as a learnable, continuous signal rather than a fixed grouping rule, our approach provides a principled and robust foundation for population-scale neurodegenerative risk modeling from multi-modal retinal and clinical data.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a continuous phenotypic similarity formulation for contrastive learning in vision-language models, replacing discrete group assignments with differentiable soft weights derived from intra-modality embeddings, improving Alzheimer's disease prediction from retinal fundus images and clinical risk narratives on UK Biobank data.
AI.motivation: Existing vision-language alignment methods for AD prediction from retinal images use discrete phenotypic grouping (hard group assignments) that imposes rigid supervision, decouples group formation from representation learning, and fails to capture the continuous spectrum of disease risk.
AI.method: Models inter-subject similarity as a differentiable weighting function from intra-modality embedding similarities in both retinal images and risk profiles; uses soft multi-positive relationships via a continuous aggregation operator; introduces a soft-target contrastive objective for end-to-end learning of cross-modal alignment and phenotypic structure.
AI.result: On UK Biobank retinal imaging data for incident AD prediction, the proposed continuous framework consistently outperforms discrete group-based contrastive learning and standard vision-language baselines.
AI.conclusion: Treating phenotypic similarity as a learnable, continuous signal instead of fixed grouping provides a principled and robust foundation for population-scale neurodegenerative risk modeling from multi-modal retinal and clinical data.

title: Zero-Inflated Gaussian Distributions Enable Parameter-Space Sparsity in Estimation-of-Distribution Algorithms
summary: Estimation-of-distribution algorithms (EDAs) are a powerful class of evolutionary methods for black-box optimization, especially when little is known about the structure of the objective. Whereas classical evolutionary algorithms rely on hand-designed mutation and crossover operators, hard to devise for unknown problem structures, and a source of bias, EDAs sidestep operator design entirely: they fit a probability distribution to the best individuals and sample the next generation from it. EDAs are well established on continuous parameter spaces, but they have not previously been generalized to sparse ones, in which most coefficients of a good solution are exactly zero. Existing sparse black-box optimizers therefore reintroduce exactly what EDAs were designed to avoid: hand-crafted sparsity operators, bi-level schemes alternating between support set and active values, zeroing thresholds, and other baked-in assumptions. We close this gap by proposing multivariate zero-inflated Gaussian (ZIG) distributions as EDA sampling laws. A latent Gaussian model with separate indicator and value dimensions represents sparsity patterns, correlations among active parameters, and the interactions between the two, so sparsity patterns and active values are optimized jointly, hierarchy-free. We show that the latent parameters of this model are identifiable from observed samples, unlike in the missing-data settings where related constructions originate, and introduce practical amortized inversion-based estimators for them. The estimators accurately recover latent correlation structures, and on the Lunar Lander benchmark the resulting ZIG-EDA converges faster and reaches higher final returns than a dense Gaussian EDA, a hand-crafted sparse evolutionary algorithm, and an ad-hoc sparse EDA, while finding controllers with only a small fraction of parameters active.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces multivariate zero-inflated Gaussian (ZIG) distributions as sampling laws for estimation-of-distribution algorithms (EDAs), enabling black-box optimization in sparse parameter spaces where most optimal coefficients are zero, without hand-crafted sparsity operators. The proposed ZIG-EDA jointly optimizes sparsity patterns and active values, with identifiable latent parameters and practical amortized estimators, achieving faster convergence and higher returns than dense EDAs, hand-crafted sparse EAs, and ad-hoc sparse EDAs.
AI.motivation: Existing EDAs work well in continuous spaces but cannot handle sparse parameter spaces where most coefficients of a good solution are exactly zero. Current sparse black-box optimizers rely on hand-crafted sparsity operators, bi-level schemes, and zeroing thresholds—exactly the kind of baked-in assumptions EDAs were designed to avoid. The authors aim to close this gap by generalizing EDAs to sparse spaces without reintroducing manual operator design.
AI.method: The authors propose using multivariate zero-inflated Gaussian (ZIG) distributions as EDA sampling laws. The ZIG distribution is a latent Gaussian model with separate indicator and value dimensions that captures sparsity patterns, correlations among active parameters, and their interactions. They show these latent parameters are identifiable from observed samples and introduce practical amortized inversion-based estimators to recover them efficiently.
AI.result: The estimators accurately recover latent correlation structures. On the Lunar Lander benchmark, the ZIG-EDA converges faster and reaches higher final returns compared to a dense Gaussian EDA, a hand-crafted sparse evolutionary algorithm, and an ad-hoc sparse EDA. Furthermore, the ZIG-EDA finds controllers with only a small fraction of parameters active.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ZIG-EDA successfully extends EDAs to sparse parameter spaces, eliminating the need for hand-designed sparsity operators while outperforming existing sparse and dense black-box optimizers. The joint optimization of sparsity patterns and active values is effective, and the identifiability of latent parameters ensures practical applicability.

title: DevOps and General Developers: Insights from Stack Overflow's 2023 Survey
comment: 17 pages, 11 tables, research paper based on the 2023 Stack Overflow Developer Survey data analysis
summary:
Purpose: To investigate the distinct roles of DevOps specialists and general software developers, examining their varying use of tools, technologies, methodologies, and demographics in the current software development environment. In addition, to differentiate these two professional groups regarding their unique contributions and challenges in the field.
  Design/Methodology/Approach: The research uses a quantitative approach to analyze data from the Stack Overflow 2023 Developer Survey. It focuses on a comparative analysis of technological preferences, demographic information, and professional experiences between DevOps specialists and general developers, highlighting key trends and differences. The data analysis was conducted using Python's Pandas library for data analysis.
  Findings: The research indicates no significant difference in the tool and technology preferences between DevOps specialists and general software developers, highlighting their complementary roles. DevOps specialists and general software developers use tools like Docker and Kubernetes, emphasizing efficiency and automation. While general developers employ diverse tools for various role demands, demographic trends show younger general developers and mid-career DevOps professionals. This age range reflects growing experience in DevOps, and both groups are adapting to remote and hybrid work models in the evolving tech industry.
  Practical Implications: This research offers perspectives on the dynamic roles within software development, emphasizing the growing importance of DevOps. It is a valuable resource for academic and industry professionals to understand the evolving dynamics in software development roles.
  Originality/Value: This research fills a significant gap in the existing literature regarding the evolving dynamics of software development roles.

AI.tldr: This study analyzes Stack Overflow 2023 survey data to compare DevOps specialists and general developers, finding no significant tool/tech preference differences, with both groups using Docker, Kubernetes, and emphasizing automation; demographic trends show younger general developers and mid-career DevOps professionals.
AI.motivation: To investigate distinct roles, tool/technology usage, methodologies, and demographics of DevOps specialists versus general software developers, and to differentiate their contributions and challenges.
AI.method: Quantitative analysis of Stack Overflow 2023 Developer Survey data using Python's Pandas library, focusing on comparative analysis of technological preferences, demographics, and professional experiences.
AI.result: No significant difference in tool/technology preferences between the two groups; both use Docker and Kubernetes; general developers use more diverse tools; younger general developers and mid-career DevOps professionals observed; both groups adapting to remote/hybrid work.
AI.conclusion: DevOps specialists and general developers have complementary rather than distinct roles; the study fills a literature gap on evolving software development role dynamics.

title: Emergent Alignment
summary: Can Large Language Models (LLMs) discern when their own outputs are misaligned with human ethics? And can they self-correct? We endow an LLM with a conscience step that reviews its own reasoning and outputs, and we extend the training loss with an alignment component using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to steer the model away from non-ethical outputs. The result is an online technique to align models in a wide range of applications: training, fine-tuning, adversarial prompting, and zero-shot learning. It does not require a weaker or stronger judge, relying instead on a frozen copy of itself. In previous work, the Emergent Misalignment scenario showed a range of emergent unethical behaviors from fine-tuning the model to hack code. Instead, we empirically show how to achieve Emergent Alignment: a single high-level introspective question steers training toward an ethical model under the same code hacking scenario.

AI.tldr: LLMs can self-detect ethical misalignment and self-correct via a conscience step using DPO, achieving Emergent Alignment without external judges.
AI.motivation: To enable LLMs to autonomously detect and correct unethical outputs without relying on external judges, addressing the Emergent Misalignment problem where fine-tuning leads to unethical behaviors like code hacking.
AI.method: Introduced a "conscience step" where the LLM reviews its own reasoning/outputs, combined with DPO-based alignment loss to steer away from unethical outputs. Uses a frozen copy of the model itself (no external judge).
AI.result: Achieved Emergent Alignment: a single high-level introspective question steers training toward ethical behavior in the code hacking scenario, reversing the Emergent Misalignment observed in prior work.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised alignment via introspective review and DPO is effective across training, fine-tuning, adversarial prompting, and zero-shot learning, with no need for weaker or stronger external judges.

title: Human-like autonomy emerges from self-play and a pinch of human data
summary: Self-play reinforcement learning has recently emerged as a way to train driving policies without any human data. It uses cheap, large-scale simulations to substitute expensive, large-scale human driving demonstrations. A key limitation of this approach is that policies trained through pure self-play can learn effective but alien driving conventions incompatible with people. Previous works attempt to mitigate such behavioral misalignments through extensive reward engineering and domain randomization, which are brittle and labor-intensive. Instead of completely discarding human demonstrations, our method treats them as a regularization objective on top of a minimal safe goal-reaching reward. Like the spice in a good stew, we find that a little human data goes a long way: our method uses only 30 minutes of human demonstrations, 2500x fewer than comparable imitation learning approaches. Resulting policies coordinate with held-out human trajectories and complete training in 15 hours on a single consumer-grade GPU. Videos and full source code are available at https://spiced-self-play.com/.

AI.tldr: A self-play RL method for driving that uses minimal human data (30 minutes) as a regularization objective, achieving human-compatible driving policies with 2500x less human data than imitation learning, trainable in 15 hours on a single consumer GPU.
AI.motivation: Pure self-play reinforcement learning for driving produces policies that are effective but follow alien driving conventions incompatible with humans. Prior solutions using reward engineering and domain randomization are brittle and labor-intensive.
AI.method: Treats a small amount of human demonstration data (30 minutes) as a regularization objective on top of a minimal safe goal-reaching reward, rather than discarding human data entirely or using it for full imitation learning.
AI.result: Uses 2500x less human data than comparable imitation learning approaches, achieves coordination with held-out human trajectories, and completes training in 15 hours on a single consumer-grade GPU.
AI.conclusion: A small amount of human data, used as regularization rather than the primary training signal, is sufficient to align self-play driving policies with human driving conventions while maintaining the scalability benefits of self-play RL.

title: Zero-VC: Zero-Lookahead Streaming Voice Conversion via Speaker Anonymization
summary: Streaming zero-shot voice conversion struggles to disentangle timbre from linguistic content without degrading utility or inflating latency. Current methods rely on information bottleneck (IB) or speaker perturbation. While IB filters out timbre, it discards prosody, forcing models to explicitly inject features like fundamental frequency. This often requires buffering future frames, creating algorithmic lookahead latency. On the other hand, existing perturbation methods largely overlook the crucial trade-off between timbre leakage and utility preservation. Recognizing this neglected trade-off, we find that the inherent objective of Speaker Anonymization (SA) aligns well with balancing these factors. Thus, we introduce SA as a novel perturbation mechanism to explicitly mitigate timbre leakage while retaining prosodic utility. Crucially, SA's robust representations significantly alleviate the generator's reliance on future context, enabling our strictly causal, zero-lookahead network. Audio samples are available at https://amphionteam.github.io/Zero-VC-demo/.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using Speaker Anonymization (SA) as a perturbation mechanism for streaming zero-shot voice conversion, which balances timbre leakage and utility preservation while enabling strictly causal, zero-lookahead operation without future frame buffering.
AI.motivation: Current streaming zero-shot VC methods either use information bottleneck (discards prosody, requires future frames causing latency) or speaker perturbation (neglects timbre-utility trade-off). The authors aim to address these limitations by finding a better perturbation approach.
AI.method: The authors introduce Speaker Anonymization (SA) as a novel perturbation mechanism that explicitly mitigates timbre leakage while retaining prosodic utility. SA's robust representations reduce the generator's reliance on future context, allowing for a strictly causal, zero-lookahead network.
AI.result: SA-based perturbation successfully balances timbre leakage and utility preservation, and its robust representations enable strictly causal (zero-lookahead) streaming voice conversion without requiring future frame buffering.
AI.conclusion: Speaker Anonymization aligns well with the objectives of streaming zero-shot VC, serving as an effective perturbation mechanism that resolves the timbre-utility trade-off and eliminates algorithmic lookahead latency.

title: JustDiag!: A Diagnostic Justification Engine for Accountable Root Cause Analysis
summary: Large language models can produce fluent root cause analyses, but fluent final answers alone are insufficient evidence for accountability in high-stakes operations. In real incident response, engineers need to know what evidence supported a diagnosis, which alternatives were considered, where contradictions remained, and whether the system resolved the case or preserved uncertainty. We address this gap with JustDiag, a diagnostic justification engine for RCA that maintains an explicit process state over evidence, findings, competing hypotheses, conflicts, and next checks. We evaluated the system on 66 real-world incidents using a two-layer protocol that separately scores final-answer quality and process quality. Relative to a matched control without diagnostic justification, JustDiag achieved stronger outcome and process scores, while accepting slightly lower terminal completion due to more calibrated non-closure. These results suggest that accountable RCA requires explicit diagnostic justification artifacts and process-aware evaluation, not only fluent final answers.

AI.tldr: JustDiag is a diagnostic justification engine for Root Cause Analysis (RCA) that maintains explicit process state over evidence, findings, competing hypotheses, conflicts, and next checks, achieving stronger outcome and process scores than a control without diagnostic justification, at the cost of slightly lower terminal completion due to more calibrated non-closure.
AI.motivation: Fluent final answers from LLMs are insufficient for accountability in high-stakes operations; engineers need evidence-supported diagnoses, considered alternatives, contradictions, and uncertainty preservation.
AI.method: JustDiag maintains an explicit process state over evidence, findings, competing hypotheses, conflicts, and next checks; evaluated on 66 real-world incidents using a two-layer protocol scoring final-answer quality and process quality, compared against a matched control without diagnostic justification.
AI.result: JustDiag achieved stronger outcome and process scores relative to the control, while accepting slightly lower terminal completion due to more calibrated non-closure.
AI.conclusion: Accountable RCA requires explicit diagnostic justification artifacts and process-aware evaluation, not only fluent final answers.

title: Learning When to Denoise: Optimizing Asynchronous Schedules for Latent Diffusion
summary: Multi-representation diffusion models can improve visual synthesis by denoising complementary views of an image, but their performance depends critically on the asynchronous schedule that determines when each representation is denoised. We propose to learn this schedule. Our method formulates asynchronous flow matching over multiple representation spaces and uses a schedule-corrected objective that keeps each representation's local noising-time weights fixed as the schedule changes. We instantiate the schedule with a flexible parametric class that is convex and monotone by construction, and learn it using a fast joint probe with less than 1% additional training compute. On ImageNet 256x256, the learned schedule substantially improves both convergence speed and final quality under a matched 675M-parameter XL backbone. With AutoGuidance, our 200-epoch model reaches FID 1.05, matching the 800-epoch SFD-XL baseline with 4x less training. Training to 600 epochs further improves to FID 1.02, outperforming the 1B-parameter SFD-XXL result of FID 1.04 while using a smaller model. In the unguided setting, our 200-epoch model reaches FID 2.37, already below the best 800-epoch SFD-XL result (2.54) at 4x less training, and improves to FID 2.14 at 600 epochs. Code is available at https://github.com/bsq532087/LWD

AI.tldr: This paper proposes learning the asynchronous denoising schedule in multi-representation diffusion models to improve both convergence speed and final image quality, achieving state-of-the-art FID scores with significantly less training compute.
AI.motivation: Multi-representation diffusion models depend critically on the asynchronous schedule that determines when each representation is denoised, but existing methods use fixed schedules. The authors propose to learn this schedule to optimize performance.
AI.method: The method formulates asynchronous flow matching over multiple representation spaces, uses a schedule-corrected objective to keep local noising-time weights fixed, instantiates the schedule with a flexible parametric class that is convex and monotone by construction, and learns it using a fast joint probe with less than 1% additional training compute.
AI.result: On ImageNet 256x256, the learned schedule substantially improves convergence speed and final quality. With AutoGuidance, the 200-epoch model reaches FID 1.05 (matching 800-epoch SFD-XL with 4x less training), and 600 epochs improves to FID 1.02 (outperforming 1B-parameter SFD-XXL). In unguided setting, 200 epochs reaches FID 2.37 (below best 800-epoch SFD-XL 2.54), improving to FID 2.14 at 600 epochs.
AI.conclusion: Learning the asynchronous schedule significantly boosts multi-representation diffusion model performance, achieving superior quality with substantially reduced training compute. The approach demonstrates that schedule optimization is a critical lever for efficiency in multi-representation diffusion frameworks.

title: ITNet: A Learnable Integral Transform That Subsumes Convolution, Attention, and Recurrence
summary: Convolutional networks, recurrent networks, and transformers each encode different inductive biases -- locality, sequential memory, and content-dependent pairwise interaction -- and have remained mathematically distinct since their inception. We show that this fragmentation reflects not a fundamental diversity in how signals should be processed, but rather incomplete views of a single underlying mathematical object: a learnable integral transform. We introduce the Integral Transform Network (ITNet), a unified architecture built around a learnable kernel that depends jointly on positions and features. This kernel is implemented as a small neural network, specifically an MLP, that models pairwise interactions, enabling the model to adapt its behavior from data. We show that convolution, self-attention (including multi-head), and autoregressive recurrence (including LSTM, GRU, S4, and Mamba) arise as special cases under appropriate parameterizations, and that ITNet is a universal approximator of continuous operators. To make this practical, we develop tiled kernel fusion, importance-weighted Monte Carlo integration, and learned low-rank factorization, enabling efficient and scalable computation. A single ITNet architecture with a shared operator and lightweight modality-specific encoders matches or exceeds specialized baselines on ImageNet-1K , GLUE, ModelNet40, VQA\,v2 and NLVR2. The results demonstrate that a single learned interaction mechanism can recover the behavior of all three architectural families from data.

AI.tldr: ITNet unifies CNNs, RNNs, and Transformers into a single learnable integral transform with a neural-network kernel, matching or exceeding specialized architectures across vision, language, and multimodal benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing architectures (CNNs, RNNs, Transformers) are treated as mathematically distinct due to different inductive biases, but this fragmentation may stem from incomplete views of a common underlying operator rather than fundamental signal-processing diversity.
AI.method: Introduces the Integral Transform Network (ITNet) with a learnable kernel (MLP) dependent on both positions and features. Develops tiled kernel fusion, importance-weighted Monte Carlo integration, and learned low-rank factorization for efficient computation. Shows convolution, self-attention (multi-head), and recurrence (LSTM, GRU, S4, Mamba) emerge as special parameterizations.
AI.result: A single ITNet architecture with shared operator and lightweight modality-specific encoders matches or exceeds specialized baselines on ImageNet-1K, GLUE, ModelNet40, VQA v2, and NLVR2.
AI.conclusion: A single learned interaction mechanism can recover the behavior of all three architectural families from data, suggesting fundamental unification is possible.

title: ProMUSE: Progressive Multi-modal Uncertainty-guided Staged Evidential Alzheimer Disease Classification
summary: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a fatal disorder that destroys memory and cognitive skills in the elderly population. Most treatments for AD are effective in the early stage, leading to an increasing demand for early AD diagnosis. AD diagnosis increasingly relies on multimodal data such as clinical assessments, structural Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), and Positron Emission Tomography (PET) imaging. However, MRI and PET acquisition remain costly and not universally accessible, making full-modality inference impractical in real-world clinical workflows. We propose ProMUSE, a Progressive Multi-modal Uncertainty Guided Staged Evidential Network that adaptively determines when additional modalities are necessary, helping reduce the overall cost of data acquisition while maintaining accuracy. ProMUSE first performs evidential classification using low-cost clinical data and quantifies uncertainty via a Dirichlet-based subjective logic model. When uncertainty exceeds a learned threshold, ProMUSE progressively incorporates MRI or PET features, fusing modality-wise belief and uncertainty through Dempster-Shafer theory to obtain a calibrated multimodal prediction. This staged acquisition strategy enables accurate diagnosis while minimizing reliance on expensive imaging. Experiments on ADNI, AIBL, and OASIS across CN-AD, CN-MCI, and MCI-AD tasks demonstrate that ProMUSE achieves competitive or superior accuracy compared to full-modality baselines while reducing MRI/PET usage by 50-90%, yielding substantial cost savings. These results highlight ProMUSE as a practical, uncertainty-aware, and resource-efficient solution for real-world AD screening.

AI.tldr: ProMUSE is a progressive multimodal uncertainty-guided network for early Alzheimer's diagnosis that adaptively determines when costly MRI/PET modalities are needed, reducing imaging usage by 50-90% while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Early AD diagnosis increasingly relies on multimodal data (clinical assessments, MRI, PET), but MRI and PET are costly and not universally accessible, making full-modality inference impractical in real clinical workflows.
AI.method: ProMUSE uses a staged evidential network: (1) performs evidential classification using low-cost clinical data with Dirichlet-based subjective logic to quantify uncertainty; (2) when uncertainty exceeds a learned threshold, progressively incorporates MRI or PET features; (3) fuses modality-wise belief and uncertainty via Dempster-Shafer theory for calibrated multimodal prediction.
AI.result: On ADNI, AIBL, and OASIS across CN-AD, CN-MCI, and MCI-AD tasks, ProMUSE achieves competitive or superior accuracy compared to full-modality baselines while reducing MRI/PET usage by 50-90%.
AI.conclusion: ProMUSE is a practical, uncertainty-aware, and resource-efficient solution for real-world AD screening that reduces reliance on expensive imaging without sacrificing diagnostic accuracy.

title: MixProLAP: Mixture-Induced Uncertainty Modeling for Probabilistic Language-Audio Pretraining
summary: Acoustic environments often contain multiple overlapping sound events, and the same acoustic scene can be described using diverse textual expressions, making audio-text alignment inherently ambiguous. This paper proposes a probabilistic audio-language pretraining framework to model many-to-many correspondence ambiguity in audio-text alignment. Unlike conventional contrastive methods that learn deterministic point embeddings, our approach represents each modality as a distribution and learns uncertainty-aware cross-modal alignment. Rather than relying on masking-based uncertainty simulation, we mix audio-text pairs to create overlapping sounds that better reflect real acoustic mixtures and capture semantic inclusion relations among sound events. We further introduce a multi-level inclusion loss to enforce representations consistent with these relations. Experiments on audio-text retrieval benchmarks show that the proposed method outperforms deterministic baselines.

AI.tldr: Proposes a probabilistic audio-language pretraining framework that models many-to-many correspondence ambiguity by representing each modality as a distribution and using mixed audio-text pairs with a multi-level inclusion loss, outperforming deterministic baselines on audio-text retrieval.
AI.motivation: Acoustic environments often contain multiple overlapping sound events and diverse textual descriptions, creating inherent many-to-many correspondence ambiguity in audio-text alignment that conventional deterministic contrastive methods fail to capture.
AI.method: Represents each modality (audio and text) as a distribution instead of a deterministic point embedding; mixes audio-text pairs to create overlapping sounds reflecting real acoustic mixtures; introduces a multi-level inclusion loss to enforce consistency with semantic inclusion relations among sound events.
AI.result: Outperforms deterministic baselines on audio-text retrieval benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A probabilistic approach that captures uncertainty and semantic inclusion relations is effective for handling many-to-many correspondence ambiguity in audio-text alignment.

title: OpenRath: Session-Centered Runtime State for Agent Systems
summary: Modern agent systems often suffer from fragmented runtime state: transcripts, tool effects, memory events, workspace placement, branch provenance, and replay evidence are recorded separately and become difficult to inspect or reproduce. OpenRath addresses this issue with a PyTorch-like programming model for multi-agent, multi-session systems. The analogy concerns the role of a central first-class runtime abstraction, not tensor computation. Its core abstraction is Session, the runtime value passed between agents and workflows. A Session is branchable, inspectable, replayable, backend-aware, and composable. It records conversation chunks, sandbox placement, lineage metadata, token usage, pending work, and tool evidence, while defining where memory interactions enter the runtime record. Since this state is carried by the same value used in program execution, fork, merge, and replay become explicit runtime operations rather than states reconstructed from external traces. OpenRath further defines Sandbox, Tool, Agent, Memory, Workflow, and Selector, with Selector turning control flow into runtime-routed decisions. This report presents the programming model, architecture, audited milestones, and evidence protocol. Its claims are limited to controlled runtime properties, while broad quantitative comparisons, live-provider quality, optional-backend availability, and memory quality are left for follow-on evaluation. The central thesis is that Session provides agent systems with a first-class runtime value for auditable composition.

AI.tldr: OpenRath introduces Session as a first-class runtime abstraction for multi-agent systems, unifying fragmented state (transcripts, tool effects, memory, provenance) into a single branchable, inspectable, replayable, and composable value that makes fork, merge, and replay explicit runtime operations.
AI.motivation: Modern agent systems suffer from fragmented runtime state where transcripts, tool effects, memory events, workspace placement, branch provenance, and replay evidence are recorded separately, making them difficult to inspect or reproduce.
AI.method: OpenRath adopts a PyTorch-like programming model (analogy in runtime abstraction, not tensor computation) centered on Session as the core first-class runtime value. Session carries conversation chunks, sandbox placement, lineage metadata, token usage, pending work, and tool evidence. The system defines Sandbox, Tool, Agent, Memory, Workflow, and Selector abstractions, where Selector turns control flow into runtime-routed decisions. Fork, merge, and replay become explicit runtime operations rather than post-hoc reconstructions.
AI.result: The paper presents the programming model, architecture, audited milestones, and evidence protocol. Quantitative comparisons, live-provider quality, optional-backend availability, and memory quality are deferred to future evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Session provides agent systems with a first-class runtime value for auditable composition, enabling transparent and reproducible multi-agent orchestration.

title: TeleMorpher: Toward Robust Simultaneous Motion-Location Editing
summary: Diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in image and video generation and editing. While recent studies have extended these efforts toward motion editing, simultaneously transforming both motion and location-despite its practical importance-remains largely unexplored. To better understand robust motion-location editing, we first analyze the fundamental factors that degrade its quality. Based on this analysis, we propose TeleMorpher, one of the first one-shot frameworks to the best of our knowledge, for simultaneous motion-location editing. Our approach leverages motion priors, a target motion-centric video generated from an off-the-shelf model as motion-editing guidance, and the ground truth motion to enable more controllable and precise motion-location editing.
