Via this, our framework works as follows: (1) we first disentangle the protagonist and the background via pre-trained segmentation and inpainting models. (2) Then, we introduce a training-free pose warping that edits the protagonist's motion with the motion prior as the guidance. (3) The result of warped motion video is directly injected into a baseline motion editor during inference, mitigating the difference between source and target motions while preserving the appearance of the source video. (4) To enhance the reliability of quantitative evaluations, we propose two new LPIPS-based metrics that measure the background consistency before and after the motion editing and the fidelity of motion editing performance via measuring the difference between the extracted protagonist's skeletons from source and target videos. Experiments with in-the-wild videos and the TaiChi dataset demonstrate that TeleMorpher achieves superior performance across both quantitative and qualitative measurements (real-human evaluation), underscoring its effectiveness.

AI.tldr: TeleMorpher is a one-shot framework for simultaneous motion-location editing in videos, leveraging motion priors, pose warping, and disentanglement of protagonist/background to achieve controllable editing; it also introduces two new LPIPS-based metrics for evaluation.
AI.motivation: Simultaneously editing both motion and location in videos remains largely unexplored despite its practical importance; existing diffusion-based editing methods have not effectively addressed this joint transformation.
AI.method: (1) Disentangle protagonist and background via pre-trained segmentation and inpainting; (2) Training-free pose warping guided by motion priors; (3) Inject warped motion video into a baseline motion editor during inference to bridge source-target motion gap while preserving appearance; (4) Propose two new LPIPS-based metrics for background consistency and motion fidelity evaluation.
AI.result: Superior performance on in-the-wild videos and TaiChi dataset across both quantitative metrics and real-human evaluation.
AI.conclusion: TeleMorpher effectively enables robust simultaneous motion-location editing in a one-shot manner, with proposed metrics improving evaluation reliability.

title: Uncertainty Decomposition for Clarification Seeking in LLM Agents
summary: Recent position papers argue that the classical aleatoric/epistemic uncertainty framework is insufficient for interactive large language model (LLM) agents and call for underspecification-aware, decomposed, and communicable uncertainty representations that can unlock new agent capabilities such as proactive clarification seeking and shared mental-model building. Practical deployment constraints -- black-box APIs, interactive latency budgets, and the absence of labeled trajectories -- rule out logprob-based, multi-sampling, and training-based methods, leaving prompt-based estimation as the most viable family for surfacing such signals at deployment time. We answer this call with a simple prompt-based decomposition that separates action confidence from request uncertainty (u), enabling the agent to ask for clarification when the task specification is ambiguous. To evaluate it, we introduce two clarification-augmented benchmarks (WebShop-Clarification and ALFWorld-Clarification) in which 50% of tasks are deliberately underspecified, and systematically compare the proposed decomposition against ReAct+UE and Uncertainty-Aware Memory (UAM) across five LLM backbones (GPT-5.1, DeepSeek-v3.2-exp, GLM-4.7, Qwen3.5-35B, GPT-OSS-120B) on these variants together with the standard WebShop, ALFWorld, and REAL benchmarks for fault detection. Averaged across the five backbones, the proposed decomposition improves clarification F1 on ALFWorld-Clarification by 73% over ReAct+UE and by 36% over UAM, and leads clarification F1 on every backbone on WebShop-Clarification and on four of five backbones on ALFWorld-Clarification, indicating that the gains generalize beyond a single LLM.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a prompt-based decomposition that separates action confidence from request uncertainty for LLM agents, enabling proactive clarification seeking in underspecified tasks. Tested on two new clarification-augmented benchmarks across five LLM backbones, it outperforms ReAct+UE and UAM, improving clarification F1 by up to 73%.
AI.motivation: Classical aleatoric/epistemic uncertainty frameworks are insufficient for interactive LLM agents. There is a need for underspecification-aware, decomposed, and communicable uncertainty representations to enable capabilities like proactive clarification seeking and shared mental-model building. Practical constraints (black-box APIs, latency budgets, no labeled trajectories) rule out logprob-based, multi-sampling, and training-based methods.
AI.method: A prompt-based uncertainty decomposition that separates action confidence from request uncertainty (u). The agent asks for clarification when task specification is ambiguous. Two new benchmarks (WebShop-Clarification, ALFWorld-Clarification) were created with 50% tasks underspecified. Comparison against ReAct+UE and UAM across five LLM backbones (GPT-5.1, DeepSeek-v3.2-exp, GLM-4.7, Qwen3.5-35B, GPT-OSS-120B).
AI.result: Across five backbones, the decomposition improves clarification F1 on ALFWorld-Clarification by 73% over ReAct+UE and 36% over UAM. It leads clarification F1 on every backbone on WebShop-Clarification and on four of five backbones on ALFWorld-Clarification, demonstrating generalization beyond a single LLM.
AI.conclusion: Prompt-based uncertainty decomposition is a viable, generalizable approach for surfacing underspecification signals in interactive LLM agents under practical deployment constraints, enabling proactive clarification seeking.

title: cAPM: Continual AI-Assisted Pace-Mapping with Active Learning
summary: Ventricular tachycardia is a life-threatening rhythm disorder and a major cause of sudden cardiac death. Pace-mapping is a clinical procedure for identifying the intervention target during catheter ablation of VT. It requires clinicians to pace different sites in the ventricles and rapidly interpret the resulting electrocardiograms to determine where to pace next or whether a target site has been identified. Active learning AI models have been proposed to guide clinicians to the next pacing site, showing promise in reducing the number of pacing sites and improving the efficiency of pace-mapping. Existing methods require retraining each target without the ability to transfer knowledge across multiple VTs within the same patient or across patients. We introduce cAPM for continuous AI-assisted pace-mapping to capture and transfer knowledge accumulated from past pace-mapping data to reduce the number of pace-mapping data needed for future target VTs. This is made possible by a task-agnostic surrogate neural network that learns the mapping from pacing sites to 12-lead ECG morphology, an active-learning strategy that refines this surrogate model by selecting the most informative pacing site for each target, and a continual learning strategy to do so sequentially while retaining knowledge from prior targets. Evaluated on an in-silico testbed consisting of sequentially-presented localization tasks across different physiological conditions and ventricular geometries, cAPM with and without replay of past data samples achieved an 81% probability of localizing within clinical tolerance (5 mm accuracy) using 4.5 pace-mapping sites, compared to the state-of-the-art active-learning method achieving 38% probability using 13.7 pacing sites. These results provide a strong basis for preparing cAPM towards in-vivo preclinical and clinical studies where it can be used to guide pace-mapping.

AI.tldr: cAPM is a continual active learning AI framework for pace-mapping that transfers knowledge across multiple ventricular tachycardia targets, achieving 81% localization probability within 5mm using only 4.5 pacing sites, significantly outperforming the prior state-of-the-art (38% with 13.7 sites).
AI.motivation: Reduce the number of pacing sites needed during pace-mapping for VT ablation by enabling knowledge transfer across multiple VT targets within the same patient or across patients, overcoming the limitation of existing active learning methods that require retraining from scratch for each target.
AI.method: cAPM combines a task-agnostic surrogate neural network (mapping pacing sites to 12-lead ECG morphology), an active-learning strategy for selecting the most informative next pacing site, and a continual learning strategy (with/without replay) to sequentially acquire knowledge across targets while retaining prior knowledge.
AI.result: On an in-silico testbed with sequential localization tasks across different physiological conditions and ventricular geometries, cAPM achieved 81% probability of localizing within 5 mm using 4.5 pacing sites, compared to the state-of-the-art active-learning method which achieved only 38% probability using 13.7 pacing sites.
AI.conclusion: cAPM demonstrates substantial efficiency gains for AI-assisted pace-mapping, providing a strong basis for advancing toward in-vivo preclinical and clinical studies.

title: FAPO: Fully Autonomous Prompt Optimization of Multi-Step LLM Pipelines
summary: Multi-step LLM pipelines fail through interactions among retrieval, reasoning, and formatting steps, so prompt-only optimization can miss bottlenecks in the chain. We present FAPO (Fully Autonomous Prompt Optimization), a framework that lets Claude Code optimize an LLM pipeline inside a standardized codebase. FAPO evaluates a pipeline, inspects intermediate steps, diagnoses failures, proposes scoped changes, and validates variants repeatedly to optimize against a score function. It first tries prompt edits and, only when prompt optimization appears insufficient, changes chain structure within the permitted scope when attribution identifies a structural bottleneck. Across six benchmarks and three task models, FAPO beats the baseline GEPA in 15 of 18 model-benchmark comparisons. In 11 model-benchmark comparisons, FAPO wins with non-overlapping mean $\pm$ trial-standard-deviation ranges, and the mean FAPO-GEPA gain is +14.1 pp. In the six HoVer and IFBench comparisons where prompt-first search escalated to structural changes, FAPO wins all six with a mean gain of +33.8 pp. FAPO also improves performance on security tasks: on CTIBench-RCM, a security CVE-to-CWE task, prompt-only FAPO lifts test accuracy by +4.0 pp on GPT-5, +7.1 pp on Foundation-Sec-8B-Instruct, and +2.0 pp on Foundation-Sec-8B-Reasoning. These results position FAPO as a state-of-the-art pipeline optimization technique for both general-purpose and security-focused tasks.

AI.tldr: FAPO is a fully autonomous framework that optimizes multi-step LLM pipelines by first optimizing prompts and, when insufficient, making structural chain changes based on attribution analysis. It outperforms GEPA in 15/18 comparisons with a mean gain of +14.1 pp, and achieves +33.8 pp gain when structural changes are needed, also showing strong results on security tasks.
AI.motivation: Multi-step LLM pipelines suffer from interactions across retrieval, reasoning, and formatting steps, making prompt-only optimization miss critical chain-level bottlenecks.
AI.method: FAPO operates inside a standardized codebase, letting Claude Code evaluate pipelines, inspect intermediate outputs, diagnose failures, propose scoped changes, and validate variants iteratively. It first attempts prompt edits; when prompt optimization is insufficient, it identifies structural bottlenecks via attribution and changes the chain structure within a permitted scope.
AI.result: FAPO beats GEPA in 15/18 model-benchmark comparisons, with 11 wins showing non-overlapping mean±trial-std ranges. Mean FAPO-GEPA gain is +14.1 pp. In 6 comparisons where prompt-first search escalated to structural changes, mean gain is +33.8 pp. On CTIBench-RCM, prompt-only FAPO lifts accuracy by +4.0 pp (GPT-5), +7.1 pp (Foundation-Sec-8B-Instruct), and +2.0 pp (Foundation-Sec-8B-Reasoning).
AI.conclusion: FAPO is a state-of-the-art pipeline optimization technique for both general-purpose and security-focused tasks, particularly effective when structural changes beyond prompt tuning are required.

title: Vortex: Multi-Modal Fusion System for Intelligent Video Retrieval
summary: This paper presents Vortex, the multimodal video retrieval system developed by our team, FocusOnFun, for the Ho Chi Minh City AI Challenge 2025, designed to advance intelligent multimedia search and temporal reasoning. The system integrates adaptive keyframe extraction, multimodal metadata generation from vision-language and speech models, and a hybrid retrieval strategy that fuses CLIP and SigLIP2 embeddings through Reciprocal Rank Fusion to balance global and fine-grained semantics. To enhance interactivity, Vortex incorporates Rocchio-based relevance feedback and a multi-stage temporal search mechanism for sequential event alignment. Built on Milvus and Elasticsearch, the architecture enables scalable indexing and efficient retrieval. Evaluated in the official competition, our FocusOnFun team's system achieved a score of 79.6/88 (90.5\%) in the Preliminary Round and was further evaluated in the Final Round, achieving an `Excellent' overall performance with `Outstanding' results in the question-answering (QA) task. This demonstrating the complementary strengths of CLIP and SigLIP2 and confirming the effectiveness of the hybrid retrieval approach. The system establishes a robust foundation for future research in intelligent, context-aware, and interactive video retrieval.

AI.tldr: Vortex is a multimodal video retrieval system developed for the Ho Chi Minh City AI Challenge 2025, integrating adaptive keyframe extraction, multimodal metadata generation, hybrid CLIP/SigLIP2 retrieval via Reciprocal Rank Fusion, Rocchio relevance feedback, and multi-stage temporal search. Built on Milvus and Elasticsearch, it achieved 90.5% in the Preliminary Round and "Excellent/Outstanding" results in the Final Round, demonstrating the effectiveness of the hybrid retrieval approach.
AI.motivation: To advance intelligent multimedia search and temporal reasoning for the Ho Chi Minh City AI Challenge 2025 by developing a robust, interactive video retrieval system that balances global and fine-grained semantic understanding.
AI.method: Adaptive keyframe extraction, multimodal metadata generation from vision-language and speech models, hybrid retrieval fusing CLIP and SigLIP2 embeddings via Reciprocal Rank Fusion, Rocchio-based relevance feedback, multi-stage temporal search, with indexing and retrieval supported by Milvus and Elasticsearch.
AI.result: Score of 79.6/88 (90.5%) in Preliminary Round; "Excellent" overall performance with "Outstanding" results in the QA task in the Final Round.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid retrieval approach combining CLIP and SigLIP2 is effective; the system establishes a robust foundation for future research in intelligent, context-aware, and interactive video retrieval.

title: Analyzing the Narration Gap in LLM-Solver Loops
summary: Formal tools such as SAT and SMT solvers are increasingly embedded in language model reasoning pipelines when a safety or security critical question can be formulated in logic. Unlike chain of thought whose steps are sampled from the model distribution without formal guarantee, a solver produces a sound and independently verifiable answer. However, the soundness guarantee can be lost in the interaction between the solver and the model. The hybrid pipeline has three components: formalizing the question, deciding it, and narrating the result. Prior work has studied the formalization and decision, but not narration, which is the step that turns a formal tool's output into the user answer. To fill the narration gap, we first model the LLM-solver loop as a verified decision procedure. We further evaluate five open-sourced models under prompt injection, and we find certificate gating makes the solver verdict sound, while an adversary can invert a verified conclusion across phrasings and channels. We study the mitigation through hardened prompt that reduces injection significantly but cannot eliminate it and still suffers under adaptive attack. Combining the formal analysis and empirical studies, we show in the LLM-solver loop, robustness does not reach to the answer that the user finally reads.

AI.tldr: LLM-solver loops lose soundness at the narration step (turning solver output into user-facing answer), where prompt injection can invert verified conclusions; certificate gating ensures solver verdict soundness but narration remains vulnerable to adversarial attacks even with hardened prompts.
AI.motivation: Prior work studied formalization and decision in LLM-solver hybrid pipelines, but overlooked the narration step (translating formal solver output to user answer), leaving a gap where soundness guarantees can be compromised.
AI.method: Model the LLM-solver loop as a verified decision procedure; evaluate five open-source LLMs under prompt injection attacks on the narration step; test certificate gating and hardened prompts as mitigations.
AI.result: Certificate gating preserves solver verdict soundness; prompt injection can invert verified conclusions across different phrasings and channels; hardened prompts significantly reduce but do not eliminate injection; adaptive attacks still succeed.
AI.conclusion: In the LLM-solver loop, robustness does not extend to the final user-facing answer; the narration step remains a critical vulnerability even when the formal decision step is sound.

title: Protein Representation Learning with Secondary-Structure and Energy-Filtered Hydrogen-Bond Graphs
summary: Graph-based representations are widely used in protein modeling, yet many existing approaches rely primarily on sequence adjacency or geometric proximity, which only partially reflect the principles governing protein folding. Proteins instead adopt complex three-dimensional conformations organized around secondary structure elements, such as $α$-helices and $β$-sheets, which encode recurring local motifs and stabilizing hydrogen-bond interactions. In this work, we introduce a secondary-structure-aware graph neural network for protein representation learning. Residue-level node representations are augmented with secondary structure assignments, and graph edges are constructed from hydrogen-bond interactions filtered by their energetic strength. This design enables the model to capture both local structural context and long-range couplings that are central to protein stability and function. We evaluate the proposed approach on commonly used protein benchmarks and observe consistent improvements over existing graph-based methods. In addition, the resulting graph representations offer enhanced biological interpretability, as the learned connectivity aligns with established structural motifs. These findings suggest that incorporating secondary structure and energy-filtered hydrogen-bond topology provides an effective inductive bias for protein representation learning. The code is released at https://github.com/mohamedmohamed2021/SSProNet

AI.tldr: SSProNet introduces a graph neural network that uses secondary structure assignments and energy-filtered hydrogen-bond edges for protein representation learning, achieving consistent improvements over existing graph-based methods with enhanced biological interpretability.
AI.motivation: Existing graph-based protein representations rely on sequence adjacency or geometric proximity, which only partially capture the principles of protein folding. Proteins organize around secondary structure elements (α-helices, β-sheets) with stabilizing hydrogen-bond interactions that are not adequately modeled.
AI.method: Residue-level node representations are augmented with secondary structure assignments; graph edges are constructed from hydrogen-bond interactions filtered by energetic strength, enabling capture of both local structural context and long-range couplings.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over existing graph-based methods on commonly used protein benchmarks; enhanced biological interpretability with learned connectivity aligning with established structural motifs.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating secondary structure and energy-filtered hydrogen-bond topology provides an effective inductive bias for protein representation learning.

title: StaminaBench: Stress-Testing Coding Agents over 100 Interaction Turns
summary: We introduce StaminaBench, a benchmark that measures the stamina of coding agents: how many consecutive interaction turns (change requests) they can handle before failing. Unlike the prevailing fraction-of-tasks-solved metric, this matches real vibe-coding where sessions run dozens or hundreds of turns. In StaminaBench, agents implement a REST API server and modify it across a tunable number of procedurally generated follow-up change requests - 100 in our experiments, resulting in codebases of up to 6,000 lines. Tests are generated fully programmatically without LLM involvement, ensuring reproducibility and reliability; change sequences are drawn from either a hardcoded or LLM-driven sampler, both constrained to a structured action space to ensure changes are valid. The agent and the server run in an isolated environment and communicate with the benchmark through HTTP, making testing fully black-box and language-agnostic. We evaluate six agent harnesses paired with seven open-source LLMs across 20 scenarios of 100 turns each and find that: (1) all the tested models fail within 5-6 turns, confirming that vibe-coding-style programming without thorough testing produces bugs; (2) passing test feedback back to the agent and allowing it to retry improves passed turn count by up to 12x; and (3) a good harness is required for strong performance: stronger models exhibit up to a 6x gap between their best and worst harness, while weaker models fail with any harness. We release the benchmark and the generated tasks to enable further research into multi-turn coding agent behavior. Benchmark code and data: github.com/amazon-science/StaminaBench.

AI.tldr: StaminaBench is a benchmark measuring how many consecutive change requests coding agents can handle before failing. Evaluations show all models fail within 5-6 turns, but retrying with test feedback improves performance up to 12x, and harness choice significantly impacts results.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks focus on fraction-of-tasks-solved, which doesn't reflect real vibe-coding scenarios where agents handle dozens or hundreds of consecutive interaction turns. StaminaBench addresses this gap by measuring agent stamina over many sequential change requests.
AI.method: Agents implement a REST API server and modify it across 100 procedurally generated change requests. Tests are generated programmatically without LLM involvement. Change sequences use hardcoded or LLM-driven samplers constrained to a structured action space. The agent and server run in an isolated environment, communicating via HTTP for black-box, language-agnostic testing.
AI.result: All tested models fail within 5-6 turns. Passing test feedback back to the agent with retry capability improves passed turn count by up to 12x. Stronger models show up to 6x performance gap between best and worst harness; weaker models fail regardless of harness.
AI.conclusion: Current coding agents lack stamina for multi-turn development. Test feedback and good harnesses are critical for performance. The benchmark is released to enable further research into multi-turn coding agent behavior.

title: Exploring Multi-Modal Large Language Models and Two-Stage Fine-Tuning for Fashion Image Retrieval
summary: Composed image retrieval retrieves a target image using a composed query of a reference image and a modified text description. In the fashion domain, this task requires understanding subtle attribute variations such as color, pattern, and texture. However, existing approaches face limitations due to scarce annotated data and simplistic negative sampling. We propose a novel framework that integrates a multi-modal large language model (LLaVA) to generate attribute-aware triplets and introduces a two-stage fine-tuning strategy to enhance contrastive learning. We leverage pretrained vision-language models, such as CLIP-ViT/B32, to generate and concatenate sentence-level prompts with the relative caption and to scale the number of negatives using static representations. Experimental results demonstrate enhanced compositional reasoning and improved fine-grained retrieval behavior, underscoring the feasibility and potential of the proposed framework for fashion retrieval.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework combining LLaVA and CLIP to improve composed image retrieval in fashion by generating attribute-aware triplets and using a two-stage fine-tuning strategy with static negative sampling.
AI.motivation: Existing composed image retrieval methods suffer from scarce annotated data and simplistic negative sampling, limiting their ability to understand subtle attribute variations (color, pattern, texture) in the fashion domain.
AI.method: Integrates LLaVA (multi-modal LLM) to generate attribute-aware triplets; uses a two-stage fine-tuning strategy to enhance contrastive learning; leverages CLIP-ViT/B32 to generate and concatenate sentence-level prompts with relative captions; scales negative samples using static representations.
AI.result: Enhanced compositional reasoning and improved fine-grained retrieval behavior in fashion retrieval tasks, demonstrating feasibility of the framework.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses data scarcity and negative sampling limitations, showing strong potential for fashion-domain composed image retrieval.

title: Configurable Clinical Information Extraction with Agentic RAG: What Works, What Breaks, and Why
summary: Patient contexts span hundreds of heterogeneous documents and thousands of structured data points, yet the document-level metadata that AI systems need for retrieval and triage is absent or incomplete. Standard retrieval-augmented generation fails on this data, mishandling temporal reasoning, cross-document dependencies, and missing metadata. We deploy ACIE (Agentic Clinical Information Extraction) at University Medicine Essen: an on-premise agentic RAG pipeline that reasons over complete patient contexts and grounds every answer in source passages for clinician verification. We quantify the metadata gap, trace the architectural decisions it shaped, and evaluate extraction alongside an independent retrospective lymphoma registry study, in which nuclear-medicine physicians verify every extracted value against its cited sources. Across 7,326 judgments, clinicians accepted 96.5\% of extractions, with per-type acceptance ranging from 80\% to 99\%.

AI.tldr: ACIE is an on-premise agentic RAG pipeline deployed at University Medicine Essen that addresses the metadata gap in heterogeneous patient documents by reasoning over complete patient contexts and grounding answers in source passages. In a lymphoma registry study with 7,326 clinician judgments, it achieved 96.5% acceptance rate (80-99% per type).
AI.motivation: Patient data is distributed across hundreds of heterogeneous documents and thousands of structured data points, but document-level metadata needed for AI retrieval and triage is missing or incomplete. Standard RAG fails on temporal reasoning, cross-document dependencies, and missing metadata.
AI.method: Deployed ACIE (Agentic Clinical Information Extraction), an on-premise agentic RAG pipeline that reasons over complete patient contexts and grounds every answer in source passages for clinician verification. Evaluated via an independent retrospective lymphoma registry study with nuclear-medicine physicians verifying extracted values against cited sources.
AI.result: Across 7,326 clinician judgments, 96.5% of extractions were accepted, with per-type acceptance ranging from 80% to 99%.
AI.conclusion: ACIE effectively bridges the metadata gap in clinical information extraction by using agentic RAG with source-grounded answers, achieving high clinician acceptance rates in a real-world hospital deployment.

title: Physics-Informed Discovery of Yield Functions in Plasticity via Convex Neural Representations
summary: Identifying anisotropic yield functions remains challenging since yielding is not directly observed in full-field mechanical measurements, directional calibration can require many loading directions, and selecting an appropriate analytical form is nontrivial. This study proposes a physics-informed framework for discovering yield functions from full-field displacement data and reaction force data, without stress observations, plastic strain measurements, direct yield surface data, or a prescribed parametric yield function. The framework identifies the yield function as a mechanically constrained constitutive component inside elastoplastic stress integration, rather than through direct stress-space supervision. The yield function is represented by a convex neural network that enforces convexity and positive homogeneity of degree one while imposing the assumed tension-compression symmetry, and this neural yield function is trained with a differentiable stress update and a physics-informed force equilibrium loss across multiple loading cases. The proposed framework is validated using finite element (FE) benchmark studies with von Mises, Hill 1948, and Yld2000-2d yield functions, assessing yield contour agreement, displacement-noise sensitivity, identifiability through plastically active stress states, epistemic uncertainty, and polynomial-surrogate deployment. This study provides a mechanics-constrained pathway for discovering anisotropic yield functions from displacement and force data while keeping the identified component within the structure of elastoplastic stress integration.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a physics-informed framework to discover anisotropic yield functions from full-field displacement and reaction force data using a convex neural network representation, without requiring stress observations, plastic strain measurements, or a prescribed parametric form. The framework is validated on FE benchmarks with von Mises, Hill 1948, and Yld2000-2d yield functions.
AI.motivation: Identifying anisotropic yield functions is challenging because yielding is not directly observable in full-field measurements, directional calibration requires many loading directions, and selecting an appropriate analytical form is nontrivial.
AI.method: The framework uses a convex neural network to represent the yield function, enforcing convexity and positive homogeneity of degree one with tension-compression symmetry. It is trained via differentiable stress integration and a physics-informed force equilibrium loss across multiple loading cases, using only full-field displacement and reaction force data.
AI.result: The framework was validated on FE benchmarks with von Mises, Hill 1948, and Yld2000-2d yield functions, assessing yield contour agreement, noise sensitivity, identifiability through plastically active stress states, epistemic uncertainty, and polynomial-surrogate deployment.
AI.conclusion: This study provides a mechanics-constrained pathway for discovering anisotropic yield functions from displacement and force data, keeping the identified component within the structure of elastoplastic stress integration.

title: Before the Pull Request: Mining Multi-Agent Coordination
comment: 9 pages, 2 tables. LNCS format. Code, dataset, and mining toolkit: https://github.com/neul-labs/grite
summary: Autonomous coding agents now open millions of pull requests, yet large-scale studies find their PRs are produced faster but accepted less often - a coordination and trust gap that pull-request-level telemetry cannot explain. We argue the missing signal lives before the PR, in how concurrent agents claim, divide, and collide over shared work. We study this process through grite, our open-source coordination substrate that needs no central server and stores its records inside git itself, so its append-only, signed event log captures the coordination process directly. We show that (i) this shared substrate reduces duplicate and conflicting work at bounded overhead - the share of work that merely re-does a teammate's task falls from 78% to 0% while useful throughput more than triples; (ii) every agent's copy of the log converges to the same state with no write silently dropped, where a file-based tracker loses concurrent writes; and (iii) the log is a mineable artefact from which concrete failure modes - conflicting edits, lock starvation, redundant rediscovery, race-to-close - are automatically recoverable with provenance, several invisible in pull-request history. We release the dataset, harness, and mining toolkit.

AI.tldr: grite is an open-source, git-native coordination substrate that eliminates duplicate and conflicting work among autonomous coding agents by embedding an append-only, signed event log directly in git, reducing redundant work from 78% to 0% and tripling useful throughput, while enabling automatic recovery of coordination failure modes invisible in PR-level telemetry.
AI.motivation: Autonomous coding agents produce PRs faster but are accepted less often due to coordination and trust gaps that PR-level telemetry cannot explain. The missing signal lies in how concurrent agents claim, divide, and collide over shared work before the PR stage.
AI.method: The authors designed grite, a coordination substrate stored inside git itself as an append-only, signed event log, requiring no central server. They evaluated its effectiveness in reducing duplicate work, ensuring log convergence, and recovering coordination failure modes.
AI.result: (i) Duplicate work dropped from 78% to 0%, useful throughput more than tripled; (ii) all agent logs converged with no silently dropped writes, unlike a file-based tracker; (iii) failure modes (conflicting edits, lock starvation, redundant rediscovery, race-to-close) are automatically recoverable with provenance, some invisible in PR history.
AI.conclusion: Embedding coordination state inside git (grite) effectively resolves pre-PR coordination failures among autonomous coding agents, providing a mineable artifact for analyzing and recovering from concurrent work conflicts.

title: NEST: Narrative Event Structures in Time for Long Video Understanding
summary: Recent progress in vision-language models has enabled the processing of increasingly long video sequences, but the ability to handle extended token streams does not translate to understanding of narrative structure in long videos. Existing long video benchmarks focus on needle-in-a-haystack retrieval rather than evaluating how low-level actions form events, how events interact across time, and how narratives progress, for example, whether a model can connect an early setback, such as a job loss to a later relationship breakup, despite long gaps, intervening scenes, or flashbacks that reframe what occurred. We introduce NEST (Narrative Event Structures in Time for Long Video Understanding), a dataset of 1005 full-length movies (avg. 98 minutes), each annotated with 102 multimodal narrative events grounded in visual content, dialogue, and audio. NEST captures multimodal narrative events with structured annotations grounded in visual content, dialogue, and audio, and links them through relations that reflect narrative structure, including temporal ordering, hierarchical composition, and long-range dependencies. We introduce baselines for event trigger detection (ETD), event localization (EL), event argument extraction (EAE), and event relation extraction (ERE). The benchmark is highly challenging for grounded event discovery, with ETD below 8%, EL under 6%, and EAE below 11%. In contrast, ERE is more tractable once events are given, reaching 35.45% F1 zero-shot and 44.42% F1 after fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: NEST is a benchmark dataset of 1,005 full-length movies with structured narrative event annotations, evaluating long video understanding beyond simple retrieval. Models struggle with event discovery tasks (ETD <8%, EL <6%, EAE <11%) but perform better on relation extraction (ERE up to 44.42% F1).
AI.motivation: Existing long video benchmarks focus on needle-in-a-haystack retrieval rather than evaluating narrative structure understanding, such as how low-level actions form events, how events interact across time, and how narratives progress (e.g., connecting an early job loss to a later relationship breakup despite gaps and flashbacks).
AI.method: NEST contains 1,005 full-length movies (avg. 98 minutes) with 102 multimodal narrative events per movie, annotated across visual, dialogue, and audio modalities. Events are linked through temporal ordering, hierarchical composition, and long-range dependency relations. Benchmarks are established for Event Trigger Detection (ETD), Event Localization (EL), Event Argument Extraction (EAE), and Event Relation Extraction (ERE).
AI.result: ETD <8%, EL <6%, EAE <11% (event discovery is highly challenging). ERE reaches 35.45% F1 zero-shot and 44.42% F1 after fine-tuning, showing relation extraction is more tractable once events are given.
AI.conclusion: NEST reveals a significant gap between current vision-language models' ability to process long videos and their understanding of narrative structure, particularly for grounded event discovery tasks.

title: Which Pairs to Compare for LLM Post-Training?
summary: Preference-based post-training has become a central paradigm for aligning language models. A common data-collection strategy is to generate a small set of completions for each prompt and label the resulting comparison pairs. However, human preference labels are often much more expensive than generating additional completions, suggesting a different use of the same labeling budget: generate a larger pool of completions, but label only the most informative comparison pairs. This paper studies which pairs should be compared in preference-based post-training. We formulate comparison curation as a sampling-design problem and evaluate designs by the quality of the final policy under the preference-based post-training objective. We instantiate this framework for Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), analyzing how the choice of labeled pairs propagates through DPO training to downstream policy performance. Our main results provide matching upper and lower bounds on the post-training optimality gap of the DPO-trained policy. The bounds show that comparison selection affects downstream performance through a single design-dependent information matrix, which links label allocation to parameter estimation error and policy suboptimality. This yields an explicit optimization criterion for budgeted comparison curation and motivates practical sampling designs for selecting informative pairs from large generated completion pools. Experiments on synthetic settings and language-model post-training benchmarks show that the proposed designs consistently improve sample efficiency over common comparison-selection heuristics.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how to optimally select which comparison pairs to label in preference-based post-training of language models, formulating it as a sampling-design problem with matching upper/lower bounds on policy optimality gap, and proposing practical designs that improve sample efficiency over common heuristics.
AI.motivation: Human preference labels are much more expensive than generating additional completions, so it is more efficient to generate a larger pool of completions but only label the most informative comparison pairs, rather than labeling all pairs from a small set.
AI.method: Formulates comparison curation as a sampling-design problem; analyzes how labeled pair choice propagates through DPO training via an information matrix; derives matching upper/lower bounds on the post-training optimality gap; proposes explicit optimization criterion and practical sampling designs.
AI.result: Matching upper and lower bounds show comparison selection affects downstream performance through a single design-dependent information matrix linking label allocation to parameter estimation error and policy suboptimality. Proposed designs consistently improve sample efficiency over common comparison-selection heuristics on synthetic and language-model benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Comparison curation can be systematically optimized in preference-based post-training via a sampling-design lens, yielding principled and practical strategies for informative pair selection under a fixed labeling budget.

title: Cost-Optimal LLM Routing with Limited User Feedback under User Satisfaction Guarantees
summary: Inference costs for large language model (LLM) applications are rapidly growing, driven by surging demand and rising infrastructure cost. Users expect high-quality responses, and in commercial settings this is formally codified in Service Level Agreements (SLAs), creating a fundamental tension between cost and quality. Recent progress on cost-aware LLM request routing has shown potential to resolve this tension, but existing approaches rely on complete feedback signals, offline training, extensive per-workload tuning, and most lack SLA guarantees or inference-time adaptivity. We introduce SLARouter, an online routing algorithm that learns a cost-optimal policy from the sparse, one-sided user feedback available in production systems. SLARouter provides theoretical guarantees for both cost optimality and strict SLA compliance. Experiments across a wide range of LLM benchmarks show that SLARouter satisfies SLA constraints without the need for per-benchmark tuning, reducing operating cost by up to 2.2x over existing baselines.

AI.tldr: SLARouter is an online routing algorithm that learns cost-optimal policies from sparse, one-sided feedback to route LLM requests while providing theoretical guarantees on SLA compliance and cost optimality, reducing operating costs by up to 2.2x without per-benchmark tuning.
AI.motivation: The growing inference costs of LLM applications create a tension between cost and quality (formalized via SLAs). Existing cost-aware routing methods rely on complete feedback, offline training, extensive per-workload tuning, and lack SLA guarantees or inference-time adaptivity.
AI.method: SLARouter is an online routing algorithm that learns a cost-optimal policy from sparse, one-sided user feedback available in production systems, providing theoretical guarantees for cost optimality and strict SLA compliance.
AI.result: SLARouter satisfies SLA constraints without per-benchmark tuning and reduces operating costs by up to 2.2x over existing baselines across a wide range of LLM benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: SLARouter effectively resolves the cost-quality tension in LLM request routing by combining online learning from sparse feedback with provable SLA guarantees and cost optimality, outperforming existing approaches without requiring extensive tuning.

title: Prompt Quality and Pull Request Outcomes: A Stage-Based Empirical Study of LLM-Assisted Development
summary: Large language model (LLM)-powered tools such as ChatGPT are increasingly used in collaborative software engineering workflows, yet little is known about how prompt structure influences downstream pull request (PR) outcomes. Prior studies primarily examine conversational helpfulness, productivity, or coarse-grained adoption metrics, leaving the role of prompt structure in collaborative integration behavior insufficiently understood. We analyze 265 manually validated developer-ChatGPT interactions derived from self-admitted ChatGPT usage in open-source pull requests. Building on prior research on developer-facing artifacts and prompt engineering, we operationalize prompt structure using three dimensions: Context, Specificity, and Verification. We first evaluate whether LLM-assisted annotation can reliably reproduce human judgments of prompt structure, finding substantial variation across dimensions and workflow contexts. Specificity shows the most stable agreement with human judgments; Context is systematically under-scored by the LLM; and Verification remains difficult to assess consistently, motivating a hybrid human-LLM annotation strategy. Using this validated framework, we then examine how prompt structure influences actionable code generation, code adoption, and integration depth across AI-assisted PR workflows. Specificity and Context are most strongly associated with actionable code generation; Verification emerges as the primary predictor of code adoption; and integration depth is most strongly associated with Context. Overall, our findings show that prompt characteristics exert distinct, stage-dependent effects across AI-assisted software engineering workflows, influencing downstream adoption and integration through contextual grounding, task specificity, and evaluability cues.

AI.tldr: This study analyzes 265 developer-ChatGPT interactions in open-source pull requests, operationalizing prompt structure via Context, Specificity, and Verification. LLM-assisted annotation shows varying reliability; Specificity aligns best with human judgments. Prompt characteristics have distinct stage-dependent effects: Specificity and Context drive actionable code generation, Verification predicts code adoption, and Context influences integration depth.
AI.motivation: Despite the increasing use of LLM-powered tools like ChatGPT in software engineering, the influence of prompt structure on downstream pull request outcomes (e.g., code adoption, integration depth) remains insufficiently understood. Prior work focused on conversational quality or coarse adoption metrics, lacking a fine-grained analysis of prompt characteristics in collaborative integration behavior.
AI.method: Analyzed 265 manually validated developer-ChatGPT interactions from open-source PRs. Operationalized prompt structure using three dimensions: Context, Specificity, and Verification. Evaluated LLM-assisted annotation reliability against human judgments, then examined how these dimensions influence actionable code generation, code adoption, and integration depth.
AI.result: LLM-assisted annotation shows substantial variation across dimensions: Specificity has the most stable agreement with human judgments; Context is systematically under-scored; Verification is difficult to assess consistently. Specificity and Context are most associated with actionable code generation; Verification predicts code adoption; Context is most associated with integration depth.
AI.conclusion: Prompt characteristics exert distinct, stage-dependent effects across AI-assisted software engineering workflows. Contextual grounding, task specificity, and evaluability cues (Verification) differentially influence downstream adoption and integration, highlighting the need for tailored prompt engineering strategies in collaborative development.

title: One-Shot Novel View and Pose Human Image Synthesis via 3D Prior Guided Diffusion Model
summary: This paper addresses the challenge of one-shot novel view and pose human image synthesis. The existing methods transfer the reference human image to a target pose using a set of 2D pose keypoints or synthesize human images based on generalizable human NeRF which uses human model priors to extract point-wise features. However, pose transfer based methods can not handle complex human pose using ambiguous 2D pose as the condition, while generalizable human NeRFs may be inaccurate to recover occluded/invisiable human parts without extracted reliable features. To solve these problems, we propose a novel approach for novel view and pose synthesis from a singe human image via conditional denoising diffusion model. Our diffusion model divides the novel view and pose synthesis problem into a sequence of conditional denoising steps. Specifically, to generate humans with complex and arbitrary poses, we introduce 3D human priors, i.e., 3D normal map and color prompt, as geometry and color conditions into the generation process. By transferring the reference human into the target human with a series of diffusion steps, our diffusion model enables high-quality synthesis including the occluded/invisible parts. Further, we propose a self-reconstruction based customized refinement to enhance fine details when tested on novel persons.Experimental results on different public datasets demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms previous methods and also shows better generalization ability across datasets. The code will be made publicly available at https://github.com/Yankeegsj/3DPGDM.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a conditional denoising diffusion model for one-shot novel view and pose human image synthesis, using 3D human priors (normal map & color prompt) to handle complex poses and occluded parts, with a self-reconstruction refinement step, outperforming previous methods across datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing pose transfer methods fail on complex poses due to ambiguous 2D keypoint conditions, while generalizable human NeRFs struggle with occluded/invisible body parts due to unreliable feature extraction. A more robust approach is needed for novel view and pose synthesis from a single image.
AI.method: A conditional denoising diffusion model that decomposes novel view/pose synthesis into sequential denoising steps, conditioned on 3D human priors (3D normal map and color prompt) as geometry and color guidance, plus a self-reconstruction customized refinement for fine detail enhancement on novel persons.
AI.result: The approach significantly outperforms previous methods on multiple public datasets and demonstrates better cross-dataset generalization ability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed diffusion-based method with 3D priors effectively solves one-shot novel view and pose human synthesis, enabling high-quality generation of occluded/invisible parts and superior generalization.

authors: Antonio de Sousa Leitão Filho; Allan Kardec Duailibe Barros Filho; Fabrício Saul Lima; Selby Mykael Lima dos Santos; Rejani Bandeira Vieira Sousa
title: Toten: Knowledge-Based Ontological Tokenization Of Physical Quantities And Technical Notation In Brazilian Portuguese
summary: Byte-Pair Encoding tokenization is statistically efficient for vocabulary compression, but semantically blind to structured technical entities, fragmenting physical quantities, numbers, units, and symbolic expressions into lexically arbitrary subwords. We present TOTEN, a knowledge-based ontological tokenization framework that replaces statistical derivation with declarative classification grounded in a formal ontology of engineering entities (OEE). We formalize TOTEN as the triple <O, classify, {inst_tau}>: the ontology gathers types, structural principles, composition relations, and preservable invariants; the classification function maps raw text into typed regions; and the instantiator family yields a self-descriptive structured representation. Robustness derives from deterministic coupling with three external oracles: Pint (dimensional), Unicode Character Database (typographic), and RSLP (Portuguese morphology). Intrinsic evaluation covers four properties verifiable by construction -- ontological atomicity, dimensional equivalence, typographic robustness, and numerical reconstruction -- over an internal, physically validated benchmark (EngQuant, N=800) and four Brazilian Portuguese external corpora (N=1771 eligible cases). We also report detection recall, distinguishing coverage from conditional atomicity. Against eight state-of-the-art baselines, TOTEN achieves unit ontological atomicity in all contrasts and numerical reconstruction of 0.775-0.904 on external corpora, vs. 0.627-0.703 for the best baseline (Quantulum3); on EngQuant, 0.780 vs. 0.340. Differences are statistically significant (McNemar with Holm correction). Spearman correlation between internal and external rankings confirms concurrent validity of the control benchmark. Dimensional equivalence shows statistical parity with Pint, the oracle from which the system inherits dimensional authority.

AI.tldr: TOTEN is a knowledge-based ontological tokenization framework that replaces statistical Byte-Pair Encoding with declarative classification using a formal ontology of engineering entities. It achieves superior ontological atomicity, dimensional equivalence, typographic robustness, and numerical reconstruction compared to eight state-of-the-art baselines, with statistically significant improvements (e.g., 0.780 vs 0.340 on EngQuant benchmark for numerical reconstruction).
AI.motivation: Byte-Pair Encoding tokenization is semantically blind to structured technical entities, fragmenting physical quantities, numbers, units, and symbolic expressions into lexically arbitrary subwords. The authors aim to overcome this limitation by introducing a knowledge-driven approach grounded in formal ontology.
AI.method: TOTEN is formalized as a triple ⟨O, classify, {inst_τ}⟩: (1) an ontology (O) gathering types, structural principles, composition relations, and preservable invariants; (2) a classification function mapping raw text into typed regions; and (3) an instantiator family yielding self-descriptive structured representations. It deterministically couples with three external oracles: Pint (dimensional analysis), Unicode Character Database (typographic), and RSLP (Portuguese morphology).
AI.result: TOTEN achieves unit ontological atomicity across all contrasts. Numerical reconstruction scores: 0.775-0.904 on external corpora vs. 0.627-0.703 for best baseline (Quantulum3); 0.780 vs. 0.340 on EngQuant. Dimensional equivalence shows statistical parity with Pint. All differences are statistically significant (McNemar with Holm correction). Spearman correlation confirms concurrent validity of the control benchmark.
AI.conclusion: TOTEN demonstrates that knowledge-based ontological tokenization significantly outperforms statistical tokenization for structured technical entities, establishing a new paradigm for tokenization in engineering and scientific domains.

title: Emyx: Fast and efficient all-atom protein generation
summary: Computational enzyme design requires generating proteins that scaffold catalytic residues and ligands, a task that demands both geometric accuracy and structural diversity from the underlying generative model. Current all-atom generators inherit expensive architectures from structure prediction, leading to high training costs and limited sample diversity. We argue that much of this complexity is unnecessary for generators, which condition on sparse geometric constraints rather than rich co-evolutionary signals. Emyx is a 140M-parameter conditional flow matching model that concentrates capacity within standard transformer blocks, replacing heavy embedding stacks with lightweight conditional representations and sparse connectivity. We additionally derive an exact reparametrisation of the flow matching interpolant into the EDM noise-level framework, bridging flow matching training efficiency with state-of-the-art sampling methods designed for diffusion models without retraining. Despite being the smallest model, Emyx outperforms both Proteína-Complexa and RFdiffusion3 against the AME enzyme design benchmark across success rate under strict evaluation requiring both global fold recovery and catalytic geometry accuracy, structural novelty, scaffold diversity, and geometric validity, while training in just $682$ GPU-hours, roughly $4\times$ less than RFdiffusion3.

AI.tldr: Emyx is a compact 140M-parameter conditional flow matching model that outperforms larger models like RFdiffusion3 and Proteína-Complexa on computational enzyme design, using standard transformer blocks, lightweight conditional representations, and a reparameterized flow matching interpolant bridging to diffusion sampling, while training in 682 GPU-hours (~4× less than RFdiffusion3).
AI.motivation: Current all-atom generative models for computational enzyme design inherit expensive architectures from structure prediction, leading to high training costs and limited sample diversity. The authors argue that much of this complexity is unnecessary for generators that condition on sparse geometric constraints rather than rich co-evolutionary signals.
AI.method: Emyx is a 140M-parameter conditional flow matching model that uses standard transformer blocks, lightweight conditional representations, and sparse connectivity. It also derives an exact reparameterization of the flow matching interpolant into the EDM noise-level framework, bridging flow matching training efficiency with diffusion model sampling methods.
AI.result: Despite being the smallest model, Emyx outperforms Proteína-Complexa and RFdiffusion3 on the AME enzyme design benchmark across success rate (requiring both global fold recovery and catalytic geometry accuracy), structural novelty, scaffold diversity, and geometric validity, while training in just 682 GPU-hours (~4× less than RFdiffusion3).
AI.conclusion: Complexity in generative architectures for enzyme design can be substantially reduced without sacrificing performance. Lightweight conditional representations and bridging flow matching to diffusion sampling frameworks enable both efficiency and state-of-the-art results.

title: Library-Aware Doubles and Iterative Repair for Large Language Model-Generated Unit Tests in OpenSIL Firmware
summary: Validating changes in low-level C firmware is expensive because unit tests (UTs) are fragile under strict build constraints, where missing headers, unresolved symbols, and dependency mismatches frequently prevent compilation and linking. This study introduces an automated UT authoring workflow for the Open-Source Silicon Initialization Library (openSIL) firmware codebase maintained by Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) that reduces manual effort through a large language model (LLM) guided multi-agent pipeline. The workflow combines automated generation of test scaffolds, library-aware creation or reuse of stubs, mocks, and fakes, and an iterative compile-dispatch repair loop driven by build logs and line-coverage feedback. We evaluate the approach using compilation success, repair iterations, dispatch success, and line coverage, with time, cost, and token usage as secondary measures. Across 76 functions under test, the workflow generated compilable UTs for 73 functions. In a configuration without line coverage guidance or retrieval augmentation, mean line coverage reached 73.9%. On a 48-function subset evaluated under both configurations, mean line coverage reached 98.8% with line-coverage guidance alone and reached 94.7% when combined with vector-database retrieval. Results show that automated generation-and-repair pipelines can substantially improve UT creation efficiency and coverage for constrained firmware environments while reducing manual debugging effort.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an LLM-guided multi-agent pipeline that automates unit test authoring for low-level C firmware (openSIL by AMD). The workflow combines automatic scaffold generation, stub/mock/fake creation, and an iterative compile-dispatch repair loop using build logs and line-coverage feedback. Across 76 functions, it achieved compilable UTs for 73 functions, with mean line coverage reaching 98.8% with coverage guidance alone.
AI.motivation: Validating low-level C firmware is expensive because unit tests are fragile under strict build constraints—missing headers, unresolved symbols, and dependency mismatches frequently prevent compilation and linking. Manual debugging effort is high, motivating an automated solution.
AI.method: An LLM-guided multi-agent pipeline that includes: (1) automated generation of test scaffolds, (2) library-aware creation or reuse of stubs, mocks, and fakes, and (3) an iterative compile-dispatch repair loop driven by build logs and line-coverage feedback.
AI.result: Across 76 functions, 73 generated compilable UTs. Without coverage guidance or retrieval augmentation, mean line coverage was 73.9%. On a 48-function subset, coverage reached 98.8% with line-coverage guidance alone and 94.7% with combined vector-database retrieval.
AI.conclusion: Automated generation-and-repair pipelines can substantially improve UT creation efficiency and coverage for constrained firmware environments while reducing manual debugging effort.

title: QueryGaussian: Scalable and Training-Free Open-Vocabulary 3D Instance Retrieval
comment: 8 pages, 4 figures, 6 tables. Accepted to the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics (SMC 2026)
summary: Efficiently retrieving specific 3D instances from large-scale scenes via natural language prompts remains a formidable challenge in multimedia analysis. Existing approaches predominantly follow a "scene-level embedding" paradigm, which requires distilling high-dimensional semantic features into every 3D primitive. This strategy suffers from a fundamental architectural bottleneck: memory and computational costs scale linearly with scene complexity, inevitably triggering out-of-memory (OOM) failures in city-scale environments. To address this barrier, we propose QueryGaussian, a training-free framework for expeditious and scalable open-vocabulary 3D instance retrieval. Unlike holistic semantic distillation, QueryGaussian employs an instance-level query mechanism that decouples semantic understanding from geometric representation. Specifically, we leverage pre-trained 2D vision models to interpret user prompts and lift segmentation masks into 3D via a concurrent maximum-weight association strategy, ensuring semantic-visual consistency. To mitigate projection ambiguity, we introduce a temporal fusion module with multi-stage adaptive density clustering. Experimental results demonstrate that QueryGaussian not only matches the accuracy of state-of-the-art methods but also delivers a decisive efficiency leap, reducing GPU memory usage by over 70% and accelerating inference by 180x. Crucially, QueryGaussian enables expeditious instance retrieval on city-scale scenes containing tens of millions of Gaussians using consumer-grade hardware.

AI.tldr: QueryGaussian is a training-free framework for open-vocabulary 3D instance retrieval that uses an instance-level query mechanism to decouple semantic understanding from geometric representation, achieving comparable accuracy to SOTA while reducing GPU memory by over 70% and accelerating inference by 180x, enabling city-scale scene retrieval on consumer hardware.
AI.motivation: Existing "scene-level embedding" approaches require distilling high-dimensional semantic features into every 3D primitive, causing memory and computational costs that scale linearly with scene complexity and lead to out-of-memory failures in city-scale environments.
AI.method: QueryGaussian employs an instance-level query mechanism that decouples semantic understanding from geometric representation. It uses pre-trained 2D vision models to interpret user prompts and lifts segmentation masks into 3D via a concurrent maximum-weight association strategy, with a temporal fusion module using multi-stage adaptive density clustering to mitigate projection ambiguity.
AI.result: Matches accuracy of state-of-the-art methods, reduces GPU memory usage by over 70%, accelerates inference by 180x, and enables instance retrieval on city-scale scenes with tens of millions of Gaussians using consumer-grade hardware.
AI.conclusion: QueryGaussian addresses the scalability bottleneck of holistic semantic distillation by introducing a training-free, instance-level query mechanism that achieves both efficiency and accuracy for open-vocabulary 3D instance retrieval at city scale.

title: AI4SE and SE4AI Exploration: A Decade Looking Back and Forward
summary: The March 2020 INCOSE INSIGHT special issue on AI and Systems Engineering (SE) became the most downloaded issue in the publication's history and launched a research community that now draws over 250 registrants to its annual workshop. In this article, we trace the progress in AI and SE across three phases (labeled here foundational, applied, and LLM inflection) based on the authors' reading of the field's core papers, and describe our opinions of where the community has converged and where critical gaps remain. Separately, a human-AI agreement literature review leveraging both human expertise and six AI models was performed to assess the relevance of 1,712 INCOSE INSIGHT articles and 889 SERC publications. The results identify five critical research gaps and offer guidance for practitioners navigating AI adoption, assurance, and workforce transformation in SE. We share the agreement data and the AI4SE/SE4AI Explorer web application so readers can compare their own relevance judgments with the human and AI raters.

AI.tldr: This paper traces progress in AI and Systems Engineering (SE) across three phases (foundational, applied, LLM inflection), analyzes 1,712 INCOSE INSIGHT articles and 889 SERC publications via a human-AI agreement literature review using six AI models, identifies five critical research gaps, and releases the AI4SE/SE4AI Explorer web application for community-based relevance judgment comparison.
AI.motivation: The March 2020 INCOSE INSIGHT special issue on AI and SE became the most downloaded issue, spawning a research community with 250+ annual workshop registrants. The authors aim to trace the field's progress, identify where the community has converged, and highlight critical remaining gaps to guide practitioners in AI adoption, assurance, and workforce transformation.
AI.method: A human-AI agreement literature review combining human expert judgments with six AI models to assess relevance of 1,712 INCOSE INSIGHT articles and 889 SERC publications. The authors also categorized the field's evolution into three phases (foundational, applied, LLM inflection) based on core papers.
AI.result: Identification of five critical research gaps in AI and SE. Release of human-AI agreement data and the AI4SE/SE4AI Explorer web application enabling readers to compare their own relevance judgments with human and AI raters.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a structured view of AI-SE progress, identifies where the community has converged and where gaps persist, and offers practical guidance for AI adoption, assurance, and workforce transformation, supported by an open tool for collaborative relevance assessment.

title: A Hybrid GNN-FEM Framework for Phase-Field Fracture Simulation. Physics-Preserving Hybridization for Generalizable Surrogate Modeling
summary: Scientific machine learning (SciML) has emerged as a promising approach for accelerating simulations of complex physical systems, yet achieving physically consistent and generalizable predictions for nonlinear, history-dependent problems remains a central challenge. In this study, we propose a hybrid GNN--FEM framework for efficient and generalizable phase-field fracture modeling. While phase-field approaches provide a robust variational framework for simulating complex crack evolution, their high computational cost limits practical applications because they require solving coupled, nonlinear, and history-dependent systems within an incremental finite element procedure. To address this challenge, a graph neural network surrogate is integrated into the conventional staggered scheme, replacing the phase-field update at each load increment while retaining the FEM-based displacement solver to enforce mechanical equilibrium and boundary conditions. By preserving the incremental solution structure, the framework remains consistent with history-dependent fracture evolution without requiring the surrogate to approximate the full solution trajectory. This selective surrogate strategy emphasizes the identification of a physically meaningful and incrementally structured learning target, rather than relying on brute-force data generation to learn the full fracture process. The proposed framework achieves strong generalization across varying geometries, loading conditions, material properties, and discretizations through dimensionless feature design, a graph-based formulation on mesh-based domains, and a physics-informed loss derived from the governing phase-field equation. Numerical experiments demonstrate that the hybrid approach reduces computational cost while maintaining accuracy compared with conventional FEM, and exhibits robust predictive performance across diverse problem settings.

AI.tldr: A hybrid GNN-FEM framework for phase-field fracture modeling uses a graph neural network surrogate to replace the costly phase-field update in a staggered finite element scheme, preserving incremental solution structure and achieving efficient, generalizable predictions across varying geometries, loading, materials, and discretizations.
AI.motivation: Phase-field fracture modeling is computationally expensive due to coupled, nonlinear, history-dependent systems solved via incremental finite element procedures. The goal is to accelerate simulations while maintaining accuracy and physical consistency.
AI.method: A GNN surrogate replaces the phase-field update step in a conventional staggered FEM scheme; the FEM displacement solver is retained for mechanical equilibrium. Key design choices include dimensionless features, mesh-based graph representation, and a physics-informed loss from the phase-field governing equation.
AI.result: The hybrid framework reduces computational cost while maintaining accuracy compared to conventional FEM, and demonstrates robust generalization across varying geometries, loading conditions, material properties, and discretizations.
AI.conclusion: Selective surrogate modeling (replacing only the phase-field update) with an incrementally structured learning target yields physically consistent, history-dependent predictions without brute-force data generation, offering a practical path for accelerating SciML simulations of complex nonlinear systems.

title: Agentic Electronic Design Automation: A Handoff Perspective
summary: Electronic design automation (EDA) is inherently multi-stage and handoff-heavy. Design artifacts, flow scripts, and engineering decisions cross tool, session, and organizational boundaries before final implementation, signoff, or release. Each transfer carries explicit and implicit requirements that may not be fully captured by stage-local checks. LLM-based agents now invoke EDA tools directly, embed retrieved knowledge in executable scripts, and hand off state across sessions and stages. Once their outputs condition downstream engineering decisions, the transferred object must satisfy a handoff contract and meet the assumptions of its next consumer. This survey introduces handoff validity as its organizing principle. A handoff is valid when the transferred object satisfies the consumer's acceptance conditions and carries sufficient context, evidence, and provenance for downstream use. We review 82 systems and classify them into three boundary classes. Stage-Bound systems establish validity within a single EDA stage or bounded verification task. Flow-Bound systems preserve coherent workflow state across tools, invocations, and sessions. Organization-Bound systems maintain source grounding, provenance, scope, and admissibility across knowledge and authority boundaries. For each class, we analyze handoff contracts, handoff objects, coordination mechanisms, and open questions. These analyses motivate a five-layer EDA agent communication protocol (EACP), covering the agent discovery, agent message, tool invocation, workflow orchestration, and security and IP protocols. We aim to provide a common vocabulary and research agenda for trustworthy agentic EDA.

AI.tldr: This survey introduces "handoff validity" as an organizing principle for trustworthy agentic EDA, reviewing 82 systems across three boundary classes (stage-bound, flow-bound, organization-bound) and proposing a five-layer EDA agent communication protocol (EACP).
AI.motivation: EDA is inherently multi-stage and handoff-heavy, with design artifacts and engineering decisions crossing tool, session, and organizational boundaries. LLM-based agents now invoke EDA tools and transfer state across sessions, but each transfer carries explicit and implicit requirements that stage-local checks may not fully capture, motivating the need for systematic handoff validity.
AI.method: The authors review 82 systems and classify them into three boundary classes (Stage-Bound, Flow-Bound, Organization-Bound). For each class, they analyze handoff contracts, handoff objects, coordination mechanisms, and open questions. These analyses motivate a five-layer EDA agent communication protocol (EACP).
AI.result: A taxonomy of 82 systems into three boundary classes with detailed analysis of handoff contracts, objects, and coordination mechanisms for each class. Proposes a five-layer EACP covering agent discovery, message, tool invocation, workflow orchestration, and security/IP protocols.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a common vocabulary and research agenda for trustworthy agentic EDA through the lens of handoff validity and the proposed EACP framework.

title: VFACamou: View-Fused Adversarial Camouflage for Environment-Adaptive Physical Evasion
summary: Adversarial camouflage in the physical world remains highly challenging, particularly under UAV reconnaissance where targets undergo continuous geometric changes and extreme illumination variations. Existing methods either optimize 2D digital perturbations that fail to generalize to dynamic viewpoints or produce visually unnatural textures that cannot be deployed in real scenarios. Therefore, we propose an end-to-end framework for adversarial camouflage generation that automatically produces wearable adversarial patterns and maintains stable attack performance in real physical environments with changing viewpoints, poses, and lighting conditions. Our method integrates UV-volume rendering with a diffusion-based texture generator, enabling consistent appearance under varying scales, poses, and lighting conditions. To ensure environmental realism, we propose an illumination color consistency estimator that extracts dominant background attributes and guides a natural texture loss to align the generated UV texture with the surrounding environment. A multi-scale dynamic training strategy further enhances robustness against viewpoint shifts and body deformation. Extensive experiments across multiple mainstream detectors demonstrate that our method achieves strong and stable physical attack performance while maintaining high perceptual naturalness, reducing human detection rates without introducing unnatural artifacts.

AI.tldr: Proposes an end-to-end framework for generating physically deployable adversarial camouflage that maintains stable attack performance under changing viewpoints, poses, and lighting conditions in UAV reconnaissance scenarios, integrating UV-volume rendering, diffusion-based texture generation, illumination color consistency, and multi-scale dynamic training.
AI.motivation: Existing adversarial camouflage methods either produce 2D digital perturbations that fail under dynamic viewpoints or generate unnatural textures unsuitable for real-world deployment, especially under UAV reconnaissance where targets undergo continuous geometric changes and extreme illumination variations.
AI.method: (1) UV-volume rendering with a diffusion-based texture generator for consistent appearance across varying conditions; (2) Illumination color consistency estimator that extracts background attributes to guide natural texture loss; (3) Multi-scale dynamic training strategy for robustness against viewpoint shifts and body deformation.
AI.result: Achieves strong and stable physical attack performance across multiple mainstream detectors while maintaining high perceptual naturalness and reducing human detection rates without introducing unnatural artifacts.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively generates wearable adversarial camouflage that is both physically realizable and perceptually natural, successfully addressing the challenges of dynamic viewpoints, poses, and lighting in real-world UAV reconnaissance scenarios.

title: BrainG3N: A Dual-Purpose Tokenizer for Controllable 3D Brain MRI Generation
summary: Three-dimensional (3D) brain MRI is central to clinical neurology and neuro-oncology, where generative models could augment under-represented cohorts, simulate disease trajectories, and support privacy-preserving data sharing. Latent diffusion has been the go-to solution for modeling imaging data, but it places two competing demands on the tokenizer: encoder embeddings must retain the clinical information that downstream tasks act on, and the decoder must reconstruct anatomically faithful volumes. Existing reconstruction-driven tokenizers achieve the second at the expense of the first. To address this, we introduce a fully volumetric masked-autoencoder (MAE) based tokenizer for 3D brain MRI latent diffusion, decoupling encoder and decoder: a frozen 3D MAE encoder produces clinically informative embeddings, while a dedicated CNN decoder reconstructs voxels from a linear projection of those embeddings. We pretrain the encoder on 35,309 volumes from 18 public cohorts spanning four modalities, ten disease categories, and 200+ acquisition sites, and demonstrate its dual utility in two settings. First, on a 23-task linear-probing benchmark, the encoder outperforms or matches SOTA models (i.e., BrainIAC, BrainSegFounder, and MedicalNet) on 21 of 23 tasks. Second, a conditional diffusion transformer (DiT) trained on these clinically informative embeddings supports both conditional generation across six variables and patient-specific longitudinal forecasting. Together these results establish a single 3D brain-MRI embedding space capable of both downstream clinical tasks and controllable generation.

AI.tldr: A 3D brain MRI latent diffusion framework using a decoupled tokenizer—frozen 3D MAE encoder for clinically informative embeddings and a dedicated CNN decoder—that outperforms or matches SOTA on 21/23 clinical tasks and enables conditional generation and longitudinal forecasting.
AI.motivation: Existing reconstruction-driven tokenizers for 3D brain MRI latent diffusion prioritize anatomical fidelity at the cost of losing clinical information critical for downstream tasks; there is a need for a tokenizer that preserves both clinical utility and generative capability.
AI.method: A decoupled tokenizer with a frozen 3D MAE encoder (pretrained on 35,309 volumes from 18 cohorts, 4 modalities, 10 disease categories, 200+ sites) producing clinically informative embeddings, and a dedicated CNN decoder reconstructing voxels from a linear projection of those embeddings. A conditional DiT is trained on these embeddings for generative tasks.
AI.result: On a 23-task linear-probing benchmark, the encoder outperforms or matches SOTA (BrainIAC, BrainSegFounder, MedicalNet) on 21 tasks. The conditional DiT supports generation across six variables and patient-specific longitudinal forecasting.
