We also discover systematic biases in LLM perception of foreignness related to non-English names. Noting these weaknesses, we introduce CRIT, a confidence calibration framework. Our method increases accuracy to 80.5\% while improving risk management steering and reducing bias.

AI.tldr: MortarBench is a new benchmark for loan origination agents that reveals poor performance of LLMs (max 77.1% accuracy) and systematic biases against non-English names. The proposed CRIT framework improves accuracy to 80.5% while reducing bias and improving risk management.
AI.motivation: Despite firms increasingly using mortgage loan agents to augment human loan officers, there is no public benchmark for evaluating loan origination agents. This gap hinders systematic assessment and improvement of such systems.
AI.method: MortarBench uses a financial data synthesis and mutation pipeline to generate diverse edge cases matching real-world distributions. The CRIT framework is a confidence calibration method that addresses LLM weaknesses and biases.
AI.result: State-of-the-art LLMs achieve at most 77.1% exact match accuracy on MortarBench. Systematic biases related to non-English names are discovered. CRIT increases accuracy to 80.5%.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs are inadequate for loan origination tasks, exhibiting both low accuracy and foreign name bias. The CRIT framework offers a viable path toward safer, fairer AI-assisted lending.

title: Learning Critical Testing Literacy Through Puzzles: an Experience Report
summary:
In this paper, we report our experiences and takeaways from workshops using puzzles to learn CTL.
  Background: Software testing is important yet difficult to teach. We introduced a BoK of puzzle-based learning activities to teach CTL, based on a model of critical tester's cognition, leading to the pedagogical framework P4TEST. We conducted thirteen workshops with students, testers, teachers, and primary school pupils to assess puzzle-based teaching of critical testing literacy.
  Experience: Across eleven workshops, we used a semi-structured approach, varying puzzles, materials, and timing. In two additional workshops, we introduced workbooks and think-aloud sessions to gather more data on the learning experience.
  Observations: Participants consistently perceived themselves as experimenting while solving puzzles. Students tended to converge on solutions, while professionals continued exploring. Emotions were visible in behaviour but hard to surface through written reflection alone. Think-aloud sessions revealed immediate reasoning; written reflections elicited more meta-cognitive reflection. The theme Sensemaking / reflection-in-action captured how participants framed problems, navigated dead ends, and shifted strategies.
  Reflections: Puzzles are not the intervention: the entire sequence of solving, debriefing, and reflecting is. Designing that sequence more deliberately is the work ahead. We also developed an open-source web application with built-in analytics to customise workshops.

AI.tldr: The paper reports on using puzzle-based workshops to teach Critical Testing Literacy (CTL), finding that the full sequence of solving, debriefing, and reflecting matters more than puzzles alone, with differences observed between student and professional learning behaviors.
AI.motivation: Software testing is important yet difficult to teach. The authors introduced a puzzle-based learning approach (P4TEST framework) grounded in a model of critical tester's cognition to improve CTL education.
AI.method: Thirteen workshops were conducted with diverse participants (students, testers, teachers, primary pupils). Eleven used a semi-structured approach with varying puzzles/materials; two added workbooks and think-aloud sessions. An open-source web app with analytics was developed.
AI.result: Participants engaged in experimentation behavior. Students converged on solutions while professionals continued exploring. Think-aloud captured immediate reasoning; written reflections elicited meta-cognitive insights. Sensemaking/reflection-in-action was a key theme.
AI.conclusion: The effective intervention is the full sequence (solving + debriefing + reflecting), not just puzzles. Deliberate design of this sequence is future work.

title: OTCHA: Optimal Transport-driven Confidence-aware Latent Hub Alignment for Multi-View Medical Image Classification
summary: Multi-view imaging, such as mammography and chest radiography, is a standard component of clinical practice. However, medical images are often unregistered and contain view-specific artifacts or irrelevant background cues that can obscure diagnostically relevant findings. Many existing methods directly fuse per-view representations, allowing such irrelevant content to contaminate the fused embedding and reducing robustness under varying view configurations. We propose OTCHA, a confidence-aware latent hub token alignment module based on optimal transport (OT) that refines patch tokens before fusion for multi-view classification. OTCHA introduces a set of learnable latent hub tokens shared across views. For each view, we compute an OT plan between patch tokens and hub tokens that jointly considers feature similarity and geometry, and augment the OT formulation with token-conditional dustbins to enable partial matching and discard irrelevant tokens. The resulting transport plan provides token-wise matching confidence, which gates hub-mediated message passing and weights a novel optimal-transport-based representation alignment loss to stabilize refinement. Experiments on three multi-view medical image datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over competing baselines across diverse anatomies and view configurations. Our code is available at https://github.com/labhai/OTCHA.

AI.tldr: OTCHA introduces a confidence-aware latent hub token alignment module using optimal transport to refine patch tokens before fusion for multi-view medical image classification, discarding irrelevant tokens via partial matching and demonstrating consistent improvements across three datasets.
AI.motivation: Multi-view medical images are often unregistered and contain view-specific artifacts or irrelevant background cues that contaminate fused embeddings in existing methods, reducing robustness under varying view configurations.
AI.method: OTCHA uses learnable latent hub tokens shared across views, computes an optimal transport plan between patch tokens and hub tokens that jointly considers feature similarity and geometry, augments OT with token-conditional dustbins for partial matching to discard irrelevant tokens, and uses transport plan-derived matching confidence to gate message passing and weight an OT-based representation alignment loss.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over competing baselines across three multi-view medical image datasets covering diverse anatomies and view configurations.
AI.conclusion: OTCHA's confidence-aware token refinement via optimal transport with partial matching effectively handles unregistered multi-view medical images with artifacts, improving classification robustness.

title: Grounded Inference: Principles for Deterministically Encapsulated Generative Models
summary: The incorporation of generative models into traditional computational systems presents both enormous opportunity and tremendous peril. Although many early adopters have realized these perils at great expense, the field still requires foundational frameworks to de-risk incorporation of AI into traditional systems. This manuscript establishes this foundation through the definition of four specific primitives of AI blended architecture, designed to enable deterministic encapsulation of probabilistic models. It further establishes two overarching anti-patterns broadly represented across industry to serve as warnings for engineers in this field. This framework was designed to enable successful integration of AI into traditional systems while providing a foundation upon which generative model providers could build the next generation of generative model interfaces.

AI.tldr: This paper defines four primitives of AI-blended architecture for deterministically encapsulating probabilistic models, and identifies two anti-patterns as warnings for engineers integrating generative AI into traditional systems.
AI.motivation: The field lacks foundational frameworks to de-risk incorporation of AI into traditional systems, despite many early adopters experiencing failures at great expense.
AI.method: The authors establish a foundation by defining four specific primitives of AI-blended architecture designed for deterministic encapsulation of probabilistic models, and identify two overarching industry anti-patterns.
AI.result: A framework comprising four architectural primitives and two anti-patterns for safe AI integration.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables successful integration of AI into traditional systems and provides a foundation for generative model providers to build next-generation interfaces.

title: 3D-DLP: Self-Supervised 3D Object-Centric Scene Representation Learning
summary: We introduce 3D-DLP, a self-supervised object-centric representation learning model that decomposes scene-level RGB-D or voxel observations into a set of 3D latent particles. Building on the Deep Latent Particles (DLP) framework, each particle encodes disentangled attributes, including 3D keypoint position, bounding box dimensions, and appearance features, and represents a distinct entity in the scene. The model learns interpretable per-particle segmentation maps through an end-to-end self-supervised reconstruction objective. We demonstrate on both simulated and real-world datasets that the learned latent space is interpretable and controllable: by manipulating particle positions and decoding, we can generate novel scene configurations. Furthermore, we show that leveraging these compact 3D latent particles for downstream robotic manipulation improves performance over baselines that either lack explicit 3D information or rely on memory-intensive dense 3D inputs without object-centric structure. Code and videos are available at https://eubooks3003.github.io/3d-dlp.

AI.tldr: 3D-DLP is a self-supervised model that decomposes 3D scene observations into latent particles (with 3D keypoints, bounding boxes, appearance) for interpretable, controllable scene representation, improving downstream robotic manipulation.
AI.motivation: To learn object-centric 3D representations from RGB-D/voxel data without supervision, enabling interpretable and controllable scene understanding for robotics.
AI.method: Extends Deep Latent Particles (DLP) to 3D; each particle encodes disentangled 3D keypoint position, bounding box dimensions, and appearance features; trained via self-supervised reconstruction with learnable per-particle segmentation maps.
AI.result: Demonstrates interpretable latent space (manipulating particles generates novel scenes) and improves robotic manipulation performance over baselines without explicit 3D or object-centric structure.
AI.conclusion: 3D object-centric latent particles provide a compact, interpretable, and controllable representation beneficial for downstream tasks like robotic manipulation.

title: N-Version Programming with Coding Agents
summary: This paper revisits the classical concept on N-version programming in the setting of contemporary AI coding agents. Revisiting the seminal Knight-Leveson experiment, we study whether diversity across agent systems, models, and implementation languages creates diverse failure modes. Using the Knight-Leveson's, Launch Interceptor Program Specification, we evaluate 48 agent-generated implementations on a shared oracle and a campaign of 1,000,000 randomized test inputs. The results show substantial common-mode failure, along the findings of Knight-Leveson. Further analysis that many of those co-occuring failures can be traced to where is specification is particularly hard or ambiguous. We also demonstrate that diversity from coding agents provides practical benefit: across majority voting three-version units, the mean failure count drops from 387.44 for single versions to 130.99 for triples, and 11,844 N-version units exhibit zero observed failures. Our original results is the strongest evidence to date that N-Version Programming with coding agents is a useful engineering strategy.

AI.tldr: This paper revisits N-version programming in the context of AI coding agents, using the Knight-Leveson Launch Interceptor Program to evaluate 48 agent-generated implementations. It finds substantial common-mode failures (mirroring the original Knight-Leveson findings), traces failures to ambiguous specification areas, and shows that diversity from coding agents provides practical benefits: majority voting across three-version units reduces mean failure count from 387.44 to 130.99, with 11,844 N-version units showing zero failures.
AI.motivation: To revisit the classical N-version programming concept (specifically the Knight-Leveson experiment) in the modern context of AI coding agents, and to investigate whether diversity across agent systems, models, and implementation languages produces diverse failure modes that can be exploited for fault tolerance.
AI.method: The authors use the Knight-Leveson Launch Interceptor Program Specification to generate 48 implementations via coding agents. They evaluate these implementations on a shared oracle with 1,000,000 randomized test inputs. They analyze common-mode failures, trace co-occurring failures to ambiguous specification areas, and evaluate majority voting across three-version units.
AI.result: Substantial common-mode failures exist, consistent with the original Knight-Leveson findings. Mean failure count drops from 387.44 (single versions) to 130.99 (triples via majority voting). 11,844 N-version units exhibit zero observed failures.
AI.conclusion: N-version programming with AI coding agents is a useful engineering strategy, providing the strongest evidence to date for this approach.

title: ViCoStream: Streaming VideoLLMs Can Run Beyond 100 FPS with Stage-Wise Coordinated Inference
summary: Streaming VideoLLMs must continuously process incoming video while maintaining low query latency, making both video-ingestion throughput and query-time responsiveness critical for real-time deployment. Existing methods largely focus on accelerating individual modules, such as visual encoding, token pruning, or KV-cache compression, but provide limited insight into whether the resulting system can sustain real-time streaming performance. We formulate streaming VideoLLM inference as a coordinated pipeline spanning visual preprocessing, visual encoding, token dropping, and LLM prefilling/decoding. Building on this formulation, we propose ViCoStream (Video Coordinated Streaming), a stage-wise coordinated streaming framework that combines chunk-wise execution, CUDA-stream overlap, visual token control, bounded visual attention, and query-side retrieval to bound per-chunk computation and memory costs. We further provide a systematic study of bottleneck migration, revealing how chunk size, token retention, attention locality, and retrieval scope shape the throughput-accuracy trade-off. Experiments with Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B-Instruct across multiple streaming benchmarks show that ViCoStream achieves 134 FPS video throughput and less than 50 ms TTFT on a single A100 GPU while maintaining accuracy close to full-history baselines.

AI.tldr: ViCoStream is a stage-wise coordinated streaming framework for VideoLLMs that achieves 134 FPS throughput and <50ms TTFT on a single A100 GPU by orchestrating visual preprocessing, encoding, token dropping, and LLM inference into a coordinated pipeline with chunk-wise execution and CUDA overlap.
AI.motivation: Existing methods focus on accelerating individual modules (visual encoding, token pruning, KV-cache compression) but provide limited insight into whether the resulting system can sustain real-time streaming performance. A holistic pipeline-level understanding is needed for real-time deployment.
AI.method: ViCoStream formulates streaming VideoLLM inference as a coordinated pipeline spanning visual preprocessing, visual encoding, token dropping, and LLM prefilling/decoding. It combines chunk-wise execution, CUDA-stream overlap, visual token control, bounded visual attention, and query-side retrieval to bound per-chunk computation and memory costs. It also provides a systematic study of bottleneck migration across chunk size, token retention, attention locality, and retrieval scope.
AI.result: ViCoStream achieves 134 FPS video throughput and less than 50 ms TTFT on a single A100 GPU while maintaining accuracy close to full-history baselines, evaluated with Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B-Instruct across multiple streaming benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: ViCoStream demonstrates that a stage-wise coordinated pipeline approach can deliver real-time streaming VideoLLM performance without sacrificing accuracy, providing a systematic framework for understanding throughput-accuracy trade-offs.

title: Optimal Scheduling in a Question-Answering Forum of Knowledge Workers
summary: As individuals turn to the Internet to find answers to questions they may have, several Question Answering (QA) forums have evolved, where users knowledgeable in certain topics can contribute their expertise to answering these requests for information. While these are currently volunteer based, we consider a future version employing knowledge workers who are experts in certain topics. In such a system, the request-answer processes forming the queuing system may utilize schedulers that assign requests in different topics to the experts in the forum, who may be able to answer them according to their expertise levels in different topics. With this model, we calculate the capacity of the system for handling the requests while keeping the system stable, and design schedulers that achieve capacity. We also investigate how collaboration between experts in answering requests can potentially increase capacity.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes a future Question Answering (QA) forum system where expert knowledge workers are assigned to answer topic-specific requests. It calculates system capacity for stable request handling, designs schedulers that achieve capacity, and explores how expert collaboration can further increase capacity.
AI.motivation: To model a future QA forum system employing topic-specific expert knowledge workers (beyond current volunteer-based models) and analyze the capacity and stability of such a request-answer queuing system.
AI.method: The paper models the request-answer process as a queuing system with schedulers that assign requests in different topics to experts based on their expertise levels. It calculates the system's capacity for stable handling of requests and designs schedulers to achieve that capacity. It also investigates the potential capacity gains from collaboration between experts.
AI.result: The paper determines the capacity of the QA forum system for handling requests while maintaining stability, proposes schedulers that achieve this capacity, and demonstrates that collaboration between experts can further increase system capacity.
AI.conclusion: A knowledge worker-based QA forum can be modeled as a stable queuing system with topic-aware schedulers, and expert collaboration offers additional capacity improvements.

title: Can In-Context Learning Support Intrinsic Curiosity?
summary: Effective machine learning depends not only on how we model data, but also on what data we choose to collect. While large sequence models have revolutionized data modeling, the problem of automated data selection, or "intrinsic curiosity", remains a significant challenge. Classic approaches incentivize exploration by rewarding an agent based on its "learning progress", which measures how much a newly acquired observation improves a world model's predictive ability. However, evaluating these rewards traditionally requires expensive inner loops of gradient descent updates within each trajectory, rendering them computationally impractical at scale. In this work, we investigate whether the emergent in-context learning (ICL) capabilities of sequence models can eliminate this bottleneck by serving as immediate, update-free world models. Specifically, we evaluate whether an exploration policy can be trained to maximize learning progress, using solely the prediction errors and counterfactual context manipulations of an in-context learner. We first prove that in general Markov decision processes, this is in fact impossible in an unbiased way: the resulting intrinsic rewards either suffer from nuisance terms that bias their estimation of true learning progress, or they cannot be implemented using an in-context learner's prediction errors. Conversely, we prove a positive result for a broad subclass of non-temporal settings, encompassing active learning and Bayesian Experimental Design: here, ICL-derived rewards successfully bound and asymptotically converge to the true learning progress. We corroborate our theory with controlled experiments across continuous and symbolic environments, demonstrating that our ICL-driven framework successfully trains curious data-collection policies that explore optimally.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether in-context learning (ICL) in sequence models can replace expensive gradient-based updates for computing learning progress rewards in exploration. The authors prove that unbiased estimation is impossible in general MDPs, but show positive results for non-temporal settings (active learning, Bayesian Experimental Design), where ICL-derived rewards converge to true learning progress. Experiments confirm the framework trains curious policies that explore optimally.
AI.motivation: Classic exploration approaches reward agents based on "learning progress" (how much a new observation improves a world model's predictive ability), but computing these rewards requires expensive inner-loop gradient descent updates within each trajectory, making them computationally impractical at scale. The authors investigate whether emergent in-context learning capabilities of sequence models can serve as immediate, update-free world models to eliminate this bottleneck.
AI.method: The authors first prove a negative result: in general Markov decision processes, using ICL prediction errors and counterfactual context manipulations to estimate learning progress is impossible in an unbiased way—rewards either suffer from nuisance biases or cannot be implemented via ICL prediction errors. They then prove a positive result for non-temporal settings (active learning, Bayesian Experimental Design), showing ICL-derived rewards bound and asymptotically converge to true learning progress. They validate with controlled experiments in continuous and symbolic environments.
AI.result: Negative result: unbiased estimation of learning progress via ICL is impossible in general MDPs. Positive result: for non-temporal settings (active learning, Bayesian experimental design), ICL-derived rewards successfully bound and asymptotically converge to true learning progress. Experiments across continuous and symbolic environments confirm the ICL-driven framework trains curious data-collection policies that explore optimally.
AI.conclusion: In-context learning can replace expensive gradient updates for computing learning progress rewards, but only in non-temporal settings. The approach enables computationally efficient intrinsic curiosity at scale for active learning and Bayesian experimental design problems.

title: Qiskit Code Migration with LLMs
summary: The rapid evolution of Quantum Development Kits (QDKs) introduces a specific form of technical debt that compromises code maintainability and hinders software reuse. In the specialized domain of Quantum Software Engineering (QSE), this challenge is intensified by the scarcity of high-quality training data and the high volatility of emerging frameworks, which often lead general-purpose Large Language Models (LLMs) to produce unreliable or hallucinated results. This paper proposes a hybrid approach integrating LLMs with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to automate the migration of Qiskit code across versions. The proposed methodology enhances the precision and reliability of migration suggestions by leveraging an automatically generated taxonomy of migration scenarios as the structured, version-specific knowledge source to guide the models. The approach is implemented through an automated, extensible workflow evaluating LLMs (Google Gemini Flash-2.5 and OpenAI Gpt-oss-20b) under different retrieval schemes (unconstrained and restrictive). Results demonstrate that the taxonomy-based RAG architecture, particularly under the restrictive scheme, significantly reduces hallucinations and improves descriptive quality, with Google Gemini Flash-2.5 showing superior performance in detecting complex refactoring scenarios. These findings confirm the potential of this data-centric methodology to foster technological independence and provide robust, intelligent assistants that mitigate API obsolescence, ensuring the long-term availability of quantum algorithms within a rapidly shifting ecosystem and flattening the learning curve within Quantum Software Engineering (QSE).

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a hybrid approach combining LLMs with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and an automatically generated taxonomy of migration scenarios to automate Qiskit code migration across versions, reducing hallucinations and improving descriptive quality, with Google Gemini Flash-2.5 showing superior performance under a restrictive retrieval scheme.
AI.motivation: The rapid evolution of Quantum Development Kits (QDKs) introduces technical debt that compromises code maintainability and hinders software reuse. In Quantum Software Engineering (QSE), this challenge is intensified by the scarcity of high-quality training data and high volatility of emerging frameworks, causing general-purpose LLMs to produce unreliable or hallucinated results.
AI.method: A hybrid approach integrating LLMs with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and an automatically generated taxonomy of migration scenarios as a structured, version-specific knowledge source. Evaluated under two retrieval schemes (unconstrained and restrictive) using Google Gemini Flash-2.5 and OpenAI Gpt-oss-20b.
AI.result: Taxonomy-based RAG architecture, especially under the restrictive scheme, significantly reduces hallucinations and improves descriptive quality. Google Gemini Flash-2.5 demonstrated superior performance in detecting complex refactoring scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The data-centric methodology fosters technological independence, provides robust intelligent assistants that mitigate API obsolescence, ensures long-term availability of quantum algorithms within a shifting ecosystem, and flattens the learning curve in Quantum Software Engineering.

title: PSCT-Net: Geometry-Aware Pediatric Skull CT Reconstruction via Differentiable Back-Projection and Attention-Guided Refinement
summary: Computed Tomography (CT) is essential for diagnosing pediatric craniofacial abnormalities, yet poses radiation risks to developing anatomies. Reconstructing 3D CT from sparse bi-planar X-rays offers a low-dose alternative but is severely ill-posed. Existing methods employ geometry-agnostic feature lifting, naively projecting 2D features into 3D without explicit spatial modeling, causing depth ambiguity and degraded osseous boundaries. We present PSCT-Net, a geometry-aware framework with differentiable back-projection. Differentiable back-projection establishes a spatially faithful volumetric prior, alleviating depth ambiguity. An Attention-Guided Projection (AGP-3D) module then learns non-linear voxel-wise correspondences between 2D regions and 3D locations. A Bidirectional Mamba (BiM-3D) module captures long-range volumetric dependencies with linear complexity. We further curate a private institutional pediatric skull CT cohort, PedSkull-CT, comprising normal and pathological cases for internal evaluation, addressing the gap in adult-centric, trunk-focused datasets.

AI.tldr: PSCT-Net is a geometry-aware framework for reconstructing 3D CT from sparse bi-planar X-rays, addressing depth ambiguity via differentiable back-projection, AGP-3D, and BiM-3D modules, evaluated on a new pediatric skull CT dataset (PedSkull-CT).
AI.motivation: CT is essential for diagnosing pediatric craniofacial abnormalities but poses radiation risks. Reconstructing 3D CT from sparse bi-planar X-rays offers a low-dose alternative, but existing methods suffer from depth ambiguity and degraded osseous boundaries due to geometry-agnostic feature lifting.
AI.method: PSCT-Net introduces: (1) differentiable back-projection to create a spatially faithful volumetric prior; (2) Attention-Guided Projection (AGP-3D) for learning non-linear voxel-wise 2D-to-3D correspondences; (3) Bidirectional Mamba (BiM-3D) for efficient long-range volumetric dependencies. A private pediatric skull CT dataset (PedSkull-CT) was curated for evaluation.
AI.result: The framework enables low-dose 3D CT reconstruction from sparse bi-planar X-rays, addressing depth ambiguity and improving osseous boundary fidelity. The PedSkull-CT dataset fills the gap in pediatric-specific, craniofacial-focused datasets.
AI.conclusion: PSCT-Net provides a geometry-aware, low-dose alternative for pediatric cranial CT reconstruction, with explicit spatial modeling and efficient long-range dependency capture.

title: Beyond Entropy: Learning from Token-Level Distributional Deviations for LLM Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has significantly advanced Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning; however, it faces a fundamental optimization instability: uniform token updates precipitate entropy collapse, leading to premature convergence to suboptimal strategies, whereas excessive Shannon Entropy maximization can cause entropy explosion, driving blind exploration toward incoherent reasoning chains. To resolve this dichotomy, we introduce the Independent Combinatorial Tokens (ICT) framework, which shifts the optimization focus from scalar uncertainty to the distributional properties of token logits. By leveraging the Jensen-Shannon (JS) divergence between token logits distributions, ICT identifies tokens with distinctive distributional patterns as critical branching points for guiding effective exploration in LLM reasoning. Our theoretical analysis, grounded in both Shannon and second-order Rényi entropy, proves that selectively updating on these tokens regulates policy concentration: it reduces the overall distribution uncertainty measured by Shannon entropy, while controlling probability concentration captured by second-order Rényi entropy. This dual effect prevents over-concentrated token generation from weakening exploration and effectively stabilizes the training landscape. Empirical results demonstrate that updating only the top 10% of unique tokens on Qwen2.5 (0.5B/1.5B/7B) models yields an average pass@4 improvement of 4.58%, with a maximum gain of 14.9%, over GRPO, 20-Entropy, and STAPO baselines across seven benchmarks spanning math, commonsense, and Olympiad-level problems.

AI.tldr: The Independent Combinatorial Tokens (ICT) framework addresses the optimization instability in RLVR for LLMs by selectively updating tokens with distinctive distributional patterns (measured via JS divergence), preventing both entropy collapse and explosion, and achieving up to 14.9% pass@4 improvement over baselines.
AI.motivation: RLVR for LLM reasoning suffers from a fundamental dichotomy: uniform token updates cause entropy collapse (premature convergence to suboptimal strategies), while excessive entropy maximization causes entropy explosion (blind exploration toward incoherent reasoning chains).
AI.method: ICT shifts optimization from scalar uncertainty to distributional properties of token logits, using JS divergence between token logits distributions to identify critical branching points. It selectively updates only the top 10% of unique tokens, grounded in theoretical analysis using both Shannon and second-order Rényi entropy.
AI.result: On Qwen2.5 (0.5B/1.5B/7B) models, ICT yields an average pass@4 improvement of 4.58% and a maximum gain of 14.9% over GRPO, 20-Entropy, and STAPO baselines across seven benchmarks spanning math, commonsense, and Olympiad-level problems.
AI.conclusion: Selective token updating via distributional analysis regulates policy concentration, prevents over-concentrated token generation, and effectively stabilizes the training landscape in LLM reasoning.

title: Insulin4RL: Real-Time Insulin Management in the Intensive Care Unit for Offline Reinforcement Learning
summary: Offline reinforcement learning (ORL) offers the potential to improve the quality of clinical decision-making using historical electronic health record (EHR) data. Current training and evaluative practices in this field rely heavily on EHR datasets that have been temporally discretised into fixed, regular time intervals. Discretisation creates fictional representations of complex clinical scenarios and compromises the generalisability of retrospective model evaluations. In this paper, we introduce Insulin4RL, a healthcare ORL dataset featuring naturally irregular inputs and actions from real clinical trajectories. Derived from MIMIC-IV, Insulin4RL comprises over 375,000 labelled decisions across 12,209 patients requiring insulin infusion titration in the Intensive Care Unit. The dataset can thus be used for research into ORL model performance under realistic clinical sampling assumptions. We provide a description of the dataset's structure and characteristics, baseline performance metrics using model-free offline reinforcement learning, and a standardised evaluation protocol using fitted Q-evaluation. We conclude with suggested areas for future research that could be addressed using this resource.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Insulin4RL, a healthcare offline reinforcement learning dataset based on MIMIC-IV, featuring naturally irregular clinical trajectories for insulin infusion titration in ICU patients, along with baseline metrics and evaluation protocols.
AI.motivation: Current ORL practices rely on EHR datasets discretised into fixed regular time intervals, which creates fictional representations of complex clinical scenarios and compromises the generalisability of retrospective model evaluations. A dataset with naturally irregular inputs and actions is needed.
AI.method: Insulin4RL is derived from MIMIC-IV, comprising over 375,000 labelled decisions across 12,209 ICU patients requiring insulin infusion titration. It features naturally irregular temporal sampling. Baseline performance is established using model-free offline RL methods, with standardised evaluation via fitted Q-evaluation.
AI.result: The dataset contains 375,000+ labelled decisions across 12,209 patients. Baseline performance metrics using model-free offline RL are provided, along with a standardised evaluation protocol using fitted Q-evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Insulin4RL enables research into ORL model performance under realistic clinical sampling assumptions. The paper suggests future research areas that could be addressed using this resource.

title: SysML Modeling of Digital Twins for Renewable Energy Communities
comment: Presented at the Workshop on Digital Twin Experiences and Model-Based Testing Methods, 12 June 2026, Västerås, Sweden, co-located with the 30th Ada-Europe International Conference on Reliable Software Technologies (AEiC 2026)
summary: Renewable Energy Communities (RECs) are emerging as a key organizational model for local and global sharing of renewable generation, storage, and flexible loads. Engineering Digital Twins of RECs is made difficult by the heterogeneity of devices, contracts, and runtime data involved. In this paper, we take a first step toward a Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) workflow for REC's Digital Twins. Starting from an industrially-validated REC domain model, we re-express a representative house subset in SysML using the open-source Modelio tool, yielding two Block Definition Diagrams - a device taxonomy and a community organizational view. We then discuss four semantic gaps that plain SysML leaves open and sketch how the SAREF4ENER ontology could be imported as a reference package to close them. Combining SysML with SAREF-based semantics for smart-energy Digital Twins remains largely unexplored, and we position this paper as a first step along that line.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a first step toward a Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) workflow for Engineering Digital Twins of Renewable Energy Communities (RECs), using SysML in Modelio to model a representative house from an industrially-validated REC domain model, and identifies four semantic gaps that could be addressed by importing the SAREF4ENERGY ontology.
AI.motivation: Engineering Digital Twins of RECs is challenging due to the heterogeneity of devices, contracts, and runtime data; there is a need for a systematic MBSE approach to model such complex systems.
AI.method: The authors re-express a representative house subset from an industrially-validated REC domain model into SysML using the open-source Modelio tool, producing two Block Definition Diagrams (a device taxonomy and a community organizational view). They then identify semantic gaps in plain SysML and propose importing the SAREF4ENER ontology as a reference package.
AI.result: Two Block Definition Diagrams were created: a device taxonomy and a community organizational view. Four semantic gaps in plain SysML were identified for REC Digital Twin modeling.
AI.conclusion: Combining SysML with SAREF-based semantics for smart-energy Digital Twins remains largely unexplored, and this work is positioned as an initial step toward that integration.

title: Multimodal Concept Bottleneck Models
comment: Present at NeurIPS 2025 Mechanistic Interpretability Workshop
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) enhance the interpretability of deep learning networks by aligning the features extracted from images with natural concepts. However, existing CBMs are constrained in their ability to generalize beyond a fixed set of predefined classes and the risk of non-concept information leakage, where predictive signals outside the intended concepts are inadvertently exploited. In this paper, we propose Multimodal Concept Bottleneck Model (MM-CBM) to address these issues and extend CBMs into CLIP. MM-CBM utilizes dual Concept Bottleneck Layers (CBLs) to align both the image and text embeddings into interpretable features. This allows us to perform new vision tasks like zero-shot classification or image retrieval in an interpretable way. Compared to existing methods, MM-CBM achieves up to 51.26% accuracy improvement on average across four standard benchmarks. Our method maintains high accuracy, staying within ~5% of black-box performance while offering greater interpretability.

AI.tldr: MM-CBM extends Concept Bottleneck Models into CLIP using dual Concept Bottleneck Layers to align image and text embeddings, enabling interpretable zero-shot classification and image retrieval with up to 51.26% accuracy improvement while maintaining within ~5% of black-box performance.
AI.motivation: Existing CBMs cannot generalize beyond a fixed set of predefined classes and suffer from non-concept information leakage, where predictive signals outside intended concepts are inadvertently exploited.
AI.method: Proposes Multimodal Concept Bottleneck Model (MM-CBM) that extends CBMs into CLIP by utilizing dual Concept Bottleneck Layers (CBLs) to align both image and text embeddings into interpretable features.
AI.result: Achieves up to 51.26% accuracy improvement on average across four standard benchmarks, maintaining high accuracy within ~5% of black-box performance.
AI.conclusion: MM-CBM enables interpretable zero-shot classification and image retrieval, addressing generalization limitations and non-concept information leakage in existing CBMs.

title: AgentFinVQA: A Deployable Multi-Agent Pipeline for Auditable Financial Chart QA
summary: Financial chart question answering in regulated settings demands more than accuracy: practitioners must know which answers to trust before acting on them, and many institutions cannot send client data to external model providers. Yet existing chart-QA agents are accuracy-focused and opaque, and most assume proprietary API access; to our knowledge, none combines auditability with on-premise deployability without significant accuracy compromise. We present AgentFinVQA, a multi-agent pipeline that decomposes each query into planning, OCR, legend grounding, visual inspection, and verification, recording every step in a traceable Model Evaluation Packet (MEP) per sample. On FinMME, AgentFinVQA improves $+7.68$ pp over a primary-backbone matched zero-shot baseline with a proprietary backbone (Gemini-3 Flash; 71.24% vs. 63.56%, McNemar $p \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-16}$), and $+4.84$ pp with open-weights Qwen3.6-27B-FP8 served locally. The verifier's verdict also serves as a useful confidence signal (68.2% vs. 55.6% exact accuracy on confirmed vs. revised answers), enabling human-in-the-loop review routing. Error analysis shows that question misunderstanding, legend confusion and extraction error account for nearly two-thirds of failures and are the categories least detected by the verifier, identifying clear directions for future work. Together these results show that auditable, on-premise financial chart QA is practical and that the open-weights system keeps most of the accuracy gains while enabling full data residency. We release our code to support reproducible evaluation.

AI.tldr: AgentFinVQA is a multi-agent pipeline for financial chart QA that achieves auditability and on-premise deployability with minimal accuracy trade-off, improving +7.68 pp (proprietary) and +4.84 pp (open-weights) over baselines, with a verifier providing confidence signals for human-in-the-loop review.
AI.motivation: Existing chart-QA agents are accuracy-focused, opaque, and assume proprietary API access; regulated financial settings require both auditability and on-premise deployment without compromising accuracy or exposing client data.
AI.method: A multi-agent pipeline decomposing queries into planning, OCR, legend grounding, visual inspection, and verification steps; each step is recorded in a traceable Model Evaluation Packet (MEP). Tested with both proprietary (Gemini-3 Flash) and open-weights (Qwen3.6-27B-FP8) backbones.
AI.result: AgentFinVQA achieves +7.68 pp (71.24% vs 63.56%) on FinMME with Gemini-3 Flash and +4.84 pp with Qwen3.6-27B-FP8 locally. The verifier provides useful confidence signals (68.2% vs 55.6% accuracy on confirmed vs. revised answers). Error analysis identifies question misunderstanding, legend confusion, and extraction errors as the main failure modes.
AI.conclusion: Auditable, on-premise financial chart QA is practical; the open-weights system retains most accuracy gains while ensuring full data residency. Code is released for reproducible evaluation.

title: Concept Flow Models: Anchoring Concept-Based Reasoning with Hierarchical Bottlenecks
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) enhance interpretability by projecting learned features into a human-understandable concept space. Recent approaches leverage vision-language models to generate concept embeddings, reducing the need for manual concept annotations. However, these models suffer from a critical limitation: as the number of concepts approaches the embedding dimension, information leakage increases, enabling the model to exploit spurious or semantically irrelevant correlations and undermining interpretability. In this work, we propose Concept Flow Models (CFMs), which replace the flat bottleneck with a hierarchical, concept-driven decision tree. Each internal node in the hierarchy focuses on a localized subset of discriminative concepts, progressively narrowing the prediction scope. Our framework constructs decision hierarchies from visual embeddings, distributes semantic concepts at each hierarchy level, and trains differentiable concept weights through probabilistic tree traversal. Extensive experiments on diverse benchmarks demonstrate that CFMs match the predictive performance of flat CBMs, while substantially mitigating information leakage by reducing effective concept usage. Furthermore, CFMs yield stepwise decision flows that enable transparent and auditable model reasoning with hierarchical class structures.

AI.tldr: Concept Flow Models (CFMs) replace flat concept bottlenecks with hierarchical decision trees to mitigate information leakage in Concept Bottleneck Models, matching predictive performance while reducing effective concept usage and enabling auditable reasoning.
AI.motivation: Flat Concept Bottleneck Models suffer from information leakage as concept count approaches embedding dimension, allowing models to exploit spurious correlations that undermine interpretability.
AI.method: CFMs construct hierarchical decision trees from visual embeddings, distribute semantic concepts per hierarchy level, and train differentiable concept weights via probabilistic tree traversal.
AI.result: CFMs match flat CBMs in predictive performance while substantially reducing information leakage through decreased effective concept usage across diverse benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical concept bottlenecks effectively mitigate information leakage while offering stepwise decision flows for transparent and auditable model reasoning with hierarchical class structures.

title: Phoenix: Safe GitHub Issue Resolution via Multi-Agent LLMs
summary: We present Phoenix, a multi-agent LLM system that resolves GitHub issues from triage through pull-request creation, combining seven layered safety controls with a baseline-aware test evaluation strategy. Phoenix decomposes the work across six specialized agents. Planner, reproducer, coder, tester, failure analyst and Pull Request (PR) agent, all coordinated by a label-based GitHub webhook state machine. Every change is checked against a baseline test run before a pull request is opened. On a 24-instance slice of SWE-bench Lite. run on the production webhook path, Phoenix oracle-resolves 75% of instances with no pass-to-pass regressions on successful runs; this curated slice is not directly comparable to full-split leaderboard results, and we discuss the limits of the comparison. A complementary pilot on 42 real issues across 14 repositories yields 100% correctness preservation (CP; mean 122s on the hard tier). Manual inspection shows that about half of the resulting pull requests are well-targeted fixes. The other half place code at incorrect paths, a planner localization limitation we are addressing with retrieval. We also report the deployment failure modes (WAF filtering, token expiry, permission boundaries, flaky CI) that motivated each safety mechanism.

AI.tldr: Phoenix is a multi-agent LLM system with seven safety layers that resolves GitHub issues through six specialized agents, achieving 75% oracle resolution on a curated SWE-bench Lite slice with no regressions, and 100% correctness preservation on 42 real issues, though half of PRs have path placement issues.
AI.motivation: To develop a safe, production-grade multi-agent LLM system that can autonomously resolve GitHub issues end-to-end, from triage to PR creation, while addressing critical safety concerns like regressions, code quality, and deployment failure modes.
AI.method: Six specialized agents (Planner, Repro, Coder, Tester, Failure Analyst, PR) coordinated by a label-based GitHub webhook state machine, with seven layered safety controls and baseline-aware test evaluation strategy that checks every change against a baseline test run before PR submission.
AI.result: 75% oracle resolution on 24-instance SWE-bench Lite slice with zero pass-to-pass regressions; 100% correctness preservation on 42 real issues across 14 repos (mean 122s hard tier); ~50% of PRs well-targeted, ~50% have incorrect path placement.
AI.conclusion: The system demonstrates strong safety and effectiveness for autonomous issue resolution, but planner localization for correct file paths remains a key limitation; addressing this with retrieval augmentation is the next step.

title: SurgVista: Long-Horizon Surgical World Modeling with Plausible Instrument-Tissue Dynamics
summary: Scaling robot policy learning for autonomous surgery is challenging, as expert demonstrations are expensive and in vivo exploration poses substantial safety risks. Surgical world models address this by generating realistic, action-conditioned future frames from an initial observation, but existing methods exhibit two persistent failure modes: spatial interaction incoherence, where visible instrument contact fails to induce spatially consistent tissue deformation, and temporal fidelity collapse, where prediction errors compound across autoregressive rollouts and progressively corrupt visual quality. We present SurgVista, a surgical world model that mitigates both failures through two training recipes. Deformation Consistency Regularization extracts scene-point trajectories from training videos and enforces cross-frame coherence through latent contrastive learning, strengthening physically consistent instrument-tissue dynamics. Drift Adaptation Training mitigates long-horizon drift by perturbing conditioning frames with online prediction residuals and photometric augmentations calibrated to long-horizon drift statistics, sustaining visual fidelity over extended rollouts. To enable rigorous evaluation, we further introduce SurgWorld-Bench, featuring diverse procedure types, long-range rollouts, and decoupled metrics for instrument-motion accuracy and tissue-response fidelity. Extensive experiments show that SurgVista consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across visual quality, temporal consistency, and interaction fidelity, with gains widening as the prediction horizon grows.

AI.tldr: SurgVista is a surgical world model that tackles spatial interaction incoherence and temporal fidelity collapse in surgical video prediction using Deformation Consistency Regularization and Drift Adaptation Training, evaluated on the new SurgWorld-Bench benchmark.
AI.motivation: Scaling robot policy learning for autonomous surgery is challenging due to the high cost of expert demonstrations and safety risks of in vivo exploration. Surgical world models can address this by generating realistic action-conditioned future frames, but existing methods suffer from spatial interaction incoherence (inconsistent tissue deformation upon instrument contact) and temporal fidelity collapse (error accumulation in autoregressive rollouts).
AI.method: SurgVista introduces two training recipes: (1) Deformation Consistency Regularization, which extracts scene-point trajectories from training videos and enforces cross-frame coherence via latent contrastive learning; (2) Drift Adaptation Training, which perturbs conditioning frames using online prediction residuals and photometric augmentations calibrated to long-horizon drift statistics to sustain visual fidelity.
AI.result: SurgVista consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across visual quality, temporal consistency, and interaction fidelity metrics, with performance gains widening as the prediction horizon grows, as evaluated on the new SurgWorld-Bench benchmark featuring diverse procedures, long-range rollouts, and decoupled metrics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methods effectively mitigate spatial interaction incoherence and temporal fidelity collapse in surgical world models, enabling more reliable long-horizon video prediction for autonomous surgery policy learning.

title: ORAgentBench: Can LLM Agents Solve Challenging Operations Research Tasks End to End?
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents for multi-step tasks in executable environments, yet their ability to perform realistic operations research (OR) work remains unclear. Existing OR evaluations often decouple modeling from solving, rely on pre-formalized or text-only instances, and rarely test the full workflow from operational artifacts to validated decisions. In this work, we introduce ORAgentBench, an execution-grounded benchmark for evaluating autonomous agents on challenging end-to-end operations research tasks. It contains 107 human-reviewed tasks across diverse operational scenarios, each packaged in an isolated environment with a natural-language brief, multi-file data, configuration artifacts, and a required submission schema. Agents must write and run solution code, and their submissions are evaluated by hidden validators for schema validity, hard-constraint feasibility, and normalized objective quality. Experiments with fourteen frontier agent-model configurations show that current agents remain far from reliable OR practice. The best agent passes only 35.51% of all tasks and 20.59% of hard tasks, and many feasible submissions still fall below the required quality threshold. Failure analysis further shows that errors are dominated by strategic weaknesses, including missed operational rules, brittle formulations, weak feasible-solution construction, and insufficient solution improvement. OR-specific procedural skills increase hard-task feasibility, but do not reliably improve solution quality or pass rate. These results suggest that progress in OR agents requires moving beyond plausible optimization code toward dependable, high-quality operational decision-making.

AI.tldr: ORAgentBench evaluates autonomous LLM agents on end-to-end operations research tasks; the best agent passes only 35.51% of tasks, with failures dominated by strategic weaknesses.
AI.motivation: Existing OR evaluations decouple modeling from solving, rely on pre-formalized or text-only instances, and fail to test the full workflow from operational artifacts to validated decisions, leaving unclear whether LLM agents can perform realistic OR work.
AI.method: Introduces ORAgentBench with 107 human-reviewed tasks across diverse operational scenarios, each in an isolated environment with a natural-language brief, multi-file data, configuration artifacts, and a submission schema. Agents write and run solution code; submissions are evaluated by hidden validators for schema validity, hard-constraint feasibility, and normalized objective quality.
AI.result: Best agent passes 35.51% of all tasks and 20.59% of hard tasks; errors are dominated by strategic weaknesses (missed operational rules, brittle formulations, weak feasible-solution construction, insufficient improvement). OR-specific procedural skills increase hard-task feasibility but do not reliably improve solution quality or pass rate.
AI.conclusion: Progress in OR agents requires moving beyond plausible optimization code toward dependable, high-quality operational decision-making.

title: Algebraic Dead Directions in LayerNorm Transformers: A Forward-Pass-Only Diagnostic at LLM Scale
comment: 34 pages, 7 figures, 6 tables. Empirical companion to arXiv:2606.05957
summary: Pretrained transformers sit near singular minima of the loss, where the Fisher information metric degenerates along dead directions: directions in parameter space along which the directional Fisher vanishes. Locating such a direction normally needs a forward pass and an eigendecomposition of activations, or a sampling-based complexity estimate; none returns a direction computable from the network's parameters alone. We give one, for LayerNorm transformers. The inverse-scale direction $γ^{-1}/\|γ^{-1}\|$ of the LayerNorm affine is an exact algebraic kernel of the post-final-norm centred activation covariance, for any input distribution, and induces a corresponding dead direction in parameter space. It is read from the LN scale parameter alone, with no forward or backward pass and no eigensolve: the cheapest dead-direction read, specific to LayerNorm. We test it on $14$ pretrained transformers ($9$ LayerNorm, $5$ RMSNorm; $160$M-$35$B; language and vision objectives). At random initialisation the predicted direction matches the measured bottom singular direction (one forward pass, direct SVD) to four decimal places on $9/9$ LayerNorm models, and is correctly absent on $5/5$ RMSNorm models, which lack the mean-subtraction projector that creates it. On the trained checkpoint the covariance eigenvalue along this direction deepens by ${\sim}10^3\times$ and further dead directions open; the random-init-to-trained gap is a one-forward-pass, per-checkpoint readout of singular structure along the predicted coordinate. Two consequences follow in closed form: the residual stream's smallest singular value is preserved block-to-block on $13/14$ transformers measured on their own input distribution, the one exception (Gemma$4$-$31$B) a genuine dead direction the same read pinpoints; and the kernel direction's presence classifies a transformer's normalisation from the parameters alone.

AI.tldr: LayerNorm transformers have a dead direction in parameter space—the inverse-scale vector of the final LayerNorm—that can be read from parameters alone, without any forward/backward pass or eigendecomposition, and this direction predicts singular structure, distinguishes LayerNorm from RMSNorm, and reveals preservation or loss of residual stream singular values.
AI.motivation: Pretrained transformers sit near singular minima where the Fisher information metric degenerates along dead directions, but existing methods to locate these directions require forward passes and eigendecompositions; the goal is to find a dead direction computable from parameters alone.
AI.method: The authors prove that the inverse-scale vector of the final LayerNorm affine parameter is an exact algebraic kernel of the centred activation covariance, yielding a dead direction in parameter space. This direction is read directly from the LN scale parameter with no data, forward pass, backward pass, or eigendecomposition. Experiments on 14 pretrained transformers (LayerNorm and RMSNorm) validate the prediction.
AI.result: At random initialization, the predicted direction matches the SVD-computed bottom singular direction to four decimal places on all 9 LayerNorm models, and is absent on all 5 RMSNorm models. After training, the eigenvalue along this direction deepens ~10^3×. The residual stream's smallest singular value is preserved block-to-block on 13/14 models; the exception (Gemma 4-31B) is a genuine dead direction the same read pinpoints. The direction's presence classifies normalization type from parameters alone.
AI.conclusion: The inverse-scale direction of LayerNorm provides the cheapest possible dead-direction readout (parameter-only, no computation), reveals singular structure evolution during training, enables block-wise residual stream analysis, and serves as a classifier for normalization type.

title: Token-Operations-Oriented Inference Optimization Techniques for Large Models
summary: Large model inference optimization serves as a key foundation for supporting the scalable, low-cost, and highly stable operation of large model services. Centered on token-oriented inference optimization technology, this paper proposes for the first time a four-layer technical architecture consisting of Multi-model Fusion, Model Optimization, Compute-Model Fusion, and Compute-Network-Model Fusion. It systematically reviews the key technologies and current industry status across these four levels and analyzes the application value of related technologies in real-world business scenarios. This paper provides a practical technical path for reducing token production costs, improving token service efficiency, ensuring the stability of token supply, and driving the transition of large model services from being merely callable to being operable.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a four-layer technical architecture (Multi-model Fusion, Model Optimization, Compute-Model Fusion, Compute-Network-Model Fusion) for large model inference optimization, systematically reviews key technologies and industry status, and analyzes their application value in real-world business scenarios to reduce token costs, improve service efficiency, ensure stability, and make large model services more operable.
AI.motivation: To address the need for scalable, low-cost, and highly stable large model inference services by proposing a structured framework for token-oriented inference optimization.
AI.method: Proposes a four-layer technical architecture: Multi-model Fusion, Model Optimization, Compute-Model Fusion, and Compute-Network-Model Fusion, accompanied by a systematic review of key technologies and industry status at each layer.
AI.result: Identifies and analyzes key inference optimization technologies across four layers, demonstrating their application value in reducing token production costs, improving token service efficiency, and ensuring token supply stability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed architecture provides a practical technical path for transitioning large model services from merely callable to fully operable, enabling cost-efficient and stable inference at scale.

title: Linear Recurrent Unit with Semantic Modulation for Image Super-Resolution
summary: Linear recurrent unit (LRU), designed with a principled formulation for stable linear recurrence, has demonstrated promising accuracy and robustness on long-range dependency tasks. However, its static parameterization and single-scan method limits its applicability to 2D vision tasks. In this study, we propose a LRU-based restoration network with a semantic modulating unit (SMU) to achieve a harmonious balance between performance and efficiency in single-image super-resolution. The SMU plays three key roles: LRU modulation, spatial categorization, and feature enhancement through learned prototype. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method quantitatively and qualitatively surpasses recent state-of-the-art methods. Notably, our approach achieves superior performance with computational complexity on par with existing methods. The source code and models are available at https://github.com/MingyuChoi-run/LSM

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an LRU-based super-resolution network enhanced by a Semantic Modulating Unit (SMU) that outperforms state-of-the-art methods with competitive computational complexity.
AI.motivation: LRU shows promise for long-range tasks but its static parameterization and single-scan method limit its applicability to 2D vision tasks like image super-resolution; the authors aim to bridge this gap.
AI.method: The method integrates a Semantic Modulating Unit (SMU) into an LRU-based restoration network, where SMU performs three functions: modulating LRU parameters, spatial categorization, and feature enhancement via learned prototypes.
AI.result: The proposed method quantitatively and qualitatively surpasses recent state-of-the-art super-resolution methods while maintaining computational complexity comparable to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: The LRU-based network with SMU achieves a harmonious balance between performance and efficiency for single-image super-resolution.

title: CombEval: A Framework for Evaluating Combinatorial Counting in Large Language Models
summary: We present CombEval, a dynamic benchmark for evaluating combinatorial counting in large language models. CombEval represents each problem as a typed Cofola specification over entities, combinatorial objects, object dependencies, and constraints, enabling controlled generation of natural-language counting problems with exact solver-verified answers. Unlike static collections, CombEval supports systematic variation of object type, entity scale, constraint count, and reasoning depth. We evaluate 11 LLMs under direct and code-augmented settings and find that models remain brittle on ordered objects, indistinguishable elements, relatively positional constraints, and nested object dependencies. Error analysis further identifies failures in constraint interpretation and counting principles. CombEval provides a diagnostic testbed for studying when and why LLMs fail at combinatorial reasoning. The code and generated benchmark suites are publicly available at \url{https://github.com/YuxuZhou-CN/combination-problem-generation}.

AI.tldr: CombEval is a dynamic benchmark for evaluating combinatorial counting in LLMs, using typed specifications to generate varied problems with verified answers. Testing 11 LLMs reveals brittleness on ordered objects, indistinguishable elements, positional constraints, and nested dependencies, with errors in constraint interpretation and counting principles.
AI.motivation: Existing static benchmarks cannot systematically vary problem features (object type, scale, constraints, reasoning depth) to diagnose LLM failures in combinatorial counting. CombEval addresses this by enabling controlled, dynamic generation of problems with exact ground-truth answers.
AI.method: Each problem is represented as a typed Cofola specification over entities, combinatorial objects, object dependencies, and constraints. This enables controlled generation of natural-language counting problems with solver-verified answers, supporting systematic variation of object type, entity scale, constraint count, and reasoning depth.
AI.result: 11 LLMs were evaluated under direct and code-augmented settings. Models show brittleness on ordered objects, indistinguishable elements, relative positional constraints, and nested object dependencies. Error analysis reveals failures in constraint interpretation and counting principles.
AI.conclusion: CombEval provides a diagnostic testbed for studying when and why LLMs fail at combinatorial reasoning, with code and benchmark suites publicly available.

title: Calibrating Generative Models to Feature Distributions with MMD Finetuning
summary: Generative models can produce individually plausible samples while deviating substantially from a target set in the distribution of key features. For example, a model pretrained on broad drug-like chemical space may generate molecules whose molecular features differ from those of a therapeutic class of interest, such as known antibiotics. Correcting such distributional miscalibration is challenging: direct finetuning on the target set can overfit and does not control which features are matched. To fill this gap, we introduce kernel Calibrating Generative Models (kCGM). kCGM minimizes a maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between generated and target feature distributions using an unbiased score-function estimator, with KL regularization to remain close to the pretrained model. On a target set of 174 antibiotics, direct finetuning sacrifices chemical validity for feature-distribution matching, whereas kCGM improves target feature matching while increasing validity. We further demonstrate kCGM in protein and DNA generation tasks, showing it can adapt autoregressive, continuous-space diffusion, and discrete diffusion models using only feature-level supervision. Code is available at https://github.com/smithhenryd/cgm.

AI.tldr: kCGM is a method that calibrates generative models to match target feature distributions using maximum mean discrepancy with KL regularization, outperforming direct finetuning on tasks like generating antibiotics, proteins, and DNA sequences.
AI.motivation: Generative models can produce plausible samples but deviate from target feature distributions (e.g., generating molecules that differ from known antibiotics). Direct finetuning on target sets overfits and lacks control over which features are matched.
AI.method: kCGM minimizes Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) between generated and target feature distributions using an unbiased score-function estimator, with KL divergence regularization to stay close to the pretrained model.
AI.result: On a target set of 174 antibiotics, direct finetuning sacrifices chemical validity for feature matching, while kCGM improves target feature matching and increases validity. Also demonstrated on protein and DNA generation with autoregressive, continuous-space diffusion, and discrete diffusion models.
AI.conclusion: kCGM effectively corrects distributional miscalibration in pretrained generative models across molecular, protein, and DNA domains, using only feature-level supervision without sacrificing validity.

title: A Model-Driven Approach for Developing Families of Reinforcement Learning Environments
summary: Virtual training environments are software-intensive systems in which reinforcement learning (RL) agents learn, adapt, and demonstrate meaningful behavior. Virtual training environments offer a safe and cost-efficient alternative to training agents in real-world settings. However, to converge, most realistic RL problems require training in multiple, mostly similar but slightly different environments - i.e., families of environment variants. The typical development process of environment families is a labor-intensive and error-prone manual endeavor that does not scale well. To alleviate these issues, in this paper, we propose a model-driven approach for developing families of RL training environments. To obtain the family of environments, we develop an approach and prototype tool. In our approach, a hybrid genetic algorithm - a combination of population-based global search and heuristic local search - generates environment families. Mutations and constraints are expressed as model transformations and are operationalized into a search process by a state-of-the-art model transformation engine. We demonstrate the soundness of our approach in a wildfire mitigation scenario and curriculum learning - a particular learning paradigm that relies on environment families.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a model-driven approach that uses a hybrid genetic algorithm with model transformations to automatically generate families of reinforcement learning training environments, demonstrated through a wildfire mitigation and curriculum learning scenario.
AI.motivation: Developing families of RL training environments is currently a labor-intensive, error-prone manual process that does not scale well, yet most realistic RL problems require training across multiple similar but slightly different environments to converge.
AI.method: A model-driven approach combining a hybrid genetic algorithm (population-based global search + heuristic local search) with model transformations and constraints expressed via a state-of-the-art model transformation engine to generate environment families automatically.
AI.result: The approach's soundness is demonstrated in a wildfire mitigation scenario and curriculum learning paradigm, showing it can generate environment families effectively.
AI.conclusion: The model-driven, search-based approach can automate and scale the development of RL training environment families, reducing manual effort and errors in the process.

title: Gaussian Process Prior Variational Autoencoder for Endoscopic Videos
summary:
Endoscopic video analysis is essential for gastrointestinal diagnosis and computer-assisted interventions, but video sequences are routinely degraded by specular reflections, motion artifacts, and missing frames. These transient corruptions can distract clinicians, reduce image interpretability, and disrupt downstream tasks such as 3D reconstruction and navigation. Effective restoration therefore requires methods that exploit temporal continuity rather than treating frames in isolation. We introduce a Gaussian Process Prior Variational Autoencoder (GPVAE) framework for endoscopic video restoration that replaces the standard factorized latent prior with a temporal Gaussian process prior, enabling interpolation of missing frames with uncertainty-aware reconstruction.
  The framework combines endoscopy-specific encoders, including a convolutional EndoVAE backbone and pretrained Vision Transformer encoders from GastroNet-5M, with two scalable GP approximations: Hierarchical Prior Approximation (HPA) and Sparse Precision Approximation (SPA). Specular reflections are handled using a DUCKNet-based masking pipeline that excludes corrupted pixels from the reconstruction objective. On the C3VDv2 colonoscopy dataset, the best GPVAE variants reduced image reconstruction RMSE by 21.9\% on average, and by up to 26.1\%, relative to matched VAE baselines. Downstream trajectory RMSE was reduced by 12.7\% on average across classical visual odometry and a pretrained PoseNet, at an average increase of 27.3\% in training time per epoch. Finally, the GP posterior provides per-frame uncertainty estimates that reflect temporal support and offer a confidence signal for restored frames.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Gaussian Process Prior Variational Autoencoder (GPVAE) framework for endoscopic video restoration that replaces standard factorized latent priors with a temporal Gaussian process prior, enabling interpolation of missing frames with uncertainty-aware reconstruction. It combines endoscopy-specific encoders (convolutional EndoVAE and pretrained Vision Transformers from GastroNet-5M) with two scalable GP approximations (HPA and SPA), and handles specular reflections via a DUCKNet-based masking pipeline. On C3VDv2, GPVAE reduces reconstruction RMSE by 21.9-26.1% and downstream trajectory RMSE by 12.7% at a 27.3% training time cost.
AI.motivation: Endoscopic video sequences are routinely degraded by specular reflections, motion artifacts, and missing frames, which distract clinicians, reduce image interpretability, and disrupt downstream tasks like 3D reconstruction and navigation. Existing restoration methods that treat frames in isolation fail to exploit temporal continuity, motivating the need for a temporally-aware restoration framework.
AI.method: The GPVAE framework replaces the standard factorized latent prior in VAEs with a temporal Gaussian process prior. It uses endoscopy-specific encoders (convolutional EndoVAE backbone and pretrained Vision Transformer encoders from GastroNet-5M) and two scalable GP approximations: Hierarchical Prior Approximation (HPA) and Sparse Precision Approximation (SPA). Specular reflections are handled via a DUCKNet-based masking pipeline that excludes corrupted pixels from the reconstruction objective.
AI.result: On C3VDv2 colonoscopy dataset, GPVAE variants reduced image reconstruction RMSE by 21.9% on average (up to 26.1%) relative to VAE baselines. Downstream trajectory RMSE was reduced by 12.7% on average across classical visual odometry and PoseNet, at an average 27.3% increase in training time per epoch.
AI.conclusion: The GP posterior provides per-frame uncertainty estimates reflecting temporal support, offering a confidence signal for restored frames. The framework effectively exploits temporal continuity for endoscopic video restoration with modest computational overhead.

title: Think Again or Think Longer? Selective Verification for Budget-Aware Reasoning
summary: Test-time reasoning is increasingly used as a serving-time control knob, but extra reasoning is not uniformly valuable: it can repair failed attempts, waste compute on already-correct answers, or introduce harmful answer changes. We study this as a deployment allocation problem rather than a new-verifier problem. We introduce \sevra, Selective Verification for Reasoning Allocation, a serving-layer controller that decides whether to preserve a frozen solver's initial answer or invoke active verification. Using a frozen Qwen3-4B solver, we log intervention outcomes and train recoverability-aware gates from serving-visible attempt state. On \mathfive, selective verification reaches 76.3\% accuracy, compared with 75.5\% for always verifying, while reducing post-generation tokens by 26.8\% and harmful flips from 2.2\% to 1.0\%. However, an 8,192-token initial solve reaches 76.0\% accuracy with 28\% fewer total model tokens, showing that selective recovery is useful but not the best tested cost frontier. In frozen transfer to \gsm, the selective policy verifies only 3.0\% of examples, improves accuracy from 93.4\% to 94.5\%, and reduces verification tokens by 91.2\% relative to always verifying; again, a longer initial solve matches its accuracy with fewer realized tokens. On CommonsenseQA, always-on verification hurts, while Self-Consistency@5 improves accuracy at about five times the realized token cost. The resulting deployment rule is: tune the initial budget first, then use selective recovery when explicit checks, bounded retries, auditability, or regression-risk control matter.

AI.tldr: SEVRA is a serving-layer controller for test-time reasoning that selectively decides when to verify a frozen solver's initial answer versus invoking active verification, achieving better accuracy-cost tradeoffs than always-verifying, though tuning the initial solve budget often yields better cost frontiers.
AI.motivation: Extra reasoning during test-time is not uniformly valuable—it can fix errors, waste compute on correct answers, or introduce harmful answer flips. The authors frame this as a deployment allocation problem (not a verifier design problem), aiming to decide when to verify rather than how to verify.
AI.method: SEVRA (Selective Verification for Reasoning Allocation) trains recoverability-aware gates from serving-visible attempt state, logging intervention outcomes from a frozen Qwen3-4B solver to decide whether to preserve the initial answer or invoke active verification.
AI.result: On MATH-500: 76.3% accuracy (vs 75.5% always-verify), 26.8% fewer post-generation tokens, harmful flips reduced from 2.2% to 1.0%. On GSM8K: verifies only 3.0% of examples, improves accuracy 93.4%→94.5%, reduces verification tokens by 91.2%. On CommonsenseQA, always-on verification hurts accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The optimal deployment rule is to first tune the initial compute budget, then supplement with selective recovery when explicit checks, bounded retries, auditability, or regression-risk control are required. Longer initial solves often match selective verification's accuracy with fewer total tokens.

title: Interactive Pareto navigation for deep multi-task learning
summary: In multi-task learning, handling an increasing number of objectives can quickly become challenging, both in terms of the computational resources and the decision maker's capacity to choose appropriate trade-offs. A widely used approach is thus to aggregate the individual losses in a single loss function by a weighted sum. This often fails to capture either the decision maker's preferences as a result of the shape of the Pareto front, or requires multiple adjustments and computations which becomes prohibitively expensive in deep learning applications. To address these issues, we introduce a novel framework, Preference Pareto Exploration (PPE), which enforces the decision maker's preferences while accounting for the geometry of the Pareto set in an interactive exploration process. PPE is based on a predictor-corrector method that performs predictor steps tangential to the manifold of Pareto-optimal solutions, following the decision maker's preference. The subsequent corrector step results in a new trade-off reflecting this preference.
