AI.method: (1) Collect and preprocess heterogeneous web data (structured, semi-structured, unstructured) into unified feature representations. (2) Transform representations into a dynamic semantic graph with attention-enhanced graph embeddings to capture local relevance and global context. (3) Apply adaptive multi-agent reinforcement learning using attention-aware semantic states to optimize web actions (recommendation, navigation, service adaptation). (4) Integrate continuous online feedback to update graph representations and policies in real time.
AI.result: MGAR-WIES achieved 80% accuracy, outperforming existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed system effectively addresses semantic understanding and adaptability challenges in web intelligent enhancement, with sustained performance through real-time feedback integration.

title: FUSE: Frequency-domain Unification and Spectral Energy Alignment for Multi-modal Object Re-Identification
summary: Despite significant progress in multi-modal Re-Identification (ReID), existing methods tend to emphasize low-frequency cues. Consequently, they focus on attributes such as color, illumination, and coarse appearance, while overlooking mid and high-frequency structures that encode geometric, textural, and identity-discriminative details. This imbalance leads to incomplete spectral representations and unstable cross-modal alignment. To overcome these limitations, we introduce FUSE, a frequency-domain framework that reformulates multi-modal ReID as a two-stage process of spectral disentanglement and energy alignment. The proposed Spectral Decomposition Module (SDM) adaptively partitions features into low, mid, and high-frequency subspaces, enabling hierarchical spectral modeling. The Cross-Modal Alignment Module (CAM) further enforces energy alignment and subspace complementarity across modalities via frequency-consistency regularization. In addition, FUSE incorporates learnable frequency modulation to enhance robustness under varying illumination and heterogeneous sensor conditions. Extensive experiments on RGBNT201, RGBNT100, and MSVR310 show that FUSE achieves 9.1\% mAP and 9.5\% Rank-1 improvements, establishing an interpretable frequency-domain paradigm for multi-modal representation learning.

AI.tldr: FUSE is a frequency-domain multi-modal ReID framework that decomposes features into low, mid, and high-frequency subspaces via a Spectral Decomposition Module and aligns them across modalities using a Cross-Modal Alignment Module with energy and frequency-consistency regularization. It achieves 9.1% mAP and 9.5% Rank-1 gains on RGBNT201, RGBNT100, and MSVR310 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-modal ReID methods overemphasize low-frequency cues (color, illumination, coarse appearance) while neglecting mid and high-frequency structures that capture geometric, textural, and identity-discriminative details, leading to incomplete spectral representations and unstable cross-modal alignment.
AI.method: FUSE introduces a two-stage pipeline: (1) Spectral Decomposition Module (SDM) adaptively partitions features into low, mid, and high-frequency subspaces for hierarchical spectral modeling; (2) Cross-Modal Alignment Module (CAM) enforces energy alignment and subspace complementarity across modalities via frequency-consistency regularization, with learnable frequency modulation for robustness under varying illumination and heterogeneous sensors.
AI.result: 9.1% mAP improvement and 9.5% Rank-1 improvement on RGBNT201, RGBNT100, and MSVR310 datasets.
AI.conclusion: FUSE establishes an interpretable frequency-domain paradigm for multi-modal representation learning, demonstrating that explicit spectral decomposition and alignment significantly outperform traditional low-frequency-biased approaches.

title: RACL: Reasoning-Agent Control Layers for Continuous Metaheuristic Learning
summary: This paper introduces RACL, a Reasoning-Agent Control Layer for metaheuristics. RACL places a reasoning agent above an existing optimizer. The agent does not replace the optimizer and does not modify business constraints. Instead, it controls the optimizer's internal search behavior by observing operational memory, reasoning over past behavior, formulating bounded hypotheses, testing interventions, evaluating outcomes, applying guardrails, consolidating useful policies and explaining its decisions. The experiment uses vehicle routing as a testbed, but the contribution is not a new routing solver, a particular ALNS configuration or a specific set of routing rules. The contribution is the RACL method: a way for a reasoning agent to discover, validate, consolidate and explain algorithmic control rules for a metaheuristic. In the current experimental setting, RACL improves or ties the Operational Memory Policy in 21 of 21 feasible cases and improves or ties a non-reasoning Stagnation-Triggered Policy in 18 of 21 feasible cases, with an average RACL vs STP cost delta of -0.641%. In the Sevilla-9/10 runtime sample, RACL improves average cost by -8.337% versus Fixed and -1.605% versus STP without showing material computational overhead. During the proof-of-concept, Codex was used as an in-the-loop reasoning agent observing executions, interpreting logs and proposing live bounded interventions. The policy proxy was later used only to make quantitative evaluation reproducible.

AI.tldr: RACL is a reasoning-agent control layer that sits above a metaheuristic optimizer, observes its behavior, formulates hypotheses, tests interventions, and consolidates control policies—demonstrated on vehicle routing where it outperforms baselines in 21/21 and 18/21 feasible cases with negligible overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing metaheuristic optimizers lack adaptive, explainable control over their internal search behavior; manual tuning or fixed policies cannot dynamically reason about search dynamics or consolidate reusable control knowledge.
AI.method: RACL places a reasoning agent (Codex-based in the proof-of-concept) above the optimizer; the agent observes operational memory, reasons about past behavior, formulates bounded hypotheses, tests interventions, evaluates outcomes, applies guardrails, consolidates useful policies, and explains decisions—without modifying the optimizer or business constraints.
AI.result: RACL improves/ties Operational Memory Policy in 21/21 feasible cases and improves/ties Stagnation-Triggered Policy in 18/21 feasible cases (avg cost delta -0.641% vs STP). On Sevilla-9/10, average cost improves -8.337% vs Fixed and -1.605% vs STP with no material computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: RACL provides a generalizable method for reasoning agents to discover, validate, consolidate, and explain algorithmic control rules for metaheuristics, demonstrated on vehicle routing but not limited to it.

title: Efficiently Representing Algorithms With Chain-of-Thought Transformers
summary: The increasing popularity of \emph{reasoning} models -- language models that output a series of reasoning or thought tokens before producing an answer -- is justified, in part, by theoretical results showing that chain-of-thought (CoT) transformers can simulate Turing machines, and thus perform arbitrary computation. However, the Turing machine, while suitable for complexity-theoretic analysis, is not convenient, intuitive, or efficient for discussing algorithms. Algorithms are typically designed and analyzed at a higher level of abstraction, captured by the \emph{Word RAM} model with random-access memory and unit-cost operations on $\bigO(\log n)$-bit words. As a result, Word RAM algorithms can be substantially more efficient than their Turing machine counterparts, raising the question: \emph{Can CoT transformers efficiently simulate Word RAM algorithms?} For instance, can they sort $n$ items in $\bigO(n \log n)$ steps or run Dijkstra's algorithm in $\bigO(E + V \log V)$ steps? We answer affirmatively, up to poly-logarithmic overhead. We first establish this for finite-precision transformers with poly-logarithmic width and rightmost unique hard attention, then strengthen the result to two more practical settings with finite width and log-precision: \emph{continuous} CoT, where reasoning takes the form of vectors rather than tokens, and a \emph{hybrid} architecture in which transformer layers sit atop a recurrent (linear RNN) layer. In all three cases, we find that CoT \emph{can} efficiently simulate any Word RAM algorithm with only a poly-logarithmic overhead in $n$. This overhead reduces to log-square when the Word RAM has a ``flat'' instruction set, and only logarithmic for multiplication-free flat instructions -- in stark contrast to known CoT simulations of Turing machines, which require quadratic overhead over Word RAM.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that chain-of-thought transformers can efficiently simulate Word RAM algorithms (like sorting or Dijkstra's) with only poly-logarithmic overhead, which is much more efficient than Turing machine simulations that require quadratic overhead.
AI.motivation: While prior work shows CoT transformers can simulate Turing machines (enabling arbitrary computation), Turing machines are inefficient for analyzing real algorithms. Word RAM is the standard model for algorithm design with unit-cost operations, so the authors ask whether CoT transformers can efficiently simulate Word RAM algorithms.
AI.method: The authors prove that CoT transformers can simulate Word RAM algorithms with poly-logarithmic overhead in three settings: (1) finite-precision transformers with poly-logarithmic width and rightmost unique hard attention, (2) continuous CoT (vector reasoning instead of tokens), and (3) a hybrid architecture with transformer layers atop a linear RNN layer.
AI.result: CoT transformers can simulate any Word RAM algorithm with only poly-logarithmic overhead. The overhead reduces to log-squared for flat instruction sets, and logarithmic for multiplication-free flat instructions. This is a stark improvement over the quadratic overhead required for Turing machine simulations.
AI.conclusion: Chain-of-thought transformers are not just Turing-complete but can efficiently simulate the Word RAM model, meaning they can run practical algorithms like sorting in O(n log n) steps or Dijkstra's in O(E + V log V) steps, bridging the gap between theoretical computability and algorithmic efficiency.

title: See-and-Reach: Precise Vision-Language Navigation for UAVs within the Field of View
summary: UAV Vision-Language Navigation (UAV-VLN) is typically formulated as a holistic search-and-reach problem, where long-range target discovery and final target approach are optimized and evaluated jointly. This formulation makes it difficult to assess a critical capability of aerial embodied agents, namely whether a UAV can accurately ground a visible target and translate vision-language evidence into precise 3D motion once the target enters its field of view. To address this limitation, we introduce UAV-VLN-FOV, a target-visible navigation task that isolates the see-and-reach stage and enables a more diagnostic evaluation of terminal reaching ability. We further propose 3DG-VLN, a vision-language waypoint prediction framework guided by dynamic 3D direction cues to enhance fine-grained visual grounding and spatial direction alignment for precise target reaching. Specifically, 3DG-VLN adaptively processes high-resolution front-view and downward-view observations to preserve fine-grained visual and geometric details for target grounding. It also updates the target-relative direction online during closed-loop navigation, allowing the agent to maintain spatial alignment with the target and reduce accumulated direction drift. To support this task, we construct a dedicated high-resolution benchmark which contains 2,717 trajectories with target-oriented high-level instructions, high-resolution front-view and downward-view egocentric observations, and continuous 3D waypoint annotations. Experiments show that 3DG-VLN outperforms competitive UAV-VLN baselines, achieving a 13.82\% improvement in success rate. Real-world trials further demonstrate the potential of 3DG-VLN for practical see-and-reach navigation. The source code and benchmark are available at https://github.com/xuefanfu/3DG-VLN.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces UAV-VLN-FOV, a target-visible navigation task isolating the see-and-reach stage for diagnostic evaluation of terminal reaching ability, and proposes 3DG-VLN, a vision-language waypoint prediction framework guided by dynamic 3D direction cues that outperforms baselines by 13.82% in success rate.
AI.motivation: Existing UAV-VLN formulations jointly optimize long-range target discovery and final target approach, making it difficult to assess whether a UAV can accurately ground a visible target and translate vision-language evidence into precise 3D motion once the target enters its field of view.
AI.method: 1) UAV-VLN-FOV task: isolates the see-and-reach stage for diagnostic evaluation. 2) 3DG-VLN framework: adaptively processes high-resolution front-view and downward-view observations for fine-grained visual/geometric details, and updates target-relative direction online during closed-loop navigation to maintain spatial alignment and reduce direction drift. 3) A dedicated benchmark with 2,717 trajectories, high-level instructions, high-resolution egocentric observations, and continuous 3D waypoint annotations.
AI.result: 3DG-VLN outperforms competitive UAV-VLN baselines with a 13.82% improvement in success rate, and real-world trials demonstrate practical see-and-reach navigation capability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed task and framework enable more diagnostic evaluation and precise target reaching in UAV-VLN, with source code and benchmark publicly available.

title: BIM-Edit: Benchmarking Large Language Models for IFC-Based Building Information Modeling
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to computer-aided design (CAD) to generate design artifacts from textual instructions. In engineering practice, this requires more than creating new geometry, models must also understand existing scenes, edit them correctly, and preserve semantics and relations. However, many CAD benchmarks focus on creating new models rather than editing existing ones, and mostly evaluate geometric correctness. We introduce BIM-Edit, a benchmark for evaluating LLMs on natural-language editing of Building Information Models (BIM) represented in the Industry Foundation Classes (IFC) format. BIM provides a challenging testbed because building models encode geometry together with semantic and relational structure. BIM-Edit contains 324 editing tasks spanning 11 realistic building models and 36 synthetic scenes. Tasks are expressed using three instruction categories - direct, spatial, and topological - covering both explicit and scene-grounded edits. We evaluate outputs along three dimensions: geometric accuracy, semantic validity, and topological consistency. Across evaluated LLMs, the best-performing model achieves only 49.5% average score across the three metrics, and no model fully solves more than 3.4% of tasks. These results demonstrate a substantial gap between current LLM capabilities and the requirements of structured engineering design workflows.

AI.tldr: BIM-Edit is a benchmark for evaluating LLMs on editing Building Information Models (IFC format) through natural language instructions. It includes 324 tasks across 11 realistic and 36 synthetic building models, testing geometric, semantic, and topological correctness. The best LLM scores only 49.5% average and no model fully solves more than 3.4% of tasks, revealing a major gap in LLM capabilities for structured engineering design.
AI.motivation: Existing CAD benchmarks focus primarily on creating new geometry rather than editing existing models, and mostly evaluate geometric correctness. There is a need to assess LLMs on understanding, editing, and preserving semantics and relations in structured engineering models like BIM.
AI.method: The authors constructed BIM-Edit with 324 editing tasks across 11 realistic building models and 36 synthetic scenes. Tasks are expressed in three instruction categories: direct, spatial, and topological. Outputs are evaluated along three dimensions: geometric accuracy, semantic validity, and topological consistency.
AI.result: The best-performing LLM achieved only 49.5% average score across the three metrics. No model fully solved more than 3.4% of the tasks, indicating a substantial performance gap.
AI.conclusion: Current LLM capabilities are insufficient for structured engineering design workflows that require understanding, editing, and preserving semantic and relational structures in BIM models.

title: Efficient Neural Network Model Selection for Few-Class Application Datasets
summary: While much effort has focused on developing and benchmarking high-performance neural networks, less attention has been given to how dataset properties, known to practitioners, can guide efficient model selection. Neural models are typically evaluated on datasets with thousands of classes, yet many real-world applications involve fewer than ten. To address this understudied but common setting, we develop a measure of classification difficulty based on data-side properties and show how it enables more efficient model selection for few-class datasets, where traditional approaches are less effective. We term this phenomenon "few-class distinctiveness". Our metric allows comparison of models and datasets 6 to 29$\times$ faster than repeated training and testing. Leveraging this insight, we extend scaled model families below the smallest published models, achieving greater efficiency at similar accuracy, for example models up to 42% smaller than YOLOv5-nano for a mobile robot task. Targeting resource-constrained applications, we demonstrate few-class model selection across mobile robot, drone, and IoT scenarios, highlighting practical gains in efficiency without sacrificing performance.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a measure of classification difficulty based on dataset properties to enable efficient model selection for few-class (≤10 classes) settings, achieving 6-29× faster comparison than traditional methods and enabling smaller, more efficient models (up to 42% smaller than YOLOv5-nano) for resource-constrained applications.
AI.motivation: Most neural network research focuses on datasets with thousands of classes, but many real-world applications have fewer than ten classes. Traditional model selection approaches are ineffective and inefficient for this understudied few-class regime.
AI.method: The authors develop a data-side metric measuring classification difficulty based on dataset properties, enabling fast model-dataset comparison (6-29× faster than repeated training/testing). They also extend scaled model families below existing smallest published models.
AI.result: The proposed metric enables 6-29× faster model selection. Extended models achieve up to 42% smaller size than YOLOv5-nano at similar accuracy. Demonstrated across mobile robot, drone, and IoT scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Few-class distinctiveness is a valuable phenomenon that can be leveraged for efficient model selection in resource-constrained applications, reducing model size without sacrificing performance.

title: Variable-Length Tokenization via Learnable Global Merging for Diffusion Transformers
summary: Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) have become dominant in visual synthesis, but their quality-compute trade-off is largely constrained by the tokenizer's fixed compression ratio. Variable-length tokenizers (VLTs) promise adaptive compression by varying token counts, allowing diffusion models to flexibly balance quality and compute. However, conventional VLTs modulate length by truncating ordered token sequences, which makes token semantics depend on token position and breaks representational alignment across lengths. This leads to a cross-length shift in the latent distribution that hinders a single variable-length diffusion model from operating effectively. To address this, we propose a novel variable-length tokenizer that modulates length by merging tokens. We show that encouraging similar tokens to merge enables direct cross-length representation alignment when the diffusion transformer operates according to the merging pattern. Since conventional merging methods are data-dependent, making the merging pattern inaccessible during generation, we introduce learnable global merging, which is data-independent, to ensure compatibility with diffusion transformers. On ImageNet 256$\times$256 generation, our merging-based variable-length tokenizer integrated with a diffusion transformer achieves a superior gFID-compute trade-off compared to prior VLT methods. Code is available at [this https URL](https://github.com/movinghoon/lgm)

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a merging-based variable-length tokenizer for Latent Diffusion Models that replaces truncation-based methods, achieving alignment across different token lengths through global merging, leading to better quality-compute trade-offs in image generation.
AI.motivation: Traditional LDMs are limited by fixed-compression tokenizers. Variable-length tokenizers (VLTs) that truncate ordered tokens suffer from cross-length distribution shift because token semantics become position-dependent, preventing a single diffusion model from working effectively across lengths.
AI.method: The paper introduces a variable-length tokenizer that modulates length by merging similar tokens rather than truncating sequences. To make merging data-independent (since conventional merging is data-dependent and inaccessible during generation), they propose learnable global merging. The diffusion transformer operates according to the merging pattern, enabling direct cross-length representation alignment.
AI.result: On ImageNet 256×256 generation, the merging-based variable-length tokenizer integrated with a diffusion transformer achieves a superior gFID-compute trade-off compared to prior VLT methods.
AI.conclusion: Merging-based variable-length tokenization with learnable global merging effectively solves the cross-length alignment issue in VLTs, enabling flexible quality-compute trade-offs in diffusion models.

title: Modularity-Free Conflict-Averse Training for Generalized PINNs
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have become a powerful framework for solving PDEs by embedding physical laws into differentiable objectives. Despite their advances, training PINNs remains fragile: recent conflict-averse optimization schemes alleviate gradient interference between residual and boundary losses, but we show that their effectiveness deteriorates as model capacity increases. In this paper, we identify a capacity-induced failure mode, where overparameterized networks undergo functional modularity, self-partitioning into task-exclusive modules that suppress cross-objective interaction and hinder convergence toward Pareto-stationary points. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework, Modular-Sparsity Synchronization (ModSync), which integrates structural optimization into conflict-averse training by penalizing task-exclusive connections while preserving interaction-promoting pathways. Extensive experiments across diverse PDE benchmarks demonstrate that ModSync consistently prevents capacity-driven failures, sustains robust cross-objective coupling, and achieves state-of-the-art accuracy. Codes are available at \url{https://github.com/heejokong/ModSync}.

AI.tldr: PINNs suffer from a capacity-induced failure mode where overparameterized networks self-partition into task-exclusive modules, hindering convergence. ModSync resolves this by penalizing task-exclusive connections while preserving interaction-promoting pathways, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy.
AI.motivation: Conflict-averse optimization schemes for PINNs deteriorate as model capacity increases due to functional modularity—overparameterized networks self-partition into task-exclusive modules that suppress cross-objective interaction and hinder Pareto-stationary convergence.
AI.method: Modular-Sparsity Synchronization (ModSync) integrates structural optimization into conflict-averse training by penalizing task-exclusive connections while preserving interaction-promoting pathways.
AI.result: ModSync consistently prevents capacity-driven failures, sustains robust cross-objective coupling, and achieves state-of-the-art accuracy across diverse PDE benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: ModSync effectively addresses the capacity-induced failure mode in PINNs through sparsity synchronization, enabling robust multi-objective optimization in overparameterized regimes.

title: OnDeFog: Online Decision Transformer under Frame Dropping
summary: In challenging real-world reinforcement learning applications, communication delays or sensor failures often cause frame dropping, in which the agent cannot receive the dropped states and associated rewards. To address the performance degradation caused by frame dropping, the Decision Transformer under Random Frame Dropping (DeFog) was developed by incorporating additional mechanisms into the decision transformer to tackle frame dropping. Although DeFog can mitigate performance degradation in frame-dropping environments, since DeFog is an offline learning method, it struggles to effectively generalize to novel states not adequately represented in the training dataset. In this study, we propose OnDeFog, which integrates the mechanisms in DeFog with the online decision transformer (ODT), an online reinforcement learning method that learns policies through direct environmental interaction. Comprehensive experimental evaluation demonstrates that our proposed OnDeFog achieves superior performance compared to ODT in environments characterized by high dropping frame rate and outperforms DeFog on datasets containing a large amount of low-reward data.

AI.tldr: OnDeFog integrates DeFog's frame-dropping mechanisms with the online decision transformer (ODT) to improve reinforcement learning performance under random frame dropping, outperforming both ODT in high-drop-rate environments and DeFog on low-reward datasets.
AI.motivation: Real-world RL applications suffer from frame dropping (due to communication delays or sensor failures), causing performance degradation. DeFog addresses this but is an offline method that cannot generalize well to novel states not in the training dataset.
AI.method: OnDeFog combines DeFog's frame-dropping handling mechanisms with the online decision transformer (ODT), an online RL method that learns policies through direct environmental interaction.
AI.result: OnDeFog achieves superior performance compared to ODT in high frame-dropping-rate environments, and outperforms DeFog on datasets with large amounts of low-reward data.
AI.conclusion: Integrating online learning with frame-dropping mitigation mechanisms yields better generalization and performance than purely offline or purely online methods in frame-dropping scenarios.

title: The Hidden Evolution of Disguised Visual Context inside the VLM
summary: Visual tokens enter Large Language Models (LLMs) as raw, foreign signals. How they are transformed into meaningful representations and interact with the language space depends entirely on the integration architecture. Whether by treating visual tokens as in-context prompts within the input sequence or injecting them directly into the LLM's intermediate layers. A controlled comparison and understanding of how these architectural choices affect visual information and its internal transformation to integrate with the LLM remains underexplored. We provide a fair comparison by evaluating in-context and layer-wise injection VLM integration paradigms under identical training conditions across single image, multi-image, and video benchmarks. In doing so, we uncover a hidden evolution where visual tokens enter the LLM as disguised visual context, raw representations lacking linguistic structure, but are progressively reshaped depending on the integration paradigm, each capturing fundamentally different frequency characteristics of the visual signal. We show that this evolution inside the LLM determines what visual features the VLM can utilize effectively, how visual representations align with the language space, and ultimately how each paradigm performs across different tasks. We further demonstrate that attention allocation alone is insufficient, and that performance is driven by the quality of visual representations at each layer.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a fair comparison of in-context vs. layer-wise injection VLM integration paradigms, revealing that visual tokens evolve from raw representations into linguistically structured signals differently depending on the architecture, which determines visual feature utilization and task performance.
AI.motivation: How visual tokens are transformed into meaningful representations and interact with the language space depends on the VLM integration architecture, but a controlled comparison of these architectural choices and their effect on visual information transformation remains underexplored.
AI.method: Fair comparison of in-context (treating visual tokens as input prompts) and layer-wise injection (injecting visual tokens into intermediate LLM layers) VLM integration paradigms under identical training conditions across single image, multi-image, and video benchmarks.
AI.result: Visual tokens enter LLMs as disguised visual context without linguistic structure but are progressively reshaped depending on the integration paradigm; each paradigm captures different frequency characteristics of visual signals; attention allocation alone is insufficient for performance.
AI.conclusion: The evolution of visual representations inside the LLM determines which visual features the VLM can utilize effectively, how visual representations align with the language space, and how each paradigm performs across tasks; performance is driven by the quality of visual representations at each layer rather than attention allocation alone.

title: Implicit Semantic-Aware Communication Based on Hypergraph Reasoning
comment: This work is accepted at IEEE Transactions on Communications
summary: Semantic-aware communication has emerged as a transformative paradigm for next-generation communication systems, shifting the fundamental goal from transmitting bit-level symbols to reliably recovering and understanding the semantic meaning of information. Previous studies have demonstrated that representing the semantic content of source messages as graph-based structures can significantly improve communication efficiency and the accuracy of semantic inference at the receiver. However, existing solutions typically employ graphs that capture only pairwise relationships, thereby neglecting higher-order implicit correlations commonly observed in real-world scenarios, such as group interactions, multi-entity associations, and complex relational contexts. This limitation reduces semantic expressiveness and makes semantic inference susceptible to ambiguity and performance degradation, particularly under noisy or corrupted channel conditions. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel hypergraph-based implicit semantic reasoning framework, HISR, which leverages hypergraphs to represent complex multi-entity relationships among semantic knowledge entities. In HISR, entities and their associated higher-order relations are mapped into dedicated semantic subspaces tailored to distinct relational contexts. This design not only disentangles diverse semantic interactions to mitigate the over-smoothing effects commonly found in traditional graph embedding methods but also enables robust semantic inference even when partial information loss occurs during transmission. Numerical results show that the proposed HISR achieves up to a 36.6% improvement in implicit semantic interpretation accuracy over the state-of-the-art benchmarks.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes HISR, a hypergraph-based implicit semantic reasoning framework that captures higher-order multi-entity relationships in semantic-aware communication, achieving up to 36.6% improvement in semantic interpretation accuracy over existing graph-based methods.
AI.motivation: Existing semantic-aware communication systems use graph-based structures that only capture pairwise relationships, neglecting higher-order implicit correlations (e.g., group interactions, multi-entity associations). This limitation reduces semantic expressiveness and makes inference susceptible to ambiguity and performance degradation, especially under noisy channel conditions.
AI.method: HISR uses hypergraphs to represent complex multi-entity relationships among semantic knowledge entities. Entities and their higher-order relations are mapped into dedicated semantic subspaces tailored to distinct relational contexts, which disentangles diverse semantic interactions and mitigates over-smoothing effects in traditional graph embedding methods.
AI.result: The proposed HISR achieves up to a 36.6% improvement in implicit semantic interpretation accuracy over state-of-the-art benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Hypergraph-based semantic representation enables more robust semantic inference under noisy channels and partial information loss by capturing higher-order implicit correlations that traditional graph-based methods miss.

title: Federated Bilevel Performative Prediction
summary: Federated bilevel optimization is widely used for nested learning problems across distributed clients, such as federated hyperparameter tuning and meta-learning under privacy and communication constraints. Most existing formulations assume fixed client data distributions, which can be violated by performativity, where deployed decisions reshape client behavior and data collection, inducing client-specific, decision-dependent distribution shift. We study federated bilevel performative prediction, where both upper-level (UL) and lower-level (LL) objectives are evaluated under client-dependent, decision-dependent distributions. We formalize the federated bilevel performatively stable (FBPS) point under a decoupled-risk perspective and provide sufficient conditions for its existence and uniqueness. We then develop two federated methods to compute the FBPS solution: FBi-RRM, which converges linearly under a contraction condition, and FBi-SGD, a communication-efficient stochastic method based on federated hypergradient estimation with convergence guarantees under diminishing step sizes when sensitivities are sufficiently small. Experiments on strategic regression and meta strategic classification validate the predicted stability thresholds and demonstrate improved meta-generalization over non-performative baselines, and CNN-based classification further demonstrates the practical effectiveness of the proposed methods in nonconvex neural network settings.

AI.tldr: This paper studies federated bilevel performative prediction, where both upper-level and lower-level objectives are evaluated under client-dependent, decision-dependent distributions. The authors formalize the federated bilevel performatively stable (FBPS) point, provide existence and uniqueness conditions, and propose two methods (FBi-RRM and FBi-SGD) to compute it. Experiments on strategic regression, meta strategic classification, and CNN-based classification validate the approach.
AI.motivation: Existing federated bilevel optimization assumes fixed client data distributions, but in practice, deployed decisions can reshape client behavior and data collection (performativity), inducing client-specific, decision-dependent distribution shifts. This work addresses this gap by studying federated bilevel performative prediction.
AI.method: The authors formalize the federated bilevel performatively stable (FBPS) point under a decoupled-risk perspective, establish sufficient conditions for its existence and uniqueness, and develop two algorithms: FBi-RRM (linear convergence under contraction) and FBi-SGD (communication-efficient stochastic method with hypergradient estimation and convergence guarantees under diminishing step sizes).
AI.result: Experiments on strategic regression and meta strategic classification validate predicted stability thresholds and demonstrate improved meta-generalization over non-performative baselines. CNN-based classification further shows effectiveness in nonconvex neural network settings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methods effectively handle performativity in federated bilevel optimization, achieving convergence guarantees and practical improvements in both convex and nonconvex settings.

title: Holo-World: Unified Camera, Object and Weather Control for Video World Model
summary: Video world models are moving toward preserving an observed world under controllable camera and object motion while allowing its environmental state to change. Yet these controls remain isolated, and weather generation typically relies on a source video or reconstructed scene that already specifies future structure. We study a first-frame-anchored source-to-state setting, where the model starts from a single image and follows explicit camera and object controls and an optional weather instruction, then generates a video that either preserves the source world or transfers it to a target weather state. To address these challenges, we first build HoloStateData, a state video dataset that turns diverse videos into unified control samples for camera, object, and weather supervision. Second, we introduce Holo-World, a unified controllable video world model that jointly controls scene from a single image. Its Unified Scene Adapter factorizes world preservation and weather transfer into distinct parameter subspaces, using rendered background, geometry buffers, and object controls to maintain controlled scene structure while modeling weather-dependent appearance and particle effects. Additionally, Scene-Weather Decomposed CFG guides scene and weather residuals separately, strengthening target weather effects without over-amplifying the full condition. Quantitative and qualitative experiments demonstrate that Holo-World maintains precise camera and object control with consistent scene structure while transferring scenes into diverse target weather state, outperforming video-to-video weather editing baselines on weather-state generation. Our project page is available at \url{https://xiangchenyin.github.io/Holo-World/}.

AI.tldr: Holo-World is a unified controllable video world model that generates videos from a single image with explicit camera/object controls and optional weather instructions, supporting both world preservation and weather transfer. It uses HoloStateData (curated dataset), Unified Scene Adapter (factorized parameter subspaces), and Scene-Weather Decomposed CFG for improved weather effects.
AI.motivation: Existing video world models treat camera/object controls separately, and weather generation typically requires source video or reconstructed scene as input. The goal is to enable first-frame-anchored source-to-state generation: from a single image with controls and optional weather instruction, produce a video that preserves or transfers weather state.
AI.method: (1) HoloStateData: dataset converting diverse videos into unified control samples with camera, object, and weather supervision. (2) Holo-World model with Unified Scene Adapter that factorizes world preservation vs. weather transfer into distinct parameter subspaces, using rendered background, geometry buffers, and object controls. (3) Scene-Weather Decomposed CFG that separately guides scene and weather residuals to enhance target weather without over-amplifying full condition.
AI.result: Holo-World achieves precise camera/object control with consistent scene structure while transferring scenes to diverse target weather states, outperforming video-to-video weather editing baselines on weather-state generation.
AI.conclusion: First-frame-anchored controllable world modeling with joint camera, object, and weather control is feasible, and factorized parameter subspaces with decomposed guidance improve weather transfer quality while preserving scene structure.

title: Apparent Psychological Profiles of Large Language Models are Largely a Measurement Artifact
summary: Psychological instruments designed for humans are increasingly used to assign large language models (LLMs) stable psychological profiles that affect their usability, safety assessment, and use as proxies for human participants in research. Using a formal psychometric framework, we show that these profiles are largely a measurement artifact. Administering a battery of personality and risk-preference instruments spanning self-reports and behavioral tasks to 56 instruction-tuned LLMs alongside large human reference samples, we report four findings. First, differences between models are driven not by the traits an instrument targets but by a directional response bias, a tendency to respond toward one end of the scale, or one labeled option, regardless of item content; a variance decomposition attributes 81-90% of between-model variation to this bias, against 9-16% in humans. Second, the bias declines with model capability but is not eliminated by it. Third, because bias rather than trait drives responding, an instrument's apparent reliability is almost entirely predicted by its response orthogonality, a term we coin for the proportion of items for which trait and bias point in opposite directions. Fourth, the profile a model appears to have shifts with the items used and can be manufactured through item selection. These results demonstrate that the apparent psychological profiles of LLMs are artifacts of the instrument used to measure them, not properties of the models themselves. As instruments borrowed from human psychology are rarely fully orthogonal and may inherently lack validity for LLMs, we call for dedicated assessments centered on response orthogonality.

AI.tldr: Psychological profiles assigned to LLMs via human instruments are largely measurement artifacts driven by directional response bias (81-90% of between-model variance) rather than genuine traits, with apparent reliability predicted by response orthogonality and profiles shiftable through item selection.
AI.motivation: To rigorously test whether psychological instruments designed for humans produce valid, trait-based profiles when applied to LLMs, given their increasing use for usability assessment, safety evaluation, and as human proxies in research.
AI.method: Administered a battery of personality and risk-preference instruments (self-reports and behavioral tasks) to 56 instruction-tuned LLMs alongside large human reference samples, then performed variance decomposition and introduced the concept of response orthogonality to analyze results.
AI.result: (1) 81-90% of between-model variance is due to directional response bias vs. 9-16% in humans; (2) bias declines with capability but persists; (3) apparent reliability is predicted by response orthogonality; (4) model profiles shift with item selection and can be manufactured.
AI.conclusion: LLMs' apparent psychological profiles are artifacts of measurement instruments, not model properties; existing human instruments lack validity for LLMs, necessitating dedicated assessments centered on response orthogonality.

title: Manifold Bandits: Bayesian Curriculum Learning over the Latent Geometry of Large Language Models
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) is a central approach for improving reasoning capabilities in large language models (LLMs), where training efficiency depends critically on how problems are sampled during optimization. Existing adaptive curriculum learning methods typically prioritize prompts of intermediate difficulty, treating problem selection as a standard bandit problem with independent arms and overlooking the structured, heterogeneous nature of the task space. In this work, we frame problem sampling as a manifold-structured bandit problem with endogenous non-stationarity: problems are related through the model's latent representation space, and sampling decisions can steer how learning signals evolve across that space. To operationalize this perspective, we introduce Bayesian Manifold Curriculum (BMC), a structure-aware framework that organizes problems into a hierarchical task tree and applies Bayesian learning to guide sampling. Empirically, we find that different sampling strategies induce non-trivial tradeoffs between productivity (learning signal), diversity (coverage of the task manifold), and utility (evaluation relevance). These results show that prioritizing difficulty alone is insufficient for strong downstream performance, highlighting the importance of incorporating structure and type-awareness into problem sampling.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Bayesian Manifold Curriculum (BMC), a structure-aware framework that models problem sampling in RL for LLMs as a manifold-structured bandit problem with endogenous non-stationarity, using a hierarchical task tree and Bayesian learning to guide sampling, showing that difficulty alone is insufficient for optimal performance.
AI.motivation: Existing adaptive curriculum learning methods treat problem selection as a standard bandit problem with independent arms, overlooking the structured, heterogeneous nature of the task space in RL for LLMs.
AI.method: BMC frames problem sampling as a manifold-structured bandit problem with endogenous non-stationarity, organizes problems into a hierarchical task tree, and applies Bayesian learning to guide sampling.
AI.result: Different sampling strategies induce non-trivial tradeoffs between productivity (learning signal), diversity (coverage of the task manifold), and utility (evaluation relevance). Prioritizing difficulty alone is insufficient for strong downstream performance.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating structure and type-awareness into problem sampling is crucial for effective RL-based training of LLMs.

title: EventVLA: Event-Driven Visual Evidence Memory for Long-Horizon Vision-Language-Action Policies
summary: Memory remains a critical bottleneck for long-horizon robotic manipulation, as standard Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies often fail when task-relevant cues become occluded or unobservable over time. While existing memory-augmented methods utilize historical context, they either suffer from severe information bottlenecks, incur high latency via decoupled dual systems, or rely on unselective buffers that accumulate massive visual redundancies. To address these limitations, we introduce EventVLA, an end-to-end framework founded on the concept of sparse visual evidence memory that comprises two core components: foundational visual anchors to retain initial and short-term contexts, and a dynamic Keyframe Evidence Memory (KEM) module. Specifically, KEM directly predicts future keyframe probabilities from the VLA's latent embeddings to autonomously capture and store sparse, task-critical visual events. This foresight-driven mechanism empowers the policy to dynamically evaluate the future causal utility of current observations, preserving transient visual evidence before it becomes unobservable. Furthermore, we propose RoboTwin-MeM, a diagnostic benchmark specifically designed to evaluate non-Markovian manipulation tasks with interactive visual evidence. Extensive evaluations show that across 17 memory-requiring simulation tasks and 4 real-world bimanual tasks, EventVLA achieves an average success rate improvement of +40% over state-of-the-art memory-augmented VLAs.

AI.tldr: EventVLA introduces sparse visual evidence memory with Keyframe Evidence Memory (KEM) that predicts future keyframe probabilities from latent embeddings, achieving +40% success rate improvement over state-of-the-art memory-augmented VLAs on memory-demanding manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Memory remains a critical bottleneck for long-horizon robotic manipulation; existing memory-augmented methods suffer from information bottlenecks, high latency, or unselective buffers with massive visual redundancies.
AI.method: EventVLA is an end-to-end framework with two core components: (1) foundational visual anchors for initial/short-term context, and (2) dynamic Keyframe Evidence Memory (KEM) that directly predicts future keyframe probabilities from the VLA's latent embeddings to autonomously capture sparse, task-critical visual events before they become unobservable. Also introduces RoboTwin-MeM, a diagnostic benchmark for non-Markovian manipulation tasks.
AI.result: Across 17 memory-requiring simulation tasks and 4 real-world bimanual tasks, EventVLA achieves an average success rate improvement of +40% over state-of-the-art memory-augmented VLAs.
AI.conclusion: EventVLA effectively addresses memory bottlenecks in VLA policies by using a foresight-driven mechanism to dynamically preserve transient visual evidence, significantly outperforming existing memory-augmented approaches.

title: Beyond Accuracy: Measuring Logical Compliance of Predictive Models
summary: Machine learning models are predominantly evaluated through predictive performance metrics such as ranking quality, prediction error, or classification accuracy. While these metrics effectively quantify how closely predictions match the ground truth, they do not assess whether model outputs respect predefined logical or domain-specific constraints. In high-stakes applications, including healthcare, finance, and autonomous systems, logical consistency can be as critical as predictive accuracy, yet no standard metric captures this dimension. We introduce the Rule Violation Score (RVS), a complementary evaluation metric that quantifies the extent to which a predictive model respects a given set of logical rules, independently of predictive accuracy. RVS treats hard rules (strict constraints) and soft rules (statistical regularities) differently, can be evaluated on any dataset and on any predictive model expressed over a relational vocabulary, and can be computed using SQL queries that are automatically generated for Horn rules. Beyond evaluating models, RVS can also evaluate the logical consistency of training datasets and help identify poorly defined rules. We evaluate RVS on three benchmarks covering knowledge graph link prediction and relational regression, including rule-based, embedding-based, and neuro-symbolic predictive models. Our results demonstrate that two models achieving comparable predictive accuracy can exhibit substantially different levels of logical compliance, revealing differences in model behavior that standard metrics fail to capture.

AI.tldr: The Rule Violation Score (RVS) is introduced as a novel evaluation metric that quantifies logical rule compliance of predictive models independently of accuracy, applicable across models and datasets via auto-generated SQL queries, revealing behavioral differences that standard metrics miss.
AI.motivation: Standard evaluation metrics (ranking quality, prediction error, classification accuracy) measure predictive accuracy but fail to assess whether model outputs respect logical or domain-specific constraints, which is critical for high-stakes applications like healthcare, finance, and autonomous systems.
AI.method: RVS treats hard rules (strict constraints) and soft rules (statistical regularities) separately, operates on any relational predictive model and dataset, and computes violations via automatically generated SQL queries for Horn rules.
AI.result: Evaluated on three benchmarks (knowledge graph link prediction and relational regression) across rule-based, embedding-based, and neuro-symbolic models; two models with comparable accuracy showed substantially different logical compliance.
AI.conclusion: RVS captures model behavior differences that standard metrics miss, offers a complementary dimension for model evaluation, and can also assess logical consistency of training data and identify poorly defined rules.

title: Learning universal approximations for partial differential equations with Physics-Informed Broad Learning System
summary: Partial differential equations (PDEs) play a central role in modeling complex physical, biological, and engineering systems. While traditional numerical solvers are robust, they often incur prohibitive computational costs due to mesh dependencies, whereas recent Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) offer a mesh-free alternative but frequently suffer from slow convergence and optimization instability. To bridge this gap, this article proposes the Physics-Informed Broad Learning System (PIBLS), a novel backpropagation-free framework that reformulates PDE solving as a direct least-squares optimization. We improved an algorithm within this framework to handle nonlinear PDEs efficiently and provide a rigorous mathematical proof establishing the universal approximation property of PIBLS for these equations. Experiments on linear and nonlinear PDEs demonstrate that PIBLS is one to three orders of magnitude faster than conventional PINNs while achieving significantly higher solution accuracy. This framework provides a computationally efficient paradigm for scientific machine learning, offering a practical, high-speed alternative for real-time simulation and design optimization tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Physics-Informed Broad Learning System (PIBLS), a backpropagation-free framework that solves PDEs via direct least-squares optimization, achieving 1-3 orders of magnitude speedup over PINNs with higher accuracy for both linear and nonlinear PDEs.
AI.motivation: Traditional numerical PDE solvers are computationally expensive due to mesh dependencies, while PINNs suffer from slow convergence and optimization instability. The goal is to develop a faster, more stable mesh-free alternative for PDE solving.
AI.method: PIBLS reformulates PDE solving as a direct least-squares optimization (backpropagation-free). An improved algorithm is introduced to handle nonlinear PDEs efficiently. A rigorous mathematical proof establishes the universal approximation property of PIBLS for nonlinear PDEs.
AI.result: PIBLS achieves 1-3 orders of magnitude faster computation than conventional PINNs while delivering significantly higher solution accuracy on both linear and nonlinear PDE benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: PIBLS provides a computationally efficient paradigm for scientific machine learning, serving as a practical, high-speed alternative for real-time simulation and design optimization tasks.

title: Augmenting Game AI with Deep Reinforcement Learning
comment: Vision paper, published in Conference on Games 2026
summary: Immersion in video games depends not only on graphics, audio, and game mechanics, but also on the quality of in-game characters. Producing believable characters, or game AI, remains a significant challenge as behavioral complexity is hard to capture with hand-coded systems. Game AI is a source of immersion and engagement; however, the limitations stemming from the challenges of creating game AI often lead to frustration and the breaking of the illusion of realism within the game. The introduction of machine learning models opens the door to creating more believable, authentic, and relatable characters in games. The promise is that they either learn from interacting with the game, or from player data, to develop true human-like behavior. In this paper, we envision more applications of reinforcement learning for game AI in the future. For this to materialize, current research limitations are prohibitive to broad deployment across game genres. Therefore, we propose a framework for training reinforcement learning models with a set of requirements in mind that are suited towards game AI and game development. We present examples of games with reinforcement learning-augmented game AI and describe the practicalities of deploying player-facing machine learning agents in modern games. Furthermore, we identify bottlenecks and hard problems in these areas, which we believe offer promising research directions to accelerate the adoption of machine learning in game AI for the video game industry.

AI.tldr: This paper discusses how reinforcement learning can improve game AI by creating more believable characters, proposes a framework for training RL models suited for game development, presents examples of RL-augmented game AI, and identifies research bottlenecks and promising directions for broader industry adoption.
AI.motivation: Hand-coded game AI systems fail to capture behavioral complexity, breaking immersion and the illusion of realism in games. Machine learning, particularly reinforcement learning, offers a path to more believable and human-like character behavior.
AI.method: The authors propose a framework for training reinforcement learning models with requirements tailored to game AI and game development, and present examples of games augmented with RL-based agents, describing deployment practicalities.
AI.result: The paper identifies current research limitations that prevent broad deployment of RL across game genres, and pinpoints bottlenecks and hard problems that need to be addressed for industry-wide adoption.
AI.conclusion: Reinforcement learning holds promise for advancing game AI, but significant research challenges remain. The proposed framework and identified directions aim to accelerate ML adoption in the video game industry.

title: An Information Theoretic Framework for Graph Novelty Generation via Latent Mixture Modeling
summary: We propose an information-theoretic framework for graph novelty generation, which aims to generate data that are distinct from existing patterns while preserving global structural consistency. Our approach embeds data into a latent space, models the latent distribution using finite mixture models, and generates novel samples by imposing explicit novelty and reliability conditions formulated in terms of description length. Specifically, novelty is enforced by requiring generated samples to be poorly explained by all existing mixture components, while reliability constrains their impact on the overall mixture structure under the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle. We provide a theoretical analysis showing that, with appropriate threshold choices, the probabilities of misclassifying non-novel or unreliable samples converge to zero with explicit rates. Experiments on synthetic and benchmark graph datasets demonstrate that the proposed method enables principled novelty generation with quantifiable risk.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an information-theoretic framework for generating novel graphs that are distinct from existing patterns while maintaining structural consistency, using latent space embedding, finite mixture models, and MDL-based novelty/reliability conditions with theoretical guarantees and empirical validation.
AI.motivation: To develop a principled method for generating graph-structured data that is genuinely novel (distinct from existing patterns) yet structurally reliable, with quantifiable guarantees on generation quality.
AI.method: The method embeds graphs into a latent space, models the latent distribution with finite mixture models, and generates novel samples under explicit novelty (poorly explained by existing components) and reliability (bounded impact on mixture structure) conditions formalized via description length and the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows that misclassification probabilities for non-novel or unreliable samples converge to zero with explicit rates under appropriate thresholds. Experiments on synthetic and benchmark graph datasets validate that the approach enables principled novelty generation with quantifiable risk.
AI.conclusion: The proposed information-theoretic framework successfully enables controlled, risk-quantifiable graph novelty generation, balancing novelty against structural reliability through MDL-based constraints.

title: Stitching and dimensionality effects on large artificially generated volume datasets
summary: Generating large images via deep learning requires patching input data to accommodate hardware memory limitations, then assembling output patches, a process that can introduce stitching artifacts when neighboring patches do not align at borders. While these artifacts are known to affect segmentation tasks, their impact on generative models for style-transfer remains poorly understood. We investigated three stitching approaches and two patch dimensionalities (2D vs 3D) using cycleGAN models trained on cryo-electron microscopy datasets. We evaluated both perceptual quality and performance on downstream mitochondria segmentation. Our key findings reveal that: (1) FID scores fail to detect subtle stitching artifacts that significantly impact downstream segmentation performance, (2) 3D models with artifact-free stitching marginally outperform 2D models on downstream tasks, though the improvement barely justifies the computational cost, and (3) 2D models train more stably due to larger batch sizes. Additionally, we demonstrate that ensembling predictions from three orthogonal directions can improve low-quality volumes but provides no benefit for high-quality outputs. These results demonstrate that maximizing generative model performance on large scientific datasets requires careful consideration and mitigation of stitching artifacts, and that perceptual metrics alone are insufficient for evaluating domain adaptation quality in biomedical imaging.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates stitching artifacts in large-image generation using cycleGAN for cryo-EM data, finding that FID scores fail to detect artifacts that degrade downstream segmentation, 3D models slightly outperform 2D but with high computational cost, and 2D models train more stably.
AI.motivation: Stitching artifacts from patching large images for deep learning are known to affect segmentation tasks, but their impact on generative models for style-transfer in biomedical imaging is poorly understood.
AI.method: Compared three stitching approaches and two patch dimensionalities (2D vs 3D) using cycleGAN models trained on cryo-electron microscopy datasets, evaluating perceptual quality and downstream mitochondria segmentation performance.
AI.result: (1) FID scores fail to detect subtle stitching artifacts that significantly impact downstream segmentation; (2) 3D models with artifact-free stitching marginally outperform 2D models but with disproportionate computational cost; (3) 2D models train more stably due to larger batch sizes; ensembling from three orthogonal directions helps low-quality volumes but not high-quality ones.
AI.conclusion: Maximizing generative model performance on large scientific datasets requires careful mitigation of stitching artifacts, and perceptual metrics alone are insufficient for evaluating domain adaptation quality in biomedical imaging.

title: Flow Map Denoisers: Traversing the Distortion-Perception Plane for Inverse Problems
summary: Image restoration faces a fundamental tradeoff: methods that minimize error produce blurry reconstructions, while those that maximize perceptual quality yield sharp but less faithful images. Existing approaches either commit to a single operating point on this distortion perception (DP) frontier or require paired-data supervision, auxiliary models, or hyperparameter tuning of the sampler to access different points. We show that flow map models, a recent extension of flow matching for few-step sampling that learns an average field, implicitly define a one-parameter family of denoisers that continuously spans the DP frontier. The lookahead parameter t acts as a control knob between the MMSE and perceptual regimes. For Gaussian targets, we prove that varying t exactly recovers the optimal DP frontier; for natural images, we observe similar behavior empirically. Within a Plug-and-Play solver, the same mechanism extends to general inverse problems, where it controls a tradeoff between perceptual alignment and data consistency. Despite the lack of exact optimality guarantees in this setting, a single trained flow map spans the DP tradeoff, matching or exceeding specialized baselines at both extremes. Extensive experiments on CelebA ($128\times 128$) and AFHQ ($256\times 256$) across several linear and nonlinear inverse tasks validate our findings.

AI.tldr: Flow map models can continuously span the distortion-perception (DP) frontier via a single parameter t, controlling the tradeoff between MMSE (low distortion) and perceptual quality without needing multiple models or hyperparameter tuning.
AI.motivation: Image restoration faces a fundamental tradeoff between minimizing error (blurry but faithful) and maximizing perceptual quality (sharp but less faithful). Existing methods either commit to a single DP operating point or require paired data, auxiliary models, or hyperparameter tuning to access different tradeoff points.
AI.method: The paper shows that flow map models (an extension of flow matching for few-step sampling that learns an average field) implicitly define a one-parameter family of denoisers via the lookahead parameter t. This t acts as a control knob between MMSE and perceptual regimes. For inverse problems, this mechanism is extended via a Plug-and-Play solver to control perceptual alignment vs. data consistency.
AI.result: For Gaussian targets, varying t exactly recovers the optimal DP frontier. For natural images (CelebA 128×128, AFHQ 256×256), empirical behavior matches this. A single trained flow map matches or exceeds specialized baselines at both extremes of the DP tradeoff across multiple linear and nonlinear inverse tasks.
AI.conclusion: Flow map models provide a practical way to continuously navigate the DP frontier with a single trained model, offering flexibility without exact optimality guarantees for general inverse problems.

title: WeGenBench: A Multidimensional Diagnostic Benchmark towards Text-to-Image Model Optimization
summary: Recent text-to-image generation models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in synthesizing highly realistic images from text inputs alone. Although existing benchmarks can evaluate the generation capabilities of various models to some extent, they struggle to comprehensively and accurately measure performance across multiple dimensions, often failing to reveal the inherent deficiencies of models in specific categories. To address these limitations, we propose WeGenBench, a novel benchmark designed for the comprehensive, multi-perspective evaluation of text-to-image generation capabilities. Our benchmark comprises a total of 4,000 test prompts across two primary categories, meticulously balanced between Chinese and English to evaluate bilingual and cross-cultural generation capabilities. Beyond macroscopic scene classification, we annotate each prompt with multi-dimensional tags tailored to the distinct content and challenges of each language, thereby refining the generation tasks into more specific sub-categories. Through a cross-dimensional evaluation mechanism leveraging both scene classifications and multi-dimensional tags, WeGenBench can precisely pinpoint model shortcomings in specific generation categories. Furthermore, to measure generation quality more accurately, we design and validate several novel evaluation metrics by integrating Vision-Language Models (VLMs), which assess model performance on domain-specific tasks from three core aspects. Crucially, our approach yields both the assessment outcomes and the detailed reasoning trajectories, facilitating a rigorous verification of the accuracy and soundness of the evaluation results. Finally, we conduct systematic benchmarking on current state-of-the-art methods and provide an in-depth analysis of the limitations present in existing models.

AI.tldr: WeGenBench is a 4,000-prompt bilingual (Chinese/English) benchmark with multi-dimensional tags and VLM-based evaluation metrics for comprehensively assessing text-to-image generation models.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks fail to comprehensively and accurately measure text-to-image generation performance across multiple dimensions and cannot reveal models' inherent deficiencies in specific categories.
AI.method: WeGenBench constructs 4,000 test prompts balanced across Chinese and English, annotated with multi-dimensional tags per language. It employs a cross-dimensional evaluation mechanism combining scene classifications and tags, and designs novel VLM-based metrics that assess generation quality from three core aspects while producing both scores and reasoning trajectories.
AI.result: Systematic benchmarking on state-of-the-art methods is conducted, identifying specific limitations in existing models through fine-grained category-level analysis.
AI.conclusion: WeGenBench enables precise identification of model weaknesses in specific generation sub-categories via its multi-dimensional, bilingual evaluation framework and interpretable VLM-based metrics.

title: Thermodynamic Measure of Intelligence
summary: Can intelligence be measured? We propose that intelligence can be defined as the lawful amplification of rare but valid futures: a system increases the probability of outcomes that would be unlikely under passive dynamics but remain admissible under the constraints of the domain. We start with the premise that an intelligent system must model the world and its own place within it. Because the system is part of the world it models, this leads naturally to recursive self-simulation: the system represents futures in which its own actions are part of the trajectory. Our central results give a necessity statement and a conditional near-sufficiency statement connecting this architecture to a precise thermodynamic measure of lawful amplification of rare-valid futures: high rare-valid lift is impossible unless the internal simulation identifies rare-valid futures with high fidelity; conversely, when rare-valid fidelity is high and the simulation contains an effective policy, the achievable lift approaches the actuation-limited optimum. Thus recursive self-simulation is not merely a plausible feature of intelligence but, under the stated assumptions, is necessary and nearly sufficient for high thermodynamic intelligence. The resulting framework makes intelligence measurable on a universal scale, from passive matter and feedback controllers, large language models, and humans as text generators to Maxwell-demon-like information engines.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes that intelligence can be defined as the lawful amplification of rare but valid futures, and proves that recursive self-simulation is necessary and nearly sufficient for high thermodynamic intelligence, making intelligence measurable on a universal scale.
AI.motivation: To establish a formal, measurable definition of intelligence grounded in thermodynamics and information theory, moving beyond vague or anthropocentric notions.
AI.method: The authors define intelligence as the lawful amplification of rare-valid futures—increasing the probability of unlikely but admissible outcomes. They derive a necessity statement and a conditional near-sufficiency statement connecting recursive self-simulation (the system modeling itself as part of the world it models) to a thermodynamic measure called "rare-valid lift."
AI.result: High rare-valid lift is impossible without high-fidelity identification of rare-valid futures by internal simulation; conversely, with high fidelity and an effective policy, the achievable lift approaches the actuation-limited optimum. Thus recursive self-simulation is necessary and nearly sufficient for high thermodynamic intelligence.
AI.conclusion: Intelligence can be measured on a universal thermodynamic scale, applicable across passive matter, feedback controllers, LLMs, humans, and Maxwell-demon-like information engines.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Reward Modeling for Stable RLHF
summary: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) aligns large language models by training reward models on preference data and optimizing policies to maximize predicted rewards. However, this pipeline faces two fundamental challenges: (1) reward models cannot signal when their predictions are unreliable, since they usually act as deterministic point estimators; and (2) modern group-based policy optimization can amplify unreliable reward signals, as exemplified by GRPO's uniform treatment of rewards during advantage computation. As policies explore increasingly diverse responses, these two limitations create a critical vulnerability: unreliable reward estimates may be granted disproportionate influence, triggering severe reward hacking. We propose Uncertainty-Aware Reward Modeling (UARM), which equips reward models with calibrated uncertainty via quantile-based conformal prediction and reweights GRPO advantages through heteroscedastic variance decomposition. Experiments across HelpSteer, UltraFeedback, and PKU-SafeRLHF demonstrate that UARM significantly improves reward model calibration, reduces reward hacking, and enhances downstream alignment quality compared to standard GRPO and uncertainty-agnostic baselines.

AI.tldr: UARM enhances RLHF by adding calibrated uncertainty estimates to reward models and reweighting GRPO advantages, reducing reward hacking and improving alignment quality.
AI.motivation: Standard RLHF reward models are deterministic point estimators that cannot signal prediction unreliability, and group-based policy optimization (e.g., GRPO) treats all rewards uniformly, creating vulnerability to reward hacking when unreliable reward estimates gain disproportionate influence during exploration.
AI.method: UARM equips reward models with calibrated uncertainty via quantile-based conformal prediction and reweights GRPO advantages through heteroscedastic variance decomposition.
AI.result: Experiments on HelpSteer, UltraFeedback, and PKU-SafeRLHF show UARM significantly improves reward model calibration, reduces reward hacking, and enhances downstream alignment quality over standard GRPO and uncertainty-agnostic baselines.
AI.conclusion: Quantifying reward model uncertainty and incorporating it into policy optimization effectively mitigates reward hacking in RLHF, leading to more reliable alignment.

title: Geometry-Preserving in 3D Gaussian Splatting for LiDAR-Camera Extrinsic Calibration
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. 15 pages (excluding references), 5 figures
summary: Accurate LiDAR-camera calibration is essential for robust multi-modal perception. Targetless approaches avoid manual setup but remain limited by the scarcity of discriminative cross-modal features. Recent methods address this by reconstructing the scene within a differentiable model, enabling extrinsic optimization through dense photometric supervision. Among these, 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has been widely adopted as a geometric proxy that bridges LiDAR and camera within a single differentiable framework. However, since 3DGS was originally designed for novel view synthesis, existing methods tend to prioritize rendering quality, causing the proxy geometry to drift from the true LiDAR structure. We propose a framework that preserves the metric geometry of the Gaussian proxy by aggregating multi-view LiDAR observations for dense depth supervision and blocking photometric gradients from updating the Gaussian spatial parameters. We validate our method on public driving datasets, where it consistently outperforms existing targetless methods in calibration accuracy.

AI.tldr: A LiDAR-camera calibration framework using 3D Gaussian Splatting that preserves metric geometry by aggregating multi-view LiDAR depth supervision and blocking photometric gradients from updating Gaussian spatial parameters, outperforming existing targetless methods on driving datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing targetless calibration methods using 3DGS as a geometric proxy suffer from geometry drift because 3DGS prioritizes rendering quality over geometric fidelity, causing the proxy to deviate from true LiDAR structure.
AI.method: Aggregates multi-view LiDAR observations for dense depth supervision and blocks photometric gradients from updating Gaussian spatial parameters to preserve metric geometry of the Gaussian proxy.
AI.result: Consistently outperforms existing targetless methods in calibration accuracy on public driving datasets.
AI.conclusion: Preserving metric geometry of the Gaussian proxy through dense depth supervision and gradient blocking yields more accurate LiDAR-camera calibration than prior 3DGS-based approaches.

title: A Multi-Agent system for Multi-Objective constrained optimization
comment: Presented at the 17th Workshop on Optimization and Learning in Multiagent Systems (OptLearnMAS, https://optlearnmas.github.io), co-located with the 25th International Conference on Autonomous Agents and Multiagent Systems (AAMAS 2026)
summary: Many decision-making problems in computing and networking systems can be naturally formulated as cost-minimization problems under performance constraints. In dynamic environments, reinforcement learning (RL) is often used to solve such problems at runtime by embedding both costs and constraint violations into a single scalar reward through weighted penalty terms, following a Lagrangian-inspired formulation. However, in this context the behavior of the learned policy critically depends on the choice of these weights, which are typically selected manually. This makes it difficult to identify an appropriate trade-off between optimizing the primary objective and effectively avoiding constraint violations, particularly in non-stationary environments where their relative importance may change. This paper presents MAMO (Multi-Agent system for Multi-Objective constrained optimization), an approach to tackle this balancing problem through multi-agent RL. MAMO decouples task execution from objective design by formulating the selection of reward weights as a learning problem, providing a !rst step towards more autonomous and robust RL-based solutions for constrained optimization problems in dynamic environments.

AI.tldr: MAMO is a multi-agent RL approach that decouples task execution from objective design by learning optimal reward weights for constrained optimization problems, eliminating manual weight tuning in dynamic environments.
AI.motivation: In RL-based constrained optimization, manually selecting reward weights for cost and constraint violations is challenging, especially in non-stationary environments where the optimal trade-off between primary objectives and constraint satisfaction may change over time.
AI.method: MAMO formulates reward weight selection as a separate learning problem using multi-agent RL, where one agent handles task execution and another agent learns appropriate reward weights, effectively decoupling task execution from objective design.
AI.result: The approach enables more autonomous and robust RL-based solutions for constrained optimization in dynamic environments, eliminating the need for manual weight tuning.
AI.conclusion: MAMO provides a first step toward autonomous RL-based constrained optimization by learning reward weights adaptively, making solutions more robust to changing environmental conditions.

title: Enhancing Graph Neural Networks Using Proximity Graphs for Dust Source Emission Forecasting
summary:
