AI.tldr: The paper shows that two unexplained phenomena in quantum vision models—(i) more entanglement improves generalization and (ii) quantum noise can boost test accuracy—are both controlled by a single quantity: the effective dimension $d_{\rm eff}$ of the (noise-shaped) quantum feature kernel. The authors provide a spectral account, exact kernel decomposition under depolarizing noise, capacity bounds, and demonstrate that noise injection acts as ridge-like regularization, achieving up to +13% test accuracy improvement.
AI.motivation: Two striking but unexplained empirical phenomena have been observed in quantum vision models (quantum vision transformers and quantum convolutional networks): (i) ansatze with more or more uniformly distributed entanglement generalize better, and (ii) injecting quantum noise can improve rather than degrade test accuracy. These observations are currently treated as curiosities discovered by grid search and explained, if at all, by hand. The authors aim to unify these phenomena under a single measurable principle.
AI.method: The authors work primarily with quantum-kernel vision models (a quantum feature map read out by a kernel classifier). They give a spectral account where entanglement structure and quantum noise are two knobs that move $d_{\rm eff}$. They provide an exact decomposition of the depolarized kernel $K_p=(1-p)^2K+\tfrac{p(2-p)}{D}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^\top$ with $d_{\rm eff}(K_p)\to1$, a contraction result for amplitude damping, a kernel-machine capacity bound, and a capacity/alignment risk decomposition.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates that both entanglement and quantum noise affect generalization through the effective dimension $d_{\rm eff}$. Amplitude damping contracts $d_{\rm eff}$ and lifts test accuracy by up to +13% along an inverted-U sweet spot. The effect's sign flips between over- and under-fitting regimes. Noise injection matches an explicit spectral-filtering frontier.
AI.conclusion: The two reported anecdotal phenomena in quantum vision models are organized into a single measurable principle for designing quantum-vision models: the effective dimension $d_{\rm eff}$ of the quantum feature kernel. This provides a principled framework for understanding how entanglement and noise shape generalization in quantum machine learning.

title: HumanScale: Egocentric Human Video Can Outperform Real-Robot Data for Embodied Pretraining
summary: Embodied foundation models are expected to benefit from data scaling like large language models, but face a much tighter data bottleneck. Teleoperated real-robot trajectories remain the dominant pretraining source due to their precise action supervision and embodiment alignment, yet their scalability is limited by high collection cost, acquisition difficulty, and low behavioral and environmental diversity. These limitations have sparked interest in egocentric human video as a scalable, substantially lower-cost, and more diverse alternative for embodied model pretraining. However, its effectiveness compared to teleoperated real-robot data remains underexplored. To address this question, we conduct a systematic study comparing egocentric human video and teleoperated real-robot trajectories as pretraining data sources for embodied foundation models, under fixed post-training and validation protocols. Surprisingly, we find that egocentric data, when processed through a carefully designed filtering and labeling pipeline, is not merely a viable substitute for model pretraining but can lead to superior performance. With the same amount of pretraining data, models pretrained on egocentric data achieve a 24% lower validation loss on real-robot action prediction, as well as 52.5% and 90% higher success rates on in-distribution and out-of-distribution real-robot task execution, respectively. This finding verifies a scalable paradigm for embodied foundation models: pretrain on egocentric human video to learn diverse world representations, then adapt with a small amount of labeled real-robot data for action-space alignment. We hope this study encourages broader exploration of egocentric data and offers guidance for data quality assessment before costly robot data collection.

AI.tldr: Egocentric human video, when properly filtered and labeled, outperforms teleoperated real-robot data as a pretraining source for embodied foundation models, achieving lower validation loss and higher success rates on real-robot tasks, suggesting a scalable pretrain-on-human-video, adapt-on-robot-data paradigm.
AI.motivation: Embodied foundation models suffer from a data bottleneck: teleoperated real-robot trajectories are expensive, scarce, and lack diversity, while egocentric human video is cheaper and more diverse but its effectiveness for pretraining has not been systematically compared.
AI.method: The authors conduct a systematic study comparing egocentric human video vs. teleoperated real-robot trajectories as pretraining data, using a carefully designed filtering and labeling pipeline for egocentric data, under fixed post-training and validation protocols.
AI.result: Egocentric data pretraining achieves 24% lower validation loss on real-robot action prediction, and 52.5% (in-distribution) and 90% (out-of-distribution) higher success rates on real-robot tasks compared to teleoperated data with the same amount of pretraining data.
AI.conclusion: The findings validate a scalable paradigm: pretrain on egocentric human video for diverse world representations, then fine-tune with a small amount of labeled real-robot data for action-space alignment, offering guidance for data quality assessment before costly robot data collection.

title: Learner-based Concept Drift Detection: Analysis and Evaluation
summary: Machine learning algorithms deployed for evolving streaming environments must handle the non-stationary data distributions, commonly referred to as concept drift. The presence of concept drift poses a major challenge for many real-world applications because it can severely degrade their predictive performance, hindering their ability to support robust decision-making. Consequently, the timely and efficient detection of drift events is critical for sustaining high accuracy over time. This study examines theoretically the concept drift characteristics and numerous drift detection algorithms across several categories. Furthermore, we evaluate their performance on both synthetic and real-world datasets exhibiting diverse streaming scenarios and drift characteristics, such as abrupt and gradual changes. This study aims to enhance understanding of the complex notion of concept drift characteristics and behavior of drift detectors, along with their applicability to diverse contexts.

AI.tldr: This paper theoretically examines concept drift characteristics and evaluates various drift detection algorithms on synthetic and real-world datasets with diverse streaming scenarios, including abrupt and gradual changes, aiming to enhance understanding of drift detectors' behavior and applicability.
AI.motivation: Concept drift in evolving streaming environments severely degrades predictive performance of machine learning models, hindering robust decision-making in real-world applications; thus timely drift detection is critical.
AI.method: Theoretical examination of concept drift characteristics and multiple drift detection algorithms across several categories, followed by empirical evaluation on both synthetic and real-world datasets exhibiting diverse streaming scenarios (abrupt and gradual changes).
AI.result: Performance evaluation results of drift detection algorithms across diverse streaming scenarios and drift characteristics.
AI.conclusion: Aims to enhance understanding of concept drift characteristics and drift detector behavior, as well as their applicability to diverse real-world contexts.

title: SARLO-80: Worldwide Slant SAR Language Optic Dataset 80cm
summary: Multimodal foundation models have advanced rapidly thanks to large optical benchmarks, but comparable resources for synthetic aperture radar (SAR) remain limited. Existing SAR--optical datasets largely rely on low-resolution, intensity-only Ground Range Detected~(GRD) products and do not preserve complex-valued SAR measurements or native acquisition geometry, which restricts physically grounded multimodal learning. In particular, large-scale public datasets combining very-high-resolution (VHR) SAR SLC, aligned optical imagery, and natural-language descriptions are still lacking. We present a VHR SAR--optical--text dataset built from open-access Umbra spotlight acquisitions distributed as Sensor Independent Complex Data (SICD). From around 2,500 worldwide scenes (VV/HH, 20cm--2m native resolution), we standardize all SAR data to an 80cm slant-range grid via band-limited FFT resampling and tile the imagery into 1024 by 1024 patches. For each SAR patch, we retrieve a high-resolution optical tile and warp it into the SAR grid using local coordinate correspondences for local pixel-level alignment. We further generate three caption variants (SHORT/MID/LONG) per sample to support vision--language training and evaluation. Our dataset contains 119,566 triplets (complex and amplitude slant-range SAR patch, aligned optical patch, natural-language description) covering 257 locations across 72 countries and a broad range of land types and infrastructures. We release fixed train/validation/test splits and the full preprocessing and baseline code to enable reproducible benchmarks for multimodal alignment on cross-modal retrieval and conditional generation in native SAR geometry. The dataset is publicly available on the Hugging Face Hub at https://huggingface.co/datasets/ONERA/SARLO-80.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SARLO-80, a large-scale very-high-resolution (VHR) SAR–optical–text dataset built from Umbra SICD acquisitions. It contains 119,566 triplets of complex/amplitude slant-range SAR patches, aligned optical tiles, and natural-language captions across 257 locations in 72 countries. Data is standardized to 80cm slant-range resolution, with pixel-level optical-to-SAR alignment and three caption variants (SHORT/MID/LONG). The dataset supports multimodal alignment research including cross-modal retrieval and conditional generation in native SAR geometry.
AI.motivation: Existing SAR–optical datasets rely on low-resolution, intensity-only GRD products lacking complex-valued SAR measurements and native acquisition geometry, which limits physically grounded multimodal learning. Large-scale public datasets combining VHR SAR SLC, aligned optical imagery, and text descriptions are absent.
AI.method: (1) Collected ~2,500 Umbra spotlight SICD scenes (VV/HH, 20cm–2m native resolution). (2) Standardized all SAR data to 80cm slant-range grid via band-limited FFT resampling, tiled into 1024×1024 patches. (3) Retrieved high-resolution optical tiles and warped them into the SAR grid using local coordinate correspondences for pixel-level alignment. (4) Generated three caption variants (SHORT/MID/LONG) per sample via vision–language models.
AI.result: Dataset contains 119,566 triplets (complex and amplitude SAR patches, aligned optical patches, natural-language descriptions) covering 257 locations, 72 countries, diverse land types and infrastructures. Public release with fixed train/validation/test splits and full preprocessing/baseline code.
AI.conclusion: SARLO-80 fills the gap in VHR multimodal SAR–optical–text resources, enabling reproducible benchmarks for cross-modal retrieval and conditional generation in native SAR slant-range geometry.

title: Boundary Embedding Shaping with Adaptive Contrastive Learning for Graph Structural Disentanglement
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) excel at aggregating neighbor information for classification, yet their performance is hindered by graph structural entanglement, where spurious correlations from semantically irrelevant neighbors contaminate node embeddings. This challenge is most acute for nodes near class boundaries in the embedding space, where amplified structural noise blurs decision boundaries and destabilizes predictions. Existing robust GNN methods largely treat all nodes uniformly, ignoring boundary vulnerabilities. In this paper, to improve classification performance, we tackle graph structural disentanglement by identifying boundary-region entanglement as the primary bottleneck and propose Boundary Embedding Shaping (BES), an adaptive contrastive learning GNN plug-in module that selectively suppresses spurious structural noise at decision boundaries with minimal model parameter perturbation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BES consistently improves boundary discrimination and outperforms existing leading methods. Notably, BES boosts GCN performance by an average of 3.3% in node classification (up to 5.0% on WikiCS) and achieves superior accuracy in link prediction.

AI.tldr: GNNs suffer from graph structural entanglement, especially near decision boundaries; the paper proposes Boundary Embedding Shaping (BES), an adaptive contrastive learning plug-in module that selectively suppresses spurious structural noise at boundaries, achieving consistent improvements in node classification (e.g., 3.3% average GCN boost) and link prediction.
AI.motivation: Existing robust GNN methods treat all nodes uniformly, ignoring that boundary-region nodes are most vulnerable to structural noise and spurious correlations from semantically irrelevant neighbors, which blurs decision boundaries and destabilizes predictions.
AI.method: Boundary Embedding Shaping (BES) — an adaptive contrastive learning GNN plug-in module that selectively suppresses spurious structural noise at decision boundaries with minimal model parameter perturbation.
AI.result: BES improves GCN performance by an average of 3.3% in node classification (up to 5.0% on WikiCS) and achieves superior accuracy in link prediction, consistently outperforming existing leading methods.
AI.conclusion: Identifying boundary-region entanglement as the primary bottleneck and applying targeted contrastive noise suppression via BES effectively improves boundary discrimination and overall graph classification/ link prediction performance.

title: VisDom: Sparse Novel View Synthesis with Visible Domain Constraint
summary: Sparse novel view synthesis (NVS) remains challenging due to the ambiguity of recovering 3D geometry from few input views. While NeRF- and Gaussian Splatting (GS)-based methods perform well with dense supervision, they often overfit in sparse settings, producing floating artifacts and inconsistent geometry. Silhouette consistency is commonly used as a regularizer, but it remains insufficient, as silhouette-consistent regions can extend beyond the true object geometry. We introduce VisDom, a learning-free geometric constraint that augments classical carving-based visual hull reconstruction by enforcing a minimum multi-view visibility requirement. Specifically, we define a visible domain as the subset of 3D space observed by at least $K$ views and use it as an additional filtering criterion on top of standard silhouette-based reconstruction. This provides a stronger spatial prior in sparse-view settings. We integrate VisDom into both implicit (NeRF) and explicit (GS) pipelines by restricting volumetric sampling and guiding Gaussian placement during optimization. Experiments on three challenging datasets show consistent improvements in sparse-view NVS, enabling high-quality object-centric reconstruction from as few as four input images. Our method is domain-agnostic, requires only silhouettes, and introduces no learned parameters, making it a simple complement to existing approaches. Applying VisDom on top of GaussianObject further improves performance on Omni3D and MipNeRF360, while matching or surpassing it at 22 $\times$ lower training cost.

AI.tldr: VisDom introduces a learning-free geometric constraint that enforces a minimum multi-view visibility requirement on top of silhouette-based reconstruction, providing stronger spatial priors for sparse-view novel view synthesis. It integrates into both NeRF and Gaussian Splatting pipelines, improving quality from as few as 4 input images with no learned parameters.
AI.motivation: Sparse novel view synthesis is challenging due to 3D geometry ambiguity from few views. Existing NeRF/GS methods overfit in sparse settings, producing floating artifacts and inconsistent geometry, and silhouette consistency alone is insufficient as a regularizer.
AI.method: VisDom defines a visible domain as the subset of 3D space observed by at least K views, used as a filtering criterion on top of standard silhouette-based reconstruction (visual hull carving). It is integrated into implicit (NeRF) and explicit (GS) pipelines by restricting volumetric sampling and guiding Gaussian placement.
AI.result: Consistent improvements on three challenging datasets for sparse-view NVS from as few as 4 images. Applying VisDom on top of GaussianObject improves performance on Omni3D and MipNeRF360, matching or surpassing it at 22× lower training cost.
AI.conclusion: VisDom is a simple, learning-free, domain-agnostic complement that requires only silhouettes, introduces no learned parameters, and consistently improves sparse-view NVS across implicit and explicit pipelines.

title: Integrating national forest inventory, airborne lidar, and satellite imagery for wall-to-wall mapping of forest structure with computer vision
summary: Remote sensing is increasingly relied upon to deliver actionable science for forest and wildfire risk management across large landscapes. Wall-to-wall, annually updated maps are a persistent need for effective forest management. Many planning systems and data collections combine disparate data sources with different purposes, vintages, and prediction quality, which leads to confounding behavior in operational planning systems. We introduce the VibrantForests framework, developed and applied to map forest attributes and provide a coherent foundation for effective forest and wildfire planning. VibrantForests includes a satellite-based forest structure model trained on lidar-derived samples and applied across the contiguous United States to concurrently generate estimates of canopy cover, canopy height, aboveground live tree biomass, basal area, and quadratic mean diameter at 10-meter resolution. We demonstrate predictive capability spanning the full spectrum of forest conditions ranging from sparse-canopy/low-biomass to dense-canopy/high-biomass. Results show that our model extends the range at which saturation is commonly encountered in comparable passive-sensor models, and reduces regression-to-mean behavior that commonly produces overestimation of forest attributes in small/sparse conditions and underestimation in large/dense conditions. The VibrantForests framework addresses a key limitation in large-area forest and wildfire planning by delivering coherent wall-to-wall estimates of management-relevant attributes at annual cadence and 10m resolution.

AI.tldr: VibrantForests is a satellite-based framework that produces wall-to-wall, annual 10m resolution maps of key forest attributes (canopy cover, height, biomass, basal area, diameter) across the contiguous US. It uses a lidar-trained model that reduces saturation and regression-to-mean biases, providing coherent estimates for forest and wildfire risk management.
AI.motivation: Existing forest planning systems combine disparate data sources with different purposes, vintages, and prediction quality, leading to confounding behavior. Wall-to-wall, annually updated coherent forest attribute maps are needed for effective forest and wildfire management across large landscapes.
AI.method: A satellite-based forest structure model trained on lidar-derived samples, applied across the contiguous US to concurrently estimate canopy cover, canopy height, aboveground live tree biomass, basal area, and quadratic mean diameter at 10m resolution.
AI.result: The model extends the range before saturation compared to typical passive-sensor models, reduces regression-to-mean behavior (overestimation in sparse conditions, underestimation in dense conditions), and demonstrates predictive capability across the full forest condition spectrum.
AI.conclusion: VibrantForests addresses a key limitation in large-area forest and wildfire planning by delivering coherent, wall-to-wall estimates of management-relevant attributes at annual cadence and 10m resolution.

title: The FID Lottery: Quantifying Hidden Randomness in Generative-Model Evaluation
summary: The Frechet Inception Distance (FID) is the de facto arbiter of image generation, yet most papers report just a single number from a single trained model using a single sampling seed. How reproducible is that number if we retrain the model, or merely resample from it? In this paper, we treat FID as a random variable on a two-axis panel of training and generation seeds, and measure its variance directly on several hundred SiT networks trained on class-conditional ImageNet 256x256. We report surprising findings: (a) Retraining the model using the same recipe with a different seed moves FID 3.2x more (in Inception feature space) than redrawing samples from a fixed network. (b) That gap is driven by three factors: random initialisation, data ordering, and the per-step Gaussian noise of the flow-matching loss. (c) Increasing compute or model size barely tightens the spread, holding the FID coefficient of variation (CoV) inside a 1-2% band. (d) Per-cell classifier-free-guidance tuning halves the spread but reshuffles which seeds work best, and a lucky training seed reaches the same FID with up to 2x less compute than an unlucky one. Based on these findings, we recommend a new FID evaluation protocol: evaluate under per-cell optimal guidance, treat any FID gap below the empirically measured ~1.3% CoV as inconclusive, and report an error bar over several training seeds rather than a single FID number.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically measures the variability of FID scores across both training seeds (retraining) and generation seeds (resampling) on hundreds of SiT networks trained on ImageNet 256x256. It finds that retraining introduces 3.2x more variance than resampling, driven by initialization, data ordering, and noise in the flow-matching loss. The coefficient of variation remains ~1-2% regardless of compute or model size. Per-cell CFG tuning reduces variance but reshuffles seed rankings. The authors recommend reporting error bars over multiple training seeds and treating FID gaps below ~1.3% CoV as inconclusive.
AI.motivation: FID is the standard metric for image generation quality, yet its reproducibility when retraining models or resampling from them is rarely assessed. The paper aims to quantify the variance of FID under different sources of randomness to determine whether reported single-number FID results are statistically meaningful.
AI.method: The authors train hundreds of SiT (flow-matching) networks on class-conditional ImageNet 256x256 with varying training seeds, then sample with different generation seeds. They measure FID variance, decompose it into contributions from initialization, data ordering, and per-step Gaussian noise, and analyze how model size, compute budget, and CFG tuning affect the spread.
AI.result: (a) Retraining moves FID 3.2x more than resampling. (b) The gap is driven by random initialization, data ordering, and flow-matching noise. (c) Scaling compute or model size does not significantly reduce the spread; CoV stays ~1-2%. (d) Per-cell CFG tuning halves the spread but reorders seed rankings; a lucky training seed can match the FID of an unlucky seed with 2x less compute.
AI.conclusion: The paper recommends a new FID evaluation protocol: use per-cell optimal guidance, treat any FID gap below ~1.3% CoV as inconclusive, and report error bars over multiple training seeds rather than a single FID number.

title: Shifting-based Optimizable Linear Relaxations for General Activation Functions
summary: The use of neural networks (NNs) is rapidly increasing, including in safety- and security-critical domains. To provide formal guarantees about NN behavior, many verification methods rely on optimizable linear relaxations of activation functions. However, existing techniques depend on hand-crafted relaxations for each activation function. Extension to state-of-the-art activation functions therefore requires substantial manual effort. In contrast, our approach SLiR (Shifting-based Linear Relaxations) is broadly applicable, requiring only a Lipschitz constant or a set of critical points. SLiR parameterizes relaxations by their slope and computes the corresponding offset via a shifting procedure that ensures sound upper and lower bounds over the input domain, enabling efficient optimization while maintaining correctness. Our experiments show that SLiR produces tight relaxations across a wide range of practical activation functions and enables verification of up to 7.8x more properties compared to state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: SLiR is a novel method for generating sound, optimizable linear relaxations of activation functions for neural network verification, requiring only a Lipschitz constant or critical points. It parameterizes relaxations by slope and computes offsets via shifting, achieving up to 7.8x more verified properties across diverse activation functions compared to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Existing neural network verification methods rely on hand-crafted linear relaxations for each activation function, requiring substantial manual effort to extend to new or state-of-the-art activation functions. A general, automated approach is needed.
AI.method: SLiR parameterizes linear relaxations by their slope and computes the corresponding offset via a shifting procedure that ensures sound upper and lower bounds. It requires only a Lipschitz constant or a set of critical points, making it broadly applicable without hand-crafted designs.
AI.result: SLiR produces tight relaxations across a wide range of practical activation functions and enables verification of up to 7.8x more properties compared to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: SLiR provides a general, automated framework for generating linear relaxations that eliminates manual effort for each activation function, improves verification scalability, and maintains soundness.

title: CalTennis: Large Multi-View Tennis Video Dataset and Benchmark of Monocular-to-3D Pose Estimation
summary: The Caltech Tennis Dataset (CalTennis) is a large-scale video benchmark for evaluating monocular-to-3D pose estimation in the wild. CalTennis comprises over 11 million frames (51 hours) of tennis practice and match play from 40 players, captured with 2-6 synchronized cameras at 60 Hz. It is 10 times larger than existing in-the-wild human motion video datasets and 3 times larger than existing MOCAP-ground-truthed datasets, and it is the first large-scale benchmark to provide synchronized multi-view recordings of expert athletic motion. The multi-view setup enables inexpensive, label-free evaluation of monocular-to-3D pose estimation algorithms. We describe a simple, standardized protocol that enables data collection without specialized equipment or expertise, along with fully automated video calibration and synchronization. Benchmarking state-of-the-art monocular-to-3D pose methods on CalTennis, we find that while 3D joint angle recovery is now quite accurate, all models struggle to estimate depth and foot contact consistently. We further propose two novel performance metrics, footwork and stability, as well as qualitatively study body shape inconsistency. These metrics expose previously underexplored failure modes and point to concrete opportunities for improvement in pose estimation and action analysis.

AI.tldr: CalTennis is a large-scale (51 hours, 11M+ frames, 40 players, multi-view) tennis video benchmark for evaluating monocular-to-3D pose estimation, which is 10x larger than existing in-the-wild datasets. It provides a standardized collection protocol, automated calibration/synchronization, and new metrics (footwork, stability) revealing that current methods struggle with depth and foot contact despite accurate joint angles.
AI.motivation: Existing in-the-wild motion datasets lack sufficient scale and multi-view ground truth; prior benchmarks are either small-scale or lack synchronized multi-view recordings of expert athletic motion, limiting label-free evaluation of monocular-to-3D pose estimation.
AI.method: A standardized, low-cost data collection protocol with 2-6 synchronized 60 Hz cameras; fully automated video calibration and synchronization pipeline; multi-view geometry enables label-free 3D pose ground truth; two novel metrics (footwork, stability) and body shape consistency analysis.
AI.result: Benchmarking SOTA monocular-to-3D methods shows accurate 3D joint angle recovery but persistent struggles with depth estimation and foot contact consistency; new metrics expose underexplored failure modes.
AI.conclusion: CalTennis enables large-scale, label-free evaluation of pose estimation in the wild; identified weaknesses (depth, foot contact) and proposed metrics point to concrete directions for improving pose estimation and action analysis.

title: Recurrent neural networks approximate continuous functions
summary: Classical approximation theorems ask for a new neural network whenever the target accuracy is improved. This paper studies the opposite possibility: can the network be chosen once and for all, and can accuracy be bought only by letting it run longer? We prove that this is possible for every continuous function on [-1,1]. More precisely, each such function is uniformly approximated by the time evolution of a single ReLU recurrent neural network with fixed weights and fixed hidden dimension. The mechanism behind the construction is a new intermediate model, the Turing machine with neural units (TMNU). This model retains the algorithmic freedom needed to implement polynomial approximation schemes, while remaining rigid enough to be simulated by RNNs with explicit bounds on hidden dimension and weight magnitude. The resulting convergence rates reflect the underlying polynomial approximation rates. We complement the construction with minimax lower bounds showing that runtime is not merely a proof artifact, but an unavoidable resource in this fixed-network approximation paradigm.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that any continuous function on [-1,1] can be uniformly approximated by a single fixed ReLU RNN (with fixed weights and hidden dimension) by letting it run longer, using a new intermediate model called TMNU, and provides minimax lower bounds showing runtime is unavoidable.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether neural networks can be fixed once and for all, with approximation accuracy improved solely by increasing runtime, contrary to classical approximation theorems that require a new network for each accuracy level.
AI.method: The authors introduce a Turing machine with neural units (TMNU) as an intermediate model, which retains algorithmic flexibility for polynomial approximation while being simulatable by RNNs with bounded hidden dimension and weights. This bridge enables a single fixed-weight RNN to approximate any continuous function through time evolution.
AI.result: Every continuous function on [-1,1] can be uniformly approximated by a single fixed ReLU RNN with fixed weights and fixed hidden dimension, with convergence rates matching polynomial approximation rates. Minimax lower bounds confirm runtime is an essential resource in this paradigm.
AI.conclusion: Fixed-network approximation is possible, but runtime is an unavoidable resource; the TMNU model provides a general framework for connecting algorithmic computation to neural network approximation.

title: SSD: Spatially Speculative Decoding Accelerates Autoregressive Image Generation
summary: Autoregressive models excel in visual generation by treating images as 1D sequences of discrete tokens, mirroring language modeling. However, this flattening discards the intrinsic 2D spatial locality of visual signals, creating severe computational bottlenecks during inference. We introduce Spatially Speculative Decoding (SSD), a framework that aligns the predictive objective with the natural geometry of images. Rather than predicting only the immediate next token in a 1D sequence, our model simultaneously predicts the adjacent horizontal token and the token directly below it. By capitalizing on this 2D spatial correlation, spatially speculative decoding overcomes the memory wall in visual inference. Our approach accelerates autoregressive image generation by up to 13.3x while maintaining high fidelity on DPG-Bench and GenEval. Our results suggest that respecting the underlying geometry of vision unlocks massive computational efficiencies, paving the way for real-time, high-resolution autoregressive generative models.

AI.tldr: SSD accelerates autoregressive image generation by up to 13.3x by predicting tokens in 2D space (adjacent horizontal and vertical) instead of the standard 1D sequential token prediction, leveraging spatial locality to overcome memory bottlenecks.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive visual models treat images as flattened 1D token sequences, discarding the intrinsic 2D spatial locality of visual signals and creating severe computational bottlenecks during inference.
AI.method: SSD aligns the predictive objective with image geometry by simultaneously predicting the adjacent horizontal token and the token directly below (2D spatial correlation), rather than only the immediate next token in a 1D sequence.
AI.result: Achieves up to 13.3x acceleration in autoregressive image generation while maintaining high fidelity on DPG-Bench and GenEval.
AI.conclusion: Respecting the underlying 2D geometry of vision unlocks massive computational efficiencies, enabling real-time, high-resolution autoregressive generative models.

title: Quantum-classical physics-informed Kolmogorov-Arnold networks for PDEs
summary: We develop QCPIKAN, the first quantum-classical physics-informed Kolmogorov-Arnold network designed to solve partial differential equations (PDEs). Built upon Chebyshev-polynomial KAN layers and parameterized quantum circuits, this hybrid framework embeds physical constraints into the training loss to enforce physical consistency. Our theoretical investigations grounded in approximation theory prove that this design accelerates high-frequency error convergence to an exponential rate and effectively mitigates numerical dispersion. We validate the framework across three typical seepage scenarios in porous media, including single-phase flow, component transport and two-phase flow. Compared with existing quantum-classical physics-informed neural networks, QCPIKAN achieves superior performance in global prediction accuracy, local error control, dynamic evolution tracking and displacement front localization. This work provides a robust and efficient alternative for solving complex PDEs.

AI.tldr: QCPIKAN is a novel hybrid quantum-classical physics-informed Kolmogorov-Arnold network using Chebyshev-polynomial KAN layers and quantum circuits to solve PDEs, demonstrating exponential convergence and superior performance on porous media seepage problems.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of existing quantum-classical physics-informed neural networks in solving PDEs, particularly regarding slow high-frequency error convergence and numerical dispersion.
AI.method: A hybrid framework combining Chebyshev-polynomial KAN layers with parameterized quantum circuits, embedding physical constraints into the training loss function. Theoretical analysis uses approximation theory to prove convergence properties.
AI.result: Validated on three porous media seepage scenarios (single-phase flow, component transport, two-phase flow). Outperforms existing quantum-classical PINNs in global accuracy, local error control, dynamic tracking, and front localization.
AI.conclusion: QCPIKAN provides a robust and efficient alternative for solving complex PDEs with proven exponential convergence and reduced numerical dispersion.

title: Current World Models Lack a Persistent State Core
summary: World models are increasingly regarded as a decisive step toward artificial general intelligence, yet modeling the physical world demands more than rendering convincing frames on demand: it requires an internal world state that keeps evolving over time, decoupled from observation, so that objects endure and events run to their conclusions whether or not a camera is watching, much as the moon holds to its orbit when no one is looking. This requirement is a blind spot of existing benchmarks, which reward surface properties such as fidelity, motion, and camera controllability while never asking whether a generated world keeps evolving once it is unobserved. We introduce \textbf{WRBench}, the first systematic diagnostic benchmark that treats camera motion as an intervention on observability and resolves evaluation into a human-calibrated chain that asks whether the camera executes the requested interaction, whether the scene stays continuous and identifiable while in view, and whether a returning target remains consistent with the event that was set in motion. Across 9{,}600 videos from 23 models spanning four control paradigms, one finding proves stubborn: current systems maintain the observed world as a tracking shot, resuming a returning target in the state at which it was abandoned rather than advancing the event while it went unseen. Because this failure recurs across control paradigms, model families, and increments of scale, robust world-state evolution does not follow from cleaner imagery, tighter control, richer geometric priors, or sheer parameter count We therefore argue that the stability of the physical state kernel and the consistency of worldlines under viewpoint intervention should become first-class objectives of world-model design, so that a world model captures how the world will unfold rather than how the next frame appears.

AI.tldr: WRBench is a benchmark revealing that current world models fail to evolve internal world states when unobserved, instead freezing scenes like a paused video rather than continuing events independently of camera viewpoint.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks reward surface properties (fidelity, motion, controllability) but never test whether a generated world evolves when unobserved. This blind spot means models may produce convincing frames without maintaining a persistent physical world state.
AI.method: WRBench treats camera motion as an intervention on observability, with a human-calibrated evaluation chain: (1) does the camera execute the requested interaction, (2) does the scene stay continuous/identifiable while in view, (3) does a returning target remain consistent with the event that was set in motion. Evaluated on 9,600 videos from 23 models across 4 control paradigms.
AI.result: Current systems maintain the observed world as a tracking shot — when a target returns to view, it resumes in the state it was abandoned rather than advancing the event that occurred while unseen. This failure is consistent across control paradigms, model families, and scales.
AI.conclusion: Robust world-state evolution does not follow from cleaner imagery, tighter control, richer geometric priors, or sheer parameter count. The stability of the physical state kernel and consistency of worldlines under viewpoint intervention should become first-class objectives of world-model design.

title: Constrained hybrid modelling to predict microbial dynamics and organic matter turnover in soil systems
summary: Soil microorganisms control organic matter cycling and largely determine how soil systems can cope with and mitigate climate change and environmental threats. Representing microbial dynamics in process-based soil models is therefore critical to predict carbon cycling in soils, albeit highly challenging to inform from data. One promising approach to improve their parametrisation is the integration of genomic data, yet modelling the complex and unknown relationship between genomes and the processes the microbes are driving is an unsolved problem. In this work, we present the first hybrid modeling framework for deriving biokinetic parameter values of a process-based soil organic matter turnover model from metagenome-inferred functional traits based on DNA sequencing data. Our model predicts biokinetic parameters of the process-based model from genomic trait data with a neural network and integrates constraints from ecological theory and literature to ensure realistic behavior, even of non-observed state variables. We evaluate our method on synthetic genomic trait datasets of varying complexity and on real data, showing that our approach improves performance over multiple baselines and learns the dynamics of unmeasurable components of the process-based model effectively, even for small training datasets.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first hybrid modeling framework that uses neural networks to derive biokinetic parameters for soil organic matter turnover models from metagenome-inferred functional traits, integrating ecological theory constraints to improve prediction of unmeasurable soil microbial dynamics, even with small datasets.
AI.motivation: Soil microorganisms critically control organic matter cycling and climate change mitigation, but representing their dynamics in process-based soil models is challenging due to the difficulty of parameterizing complex, unknown relationships between genomes and microbial processes from data.
AI.method: A hybrid modeling framework combining a neural network (predicting biokinetic parameters from metagenome-inferred genomic functional traits) with a process-based soil organic matter turnover model, integrating constraints from ecological theory and literature to ensure realistic behavior of unobserved state variables.
AI.result: The approach outperforms multiple baselines on both synthetic and real genomic trait datasets, effectively learns dynamics of unmeasurable process-based model components, and performs well even with small training datasets.
AI.conclusion: Integrating genomic data via hybrid (neural network + process-based) modeling is a promising and data-efficient approach for improving soil carbon cycling predictions by deriving biokinetic parameters from metagenomic information.

title: Thinking in Boxes: 3D Editing in Real Images Made Easy
summary: Text and 2D-conditioning interfaces provide weak, ambiguous control over spatial transformations in image editing -- particularly under large object motions and camera changes. Prior work has used 3D primitives such as boxes, but only as loose conditioning signals indicating approximate object location rather than specifying the transformation. We instead use 3D boxes as structured specifications: the user provides the input and output boxes of the edit, casting editing as a well-posed geometry problem. This ``thinking in boxes'' interface, where each box face is color-coded to convey 3D orientation, gives precise control over translation, rotation, scaling, and viewpoint changes in real images while preserving scene and object identity, and recovering previously unseen object regions. To ground transformations in scene appearance, we introduce a depth-aligned planar floor as a global reference frame, shaded with depth-aware cues. Conditioned on this structure, an image generator produces consistent results under large transformations. Trained in two stages -- on synthetic multi-object scenes and a small set of real-world videos from Objectron -- the system generalizes to complex, in-the-wild real images. Our method operates directly on real photographs and substantially outperforms recent state-of-the-art methods on large 3D edits.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "thinking in boxes" — a 3D box-based interface for precise spatial image editing (translation, rotation, scaling, viewpoint changes) that uses color-coded boxes and a depth-aligned floor reference to guide an image generator, outperforming prior methods on large 3D edits in real photographs.
AI.motivation: Text and 2D-conditioning interfaces provide weak, ambiguous control over spatial transformations in image editing, especially under large object motions and camera changes. Prior 3D box methods only offer loose conditioning without specifying exact transformations.
AI.method: Uses 3D boxes with color-coded faces as structured specifications (input/output boxes) to cast editing as a well-posed geometry problem. Introduces a depth-aligned planar floor as a global reference frame with depth-aware cues. Trained in two stages: synthetic multi-object scenes and a small set of real-world Objectron videos.
AI.result: Substantially outperforms recent state-of-the-art methods on large 3D edits in complex, in-the-wild real images. Preserves scene/object identity and recovers previously unseen object regions.
AI.conclusion: The "thinking in boxes" interface provides precise, interpretable control over 3D transformations in real photographs, enabling accurate large-motion edits that prior text/2D methods cannot achieve.

title: Critical Percolation as a Synthetic Data Model for Interpretability
comment: 21 pages, 10 figures, accepted to the Mechanistic Interpretability Workshop at ICML 2026
summary: Neural networks learn features that reflect the hierarchical, multi-scale structure of natural data. Synthetic datasets used to evaluate interpretability methods typically lack this structure, limiting their value as realistic toy models. To close this gap, we introduce a family of synthetic datasets consisting of hierarchical functions defined on critical mean-field percolation clusters embedded in a high-dimensional data space. The percolation data consists of sparse, low-dimensional fractal clusters with a power-law size distribution. Latent variables modeling a taxonomic hierarchy generate each data point's target value. The data model is analytically tractable with known critical exponents that fix its properties without requiring hyperparameter tuning. We leverage a mapping between percolation clusters, random trees, and additive coalescence to propose an almost linear-time algorithm to jointly sample a random tree and its hierarchical latent decomposition, enabling data generation at arbitrary scale. Using probing experiments, we find that the model's ground-truth latent variables can be linearly decoded from neural network activations. Together, sparsity, self-similarity, power-law statistics, and analytical tractability make critical percolation a principled testbed for interpretability research.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a family of synthetic datasets based on critical mean-field percolation clusters that exhibit hierarchical, multi-scale structure—mimicking real-world data—to serve as a more realistic testbed for evaluating interpretability methods in neural networks.
AI.motivation: Existing synthetic datasets used for interpretability research lack the hierarchical, multi-scale structure inherent in natural data, limiting their usefulness as realistic toy models for evaluating interpretability methods.
AI.method: The authors construct datasets from hierarchical functions defined on critical mean-field percolation clusters embedded in high-dimensional space. They leverage a mapping between percolation clusters, random trees, and additive coalescence to design an almost linear-time algorithm that jointly samples a random tree and its hierarchical latent decomposition.
AI.result: The data model is analytically tractable with known critical exponents, requires no hyperparameter tuning, and supports arbitrary-scale data generation. Probing experiments show that ground-truth latent variables can be linearly decoded from neural network activations.
AI.conclusion: The combination of sparsity, self-similarity, power-law statistics, and analytical tractability makes critical percolation a principled and scalable testbed for interpretability research.

title: UNIEGO: Proxies as Mediators for Unified Egocentric Video Representation Learning
summary: Egocentric video understanding is inherently limited by the narrow perspective of wearable cameras: a single viewpoint, a single modality, a single model cannot capture the full richness of human action. We argue that a truly expressive egocentric representation must subsume complementary knowledge across viewpoints, modalities, and foundation model representations, yet remain deployable from egocentric video alone. To this end, we introduce a hierarchical multi-teacher distillation framework that produces UNIEGO, a unified egocentric encoder trained with nine teachers spanning ego-exo viewpoints, RGB, depth, and skeleton modalities, and four foundation models. Rather than distilling directly from heterogeneous teachers whose incompatible architectures and feature geometries induce conflicting gradients, our framework interposes a layer of representation-specific Proxy models that translate diverse teacher knowledge into a homogeneous egocentric space. A second distillation stage, Selective Proxy Distillation (SPD), then adaptively selects, for each training sample, the subset of proxies that are both correct and confident, distilling exclusively from reliable supervision and suppressing erroneous signals. SPD is further stabilized by initializing UNIEGO as a learned convex combination of proxy parameters, placing the unified model in a well-conditioned region of the loss landscape before distillation begins. UNIEGO achieves state-of-the-art performance across three egocentric video understanding tasks - action recognition, video retrieval, and action segmentation on three challenging ego-exo benchmarks, outperforming naive multi-teacher distillation baselines and demonstrating that structured, proxy-mediated knowledge transfer yields richer and more discriminative egocentric representations.

AI.tldr: UNIEGO is a unified egocentric video encoder trained via hierarchical multi-teacher distillation from nine teachers (ego/exo views, RGB/depth/skeleton modalities, four foundation models) using Proxy models and Selective Proxy Distillation (SPD) to resolve conflicting gradients, achieving SOTA on action recognition, retrieval, and segmentation across three benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Egocentric video understanding is limited by single-viewpoint, single-modality, single-model representations. A truly expressive representation must integrate complementary knowledge across viewpoints, modalities, and foundation models while remaining deployable from egocentric video alone.
AI.method: A hierarchical multi-teacher distillation framework with two stages: (1) Representation-specific Proxy models translate heterogeneous teacher knowledge into a homogeneous egocentric space; (2) Selective Proxy Distillation (SPD) adaptively selects correct and confident proxies per sample, distilling reliable supervision while suppressing errors. UNIEGO is initialized as a convex combination of proxy parameters for well-conditioned optimization.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on action recognition, video retrieval, and action segmentation across three ego-exo benchmarks, outperforming naive multi-teacher distillation baselines.
AI.conclusion: Structured, proxy-mediated knowledge transfer yields richer and more discriminative egocentric representations than direct multi-teacher distillation, enabling unified understanding from egocentric video alone.

title: On the Variance of Temporal Difference Learning and its Reduction Using Control Variates
summary: We analyze the variance of temporal difference (TD) learning using the phased setting with tabular representation, and show that one of the mechanisms behind its ability to reduce variance is by effectively aggregating over a larger number of independent trajectories. Based on this insight, we demonstrate that (1) the variance of TD is asymptotically bounded from above by Monte Carlo (MC) estimators, and (2) shorter horizon updates incurs less variance for a fixed number of samples. Beyond TD, we show that Direct Advantage Estimation (DAE), a method for estimating the advantage function, can be seen as a type of regression-adjusted control variate, which achieves a tighter bound on the variance compared to TD in the large-sample limit. Finally, we numerically illustrate the behaviors of these estimators with carefully designed environments.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the variance of TD learning, showing it aggregates over independent trajectories to reduce variance, bounds its variance above by MC estimators, and finds shorter horizons reduce variance. It also shows DAE acts as a regression-adjusted control variate with tighter variance bounds than TD.
AI.motivation: To understand the mechanisms behind variance reduction in temporal difference (TD) learning and compare its variance properties to Monte Carlo estimators and other advantage estimation methods.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis using the phased setting with tabular representation, comparing variance of TD, MC, and DAE estimators. Demonstrates TD's variance is bounded by MC's, shorter horizons reduce variance, and DAE functions as a regression-adjusted control variate.
AI.result: (1) TD variance is asymptotically bounded above by MC estimators; (2) shorter horizon updates yield less variance for fixed sample size; (3) DAE achieves tighter variance bounds than TD in the large-sample limit.
AI.conclusion: TD learning reduces variance by effectively aggregating over independent trajectories, and DAE further improves upon TD via control variate techniques for advantage function estimation.

title: TimeProVe: Propose, then Verify for Efficient Long Video Temporal Reasoning in Activities of Daily Living
summary: Long Video Question Answering (LVQA) requires identifying sparse, query-relevant evidence within hours-long untrimmed videos. Existing approaches either process videos densely with large vision-language models (VLMs), incurring prohibitive computational cost, or rely on sparse caption-based reasoning, which often misses temporally localized and motion-centric evidence. We introduce TimeProVe, a cost-efficient hybrid framework for temporally grounded reasoning in long videos. TimeProVe first employs lightweight modules to generate action-grounded answer--evidence hypotheses and subsequently invokes an expensive VLM only for targeted verification. The core of our framework lies in the Action-based Candidate Evidence (ACE) module, which converts temporally localized actions into query-conditioned candidate answers and supporting evidence windows through lightweight LLM reasoning. We further introduce OpenTSUBench (OTB), an open-ended benchmark designed to evaluate temporally grounded reasoning in real-world Activities of Daily Living (ADL) scenarios. Experiments show that TimeProVe outperforms the strongest baseline on OTB by 7.3%, while reducing VLM calls by 75% and inference cost by 93%. Furthermore, without explicit temporal grounding training, TimeProVe achieves competitive performance on Charades-STA, and reaches state-of-the-art results when enhanced with grounding VLMs.

AI.tldr: TimeProVe is a hybrid framework for Long Video Question Answering that uses lightweight modules to generate action-grounded hypotheses and invokes expensive VLMs only for targeted verification, achieving 7.3% improvement over baselines while reducing VLM calls by 75% and inference cost by 93%.
AI.motivation: Long Video Question Answering (LVQA) is computationally prohibitive when processing hours-long videos densely with large VLMs, while sparse caption-based methods miss temporally localized and motion-centric evidence. A cost-efficient solution is needed.
AI.method: TimeProVe uses a two-stage hybrid framework: (1) lightweight modules (including the ACE module) generate action-grounded answer-evidence hypotheses via lightweight LLM reasoning, converting temporally localized actions into query-conditioned candidate answers and supporting evidence windows; (2) an expensive VLM is invoked only for targeted verification of these candidates.
AI.result: TimeProVe outperforms the strongest baseline on OpenTSUBench (OTB) by 7.3%, reduces VLM calls by 75%, and reduces inference cost by 93%. It achieves competitive performance on Charades-STA without explicit temporal grounding training and state-of-the-art results when enhanced with grounding VLMs.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid lightweight-then-expensive verification approach is highly effective for temporally grounded reasoning in long videos, dramatically reducing computational cost while improving accuracy. The introduced OpenTSUBench benchmark supports further research in ADL scenarios.

title: Train, Retrieve, or Both? A Four-Arm Head-to-Head for Correct Statutory Citation on the Ontario Residential Tenancies Act
summary: Self-represented tenants, landlords, and help-desk staff need to be pointed at the provision of law that actually governs a question, with a correct statutory citation. We study this task on the Ontario Residential Tenancies Act, 2006 (RTA) and its core regulation, asking the operator's question empirically: is fine-tuning enough, or is hybrid retrieval needed? We run a four-arm head-to-head on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct (base zero-shot, LoRA SFT-only, RAG-only, and an SFT+RAG hybrid), scored on citation exact-match (section+subsection) over a small, human-verification-pending real eval set. The base model cannot cite the RTA and SFT-only mis-recalls sections; retrieval is essential and drives hallucination to zero by construction; and the SFT+RAG hybrid scores highest at 0.481 exact-match with zero hallucinated citations. Its edge comes from SFT making provision selection more robust to the higher-recall candidate sets that hurt zero-shot RAG. Notably, this cheap bge-small hybrid matches or beats a pipeline built on bigger, specialized retrieval models (a larger embedder and a cross-encoder reranker), and a larger/improved training set does not help either: strong statutory-citation performance here does not require specialized retrieval models or more data. The artifact zeroes hallucination and clears the lift-over-base bar but does not reach the aspirational 0.70 exact-match target. All results are on a small, human-verification-pending real eval set and are reported as preliminary.

AI.tldr: This paper studies statutory citation in the Ontario Residential Tenancies Act, comparing four approaches (base zero-shot, LoRA SFT-only, RAG-only, SFT+RAG hybrid). The SFT+RAG hybrid achieves the best exact-match score (0.481) with zero hallucination, outperforming larger specialized retrieval models. Results are preliminary on a small eval set.
AI.motivation: Self-represented tenants, landlords, and help-desk staff need accurate statutory citations for legal questions. The paper empirically investigates whether fine-tuning alone suffices or hybrid retrieval is necessary for this task.
AI.method: Four-arm comparison on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct: base zero-shot, LoRA SFT-only, RAG-only (bge-small), and SFT+RAG hybrid. Evaluated on citation exact-match (section+subsection) over a small human-verification-pending eval set of Ontario Residential Tenancies Act queries.
AI.result: Base model cannot cite the RTA; SFT-only mis-recalls sections; RAG drives hallucination to zero; SFT+RAG hybrid scores highest at 0.481 exact-match with zero hallucinated citations. The cheap bge-small hybrid matches or beats pipelines using larger embedders and cross-encoder rerankers. A larger training set did not improve performance.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval is essential for statutory citation; SFT enhances zero-shot RAG by making provision selection more robust to high-recall candidate sets. Strong performance does not require specialized retrieval models or more data. The artifact clears the lift-over-base bar but does not reach the aspirational 0.70 exact-match target.

title: JanusMesh: Fast and Zero-Shot 3D Visual Illusion Generation via Cross-Space Denoising
summary: Creating 3D visual illusions, a single 3D mesh that reveals entirely different semantics from various viewing angles, is a fascinating but tough challenge. Existing optimization-based methods are slow and can produce oversaturated colors. In contrast, naive stitching approaches fail to produce geometrically coherent objects. This results in visible unnatural seams and semantic leaks. In this paper, we present a fast and training-free framework for generating text-driven 3D visual illusions. Our approach decouples the generation into two stages. First, we propose a cross-space dual-branch denoising process. This process dynamically decodes 3D latents into voxel space for CLIP-guided orientation alignment and Signed Distance Field (SDF) blending, which ensures seamless geometric fusion. Second, we introduce a view-conditioned texture synthesis module that projects and aggregates view-specific 2D diffusion priors onto the fused geometry. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method generates highly realistic, dual-semantic 3D illusions in just 3-5 minutes. It significantly outperforms existing methods in geometric integrity, semantic recognizability, and efficiency. Project page: https://siang1105.github.io/JanusMesh.github.io/

AI.tldr: A fast, training-free framework for generating text-driven 3D visual illusions that decouples generation into a cross-space dual-branch denoising process for geometric fusion and a view-conditioned texture synthesis module, producing realistic dual-semantic 3D illusions in 3-5 minutes.
AI.motivation: Existing optimization-based methods for 3D visual illusions are slow and produce oversaturated colors, while naive stitching approaches fail to produce geometrically coherent objects with unnatural seams and semantic leaks.
AI.method: A two-stage framework: (1) cross-space dual-branch denoising process that dynamically decodes 3D latents into voxel space for CLIP-guided orientation alignment and Signed Distance Field (SDF) blending for seamless geometric fusion; (2) view-conditioned texture synthesis module that projects and aggregates view-specific 2D diffusion priors onto the fused geometry.
AI.result: Generates highly realistic, dual-semantic 3D illusions in just 3-5 minutes, significantly outperforming existing methods in geometric integrity, semantic recognizability, and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed training-free framework achieves fast, high-quality 3D visual illusion generation with superior geometric coherence and semantic clarity compared to prior work.

title: Judging to Improve: A De-biased VLM-as-3D-Judge Protocol for Single-Image 3D Generation
summary: A companion study established a de-biased, cross-model VLM-as-3D-judge that reliably ranks single-image-to-3D mesh quality where cheap geometry and CLIP proxies fall short. This paper asks: can that judge's preferences specialize a strong open generator, TRELLIS, on one asset class (furniture), cheaply and without human labels? Taking the judge from ranking to optimization is where the work lives. Pushing a VLM judge into the training and evaluation loop exposes failure modes ranking never triggered, so our contribution is an optimization-grade hardening of the judge: a training judge (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) held distinct from an evaluation judge (InternVL3-8B) to break circularity; position-bias correction; and fixes for three failure modes (image overload, geometry-hiding splat renders, and reference-free judging that rewards clean-but-wrong outputs), with calibration evidence (clear-gap win-rate 0.83-1.0; base-vs-base ~0.5). Using this protocol as an independent evaluator, and working only from public models and data with lightweight parameter-efficient adaptation, we find our methods match the strong base rather than exceed it. Independent base samples carry essentially no learnable preference (0.94 order-flip rate), so signal must be engineered by quality-contrastive construction. Across six adaptation methods, two input regimes, and a severity sweep, the most targeted - conditioner repair under severe degradation - reaches parity (0.50) with the base, while no method clears the >=65% win-rate target. The result is mechanistic: clean inputs saturate the judge, flow-DIT fine-tuning washes out through the sampler, and conditioning repair is the locus that moves geometry. Win-rates are directional at n=8 objects. Matching a strong public-data base with cheap adaptation is itself informative: exceeding it needs more than lightweight PEFT on public data, and the judge protocol is reusable.

AI.tldr: This paper attempts to use a VLM-as-judge to optimize a 3D mesh generator (TRELLIS) for furniture, but finds lightweight adaptation fails to exceed the strong base model, revealing that preference optimization requires quality-contrastive construction and that matching a strong public-data base with cheap PEFT is the ceiling without more resources.
AI.motivation: Can a VLM judge's preferences be used to specialize a strong open 3D generator (TRELLIS) on a specific asset class (furniture) cheaply and without human labels, by converting a ranking judge into an optimization signal?
AI.method: The authors harden a VLM judge for optimization by: (1) using separate training (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) and evaluation (InternVL3-8B) judges to break circularity; (2) position-bias correction; (3) fixing three failure modes (image overload, geometry-hiding splat renders, reference-free judging). They apply parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) across six adaptation methods and two input regimes on TRELLIS, with quality-contrastive construction of training pairs.
AI.result: No adaptation method exceeds the base model (target >=65% win-rate); conditioner repair under severe degradation reaches parity (0.50 win-rate). Independent base samples show 0.94 order-flip rate (no learnable preference). Flow-DiT fine-tuning washes out through the sampler; conditioning repair is the only locus that moves geometry. Win-rates are directional at n=8.
AI.conclusion: Matching a strong public-data base with cheap adaptation is informative but exceeding it requires more than lightweight PEFT on public data. The judge protocol is reusable for future work.

title: CRAX: Fast Safe Reinforcement Learning Benchmarking
summary: Safety is a core concern for deploying reinforcement learning (RL) agents in real-world domains such as robotics and autonomous driving. While benchmarks have been central to progress in RL, existing safety benchmarks with high-fidelity 3D physics remain computationally slow, limiting large-scale experimentation and rapid prototyping. To address this gap, we propose CRAX (Constrained RL Accelerated with JAX). Built on top of the MuJoCo XLA (MJX) physics engine with realistic 3D dynamics, CRAX leverages vectorized operations and hardware acceleration, yielding up to ~100x speedups over comparable CPU-based safety benchmarks. The benchmark features six environment suites and three agent-specific tasks, each spanning three difficulty levels. Evaluating six popular safe RL methods shows that no single approach dominates across all tasks, and reveals the trade-offs between performance and safety. We find that curriculum learning across difficulty levels and safety transfer can improve performance over direct training in harder settings.

AI.tldr: CRAX is a high-speed safety benchmark for constrained RL built on MJX, achieving ~100x speedups over CPU-based benchmarks, featuring six environments and three agent tasks across three difficulty levels, and revealing trade-offs between performance and safety across six evaluated methods.
AI.motivation: Existing safety RL benchmarks with high-fidelity 3D physics are computationally slow, limiting large-scale experimentation and rapid prototyping.
AI.method: CRAX is built on the MuJoCo XLA (MJX) physics engine, leveraging vectorized operations and hardware acceleration (JAX) to achieve up to ~100x speedups over CPU-based safety benchmarks. It includes six environment suites and three agent-specific tasks, each with three difficulty levels.
AI.result: Up to ~100x speedup over comparable CPU-based benchmarks; evaluation of six safe RL methods shows no single approach dominates across all tasks, revealing performance-safety trade-offs; curriculum learning and safety transfer improve performance over direct training in harder settings.
AI.conclusion: CRAX enables efficient large-scale safety RL experimentation, and curriculum learning with safety transfer can be beneficial for training in challenging settings.

title: Towards Modality-imbalanced Federated Graph Learning: A Data Synthesis-based Approach
summary: MultiModal Federated Graph Learning (MM-FGL) offers a natural collaborative training paradigm, but its practical deployment is challenged by two granularities of modality imbalance. Client-level imbalance occurs when certain clients lack entire modalities, while node-level imbalance occurs when individual nodes exhibit missing visual or textual attributes. While several relevant studies exist, our investigation reveals that they predominantly target graph-agnostic or centralized scenarios, rendering them difficult to adapt directly. To address these challenges, we formalize modality-imbalanced MM-FGL as an implicit graph-aware latent semantic representation synthesis problem. This paradigm recovers missing modal semantics directly within the representation space, thereby maximizing alignment with the original data's semantic distribution and mitigating the high variance induced by missing modalities. To this end, we propose FedMGS (Federated Modality-aware Graph Synthesis), which integrates three core components. The availability-aware graph encoder prevents missing modalities from contaminating local structural propagation. The prototype-guided latent semantic synthesizer establishes cross-client semantic anchors for unavailable modalities. The reliability-calibrated semantic fusion mechanism regulates the impact of recovered latent representations prior to predictive readout. Extensive experiments on four tasks show that FedMGS consistently outperforms competitive baselines with gains up to 17.41% with best efficiency-performance tradeoff.

AI.tldr: FedMGS addresses dual granularities of modality imbalance (client-level and node-level) in MultiModal Federated Graph Learning by formulating it as an implicit graph-aware latent semantic representation synthesis problem, integrating availability-aware graph encoding, prototype-guided latent semantic synthesis, and reliability-calibrated semantic fusion, achieving up to 17.41% improvement over baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing MM-FGL methods fail to handle two granularities of modality imbalance (client-level missing entire modalities, node-level missing visual/textual attributes), and prior graph-agnostic or centralized approaches cannot be directly adapted to this federated graph setting.
AI.method: Formulates modality-imbalanced MM-FGL as an implicit graph-aware latent semantic representation synthesis problem with three components: (1) availability-aware graph encoder that prevents missing modalities from contaminating structural propagation; (2) prototype-guided latent semantic synthesizer that establishes cross-client semantic anchors; (3) reliability-calibrated semantic fusion mechanism that regulates recovered latent representations before predictive readout.
AI.result: Outperforms competitive baselines on four tasks with gains up to 17.41% while achieving the best efficiency-performance tradeoff.
AI.conclusion: Directly recovering missing modal semantics in the representation space effectively aligns with the original data's semantic distribution and mitigates high variance induced by missing modalities in federated graph learning.

title: The Significance of Style Diversity in Annotation-Free Synthetic Data Generation
summary: Generating high-utility synthetic data for intent classification typically requires human-annotated seed data, which is often unavailable in fast-paced industrial settings. In this paper, we propose a framework for synthetic dialogue generation that works entirely without human-annotated data, relying solely on intent definitions. Our proposed dialogue generation framework utilizes two different types of topic and style attributes to improve data diversity. Also, we propose two novel post-hoc stylization models called Univ and Exam to transform synthetic LLM-generated utterances into more varied, human-like linguistic styles. To enhance data quality, we utilize an LLM-as-a-judge filtering process. Experimental results on both industrial and public datasets demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves up to 93.3% of the performance obtained using human-annotated training data. Crucially, the findings reveal that style diversity is more critical than topic diversity for synthetic data utility, as it prevents models from learning spurious stylistic correlations. Furthermore, the study shows that incorporating style attributes during the generation process is more effective than post-hoc style adaptation.

AI.tldr: A framework for generating synthetic dialogue data for intent classification without human-annotated seed data, using only intent definitions, topic/style attributes, post-hoc stylization models (Univ, Exam), and LLM-as-a-judge filtering, achieving up to 93.3% of human-annotated data performance.
AI.motivation: High-utility synthetic data for intent classification typically requires human-annotated seed data, which is often unavailable in fast-paced industrial settings.
AI.method: (1) Synthetic dialogue generation using only intent definitions with topic and style attributes for diversity; (2) Two post-hoc stylization models (Univ and Exam) for transforming LLM utterances into varied human-like styles; (3) LLM-as-a-judge filtering for quality control.
AI.result: Achieves up to 93.3% of human-annotated training data performance on industrial and public datasets.
AI.conclusion: Style diversity is more critical than topic diversity for synthetic data utility; incorporating style attributes during generation is more effective than post-hoc style adaptation.

title: Direct Advantage Estimation for Scalable and Sample-efficient Deep Reinforcement Learning
summary: Direct Advantage Estimation (DAE) has been shown to improve the sample efficiency of deep reinforcement learning algorithms. However, its reliance on full environment observability limits its applicability in realistic settings, and its requirement to model transition probabilities incurs substantial computational overhead for high-dimensional observations. In the present work, we address both limitations. First, we extend the theoretical framework of DAE to partially observable domains with minimal modifications. Second, we reduce its computational complexity by introducing discrete latent dynamics models that efficiently approximate transition probabilities. We evaluate our approach on the Arcade Learning Environment and find that DAE scales effectively with function approximator capacity while retaining high sample efficiency.

AI.tldr: This work extends Direct Advantage Estimation (DAE) to partially observable environments and reduces its computational complexity using discrete latent dynamics models, achieving efficient scaling with function approximator capacity on the Arcade Learning Environment.
AI.motivation: DAE improves sample efficiency but is limited by requiring full observability and incurring high computational costs from modeling transition probabilities in high-dimensional settings.
AI.method: Extends DAE's theoretical framework to partially observable domains with minimal modifications, and introduces discrete latent dynamics models to efficiently approximate transition probabilities, reducing computational overhead.
AI.result: On the Arcade Learning Environment, DAE scales effectively with function approximator capacity while retaining high sample efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed modifications make DAE applicable to more realistic settings by removing the full observability requirement and reducing computational complexity, without sacrificing sample efficiency.

title: Pseudo-Feature Padding: A Lightweight Defense Against False Data Injection in Power Grids
summary: Deep Neural Networks DNNs have achieved remarkable accuracy in various tasks including their application in CyberPhysical Systems CPS for detecting False Data Injection Attacks FDIA during critical operations However the unique infrastructure of CPS makes DNNs vulnerable to exploitation by attackers aiming to evade detection Additionally the distinct nature of CPS presents challenges for conventional defense mechanisms against FDIA This paper proposes an innovative defense framework that strengthens DNNs against such attacks by introducing an additional input layer that performs padding in the input samples using pseudofeature values derived from the inputs statistical distribution This padding increases the input dimensionality in a randomized and dataaware manner making adversarial attacks computationally infeasible due to the nontransferable nature of crafted perturbations and the unpredictability of the padded structure Our method is lightweight modelagnostic and requires no modifications to the core architecture making it highly deployable in realworld CPS settings We evaluated our framework on critical power grid applications such as state estimation using the IEEE 14bus 30bus 118bus and 300bus systems Experiments under adversarial settings demonstrate that our padding strategy significantly improves model robustness with negligible impact on performance and effectively mitigates attacks that would otherwise bypass conventional defenses

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a lightweight defense framework for DNNs in CPS that adds randomized, data-aware padding using pseudo-features to input samples, making adversarial attacks computationally infeasible by increasing input dimensionality and preventing perturbation transferability.
AI.motivation: DNNs used in CPS for detecting FDIA are vulnerable to adversarial exploitation, and conventional defense mechanisms are ineffective due to CPS's unique infrastructure.
AI.method: An additional input layer performs padding on input samples using pseudo-feature values derived from the input's statistical distribution, increasing input dimensionality in a randomized and data-aware manner.
AI.result: Evaluated on IEEE 14-bus, 30-bus, 118-bus, and 300-bus power grid systems; the padding strategy significantly improves model robustness with negligible performance impact and effectively mitigates attacks that bypass conventional defenses.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method is lightweight, model-agnostic, requires no core architecture modifications, and is highly deployable in real-world CPS settings.

title: On the Redundancy of Timestep Embeddings in Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models rely heavily on explicit timestep embeddings to modulate the denoising process across various noise scales. In this work, we challenge the necessity of these temporal signals by analyzing their impact on U-Net and Diffusion Transformer architectures.
