Beyond empirical evidence, we provide a theoretical framework demonstrating that, under certain conditions, the global minimizer of the diffusion training objective can be achieved without explicit timestep conditioning. Our findings reveal a surprising robustness when timestep embeddings are completely removed. Extensive ablation studies on the CelebA and CIFAR-10 datasets show that these time-agnostic models can maintain high structural fidelity and even surpass their conditioned counterparts in competitive metrics, including FID, precision, and recall. Our analysis suggests these architectures can implicitly infer noise scales from the corrupted input under specific assumptions, rendering explicit temporal conditioning redundant. This study challenges long-standing temporal conditioning paradigms and paves the way for more efficient and structurally focused generative architectures.

AI.tldr: Diffusion models can achieve competitive or superior performance without explicit timestep embeddings, challenging the long-standing assumption that temporal conditioning is necessary for effective denoising.
AI.motivation: To challenge the necessity of explicit timestep embeddings in diffusion models by analyzing their impact on U-Net and Diffusion Transformer architectures, both empirically and theoretically.
AI.method: The authors provide a theoretical framework demonstrating that the global minimizer of the diffusion training objective can be achieved without explicit timestep conditioning under certain conditions, supported by extensive ablation studies on CelebA and CIFAR-10 datasets comparing time-agnostic vs. conditioned models. Experiments on U-Net and Diffusion Transformer architectures evaluate FID, precision, and recall metrics.
AI.result: Time-agnostic models maintain high structural fidelity and can surpass conditioned counterparts in competitive metrics (FID, precision, recall) on CelebA and CIFAR-10 datasets. The architectures can implicitly infer noise scales from corrupted input under specific assumptions.
AI.conclusion: Explicit temporal conditioning is rendered redundant under specific assumptions, as architectures can implicitly infer noise scales. This challenges long-standing temporal conditioning paradigms and paves the way for more efficient, structurally focused generative architectures.

title: Neural network surrogates with uncertainty quantification for inverse problems in partial differential equations
summary: Inverse problems for differential equations arise throughout science and engineering, where one seeks to infer unknown model parameters from noisy or incomplete observations. Traditional numerical methods for these problems are often computationally expensive, particularly in Bayesian settings where evaluating the likelihood becomes costly for complex forward models and high-dimensional parameter spaces. To address this challenge, we introduce DeepGaLA, a neural-network surrogate for differential equation solvers that provides uncertainty-aware predictions, reducing overconfident inference when training data are limited. To evaluate the fidelity of the surrogate-induced posterior approximations in practice, we show that a short run of delayed-acceptance Markov chain Monte Carlo can serve as an effective diagnostic. Across a range of numerical experiments, DeepGaLA delivers forward-model approximations with accuracy comparable to established Gaussian-process surrogates, while better maintaining efficiency as parameter dimension grows. Moreover, it can incorporate differential-equation constraints, including in nonlinear settings. Overall, these results indicate that uncertainty-quantified neural surrogates can enable scalable and reliable Bayesian inference for inverse problems in complex systems.

AI.tldr: DeepGaLA is a neural-network surrogate for differential equation solvers that provides uncertainty-aware predictions for Bayesian inverse problems, using delayed-acceptance MCMC as a diagnostic tool. It matches Gaussian-process surrogate accuracy while scaling better with parameter dimension and can incorporate nonlinear differential-equation constraints.
AI.motivation: Traditional numerical methods for Bayesian inverse problems involving differential equations are computationally expensive, especially for complex forward models and high-dimensional parameter spaces. There is a need for scalable surrogates that provide uncertainty quantification to avoid overconfident inference when training data are limited.
AI.method: DeepGaLA: a neural-network surrogate for differential equation solvers that produces uncertainty-aware predictions. A short run of delayed-acceptance Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) is used as a diagnostic to evaluate the fidelity of the surrogate-induced posterior approximations.
AI.result: DeepGaLA delivers forward-model approximations with accuracy comparable to established Gaussian-process surrogates, while maintaining better efficiency as parameter dimension grows. It can also incorporate differential-equation constraints, including in nonlinear settings.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-quantified neural surrogates like DeepGaLA can enable scalable and reliable Bayesian inference for inverse problems in complex systems.

title: Sparsity, Superposition, and Forgetting: A Mechanistic Study of Representation Retention in Continual Learning
summary: Continual learning (CL) systems often forget previously acquired knowledge, yet the mechanisms driving forgetting remain hard to isolate in practice because real datasets entangle many factors. We present a controlled, toy-world framework that makes these mechanisms observable and testable. Using a synthetic generator-separator pipeline, we define ground-truth latent features, build tasks with tunable sparsity and overlap, and introduce measurable quantities for representation strength and superposition (directional overlap among features). We then study retention dynamics-the temporal change of representation strength by fitting sparse dynamical relations (via SINDy) between retention, superposition, and exposure history. A complementary task-level analysis based on effective rank characterizes how representational capacity is allocated across tasks. Our controlled experiments yield three takeaways. (1) Superposition tends to increase over time with transient dips at task boundaries, suggesting boundary-specific interference rather than steady drift. (2) Higher feature sparsity induces more superposition yet does not inevitably cause forgetting; when representations remain strong, forgetting can be reduced despite overlap. (3) Task-level effective rank grows with sparsity, indicating broader capacity usage under sparse regimes. Together, these results nuance the common intuition that more superposition leads to more forgetting by showing that overlap interacts with representation strength and capacity allocation. Our toy analysis provides falsifiable hypotheses and diagnostic tools for CL.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a synthetic toy-world framework for continual learning that makes forgetting mechanisms observable by controlling latent features, sparsity, and overlap. Using SINDy-based dynamical analysis and effective rank, they find superposition increases with transient dips at task boundaries, higher sparsity increases superposition but not necessarily forgetting, and that forgetting depends on the interaction between representation strength, superposition, and capacity allocation.
AI.motivation: Real continual learning datasets entangle many factors, making it hard to isolate the mechanisms driving catastrophic forgetting. The authors aim to create a controlled framework where these mechanisms become observable and testable.
AI.method: A synthetic generator-separator pipeline with ground-truth latent features, tunable sparsity and overlap. Uses SINDy to fit sparse dynamical relations between retention, superposition, and exposure history. Task-level effective rank analysis characterizes representational capacity allocation.
AI.result: (1) Superposition increases over time with transient dips at task boundaries (boundary-specific interference). (2) Higher feature sparsity increases superposition but does not inevitably cause forgetting if representations remain strong. (3) Task-level effective rank grows with sparsity, indicating broader capacity usage under sparse regimes.
AI.conclusion: The common intuition that superposition leads to forgetting is nuanced: overlap interacts with representation strength and capacity allocation. The framework provides falsifiable hypotheses and diagnostic tools for CL research.

title: Evolutionary Two-Stage Hyperparameter Optimization Strategies for Physics-Informed Neural Networks
comment: Equal advising: Daria Pugacheva and Fedor Ratnikov. Accepted to the ICLR 2026 Workshop on AI and PDEs
summary: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) solve Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) by embedding physical laws into neural network training. However, their performance suffers from unstable convergence, training plateaus, and strong sensitivity to architectural and optimization hyperparameters due to the highly non-convex and multi-term structure of the physics-informed loss. In this setting, the outer-loop hyperparameter search is a noisy and black-box optimization problem over heterogeneous parameters, where classical local or gradient-based strategies are easily trapped in suboptimal regions. Evolutionary algorithms, with their population-based exploration and ability to handle mixed, non-differentiable search spaces, provide a more robust mechanism for discovering promising configurations. We propose and investigate a two-stage approach based on evolutionary algorithms that combines exploration and exploitation parts of PINNs training to improve solution accuracy and robustness under fixed computational budgets. In the first stage, we perform low-fidelity training runs with truncated epochs to rapidly screen candidate configurations, treating hyperparameter selection as a black-box outer-loop problem. In the second stage, only the most promising candidates are fully trained with standard gradient-based optimizers to refine the solution. Evaluated on three popular problems, namely Advection, Klein-Gordon and Helmholtz equations, our method consistently outperforms standard training and achieves significantly lower mean error within constrained computational resources.

AI.tldr: PINNs suffer from unstable convergence and hyperparameter sensitivity. The paper proposes a two-stage evolutionary algorithm approach: first, low-fidelity training screens hyperparameters as a black-box optimization; second, top candidates are fully trained with gradient-based optimizers. This method achieves lower mean error on Advection, Klein-Gordon, and Helmholtz equations under fixed computational budgets.
AI.motivation: PINNs face unstable convergence, training plateaus, and high sensitivity to hyperparameters due to the non-convex, multi-term physics-informed loss. Existing local/gradient-based hyperparameter tuning methods get trapped in suboptimal regions, necessitating a more robust optimization strategy.
AI.method: A two-stage evolutionary algorithm approach: Stage 1 uses low-fidelity training with truncated epochs to treat hyperparameter selection as a black-box outer-loop optimization; Stage 2 fully trains the top candidates using standard gradient-based optimizers for refinement.
AI.result: Evaluated on Advection, Klein-Gordon, and Helmholtz equations, the method consistently outperforms standard training, achieving significantly lower mean error under constrained computational budgets.
AI.conclusion: Evolutionary algorithms provide a robust mechanism for hyperparameter search in PINNs, and the two-stage approach effectively balances exploration and exploitation to improve solution accuracy and robustness within fixed computational resources.

title: Data Bias Mitigation under Coverage Constraints & The Price of Fairness
summary: Machine learning models have been shown to exhibit discriminatory outcomes or degraded performance for individuals at the intersection of multiple sensitive attributes, such as race and gender. This stems in part from two interrelated challenges: the lack of principled measures for quantifying bias (potentially intersectional), and insufficient representation of intersectional subgroups in training data. We extend a recent bias mitigation framework to incorporate coverage constraints that enforce sufficient representation across groups, including intersectional subgroups. Since achieving exactly zero bias for all groups may not be data efficient (meaning it may require large amounts of data), our solution trades small approximation errors in bias for greater data efficiency while satisfying coverage constraints. We also formulate bias mitigation as an integer linear program that optimizes over all mitigation strategies, and characterize the price of fairness, the minimum data modification cost, as a function of fairness tolerance. This is essential both for legal compliance, where regulations may mandate specific fairness thresholds, and for data governance, enabling practitioners to make informed trade-offs between bias reduction and data modification (particularly, data purchasing) costs. We evaluate our techniques on publicly available datasets, demonstrating that bias mitigation via our framework preserves predictive accuracy across multiple classifiers, and that coverage constraints, while motivated by statistical considerations, are essential for preserving downstream ML performance.

AI.tldr: This paper extends a bias mitigation framework with coverage constraints to ensure representation across intersectional subgroups (e.g., race & gender), formulates bias mitigation as an integer linear program to trade off small bias approximation errors for data efficiency, and characterizes the price of fairness in terms of data modification costs.
AI.motivation: Machine learning models exhibit discriminatory outcomes or degraded performance at the intersection of multiple sensitive attributes (e.g., race and gender), due to lack of principled bias measures and insufficient representation of intersectional subgroups in training data.
AI.method: The authors extend a bias mitigation framework with coverage constraints for intersectional subgroups, formulate bias mitigation as an integer linear program optimizing over all mitigation strategies, and introduce a trade-off where small bias approximation errors yield greater data efficiency while satisfying coverage constraints.
AI.result: Evaluation on public datasets shows the framework preserves predictive accuracy across multiple classifiers; coverage constraints, motivated by statistical considerations, are essential for preserving downstream ML performance. The price of fairness (minimum data modification cost) is characterized as a function of fairness tolerance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach enables informed trade-offs between bias reduction and data modification/purchasing costs, addressing both legal compliance (specific fairness thresholds) and data governance needs.

title: Agentic Symbolic Search: Characterizing PDEs Beyond Hand-crafted Expressions, Meshes, and Neural Networks
summary: Mathematicians understand a PDE solution through mathematical structures rather than tables of computed values. Historically, this has been the product of mathematical analysis, carried out by hand for each problem individually. Neither numerical simulation nor neural networks produce those structures directly. We propose Agentic Symbolic Search (ASYS), a prior-guided framework in which an agent translates PDE theory, public problem constraints, and accumulated search experience into testable differentiable symbolic programs. The mathematical forms are refined under evolutionary search, while their continuous parameters are fit by gradient-based optimization. This makes the search an automated form of inductive-bias injection rather than blind symbolic regression. For problems with known analytical forms, ASYS recovers these forms naturally; for other problems, ASYS constructs analytical approximations which can guide mathematicians toward further analysis. In our experiments, across five problems spanning bounded dynamics, finite-time blow-up, and free-boundary focusing, ASYS produces interpretable representations, including a geometric interface formula for Allen-Cahn 2D dynamics and a nine-parameter contraction law for Keller-Segel chemotactic blow-up, in settings where no closed-form description was previously available. ASYS shows the possibility of a new paradigm for characterizing PDE solutions, beyond handcrafted analytical solutions, mesh-based numerical solutions, and neural network approximations.

AI.tldr: ASYS is a prior-guided framework using an agent to translate PDE theory into differentiable symbolic programs, refined by evolutionary search and gradient optimization, producing interpretable analytical approximations for PDE solutions where no closed-form descriptions previously existed.
AI.motivation: Traditional PDE understanding relies on handcrafted mathematical analysis per problem. Numerical simulations and neural networks produce computed values or approximations but not the structural mathematical forms that mathematicians need for insight.
AI.method: An agent translates PDE theory, constraints, and search experience into differentiable symbolic programs. Symbolic forms are refined via evolutionary search while continuous parameters are optimized through gradient-based methods, enabling automated inductive-bias injection rather than blind symbolic regression.
AI.result: Across five problems (bounded dynamics, finite-time blow-up, free-boundary focusing), ASYS recovers known analytical forms, produces a geometric interface formula for Allen-Cahn 2D dynamics, and constructs a nine-parameter contraction law for Keller-Segel chemotactic blow-up where no closed-form description previously existed.
AI.conclusion: ASYS demonstrates a new paradigm for characterizing PDE solutions beyond handcrafted analytical solutions, mesh-based numerical methods, and neural network approximations, enabling automated discovery of interpretable mathematical structures.

title: Fisher-Geometric Sharpness and the Implicit Bias of SGD toward Flat Minima
summary: A widely held intuition in deep learning is that stochastic gradient descent (SGD) implicitly favors flat minima and that flat minima generalize better, but standard Euclidean measures of flatness such as the trace or maximum eigenvalue of the loss Hessian are not invariant under reparametrizations that preserve the network function, which undermines the theoretical foundations of this narrative. In this study we resolve this issue by grounding flatness in the Riemannian geometry of the statistical manifold induced by the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM). We define Riemannian sharpness mathematically and prove that it is invariant under smooth, function-preserving reparametrizations, which directly addresses the critique of Dinh et al. in the paper ``Sharp minima can generalize for deep nets''.We note that this invariance is a property of the true FIM; the diagonal empirical estimator used in practice (and in all experiments below) inherits invariance only approximately, and exact invariance under arbitrary reparametrizations would require structured estimators such as K-FAC. We formalize the gradient noise of mini-batch SGD as having a covariance structure proportional to the FIM, derive the stationary distribution of the resulting stochastic differential equation, and then show that the probability mass is exponentially concentrated at Riemannian-flat minima. A PAC-Bayes generalization bound controlled explicitly by SR formally links this geometric bias to test performance. Our experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 confirm that SR reliably tracks generalization in ways that Euclidean sharpness does not, and that its scaling with $η/B$ matches the theoretical predictions. Together these results provide a rigorous, reparametrization-invariant account of why flat minima generalize.

AI.tldr: The paper resolves the reparametrization-invariance critique of flat minima theory by defining Riemannian sharpness (SR) via the Fisher Information Matrix's induced geometry. It proves SR is invariant under smooth function-preserving reparametrizations, shows SGD's stationary distribution concentrates exponentially at Riemannian-flat minima, and provides a PAC-Bayes bound linking SR to generalization. Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 confirm SR tracks generalization better than Euclidean measures.
AI.motivation: The standard intuition that SGD favors flat minima and flat minima generalize better is undermined because Euclidean flatness measures (e.g., trace/eigenvalue of Hessian) are not invariant under reparametrizations that preserve the network function, as pointed out by Dinh et al. The authors aim to resolve this foundational issue by grounding flatness in a reparametrization-invariant geometric framework.
AI.method: The authors define Riemannian sharpness using the Fisher Information Matrix's induced Riemannian geometry on the statistical manifold. They prove invariance under smooth function-preserving reparametrizations for the true FIM. They formalize mini-batch SGD's gradient noise as having covariance proportional to the FIM, derive the stationary distribution of the corresponding SDE, and show exponential concentration at Riemannian-flat minima. A PAC-Bayes bound controlled by SR links this bias to generalization.
AI.result: Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 show that Riemannian sharpness (SR) reliably tracks generalization where Euclidean sharpness fails, and its scaling with η/B matches theoretical predictions.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a rigorous, reparametrization-invariant account of why flat minima generalize, resolving the theoretical critique while maintaining consistency with empirical observations.

title: UltraQuant: 4-bit KV Caching for Context-Heavy Agents
summary: Context-heavy agents place unusual pressure on the key-value (KV) cache: long prefixes are reused across many short turns, while concurrency determines whether the serving system can keep GPUs utilized. We study 4-bit KV-cache compression for this setting, using TurboQuant-style rotation and codebook quantization as a quality anchor and vLLM FP8 KV caching as the deployment anchor. We report three contributions. First, we frame 4-bit KV caching around multi-round agent workloads where task quality, cache residency, and serving throughput must be measured jointly. Second, we describe the practical design choices needed to make the 4-bit path robust, including asymmetric K/V treatment, Walsh-Hadamard rotation, QJL removal, and block-scale variants. Third, we present serving optimizations on AMD GPUs, including optimized decode-attention kernels and UltraQuant, an FP4 approximation path that uses FP8 queries, FP4 KV tensors, UE8M0 group scales, and native scaled-MFMA support on CDNA4. On a long-context, multi-turn agentic workload, UltraQuant cuts P50 time-to-first-token by 3.47x in the cache-pressured late rounds (2.3x across all rounds) and raises output throughput by 1.63x over the FP8 KV baseline.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes UltraQuant, a 4-bit KV-cache compression method for multi-round agent workloads, achieving 3.47x lower TTFT in late rounds and 1.63x higher throughput over FP8 baselines on AMD GPUs.
AI.motivation: Context-heavy agent workloads place extreme pressure on KV cache due to long reused prefixes and high concurrency; existing FP8 caching is insufficient for throughput and latency under these conditions.
AI.method: Uses TurboQuant-style rotation and codebook quantization as quality anchor, with practical design choices: asymmetric K/V treatment, Walsh-Hadamard rotation, QJL removal, block-scale variants. Introduces UltraQuant: FP8 queries, FP4 KV tensors, UE8M0 group scales, scaled-MFMA on CDNA4.
AI.result: On long-context multi-turn agentic workload, UltraQuant reduces P50 TTFT by 3.47x in cache-pressured late rounds (2.3x overall) and increases output throughput by 1.63x over FP8 KV baseline.
AI.conclusion: 4-bit KV-cache compression is effective for agent workloads; careful design (asymmetric treatment, rotation, block-scales) plus hardware-aware optimizations (UltraQuant on AMD CDNA4) yield substantial latency and throughput gains.

title: Marginal Advantage Accumulation for Memory-Driven Agent Self-Evolution
summary: In batch-style trace distillation, the same memory operation may receive contradictory feedback across different batches. Existing methods lack a cross-batch, operation-level evidence accumulation mechanism, making it impossible to distinguish stably effective operations from accidental hits. This paper formalizes the requirement as two structural conditions, alignability and comparability, and proposes Marginal Advantage Accumulation (MAA). MAA constructs differential signals to make them comparable across batches, accumulates signed evidence per operation via EMA, and ensures cross-batch traceability through semantic identity merging. As a post-processing architecture, MAA achieves the best results in 14 out of 16 settings across 4 benchmarks and 4 target models, consistently outperforming existing batch-level distillation baselines and matching or surpassing online alternatives in most settings, while reducing optimization-phase token consumption by approximately 75%.

AI.tldr: MAA is a post-processing architecture that accumulates cross-batch, operation-level evidence via differential signals and EMA, achieving state-of-the-art results in batch-style trace distillation while reducing token consumption by ~75%.
AI.motivation: Existing batch-style trace distillation methods lack a cross-batch, operation-level evidence accumulation mechanism, making them unable to distinguish stably effective operations from accidental hits due to contradictory feedback across batches.
AI.method: MAA constructs differential signals to make operations comparable across batches, accumulates signed evidence per operation via EMA, and ensures cross-batch traceability through semantic identity merging.
AI.result: MAA achieves best results in 14/16 settings across 4 benchmarks and 4 target models, consistently outperforms batch-level baselines, matches/surpasses online alternatives, and reduces optimization-phase token consumption by ~75%.
AI.conclusion: Formalizing the requirement as alignability and comparability, MAA effectively solves the contradictory feedback problem in batch-style trace distillation with significant efficiency gains.

title: Contagion Networks: Evaluator Bias Propagation in Multi-Agent LLM Systems
summary: When large language models serve as evaluators in multi-agent systems, their systematic evaluation biases propagate through the agent network. We introduce Contagion Networks, a formal framework for measuring how evaluator biases spread across interacting LLM agents. In a controlled 3-agent experiment using DeepSeek-chat with three distinct evaluator bias profiles (structured, balanced, evidence-based), we measure the Cross-Agent Contagion Matrix Gamma_3 and find that evaluator biases consistently propagate between agents (gamma in [0.157, 0.352]), even within the same underlying model. We identify three propagation regimes governed by the spectral radius rho(Gamma_N), and demonstrate that homogeneous-model agents produce contagion coefficients 3-5x weaker than cross-model coefficients observed in prior work (MM-EPC: gamma approx 0.85-1.3), placing them in the suppression regime. We show that increasing evaluator committee size from k=1 to k=3 reduces effective contagion by 72.4%, providing an actionable mitigation strategy. We release the open-source Contagion Network experimental framework.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Contagion Networks to measure how evaluator biases propagate across LLM agents in multi-agent systems, finding that biases consistently spread (gamma 0.157-0.352) even among same-model agents, with homogeneous models showing 3-5x weaker contagion than cross-model settings. Increasing evaluator committee size from 1 to 3 reduces contagion by 72.4%.
AI.motivation: Large language models serving as evaluators in multi-agent systems exhibit systematic evaluation biases that can propagate through the agent network, yet there is no formal framework to measure this propagation effect.
AI.method: The authors propose Contagion Networks, a formal framework with a Cross-Agent Contagion Matrix Gamma_N and spectral radius rho(Gamma_N) to quantify bias propagation. A controlled 3-agent experiment using DeepSeek-chat with three evaluator bias profiles (structured, balanced, evidence-based) is conducted.
AI.result: Evaluator biases consistently propagate between agents (gamma in [0.157, 0.352]) even within the same underlying model. Homogeneous-model agents produce contagion coefficients 3-5x weaker than cross-model coefficients (MM-EPC: gamma approx 0.85-1.3). Increasing evaluator committee size from k=1 to k=3 reduces effective contagion by 72.4%.
AI.conclusion: Three propagation regimes exist governed by spectral radius rho(Gamma_N). Homogeneous models fall into the suppression regime. Committee size increase is an actionable mitigation strategy. The open-source Contagion Network experimental framework is released.

title: Execution-State Capsules: Graph-Bound Execution-State Checkpoint and Restore for Low-Latency, Small-Batch, On-Device Physical-AI Serving
summary: Mainstream LLM serving systems reuse prefix work mainly through paged or radix key-value (KV) caches. This is highly effective for high-throughput, high-concurrency serving, but it manages only one positional fragment of execution state: the KV cache. We study the opposite regime: low-latency, small-batch, on-device physical-AI serving, where interactive LLM agents, speech systems, and robot policies repeatedly branch, reset, interrupt, and re-enter under tight responsiveness budgets. We introduce execution-state capsules, a graph-bound checkpoint and restore mechanism for the complete restorable state at a committed boundary. FlashRT is a white-box, backend-facing kernel runtime whose evaluated NVIDIA CUDA backend runs captured graph plans over contiguous static buffers with no block-table indirection. Because the live state is a closed set of named buffers, a capsule can snapshot, restore, fork, or roll back the whole execution boundary, including KV, recurrent state, convolution state, MTP state, and metadata. This moves reuse from token-addressed KV fragments to graph-bound execution-state boundaries. On an RTX 5090, capsule restore is byte-exact at the stored-state level and token-identical under greedy decode. A KV-only ablation diverges, showing that recurrent state is load-bearing. GPU-resident snapshot and restore are sub-millisecond, and TTFT speedup over cold prefill grows from 3.9x at 2k tokens to 27x at 16k tokens. On Jetson AGX Thor and DGX Spark, the same correctness and structural properties hold. Capsules are not a replacement for high-throughput KV-cache serving; they define a complementary latency-first serving point for explicit execution-state reuse.

AI.tldr: FlashRT introduces execution-state capsules that enable complete checkpoint/restore of entire execution state (not just KV cache) for low-latency, on-device AI serving, achieving sub-millisecond GPU-resident snapshots with 3.9x-27x TTFT speedup over cold prefill.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM serving systems optimize for high-throughput, high-concurrency scenarios by reusing prefix work via paged or radix KV caches, but they manage only the KV cache fragment. This fails to address the needs of low-latency, small-batch, on-device physical-AI serving (interactive agents, speech systems, robot policies) where repeated branching, resetting, and interruption under tight responsiveness budgets require complete execution-state reuse beyond just KV cache.
AI.method: FlashRT introduces execution-state capsules as a graph-bound checkpoint and restore mechanism that captures the complete restorable state at a committed boundary. It uses a white-box, backend-facing kernel runtime with NVIDIA CUDA backend that runs captured graph plans over contiguous static buffers with no block-table indirection. The live state is a closed set of named buffers enabling snapshot, restore, fork, or rollback of the entire execution boundary including KV cache, recurrent state, convolution state, MTP state, and metadata.
AI.result: On RTX 5090, capsule restore achieves byte-exact correctness at stored-state level and token-identical under greedy decode. KV-only ablation diverges, confirming recurrent state is load-bearing. GPU-resident snapshot and restore complete in sub-millisecond. TTFT speedup over cold prefill grows from 3.9x (2k tokens) to 27x (16k tokens). Same correctness and structural properties hold on Jetson AGX Thor and DGX Spark.
AI.conclusion: Execution-state capsules are not a replacement for high-throughput KV-cache serving; they define a complementary latency-first serving paradigm for explicit execution-state reuse in on-device, interactive AI workloads.

title: Multi-Task Bayesian In-Context Learning
summary: Bayesian predictive inference provides a principled framework for uncertainty quantification, data efficiency, and robust generalization. However, exact inference is often intractable, and scalable approximations may remain computationally expensive or require restrictive modeling assumptions that degrade predictive performance. Prior-Data Fitted and in-context models have recently emerged as an amortized alternative by learning to map datasets directly to predictive distributions, but existing approaches are tightly coupled to the support of the training prior and lack explicit mechanisms for adapting to new priors at test time, resulting in limited robustness under distribution shift. We introduce a multi-task in-context learning framework for amortized hierarchical Bayesian predictive inference that explicitly represents prior information as a prefix of in-context datasets. A transformer trained on sequences of prior and target tasks learns to adapt its predictions across families of priors. On a suite of evaluations with increasing difficulty, including out-of-meta-distribution priors and priors with high-dimensional latent structures, our method matches oracle Bayesian predictors while being orders of magnitude faster. We further demonstrate its practical relevance on a real-world spatiotemporal temperature prediction benchmark. Code is available at https://github.com/martianmartina/multi-task-bayesian-icl/.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a multi-task in-context learning framework for amortized hierarchical Bayesian predictive inference that uses prior information as a prefix in in-context datasets, enabling adaptation across different priors at test time. The transformer-based method matches oracle Bayesian predictors with orders of magnitude faster speed, demonstrated on challenging evaluations including out-of-meta-distribution priors and real-world temperature prediction.
AI.motivation: Existing amortized Bayesian inference approaches (Prior-Data Fitted and in-context models) are tightly coupled to the support of the training prior and lack explicit mechanisms for adapting to new priors at test time, resulting in limited robustness under distribution shift.
AI.method: A multi-task in-context learning framework where a transformer is trained on sequences of prior and target tasks, explicitly representing prior information as a prefix of in-context datasets, enabling amortized hierarchical Bayesian predictive inference.
AI.result: The method matches oracle Bayesian predictors on evaluations with increasing difficulty (including out-of-meta-distribution priors and high-dimensional latent structures) while being orders of magnitude faster. Practical relevance demonstrated on real-world spatiotemporal temperature prediction.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-task in-context learning approach successfully addresses the robustness limitation of existing amortized Bayesian inference methods under prior distribution shift, achieving oracle-level performance with dramatic computational speedup.

title: Predictability as a Fine-Grained Measure for Privacy
summary: Differential privacy (DP) ensures rigorous individual-level privacy guarantees against even the most knowledgeable attackers, but its worst-case nature can impose a costly privacy-accuracy tradeoff. We introduce privacy via predictability, a fine-grained framework that explicitly incorporates the attacker's core knowledge, a compromised portion of the dataset generated by a stochastic process, and a specified family of queries. Predictability measures privacy leakage as the incremental gain in an attacker's ability to predict sensitive information about unknown individuals after observing the algorithm's output, beyond what can already be inferred from the compromised data. We show that predictability and DP are generally incomparable: each can be small while the other is large. However, in the worst-case regime where all but one individual is compromised, and all binary queries are considered sensitive, predictability implies mutual-information DP. More generally, predictability provides a finer-grained privacy metric tailored to specific sensitive information and specific attacker models. We introduce a general framework, using the generalized method of moments (GMM), to analyze asymptotic predictability when the compromised data is generated by a stationary, ergodic, mixing process. Using this analysis, we derive a predictability-calibrated output perturbation scheme for ERM. Our approach is complementary to DP and can be used alongside DP to provide fine-grained privacy control.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "privacy via predictability," a fine-grained framework that measures privacy leakage through an attacker's incremental predictive gain after observing algorithm outputs, given compromised background data. It is generally incomparable with DP but can imply mutual-information DP in worst-case scenarios, and provides a complementary, finer-grained privacy metric with a GMM-based analysis and calibrated perturbation scheme for ERM.
AI.motivation: Differential privacy's worst-case nature imposes a costly privacy-accuracy tradeoff. The paper aims to develop a finer-grained framework that explicitly accounts for the attacker's core knowledge (compromised stochastic data) and specific query families, enabling more tailored privacy-utility balances than DP's all-or-nothing approach.
AI.method: The paper defines "privacy via predictability" as the incremental gain in an attacker's predictive ability about sensitive information of unknown individuals after observing algorithm outputs, given compromised background data. It establishes theoretical relationships with DP, showing they are generally incomparable but converge under worst-case regimes. It provides a GMM-based asymptotic analysis for stationary, ergodic, mixing processes and derives a predictability-calibrated output perturbation scheme for empirical risk minimization (ERM).
AI.result: Predictability and DP are generally incomparable (each can be small while the other is large). In the worst-case regime (all but one compromised, all binary queries sensitive), predictability implies mutual-information DP. The GMM framework enables asymptotic predictability analysis for mixing processes, and the calibrated perturbation scheme for ERM is derived from this analysis.
AI.conclusion: Privacy via predictability offers a complementary, finer-grained privacy metric tailored to specific attacker models and sensitive information. It can be used alongside DP to provide more nuanced privacy control, potentially alleviating the harsh tradeoffs imposed by DP's worst-case guarantees.

title: Optimal Deterministic Multicalibration and Omniprediction
summary:
A model is multicalibrated on a collection of group weights $G$ if it is calibrated -- i.e. unbiased even conditional on its prediction -- not just overall, but also after reweighting contexts by each $g \in G$. It is a useful property for many downstream applications and is a basic desideratum of trustworthy machine learning. Before this work, all predictors known to attain the minimax-optimal $\widetilde O(\varepsilon^{-3})$ sample complexity rate for $\varepsilon$-multicalibration were randomized, while deterministic predictors were known only with substantially worse sample complexity. Whether randomization is necessary for optimal sample complexity in multicalibration was explicitly asked by [CLNR26] and implicitly in several prior works.
  We resolve this open problem by giving a minimax-optimal multicalibration algorithm that outputs a deterministic predictor. We then generalize the algorithm to produce optimal deterministic predictors that satisfy outcome indistinguishability (OI) with respect to finite or finitely covered collections of tests. As an application, this also gives deterministic omnipredictors and panpredictors with optimal sample complexity, resolving open problems posed by [OKK25] and [BHHLZ25].

AI.tldr: This paper resolves the open problem of whether randomization is necessary for optimal sample complexity in multicalibration, providing a deterministic predictor that achieves the minimax-optimal $\widetilde O(\varepsilon^{-3})$ rate, and extends this to outcome indistinguishability, omnipredictors, and panpredictors.
AI.motivation: Before this work, randomized predictors achieved the minimax-optimal sample complexity rate for multicalibration, while deterministic predictors had substantially worse rates. The question of whether randomization is necessary for optimal sample complexity was explicitly asked by prior works.
AI.method: The authors develop a new deterministic multicalibration algorithm that achieves the minimax-optimal $\widetilde O(\varepsilon^{-3})$ sample complexity, then generalize it to produce optimal deterministic predictors satisfying outcome indistinguishability for finite or finitely covered collections of tests.
AI.result: A deterministic predictor achieving minimax-optimal sample complexity for multicalibration, and generalizations to outcome indistinguishability, omnipredictors, and panpredictors with optimal sample complexity.
AI.conclusion: Randomization is not necessary for optimal sample complexity in multicalibration; deterministic predictors can achieve the same optimal rates, resolving multiple open problems in the literature.

title: How Transparent is DiffusionGemma?
comment: 20 main text pages and 6 pages of references and appendices
summary: LLM reasoning transparency is a critical affordance for understanding model decisions, mitigating misuse and misalignment, and debugging surprising model behaviors. However, DiffusionGemma performs a larger fraction of its computation in a continuous latent space; does this make its reasoning less transparent? We study this question by decomposing transparency into two components: variable transparency, whether we understand intermediate snapshots of a model's computational state; and algorithmic transparency, whether we can use these snapshots to reconstruct the process by which the model arrived at its outputs. Naively, DiffusionGemma has poor variable transparency: its opaque serial depth, the amount of serial computation that occurs in between interpretable model states, seems at first 28.6X higher than the corresponding autoregressive Gemma 4 model. However, we show that we can map the information flowing between denoising steps through an interpretable token bottleneck with no decrease in downstream performance. Treating these intermediate states as interpretable reduces the opaque serial depth to just 1.1X that of Gemma 4. Algorithmic transparency is harder for diffusion models than for autoregressive models because all token predictions in the canvas can change at every denoising step, giving the model the power to implement complicated distributed algorithms during the denoising process. To begin bridging this gap, we conduct a suite of interpretability case studies, uncovering initial evidence of novel diffusion-specific phenomena such as non-chronological reasoning, token and sequence smearing, and intermediate-context reasoning. Finally, we test monitorability, a key application of transparency that measures whether model outputs are useful for downstream tasks. We find that DiffusionGemma is similarly monitorable to Gemma 4.

AI.tldr: DiffusionGemma's reasoning transparency is analyzed across variable transparency, algorithmic transparency, and monitorability. Despite higher opaque serial depth, interpretable token bottlenecks reduce it to near Gemma 4 levels. Novel diffusion-specific phenomena (non-chronological reasoning, smearing) are identified. Monitorability is comparable to Gemma 4.
AI.motivation: LLM reasoning transparency is critical for understanding model decisions, mitigating misuse and misalignment, and debugging surprising behaviors. The paper investigates whether DiffusionGemma's continuous latent space computation makes its reasoning less transparent than autoregressive models like Gemma 4.
AI.method: Transparency is decomposed into variable transparency (understanding intermediate snapshots) and algorithmic transparency (reconstructing the reasoning process). The opaque serial depth is measured and reduced via interpretable token bottlenecks. A suite of interpretability case studies is conducted to uncover diffusion-specific phenomena.
AI.result: Naive opaque serial depth is 28.6X higher than Gemma 4, but token bottleneck mapping reduces it to 1.1X. Algorithmic transparency is harder due to all tokens changing per denoising step. Novel phenomena: non-chronological reasoning, token/sequence smearing, intermediate-context reasoning. Monitorability is similar to Gemma 4.
AI.conclusion: DiffusionGemma's reasoning transparency is more tractable than initially assumed after applying interpretable bottlenecks, though algorithmic transparency remains a challenge. The model is as monitorable as Gemma 4, suggesting diffusion-based architectures can achieve comparable transparency for downstream tasks.
